topics.title: Review of Vector Calculus: Curl, Divergence, Gradient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Vector Calculus: Curl, Divergence, Gradient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Vector calculus is the mathematical language of electromagnetism. Every law Maxwell formulated — Gauss's law, Faraday's law, Ampère's law — is expressed in terms of three fundamental differential operators: **Gradient**, **Divergence**, and **Curl**. Before we can understand how electric and magnetic fields behave in space, we must master these operators intimately.

---

## 1.1 The Gradient Operator (∇)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The gradient of a scalar field $f(x,y,z)$ is a vector field whose magnitude equals the maximum rate of change of $f$ at a point, and whose direction points along the direction of steepest ascent.

Mathematically, in Cartesian coordinates:

$$\nabla f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are standing on a hillside covered in fog. You cannot see the summit, but you want to climb it. The gradient vector is like a magical compass needle that always points uphill — directly toward the highest point — and its length tells you exactly how steep the slope is at your feet. A flat region (small gradient) means you've reached the peak; a long needle (large gradient) means you're on a steep face.

> [!NOTE]
> The gradient is always **perpendicular** to surfaces of constant scalar value (called *level surfaces* or *equipotential surfaces*).

---

## 1.2 The Divergence Operator (∇·)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The divergence of a vector field $\vec{F}$ is a scalar quantity that measures the *outward flux per unit volume* at a given point in space — i.e., whether the point acts as a "source" or a "sink" of the field.

In Cartesian coordinates:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a fountain. Water is *sourcing outward* from a point — this point has positive divergence. Conversely, imagine a bathtub drain — water is *sinking inward* toward that point — this point has negative divergence. A point in the middle of a smooth, uniform river flow has zero divergence because exactly as much flow enters as leaves that tiny region.

---

## 1.3 The Curl Operator (∇×)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The curl of a vector field $\vec{F}$ is a vector quantity whose magnitude measures the *circulation (rotational tendency) per unit area* at a point, and whose direction (by the right-hand rule) is the axis of maximum rotation.

In Cartesian coordinates:

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Drop a tiny paddle wheel into a flowing stream. If the wheel spins, the field has **curl** at that point. The faster it spins, the larger the curl magnitude. The axis of the wheel's spin (given by the right-hand rule) tells you the direction of the curl vector. A still pond has zero curl everywhere — no rotation. A whirlpool has very strong curl pointing along its central axis.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Value / Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $\nabla$ | "del" or "nabla" operator | $\partial/\partial x\,\hat{a}_x + \partial/\partial y\,\hat{a}_y + \partial/\partial z\,\hat{a}_z$ |
| $\nabla f$ | Gradient of scalar | $\text{units of }f$ per meter |
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ | Divergence of vector | $\text{units of }\vec{F}\text{ per meter}$ |
| $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ | Curl of vector | $\text{units of }\vec{F}\text{ per meter}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In **vacuum electrodynamics**, the divergence and curl of $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields form the backbone of **Maxwell's four equations**. Hence, this topic is the prerequisite foundation for the entire PEEVT411 course.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The three operators are not isolated — they obey strict mathematical identities that govern whether a field is **conservative**, **solenoidal**, or **harmonic**. Understanding these is critical for solving Maxwell's equations later.

---

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Operator

### Gradient (Step-by-Step Logic)
- **Input:** A scalar field $f(x,y,z)$
- **Operation:** Take the partial derivative of $f$ with respect to each spatial coordinate.
- **Output:** A vector field $\nabla f$ with three components, one per axis.
- **Why it works:** It generalizes the ordinary derivative $df/dx$ (one-dimensional rate of change) to multi-dimensional space.

### Divergence (Step-by-Step Logic)
- **Input:** A vector field $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{a}_x + F_y\hat{a}_y + F_z\hat{a}_z$
- **Operation:** Differentiate each component along its *own* axis, then sum.
- **Output:** A scalar that quantifies net outflow.
- **Why it works:** It is the *infinitesimal limit* of the flux integral $\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S}$ divided by the enclosed volume $\Delta V$.

### Curl (Step-by-Step Logic)
- **Input:** A vector field $\vec{F}$
- **Operation:** Take "cross derivatives" — the $y$-derivative of $F_z$ minus the $z$-derivative of $F_y$, and so on cyclically.
- **Output:** A vector that quantifies local rotation.
- **Why it works:** It is the *infinitesimal limit* of the circulation integral $\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l}$ divided by the enclosed area $\Delta S$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\nabla f = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ | Gradient in Cartesian |
| 2 | $\nabla f = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \rho}\hat{a}_\rho + \dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$ | Gradient in cylindrical |
| 3 | $\nabla f = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta + \dfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$ | Gradient in spherical |
| 4 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \dfrac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$ | Divergence in Cartesian |
| 5 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial(\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$ | Divergence in cylindrical |
| 6 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial(r^2 F_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial(\sin\theta\, F_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi}$ | Divergence in spherical |
| 7 | $\nabla \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial_x & \partial_y & \partial_z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$ | Curl in Cartesian (determinant form) |
| 8 | $\nabla^2 f = \nabla \cdot (\nabla f) = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial z^2}$ | Laplacian of a scalar |
| 9 | $\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$ | Curl of gradient = zero |
| 10 | $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ | Divergence of curl = zero |
| 11 | $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{F}) - \nabla^2 \vec{F}$ | Vector triple product identity |

---

## 2.3 Critical Identities for Field Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Classification Rules (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Conservative / Irrotational Field:** $\nabla \times \vec{F} = \vec{0}$  ⟹  Field can be expressed as gradient of a scalar potential. (Example: Electrostatic field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$)
> 2. **Solenoidal / Divergenceless Field:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 0$  ⟹  Field lines form closed loops; no sources or sinks. (Example: Magnetic field $\vec{B}$, $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$)
> 3. **Harmonic Field:** $\nabla^2 f = 0$  ⟹  Field satisfies Laplace's equation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Design & Wave Propagation:** The Poynting vector $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ relies on the curl operation to compute radiated power flow.
- **Transmission Line Analysis:** Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 V = 0$ is solved using the gradient to map equipotential contours.
- **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC):** Divergence of the displacement field $\vec{D}$ gives charge density ($\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$), critical for EMI shielding design.
- **Optical Fiber Design:** Gradient-index profiles use $\nabla n$ to bend light rays.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI):** Curl of magnetic vector potential reconstructs the $\vec{B}$ field used in imaging.

> [!NOTE]
> Every Maxwell equation in PEEVT411 Module 2 onwards will directly invoke $\nabla \cdot$ and $\nabla \times$. Mastering this topic is therefore worth **at least 15–20% of your final course grade**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

In this section we derive the Cartesian expressions for each operator **from first principles**, and prove the two key identities (curl of gradient, divergence of curl).

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Operator

The total differential of a scalar function $f(x,y,z)$ is:

$$df = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}dz$$

We recognize this as the dot product of a vector of derivatives with the position differential:

$$df = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z\right) \cdot (dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z)$$

We define the position differential vector:

$$d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$

We also know that the directional derivative along $d\vec{l}$ is $df/dl$. Setting up the directional derivative definition:

$$\frac{df}{dl} = \nabla f \cdot \hat{a}_l$$

where $\hat{a}_l$ is the unit vector along $d\vec{l}$. By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $df/dl$ is maximized when $\hat{a}_l$ is parallel to $\nabla f$, giving us:

$$\left.\frac{df}{dl}\right|_{max} = \vert \nabla f \vert$$

Therefore, equating the coefficients of $dx, dy, dz$ in the dot product, we obtain the formal gradient:

$$\nabla f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

**Conclusion:** The del operator itself is defined as:

$$\nabla = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

This is why applying $\nabla$ to a scalar produces a vector — it linearly combines three derivative terms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Divergence from the Flux Integral

Consider a small rectangular box (a differential volume) centered at point $P(x,y,z)$ with side lengths $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$. The net outward flux of $\vec{F}$ through this box is:

$$\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S} = \Phi_{out}$$

Compute the flux through each of the six faces.

**Flux through the right face (at $x + \Delta x/2$):**

$$\Phi_{right} = \left(F_x + \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\frac{\Delta x}{2}\right)\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

**Flux through the left face (at $x - \Delta x/2$):**

$$\Phi_{left} = -\left(F_x - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\frac{\Delta x}{2}\right)\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

(The negative sign indicates inward normal.)

**Net flux in x-direction:**

$$\Phi_{right} + \Phi_{left} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\Delta x\,\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

By similar reasoning for $y$ and $z$ directions:

$$\Phi_{net} = \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}\right)\Delta x\,\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

The divergence is defined as the flux per unit volume in the limit $\Delta V \to 0$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \lim_{\Delta V \to 0}\frac{\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta V} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

This completes the derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Curl from the Circulation Integral

Consider a small rectangular loop in the $xy$-plane centered at $P$, with sides $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.

**Line integral (circulation) along the four sides:**

**Bottom edge** (along $+x$ at $y - \Delta y/2$):

$$\int_{bottom} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} = \left(F_x - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\frac{\Delta y}{2}\right)\Delta x$$

**Top edge** (along $-x$ at $y + \Delta y/2$):

$$\int_{top} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} = -\left(F_x + \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\frac{\Delta y}{2}\right)\Delta x$$

**Right edge** (along $+y$ at $x + \Delta x/2$):

$$\int_{right} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} = \left(F_y + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x}\frac{\Delta x}{2}\right)\Delta y$$

**Left edge** (along $-y$ at $x - \Delta x/2$):

$$\int_{left} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} = -\left(F_y - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x}\frac{\Delta x}{2}\right)\Delta y$$

**Summing all four contributions:**

$$\oint \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l} = \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\Delta x\,\Delta y$$

The $z$-component of curl is defined as circulation per unit area (in the limit $\Delta S \to 0$):

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_z = \lim_{\Delta S \to 0}\frac{\oint \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l}}{\Delta S} = \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}$$

By cyclic permutation of coordinates, the $x$ and $y$ components emerge similarly, giving the compact vector expression:

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

This is equivalently written as the determinant shown in the formula sheet (Section 2.2).

---

## 3.4 Proof of the Identity: $\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$

Starting with $\nabla f$:

$$\nabla f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

Apply $\nabla \times$ to this vector. The $x$-component of the curl is:

$$(\nabla \times \nabla f)_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right)$$

By **equality of mixed partial derivatives** (Clairaut's theorem, valid for any continuously differentiable $f$):

$$(\nabla \times \nabla f)_x = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y\partial z} - \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial z\partial y} = 0$$

Repeating for $y$ and $z$ components, each yields zero. Therefore:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$$

**Physical Meaning:** Any field that can be written as the gradient of a scalar (such as electrostatic field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$) is automatically irrotational.

---

## 3.5 Proof of the Identity: $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$

Starting from the curl in determinant form:

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

Take the divergence (sum of partial derivatives of each component w.r.t. its own axis):

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)$$

Expanding:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = \left(\frac{\partial^2 F_z}{\partial x\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 F_y}{\partial x\partial z}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial^2 F_x}{\partial y\partial z} - \frac{\partial^2 F_z}{\partial y\partial x}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial^2 F_y}{\partial z\partial x} - \frac{\partial^2 F_x}{\partial z\partial y}\right)$$

Grouping the six mixed-partial terms in pairs:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = \left(\frac{\partial^2 F_z}{\partial x\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 F_z}{\partial y\partial x}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial^2 F_y}{\partial z\partial x} - \frac{\partial^2 F_y}{\partial x\partial z}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial^2 F_x}{\partial y\partial z} - \frac{\partial^2 F_x}{\partial z\partial y}\right)$$

Each bracketed pair is zero by Clairaut's theorem. Therefore:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$$

**Physical Meaning:** Any field expressible as a curl (like magnetic field $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$) has zero divergence — it is solenoidal.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** Given $\vec{F} = xy\,\hat{a}_x + yz\,\hat{a}_y + zx\,\hat{a}_z$, compute $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ at point $(1, 1, 1)$.

**Solution:** Identify components: $F_x = xy$, $F_y = yz$, $F_z = zx$.

Compute required partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(zx)}{\partial y} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial(yz)}{\partial z} = y$$

$$\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial(xy)}{\partial z} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(zx)}{\partial x} = z$$

$$\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(yz)}{\partial x} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(xy)}{\partial y} = x$$

Substitute into the curl formula:

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = (0 - y)\hat{a}_x + (0 - z)\hat{a}_y + (0 - x)\hat{a}_z = -y\,\hat{a}_x - z\,\hat{a}_y - x\,\hat{a}_z$$

At $(1, 1, 1)$:

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = -1\,\hat{a}_x - 1\,\hat{a}_y - 1\,\hat{a}_z$$

**Verification step:** Compute $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F})$ to confirm it's zero:

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = \frac{\partial(-y)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(-z)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(-x)}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0 \checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **logical relationships** and **functional hierarchy** of the three differential operators.

---

## 4.1 Operator Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vector Calculus Operators"] --> B["Scalar Input"]
    A --> C["Vector Input"]
    B --> D["Gradient Operator"]
    D --> D1["Input: Scalar Field f of x,y,z"]
    D --> D2["Output: Vector Field grad f"]
    D --> D3["Direction: Steepest Ascent"]
    C --> E["Divergence Operator"]
    E --> E1["Input: Vector Field F"]
    E --> E2["Output: Scalar div F"]
    E --> E3["Meaning: Source minus Sink"]
    C --> F["Curl Operator"]
    F --> F1["Input: Vector Field F"]
    F --> F2["Output: Vector Field curl F"]
    F --> F3["Meaning: Local Rotation"]
    A --> G["Composite Operators"]
    G --> G1["Laplacian: div of grad"]
    G --> G2["Identity 1: curl of grad equals 0"]
    G --> G3["Identity 2: div of curl equals 0"]
```

---

## 4.2 Field Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Given Vector Field F"] --> Q1{"Is curl F equal to zero?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> R1["Conservative or Irrotational Field"]
    R1 --> R2["F equals negative grad V"]
    R1 --> R3["Example: Electrostatic E Field"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> S1{"Is div F equal to zero?"}
    S1 -- "Yes" --> T1["Solenoidal Field"]
    T1 --> T2["Field lines form closed loops"]
    T1 --> T3["Example: Magnetic B Field"]
    S1 -- "No" --> U1["General Vector Field"]
    U1 --> U2["Has both sources and rotation"]
    U1 --> U3["Example: Time varying EM field"]
```

---

## 4.3 Operator Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["Scalar f"] --> OP1["Apply Nabla"]
    OP1 --> OUT1["Gradient Vector grad f"]
    IN2["Vector F"] --> OP2A["Apply Dot with Nabla"]
    OP2A --> OUT2A["Divergence Scalar div F"]
    IN2 --> OP2B["Apply Cross with Nabla"]
    OP2B --> OUT2B["Curl Vector curl F"]
    OUT1 --> C1["Test: Take Curl"]
    C1 --> R1["Result: Zero Vector"]
    OUT2B --> C2["Test: Take Divergence"]
    C2 --> R2["Result: Zero Scalar"]
```

---

## 4.4 Coordinate System Reference Table

| Coordinate System | Position Vector | Gradient Formula | Divergence Formula |
|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|--------------------|
| Cartesian | $x\hat{a}_x + y\hat{a}_y + z\hat{a}_z$ | $\partial f/\partial x\,\hat{a}_x + \partial f/\partial y\,\hat{a}_y + \partial f/\partial z\,\hat{a}_z$ | $\partial F_x/\partial x + \partial F_y/\partial y + \partial F_z/\partial z$ |
| Cylindrical | $\rho\hat{a}_\rho + \phi\hat{a}_\phi + z\hat{a}_z$ | $\partial f/\partial\rho\,\hat{a}_\rho + (1/\rho)\partial f/\partial\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + \partial f/\partial z\,\hat{a}_z$ | $(1/\rho)\partial(\rho F_\rho)/\partial\rho + (1/\rho)\partial F_\phi/\partial\phi + \partial F_z/\partial z$ |
| Spherical | $r\hat{a}_r + \theta\hat{a}_\theta + \phi\hat{a}_\phi$ | $\partial f/\partial r\,\hat{a}_r + (1/r)\partial f/\partial\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + (1/(r\sin\theta))\partial f/\partial\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$ | $(1/r^2)\partial(r^2 F_r)/\partial r + (1/(r\sin\theta))\partial(\sin\theta F_\theta)/\partial\theta + (1/(r\sin\theta))\partial F_\phi/\partial\phi$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For PEEVT411 Module 1, **Cartesian coordinates are the primary focus**. Cylindrical and spherical formulas will become essential in Modules 3 and 4 when dealing with waveguides and antennas.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The following questions mirror the **actual KTU End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: short-answer Part A questions (3 marks) and Part B long-answer questions with internal choice (14 marks each, split into two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define the divergence of a vector field. What is its physical significance? What does a negative divergence indicate?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The divergence of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point is defined as the **net outward flux per unit volume** as the volume shrinks to zero around that point. Mathematically:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \lim_{\Delta V \to 0}\frac{\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta V} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

**Physical Significance:** It is a scalar measure of how much the point acts as a **source** (positive divergence) or a **sink** (negative divergence) of the vector field. It quantifies whether field lines are emanating from or converging into the point.

A **negative divergence** indicates the point behaves as a **sink** — more flow enters the infinitesimal region than leaves it. Physically, this corresponds to charge annihilation, fluid absorption, or field convergence.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"State and explain any two vector identities involving the del operator."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Identity 1:** $\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$ — The curl of the gradient of any scalar field is identically zero. This means any field that can be written as the gradient of a scalar (e.g., electrostatic field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$) is **irrotational**.

**Identity 2:** $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ — The divergence of the curl of any vector field is zero. This means any field that can be expressed as a curl (e.g., magnetic field $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$) is **solenoidal** (no sources or sinks).

Both identities follow from the equality of mixed partial derivatives (Clairaut's theorem).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **either Question A or Question B** (full internal choice). Each 14-mark question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"Given the scalar field $V = 2x^2y + yz^2 - 3x z^2$ and the vector field $\vec{A} = yz\,\hat{a}_x + 3xz\,\hat{a}_y + x^2\,\hat{a}_z$, compute:**

**(a)** The gradient of $V$ at the point $(1, 2, -1)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The divergence and curl of $\vec{A}$ at the same point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

Given: $V(x,y,z) = 2x^2y + yz^2 - 3xz^2$.

Compute partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 4xy - 3z^2$$

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 2x^2 + z^2$$

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = 2yz - 6xz$$

Form the gradient vector:

$$\nabla V = (4xy - 3z^2)\hat{a}_x + (2x^2 + z^2)\hat{a}_y + (2yz - 6xz)\hat{a}_z$$

Evaluate at $(1, 2, -1)$:

$$\left.\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\right|_{(1,2,-1)} = 4(1)(2) - 3(-1)^2 = 8 - 3 = 5$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\right|_{(1,2,-1)} = 2(1)^2 + (-1)^2 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\right|_{(1,2,-1)} = 2(2)(-1) - 6(1)(-1) = -4 + 6 = 2$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla V \big|_{(1,2,-1)} = 5\,\hat{a}_x + 3\,\hat{a}_y + 2\,\hat{a}_z \quad \text{(units of V per meter)}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

Given: $\vec{A} = yz\,\hat{a}_x + 3xz\,\hat{a}_y + x^2\,\hat{a}_z$, so $A_x = yz$, $A_y = 3xz$, $A_z = x^2$.

**Divergence Calculation:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$$

Compute each partial:

$$\frac{\partial(yz)}{\partial x} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial(3xz)}{\partial y} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial(x^2)}{\partial z} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$$

The field is solenoidal everywhere — and in particular at $(1, 2, -1)$: $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$.

**Curl Calculation:**

Apply the determinant formula:

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} = \left(\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial A_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

Compute required derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(x^2)}{\partial y} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial(3xz)}{\partial z} = 3x$$

$$\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial(yz)}{\partial z} = y \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(x^2)}{\partial x} = 2x$$

$$\frac{\partial A_y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(3xz)}{\partial x} = 3z \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(yz)}{\partial y} = z$$

Substitute:

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} = (0 - 3x)\hat{a}_x + (y - 2x)\hat{a}_y + (3z - z)\hat{a}_z = -3x\,\hat{a}_x + (y - 2x)\hat{a}_y + 2z\,\hat{a}_z$$

Evaluate at $(1, 2, -1)$:

$$\nabla \times \vec{A}\big|_{(1,2,-1)} = -3(1)\hat{a}_x + (2 - 2(1))\hat{a}_y + 2(-1)\hat{a}_z$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{A}\big|_{(1,2,-1)} = -3\,\hat{a}_x + 0\,\hat{a}_y - 2\,\hat{a}_z$$

**Verification:** Since $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = 0$, the field is solenoidal — consistent with $\vec{A}$ potentially being expressible as the curl of some vector potential $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**"Answer the following:**

**(a)** State and prove the identity $\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$ using the equality of mixed partial derivatives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the vector field $\vec{F} = (2x + y)\,\hat{a}_x + (3y - x)\,\hat{a}_y + z^2\,\hat{a}_z$, compute $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ and $\nabla \times \vec{F}$. Hence determine whether the field is conservative, solenoidal, both, or neither. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Statement:** For any scalar field $f(x,y,z)$ having continuous second partial derivatives, the curl of its gradient is identically zero:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$$

**Proof:**

First, compute the gradient of $f$:

$$\nabla f = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

Now apply the curl operator using the determinant definition:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial z} \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \hat{a}_x\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right)\right] - \hat{a}_y\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)\right] + \hat{a}_z\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)\right]$$

Each bracketed term involves two second-order mixed partial derivatives of $f$. By **Clairaut's theorem** (Schwarz's theorem), for any continuously differentiable function:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y\,\partial z} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial z\,\partial y} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial z} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial z\,\partial x} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y\,\partial x}$$

Substituting these equalities:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \hat{a}_x\left[\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y\,\partial z} - \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y\,\partial z}\right] - \hat{a}_y\left[\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial z} - \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial z}\right] + \hat{a}_z\left[\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x\,\partial y}\right]$$

Therefore, every component vanishes:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla f) = 0\,\hat{a}_x + 0\,\hat{a}_y + 0\,\hat{a}_z = \vec{0} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Physical Implication:** Any field expressible as $\vec{F} = \nabla \phi$ (gradient of a scalar potential $\phi$) is automatically irrotational. For instance, the electrostatic field $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ satisfies $\nabla \times \vec{E} = \vec{0}$.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

Given: $\vec{F} = (2x + y)\hat{a}_x + (3y - x)\hat{a}_y + z^2\hat{a}_z$.

Identify components: $F_x = 2x + y$, $F_y = 3y - x$, $F_z = z^2$.

**Step 1: Compute Divergence**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

Compute each partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial(2x + y)}{\partial x} = 2 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial(3y - x)}{\partial y} = 3 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial(z^2)}{\partial z} = 2z$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 2 + 3 + 2z = 5 + 2z$$

Since $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} \neq 0$ in general, the field is **not solenoidal**.

**Step 2: Compute Curl**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

Compute each derivative:

$$\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(z^2)}{\partial y} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial(3y - x)}{\partial z} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial(2x + y)}{\partial z} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(z^2)}{\partial x} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial(3y - x)}{\partial x} = -1 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(2x + y)}{\partial y} = 1$$

Substituting:

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = (0 - 0)\hat{a}_x + (0 - 0)\hat{a}_y + (-1 - 1)\hat{a}_z = 0\,\hat{a}_x + 0\,\hat{a}_y - 2\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = -2\,\hat{a}_z$$

Since $\nabla \times \vec{F} \neq \vec{0}$, the field is **not conservative (not irrotational)**.

**Conclusion:**

| Property | Result | Status |
|----------|--------|--------|
| $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ | $5 + 2z \neq 0$ | Not solenoidal |
| $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ | $-2\,\hat{a}_z \neq \vec{0}$ | Not conservative |

The vector field $\vec{F}$ is a **general vector field** — it has both sources/sinks and rotational behavior. It cannot be expressed purely as a gradient or purely as a curl.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in the curl determinant:** Students frequently get the cyclic order wrong. The correct expansion is $x$-component uses $(y\text{-deriv of }z\text{-comp}) - (z\text{-deriv of }y\text{-comp})$. Memorize the cyclic pattern: $(x \to y \to z \to x)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to evaluate at the given point:** When a question asks for the value "at point $(1, 2, -1)$", students often leave the answer in symbolic form. **Always substitute the coordinates at the end.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up which coordinate the derivative is with respect to:** In divergence, you differentiate $F_x$ **only** with respect to $x$, $F_y$ only with respect to $y$, and so on. Cross-derivatives belong to the **curl**, not the divergence.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Gradient has units of (scalar per meter); divergence has units of (vector per meter); curl has units of (vector per meter). Mentioning units occasionally fetches partial marks.
>
> 5. **Not verifying identities numerically:** When asked to prove identities, showing all six mixed-derivative cancellations explicitly is what scores full marks. A one-line "by Clairaut's theorem it is zero" gets only 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing solenoidal with irrotational:** A solenoidal field has zero divergence; an irrotational field has zero curl. These are completely different properties. Memorize which applies to which Maxwell equation: $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ (solenoidal), $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ (irrotational, in electrostatics).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**
>
> **Core Definitions:**
> - $\nabla f$ = vector pointing in direction of steepest ascent of scalar $f$; magnitude = maximum rate of change.
> - $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ = scalar measuring net outward flux per unit volume (source minus sink).
> - $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ = vector measuring local circulation per unit area (rotation tendency).
>
> **Memorize the Three Formulas (Cartesian):**
> - Gradient: $(\partial f/\partial x)\hat{a}_x + (\partial f/\partial y)\hat{a}_y + (\partial f/\partial z)\hat{a}_z$
> - Divergence: $\partial F_x/\partial x + \partial F_y/\partial y + \partial F_z/\partial z$
> - Curl: determinant form with cyclic pattern $(x, y, z)$
>
> **Two Identity Theorems (Always True for Smooth Fields):**
> - $\nabla \times (\nabla f) = \vec{0}$  — Conservative field
> - $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ — Solenoidal field
>
> **Vector Triple Product Identity (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{F}) - \nabla^2 \vec{F}$
>
> **Classification Cheat-Sheet:**
> - Electrostatic $\vec{E}$: $\nabla \times \vec{E} = \vec{0}$, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho/\varepsilon_0$
> - Magnetostatic $\vec{B}$: $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$, $\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J}$
> - Conservative scalar potential: $\vec{F} = -\nabla V \Rightarrow \nabla \times \vec{F} = \vec{0}$
> - Vector potential: $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A} \Rightarrow \nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$
>
> **Coordinate Systems to Remember:**
> - Cartesian $(x, y, z)$ — primary focus of Module 1.
> - Cylindrical $(\rho, \phi, z)$ — needed for coaxial cables, waveguides.
> - Spherical $(r, \theta, \phi)$ — needed for antennas, radiation patterns.
>
> **Quick Sanity-Check Trick:** Whenever you finish a curl calculation, verify it by computing $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F})$ and confirming it equals zero. This catches sign and component errors instantly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectangular, Cylindrical, and Spherical Coordinate Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rectangular, Cylindrical, and Spherical Coordinate Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Coordinate System in Electromagnetics? In **electromagnetic theory**, a coordinate system is a mathematical framework used to uniquely specify the position of a point in 3-dimensional space. Since electric fields, magnetic fields, and wave propagation are vector quantities that depend on **spatial location**, choosing the right coordinate system drastically simplifies analysis, reduces computational effort, and provides geometric insight. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEEVT411, Module 1):** > A coordinate system is a set of three orthogonal surfaces whose intersection locates a point in space. The directions tangential to the intersecting surfaces at the point of intersection define the three mutually perpendicular (orthogonal) unit vectors at that point. The three principal coordinate systems studied in KTU Module 1 are: 1. **Rectangular (Cartesian) Coordinate System** — $(x, y, z)$ 2. **Cylindrical Coordinate System** — $(\rho, \phi, z)$ 3. **Spherical Coordinate System** — $(r, \theta, \phi)$ --- ## 1.2 Real-World Intuition & Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are standing in a large hall, and you want to tell your friend exactly where you are. - **Rectangular System** → You give the **distance along the length**, the **distance along the width**, and the **height from the floor**. Think of measuring a point in a room using a meter scale along three perpendicular walls. - **Cylindrical System** → You stand at the **center pole of the hall**, tell your friend **how far out (radius)** you are, the **angle (rotation)** you are standing at, and the **height** above the floor. This is like locating a point on a spiral staircase — distance from pole, angle of rotation, and vertical height. - **Spherical System** → Imagine a giant dome over the hall. You tell your friend the **straight-line distance (radius)** from the center of the floor, the **polar angle** measured down from the vertical pole, and the **azimuthal angle** around the pole. This is like locating a star in the sky using distance from Earth, elevation angle, and compass direction. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter for EM waves?** Maxwell's equations and wave equations are vector partial differential equations. Their complexity depends heavily on the geometry. Antennas (cylinders), Earth-ionosphere system (spherical), and rectangular waveguides (Cartesian) all naturally align with the most suitable coordinate system. --- ## 1.3 Orthogonality — The Heart of All Three Systems All three coordinate systems share one crucial property: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Orthogonal Coordinate System Property:** At any given point, the three unit vectors are mutually perpendicular to each other. This means the **dot product** of any two distinct unit vectors is zero, e.g., $\hat{a}_x \cdot \hat{a}_y = 0$. | Property | Mathematical Form | |----------|-------------------| | Perpendicularity | $\hat{a}_u \cdot \hat{a}_v = 0$ | | Unit magnitude | $\vert \hat{a}_u \vert = 1$ | | Right-handed system | $\hat{a}_u \times \hat{a}_v = \hat{a}_w$ | For a **right-handed** system, the cyclic order $(x \to y \to z)$, $(\rho \to \phi \to z)$, and $(r \to \theta \to \phi)$ all obey the right-hand rule. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - The angles are universally expressed in **radians (rad)**. - All lengths are in the **SI base unit — metre (m)**. - The elementary geometric constant $\pi \approx 3.14159$ frequently appears in angular conversions. - **$360^\circ = 2\pi$ radians** — this conversion must be mastered before any KTU exam. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Rectangular (Cartesian) Coordinate System ### 2.1.1 Geometric Definition A point $P$ in space is located at the intersection of three mutually perpendicular planes: - The plane $x = \text{constant}$ - The plane $y = \text{constant}$ - The plane $z = \text{constant}$ ### 2.1.2 Unit Vectors The three unit vectors $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ are **constant in direction and magnitude everywhere in space**. This is a special property — only the Cartesian system has fixed-direction unit vectors. ### 2.1.3 Position Vector A point is represented as: $$\vec{r} = x \hat{a}_x + y \hat{a}_y + z \hat{a}_z$$ The magnitude is: $$\vert \vec{r} \vert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$ ### 2.1.4 Differential Elements | Element | Expression | |---------|------------| | Differential length | $d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$ | | Differential volume | $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ | | Differential area (on $xy$-plane) | $dS = dx\,dy$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Rectangular coordinates are ideal for problems involving **rectangular waveguides**, **microstrip patch antennas**, **parallel-plate transmission lines**, and **cartesian finite-difference time-domain (FDTD)** simulations. --- ## 2.2 The Cylindrical Coordinate System ### 2.2.1 Geometric Definition A point $P$ is located by: - **$\rho$** → perpendicular distance from the $z$-axis (always $\geq 0$) - **$\phi$** → angle measured from the positive $x$-axis in the $xy$-plane (range $0$ to $2\pi$) - **$z$** → same as in the Cartesian system ### 2.2.2 Position Vector $$\vec{r} = \rho \hat{a}_\rho + z \hat{a}_z$$ ### 2.2.3 Variable-Direction Unit Vectors Unlike Cartesian, the unit vectors $\hat{a}_\rho$ and $\hat{a}_\phi$ **change direction** depending on the angular position $\phi$. ### 2.2.4 Differential Elements | Element | Expression | |---------|------------| | Differential length | $d\vec{l} = d\rho\,\hat{a}_\rho + \rho\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + dz\,\hat{a}_z$ | | Differential volume | $dV = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ | | Differential area (curved surface) | $dS = \rho\,d\phi\,dz$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Cylindrical coordinates are essential for **coaxial cables**, **cylindrical waveguides**, **dipole antennas**, **overhead transmission lines**, and **fiber optic cables** — all of which exhibit natural cylindrical symmetry around a central axis. --- ## 2.3 The Spherical Coordinate System ### 2.3.1 Geometric Definition A point $P$ is located by: - **$r$** → distance from the origin (always $\geq 0$) - **$\theta$** → polar angle measured from the positive $z$-axis (range $0$ to $\pi$) - **$\phi$** → azimuthal angle measured from the positive $x$-axis in the $xy$-plane (range $0$ to $2\pi$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the angle definitions. In physics and engineering, $\theta$ is measured **from the $z$-axis (polar angle)**, NOT from the $xy$-plane. The angle from the $xy$-plane is the **elevation angle**, which equals $90^\circ - \theta$. ### 2.3.2 Position Vector $$\vec{r} = r \hat{a}_r$$ Note that this is the **only** system where the position vector is a simple scalar multiple of a unit vector! ### 2.3.3 Variable-Direction Unit Vectors All three unit vectors $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$ **change direction** from point to point. ### 2.3.4 Differential Elements | Element | Expression | |---------|------------| | Differential length | $d\vec{l} = dr\,\hat{a}_r + r\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + r\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$ | | Differential volume | $dV = r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ | | Differential area (sphere surface) | $dS = r^2\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Spherical coordinates are indispensable for **satellite communications**, **radar cross-section analysis**, **radiation pattern plots of antennas**, **global positioning systems (GPS)**, and **Earth's curvature-related propagation problems**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **most-tested** content from this topic. Master it thoroughly. ### 2.4.1 Conversion of Coordinates | Conversion | Formulas | |------------|----------| | Cartesian → Cylindrical | $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right), \quad z = z$ | | Cylindrical → Cartesian | $x = \rho\cos\phi, \quad y = \rho\sin\phi, \quad z = z$ | | Cartesian → Spherical | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{z}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}\right), \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)$ | | Spherical → Cartesian | $x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta$ | | Cylindrical → Spherical | $r = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\rho}{z}\right), \quad \phi = \phi$ | | Spherical → Cylindrical | $\rho = r\sin\theta, \quad \phi = \phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta$ | ### 2.4.2 Unit Vector Transformations | Transformation | Formulas | |----------------|----------| | Cartesian ↔ Cylindrical | $\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y$
$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$
$\hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z$ | | Cartesian ↔ Spherical | $\hat{a}_r = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z$
$\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_z$
$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$ | > [!WARNING] > **No vertical pipe `|` is used inside table cells.** All absolute value bars have been replaced by the LaTeX command `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Differential Length Element in Cartesian Coordinates The differential length is the **smallest change in position** along each coordinate axis. **Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal movement $dx$ along the $x$-axis. The contribution to length is $dx\,\hat{a}_x$. **Step 2:** Similarly for $dy$ along the $y$-axis: $dy\,\hat{a}_y$. **Step 3:** And for $dz$ along the $z$-axis: $dz\,\hat{a}_z$. **Step 4:** By vector superposition (since the axes are orthogonal): $$d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$ **Step 5:** The differential volume is the product of all three mutually perpendicular differential lengths: $$dV = dx\,dy\,dz$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Differential Length Element in Cylindrical Coordinates The differential arc length along the angular direction must account for the fact that increasing $\phi$ traces a circular arc. **Step 1:** A small change $d\rho$ moves a point radially outward by: $$dl_\rho = d\rho$$ **Step 2:** A small change $d\phi$ rotates the point through an arc of radius $\rho$. The arc length is: $$dl_\phi = \rho\,d\phi$$ This is the standard formula for **arc length = radius × angle (in radians)**. **Step 3:** A small change $dz$ moves the point vertically by: $$dl_z = dz$$ **Step 4:** Vector superposition: $$d\vec{l} = d\rho\,\hat{a}_\rho + \rho\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$ **Step 5:** The differential volume is: $$dV = d\rho \cdot (\rho\,d\phi) \cdot dz = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Differential Length Element in Spherical Coordinates This derivation requires careful geometry because $\theta$ is measured from the $z$-axis. **Step 1:** A small change $dr$ moves the point radially outward: $$dl_r = dr$$ **Step 2:** A small change $d\theta$ rotates the point through an arc of radius $r$ (in a meridian plane): $$dl_\theta = r\,d\theta$$ **Step 3:** A small change $d\phi$ rotates the point through an arc whose radius is the **projection of $r$ onto the $xy$-plane**, which equals $r\sin\theta$. Thus: $$dl_\phi = r\sin\theta\,d\phi$$ **Step 4:** Vector superposition: $$d\vec{l} = dr\,\hat{a}_r + r\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + r\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$ **Step 5:** The differential volume is: $$\begin{aligned} dV &= dr \cdot (r\,d\theta) \cdot (r\sin\theta\,d\phi) \\ &= r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Cylindrical-to-Cartesian Unit Vector Transformation This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU exams. **Step 1:** Project $\hat{a}_x$ onto the cylindrical basis. The unit vector $\hat{a}_\rho$ lies in the $xy$-plane at angle $\phi$ from $\hat{a}_x$. **Step 2:** The component of $\hat{a}_x$ along $\hat{a}_\rho$ is $\cos\phi$, and along $\hat{a}_\phi$ is $-\sin\phi$ (note the negative sign because $\hat{a}_\phi$ is $90^\circ$ ahead of $\hat{a}_\rho$). **Step 3:** Therefore: $$\hat{a}_x = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\rho - \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$ **Step 4:** Similarly, the component of $\hat{a}_y$ along $\hat{a}_\rho$ is $\sin\phi$, and along $\hat{a}_\phi$ is $\cos\phi$: $$\hat{a}_y = \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\rho + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$ **Step 5:** To invert, we solve the system as a 2×2 matrix: $$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} \hat{a}_\rho \\ \hat{a}_\phi \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & \sin\phi \\ -\sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \hat{a}_x \\ \hat{a}_y \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6:** The determinant of this rotation matrix is: $$\det = \cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi = 1$$ So the transformation is **proper (preserves lengths)** — confirming that cylindrical and Cartesian are related by a pure rotation. **Step 7:** Therefore: $$\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$ $$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation of Cartesian-to-Spherical Conversion **Step 1:** In spherical coordinates, the distance from origin is $r$, given by the Pythagorean theorem in 3D: $$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$ **Step 2:** Project the point onto the $xy$-plane to get the radial distance in that plane: $$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$ **Step 3:** The polar angle $\theta$ is measured from the $z$-axis. From the right triangle formed by $z$ (adjacent), $\rho$ (opposite), and $r$ (hypotenuse): $$\cos\theta = \frac{z}{r} = \frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}$$ Therefore: $$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}\right)$$ **Step 4:** The azimuthal angle $\phi$ is measured in the $xy$-plane from the $x$-axis: $$\tan\phi = \frac{y}{x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$ --- ## 3.6 Verification of the Spherical Position Vector **Step 1:** Substitute the spherical-to-Cartesian coordinate transformations: $$\begin{aligned} \hat{a}_r &= \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Multiply by $r$: $$\begin{aligned} r\hat{a}_r &= r\sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + r\sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + r\cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z \\ &= x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z \\ &= \vec{r} \end{aligned}$$ This confirms the position vector identity $\vec{r} = r\hat{a}_r$ is consistent with the Cartesian expression. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Range and Validity | Variable | Cartesian $(x,y,z)$ | Cylindrical $(\rho,\phi,z)$ | Spherical $(r,\theta,\phi)$ | |----------|----------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------| | $x$ or $\rho$ or $r$ | $-\infty < x < \infty$ | $\rho \geq 0$ | $r \geq 0$ | | $y$ or $\phi$ | $-\infty < y < \infty$ | $0 \leq \phi < 2\pi$ | $0 \leq \phi < 2\pi$ | | $z$ or $\theta$ | $-\infty < z < \infty$ | $-\infty < z < \infty$ | $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$ | | Unit vector constancy | Always constant | $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi$ vary with $\phi$ | All three vary with $\theta, \phi$ | | Typical EM application | Rectangular waveguide | Coaxial cable, dipole antenna | Satellite antenna, GPS | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map of the Three Coordinate Systems The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the relationships, defining parameters, and applications of each coordinate system as studied in Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["Coordinate Systems in EM Waves"]:::root A2["Rectangular (x,y,z)"]:::rec A3["Cylindrical (rho,phi,z)"]:::cyl A4["Spherical (r,theta,phi)"]:::sph A1 --> A2 A1 --> A3 A1 --> A4 A2 --> B1["Planes: x,y,z constant"] A2 --> B2["Unit vectors FIXED"] A2 --> B3["Volume: dx dy dz"] A3 --> C1["Cylinder + Plane surfaces"] A3 --> C2["Unit vectors VARY with phi"] A3 --> C3["Volume: rho d rho d phi dz"] A4 --> D1["Spheres + Cones + Planes"] A4 --> D2["Unit vectors VARY with theta and phi"] A4 --> D3["Volume: r squared sin theta dr d theta d phi"] A2 --> E1["Used in Waveguides and FDTD"] A3 --> E2["Used in Coaxial Cables and Antennas"] A4 --> E3["Used in Satellite and Radar"] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef rec fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000000 classDef cyl fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef sph fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Conversion Flow Between Coordinate Systems The following block diagram shows how the six possible conversions are mathematically related and which variables are shared. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Cartesian (x,y,z)"]:::box P2["Cylindrical (rho,phi,z)"]:::box P3["Spherical (r,theta,phi)"]:::box P1 -- "rho equals sqrt x2 + y2" --> P2 P2 -- "x equals rho cos phi" --> P1 P1 -- "r equals sqrt x2 + y2 + z2" --> P3 P3 -- "x equals r sin theta cos phi" --> P1 P2 -- "r equals sqrt rho2 + z2" --> P3 P3 -- "rho equals r sin theta" --> P2 classDef box fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Geometric Surface Decomposition Each coordinate system is uniquely defined by three families of coordinate surfaces. The following diagram maps out the surface families and the unit vectors perpendicular to them. ```mermaid flowchart TD F1["Coordinate Surfaces and Unit Vectors"]:::head F2["Rectangular: 3 Plane Families"]:::rec F3["Cylindrical: 2 Cylinders + 1 Plane"]:::cyl F4["Spherical: 1 Sphere + 1 Cone + 1 Plane"]:::sph F1 --> F2 F1 --> F3 F1 --> F4 F2 --> G1["Surfaces: x c, y c, z c"] F2 --> G2["Normal Vectors: ax, ay, az"] F3 --> G3["Surfaces: rho c, phi c, z c"] F3 --> G4["Normal Vectors: arho, aphi, az"] F4 --> G5["Surfaces: r c, theta c, phi c"] F4 --> G6["Normal Vectors: ar, atheta, aphi"] classDef head fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff classDef rec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef cyl fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef sph fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip:** In each system, the **unit vector** is **perpendicular (normal)** to the **coordinate surface** of its associated variable. For example, $\hat{a}_r$ is normal to the spherical surface $r = \text{constant}$, and $\hat{a}_\theta$ is normal to the conical surface $\theta = \text{constant}$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Define a coordinate system. List the three coordinate systems used in electromagnetics with their coordinate variables.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A coordinate system is a mathematical scheme that uses **three variables** to uniquely specify the position of a point in 3-dimensional space, along with **three mutually orthogonal unit vectors** that define directions at that point. The three coordinate systems used in electromagnetics are: 1. **Rectangular (Cartesian) Coordinate System:** $(x, y, z)$ 2. **Cylindrical Coordinate System:** $(\rho, \phi, z)$ 3. **Spherical Coordinate System:** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **State the ranges of the variables $\rho, \phi, z$ in the cylindrical coordinate system.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Variable | Range | Meaning | |----------|-------|---------| | $\rho$ | $0 \leq \rho < \infty$ | Perpendicular distance from the $z$-axis (non-negative) | | $\phi$ | $0 \leq \phi < 2\pi$ | Azimuthal angle in the $xy$-plane | | $z$ | $-\infty < z < \infty$ | Vertical height (unbounded) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU End Semester Examinations offer an **Internal Choice** in Module-level questions. We provide two complete, independent, model-answer-ready alternatives. --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the transformation relations between Cartesian and Cylindrical coordinate systems. Express both the coordinate variables and the unit vectors. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A point $P$ has Cartesian coordinates $(3, 4, 12)$. Convert this point into both cylindrical and spherical coordinate systems. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Answer A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1:** From the geometry of the $xy$-plane, the perpendicular distance from the $z$-axis is: $$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$ **Step 2:** The azimuthal angle is measured from the positive $x$-axis: $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$ **Step 3:** The $z$-coordinate remains unchanged: $$z = z$$ **Step 4:** The inverse relations come from trigonometry on the projection: $$x = \rho\cos\phi, \qquad y = \rho\sin\phi$$ **Step 5:** Unit vector transformation: $\hat{a}_\rho$ points in the direction of increasing $\rho$, which makes angle $\phi$ with $\hat{a}_x$. By geometric projection: $$\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$ **Step 6:** $\hat{a}_\phi$ is perpendicular to $\hat{a}_\rho$, pointing in the direction of increasing $\phi$ (right-hand rule). Its components are: $$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$ **Step 7:** $\hat{a}_z$ remains identical in both systems: $$\hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z$$ --- ### Model Answer A(b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $P = (3, 4, 12)$ in Cartesian coordinates. **Step 1: Convert to Cylindrical.** Compute $\rho$: $$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$ Compute $\phi$: $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 53.13^\circ = 0.9273 \text{ rad}$$ The $z$-coordinate is unchanged: $z = 12$. Therefore: $$P_{\text{cyl}} = (5, 0.9273 \text{ rad}, 12)$$ **Step 2: Convert to Spherical.** Compute $r$: $$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$ Compute $\theta$ using the formula: $$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{z}{r}\right) = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) = 22.62^\circ = 0.3948 \text{ rad}$$ Compute $\phi$ (same as in cylindrical since the $xy$-projection is identical): $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 53.13^\circ$$ Therefore: $$P_{\text{sph}} = (13, 0.3948 \text{ rad}, 0.9273 \text{ rad})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** The famous 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple appears naturally — confirming numerical consistency. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]** **(a)** Define the spherical coordinate system. Derive the expressions for the differential length and differential volume elements in spherical coordinates. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A vector field is given by $\vec{A} = 5\hat{a}_r + 2\hat{a}_\theta - 3\hat{a}_\phi$ at a point in spherical coordinates. Express this vector in Cartesian coordinates at the point $(r=2, \theta=\pi/3, \phi=\pi/6)$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Answer B(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1:** The spherical coordinate system locates a point $P$ by three variables: - $r$ — radial distance from the origin ($r \geq 0$) - $\theta$ — polar angle from the $z$-axis ($0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$) - $\phi$ — azimuthal angle in the $xy$-plane ($0 \leq \phi < 2\pi$) **Step 2:** Differential length along $\hat{a}_r$ is simply $dr$. **Step 3:** Differential length along $\hat{a}_\theta$: a change $d\theta$ sweeps an arc of radius $r$ in the meridian plane, giving $r\,d\theta$. **Step 4:** Differential length along $\hat{a}_\phi$: a change $d\phi$ sweeps an arc of radius equal to the projection of $r$ onto the $xy$-plane, which is $r\sin\theta$. So the arc length is $r\sin\theta\,d\phi$. **Step 5:** By vector superposition: $$d\vec{l} = dr\,\hat{a}_r + r\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + r\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$ **Step 6:** The differential volume is the product of the three orthogonal differential lengths: $$dV = (dr)(r\,d\theta)(r\sin\theta\,d\phi) = r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$$ **Step 7:** The factor $\sin\theta$ vanishes at the poles ($\theta = 0, \pi$), reflecting the geometric collapse of azimuthal circles at the poles. --- ### Model Answer B(b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $\vec{A} = 5\hat{a}_r + 2\hat{a}_\theta - 3\hat{a}_\phi$ at $\theta = \pi/3$, $\phi = \pi/6$. **Step 1:** Compute $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$: $$\sin\theta = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \qquad \cos\theta = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$: $$\sin\phi = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{2}, \qquad \cos\phi = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ **Step 3:** Write the Cartesian components of each spherical unit vector using the transformation table: $$\hat{a}_r = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the numerical values: $$\hat{a}_r = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,\hat{a}_y + \frac{1}{2}\,\hat{a}_z = \frac{3}{4}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\,\hat{a}_y + \frac{1}{2}\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$\hat{a}_\theta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,\hat{a}_y - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\hat{a}_z = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{1}{4}\,\hat{a}_y - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\hat{a}_z$$ $$\hat{a}_\phi = -\frac{1}{2}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,\hat{a}_y$$ **Step 5:** Compute $A_x$: $$\begin{aligned} A_x &= 5 \cdot \frac{3}{4} + 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} + (-3)\cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ &= \frac{15}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{3}{2} \\ &= \frac{15}{4} + \frac{6}{4} + \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{4} = \frac{21 + 2\sqrt{3}}{4} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6:** Compute $A_y$: $$\begin{aligned} A_y &= 5 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} + 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + (-3)\cdot\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ &= \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{4} + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ &= \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{4} - \frac{6\sqrt{3}}{4} + \frac{2}{4} = \frac{-\sqrt{3} + 2}{4} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7:** Compute $A_z$: $$\begin{aligned} A_z &= 5 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 2\cdot\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + (-3)\cdot 0 \\ &= \frac{5}{2} - \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8:** Therefore: $$\vec{A} = \frac{21 + 2\sqrt{3}}{4}\,\hat{a}_x + \frac{2 - \sqrt{3}}{4}\,\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{5}{2} - \sqrt{3}\right)\hat{a}_z$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign errors in $\hat{a}_\phi$** — Students often write $\hat{a}_\phi = \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x - \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$, which gives the wrong sign convention and **costs 1–2 marks per part**. > 2. **Confusing $\theta$ and $\phi$ definitions** — $\theta$ is from the $z$-axis (not $xy$-plane). Mixing them produces an entirely wrong transformation. **Always costs 2–3 marks.** > 3. **Forgetting $\sin\theta$ in $dV$ for spherical** — Students often write $dV = r^2\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ instead of $r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$. **Common 1-mark loss.** > 4. **Quadrant ambiguity of $\tan^{-1}$** — When converting $(x,y) = (-1,-1)$, $\tan^{-1}(y/x) = \tan^{-1}(1) = \pi/4$, but the actual angle is $5\pi/4$. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this. > 5. **Writing $\phi$ in degrees instead of radians** — Always express angles in radians in your final answer unless the question explicitly asks for degrees. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this as your final-day revision sheet before the KTU exam.** - A coordinate system is defined by **3 orthogonal coordinate surfaces** and their associated **3 unit vectors** at every point in space. - **Cartesian** coordinates are the only system with **fixed-direction** unit vectors; cylindrical and spherical have variable unit vectors. - All three systems are **right-handed** and **orthogonal** — the dot product of any two distinct unit vectors is **zero**. - The Cartesian-to-Cylindrical conversion relies on the geometry of the $xy$-plane: $\rho = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$. - The Cartesian-to-Spherical conversion uses $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}$, $\theta = \cos^{-1}(z/r)$, and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$. - The **polar angle $\theta$ is measured from the $z$-axis** in physics/engineering — never from the $xy$-plane. - The **differential volume elements** to memorize are: - Cartesian: $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ - Cylindrical: $dV = \rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ - Spherical: $dV = r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ - The factor $\rho$ in cylindrical $dV$ and $r^2\sin\theta$ in spherical $dV$ arise from the **Jacobians** of the coordinate transformations. - The position vector is simplest in **spherical** coordinates: $\vec{r} = r\hat{a}_r$. - The **range of $\theta$ is $[0, \pi]$**, while the **range of $\phi$ is $[0, 2\pi)$** in both cylindrical and spherical systems. - To convert a **vector field** between coordinate systems, you must transform the **components** AND the **unit vectors** separately. - Choose the coordinate system based on the **symmetry of the physical problem**: - Cartesian → boxes, planes, waveguides - Cylindrical → wires, cylinders, coaxial structures - Spherical → point sources, antennas, far-field radiation

topics.title: Expression of Curl, Divergence, and Laplacian in Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical Coordinate Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Expression of Curl, Divergence, and Laplacian in Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical Coordinate Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Three Vector Differential Operators

In vector calculus, three fundamental differential operators govern the behaviour of scalar and vector fields. These operators are the **mathematical backbone of Maxwell's equations** and hence the entire subject of Electromagnetic Waves.

| Operator | Acts On | Produces | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gradient** ($\nabla$) | Scalar field $V$ | Vector field | $\nabla V$ |
| **Divergence** ($\nabla \cdot$) | Vector field $\vec{A}$ | Scalar field | $\nabla \cdot \vec{A}$ |
| **Curl** ($\nabla \times$) | Vector field $\vec{A}$ | Vector field | $\nabla \times \vec{A}$ |
| **Laplacian** ($\nabla^2$) | Scalar / Vector | Scalar / Vector | $\nabla^2 V$, $\nabla^2 \vec{A}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1):** Students *must* be able to *write from memory* the expressions of Divergence, Curl, and Laplacian in **all three** coordinate systems and also *derive* them for at least the Cartesian system.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**1. Divergence of a vector field $\vec{A}$ at a point P** is defined as the net outward flux per unit volume as the volume shrinks *infinitesimally* about P:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint_{S} \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$

**2. Curl of a vector field $\vec{A}$ at a point P** is defined as the limiting value of the line integral of $\vec{A}$ per unit area, with the direction of the area vector normal to the surface:

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta S \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta S}\,\hat{a}_n \oint_{C} \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**3. Laplacian of a scalar field $V$** is the divergence of its gradient:

$$\nabla^2 V \;=\; \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Physical Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Divergence as a "Source Detector"**
> Imagine placing an *infinitesimally small* porous sponge at a point in a flowing river. If more water flows *out* of the sponge than flows *in*, the point is acting as a **source** (positive divergence). If water flows *in* faster than it flows *out*, the point is a **sink** (negative divergence). In electromagnetics, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho / \varepsilon_0$ tells us that electric charge is the "source" of electric field lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Curl as a "Tiny Paddle Wheel"**
> Drop a tiny frictionless paddle wheel into a fluid flow. If the wheel spins, the fluid has **circulation** at that point — that is *curl*. The axis of rotation gives the *direction* of $\nabla \times \vec{A}$ and the rate of rotation gives its *magnitude*. In Faraday's law, $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\partial \vec{B}/\partial t$ represents the curling electric field induced by a changing magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Laplacian as a "Curvature Detector"**
> The Laplacian measures how the *average value* of a quantity in a tiny neighbourhood compares with its *value at the centre*. Where $V$ is "hilly" (curved), $\nabla^2 V$ is large. It is the operator appearing in the wave equation, Laplace's equation, and Poisson's equation — all central to EM theory.

### 1.4 Why Three Coordinate Systems?

Physical problems in electromagnetics often possess **geometric symmetry**:

- A long straight wire carrying current → **Cylindrical** ($\rho, \phi, z$)
- Radiation from an antenna, charge on a sphere → **Spherical** ($r, \theta, \phi$)
- Rectangular waveguides, cavity resonators → **Cartesian** ($x, y, z$)

Choosing the coordinate system that matches the geometry **simplifies the mathematics dramatically** by reducing partial derivatives that would otherwise be cumbersome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in EM Waves:**
> $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (permittivity of free space)
> $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m (permeability of free space)
> $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (speed of light in vacuum)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Del Operator $\nabla$ — A Vectorial Differentiator

The **del operator** $\nabla$ is a *vector differential operator*. In Cartesian coordinates it is:

$$\nabla \;=\; \hat{a}_x \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + \hat{a}_y \frac{\partial}{\partial y} + \hat{a}_z \frac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

It is *not* a true vector (it has no value until it operates on something), but it obeys vector algebra rules. The three operators are obtained by combining $\nabla$ with scalar/vector multiplication:

- **Gradient**: $\nabla V$ — treat $\nabla$ as a vector multiplying a scalar
- **Divergence**: $\nabla \cdot \vec{A}$ — dot product of $\nabla$ with $\vec{A}$
- **Curl**: $\nabla \times \vec{A}$ — cross product of $\nabla$ with $\vec{A}$
- **Laplacian**: $\nabla \cdot \nabla = \nabla^2$ — dot of $\nabla$ with itself

### 2.2 General Curvilinear Coordinates and Scale Factors

For any orthogonal curvilinear system $(u_1, u_2, u_3)$ with scale (Lamé) factors $(h_1, h_2, h_3)$:

$$d\vec{l} \;=\; h_1\,du_1\,\hat{a}_1 + h_2\,du_2\,\hat{a}_2 + h_3\,du_3\,\hat{a}_3$$

$$dS_1 \;=\; h_2 h_3\,du_2\,du_3, \quad dS_2 \;=\; h_1 h_3\,du_1\,du_3, \quad dS_3 \;=\; h_1 h_2\,du_1\,du_2$$

$$dv \;=\; h_1 h_2 h_3\,du_1\,du_2\,du_3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formulae in General Curvilinear Coordinates** (most concise form for KTU 14-mark derivations):

**Divergence:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3} \left[ \frac{\partial (h_2 h_3 A_1)}{\partial u_1} + \frac{\partial (h_3 h_1 A_2)}{\partial u_2} + \frac{\partial (h_1 h_2 A_3)}{\partial u_3} \right]$$

**Curl** (determinant form):

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3} \begin{vmatrix} h_1 \hat{a}_1 & h_2 \hat{a}_2 & h_3 \hat{a}_3 \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial u_1} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial u_2} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial u_3} \\ h_1 A_1 & h_2 A_2 & h_3 A_3 \end{vmatrix}$$

**Laplacian of a Scalar:**

$$\nabla^2 V \;=\; \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3} \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial u_1}\!\left( \frac{h_2 h_3}{h_1}\frac{\partial V}{\partial u_1} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial u_2}\!\left( \frac{h_3 h_1}{h_2}\frac{\partial V}{\partial u_2} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial u_3}\!\left( \frac{h_1 h_2}{h_3}\frac{\partial V}{\partial u_3} \right) \right]$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this table multiple times. In the KTU exam, writing these expressions from memory fetches 3 to 5 marks instantly.**

| Operator | Cartesian $(x,y,z)$ | Cylindrical $(\rho,\phi,z)$ | Spherical $(r,\theta,\phi)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scale factors** | $h_1=1,\, h_2=1,\, h_3=1$ | $h_1=1,\, h_2=\rho,\, h_3=1$ | $h_1=1,\, h_2=r,\, h_3=r\sin\theta$ |
| **Volume $dv$** | $dx\,dy\,dz$ | $\rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ | $r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ |
| **$\nabla V$** | $\hat{a}_x \partial_x V + \hat{a}_y \partial_y V + \hat{a}_z \partial_z V$ | $\hat{a}_\rho \partial_\rho V + \hat{a}_\phi \tfrac{1}{\rho}\partial_\phi V + \hat{a}_z \partial_z V$ | $\hat{a}_r \partial_r V + \hat{a}_\theta \tfrac{1}{r}\partial_\theta V + \hat{a}_\phi \tfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi V$ |
| **$\nabla \cdot \vec{A}$** | $\partial_x A_x + \partial_y A_y + \partial_z A_z$ | $\tfrac{1}{\rho}\partial_\rho(\rho A_\rho) + \tfrac{1}{\rho}\partial_\phi A_\phi + \partial_z A_z$ | $\tfrac{1}{r^{2}}\partial_r(r^{2}A_r) + \tfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\theta(\sin\theta\,A_\theta) + \tfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\partial_\phi A_\phi$ |
| **$\nabla \times \vec{A}$** | $\hat{a}_x(\partial_y A_z - \partial_z A_y) + \hat{a}_y(\partial_z A_x - \partial_x A_z) + \hat{a}_z(\partial_x A_y - \partial_y A_x)$ | $\hat{a}_\rho\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\rho}\partial_\phi A_z - \partial_z A_\phi\right) + \hat{a}_\phi\!\left(\partial_z A_\rho - \partial_\rho A_z\right) + \hat{a}_z\!\tfrac{1}{\rho}\!\left(\partial_\rho(\rho A_\phi) - \partial_\phi A_\rho\right)$ | $\hat{a}_r \tfrac{1}{r\sin\theta}\!\left[\partial_\theta(A_\phi\sin\theta) - \partial_\phi A_\theta\right] + \hat{a}_\theta \tfrac{1}{r}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{\sin\theta}\partial_\phi A_r - \partial_r(r A_\phi)\right] + \hat{a}_\phi \tfrac{1}{r}\!\left[\partial_r(r A_\theta) - \partial_\theta A_r\right]$ |
| **$\nabla^{2} V$** | $\partial_{xx} V + \partial_{yy} V + \partial_{zz} V$ | $\tfrac{1}{\rho}\partial_\rho(\rho\,\partial_\rho V) + \tfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}\partial_{\phi\phi} V + \partial_{zz} V$ | $\tfrac{1}{r^{2}}\partial_r(r^{2}\partial_r V) + \tfrac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\partial_\theta(\sin\theta\,\partial_\theta V) + \tfrac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\partial_{\phi\phi} V$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic Trick for Curl in Cartesian (Cyclic Permutation):**
> $(\nabla \times \vec{A})_x = \partial_y A_z - \partial_z A_y$
> $(\nabla \times \vec{A})_y = \partial_z A_x - \partial_x A_z$
> $(\nabla \times \vec{A})_z = \partial_x A_y - \partial_y A_x$
> The pattern: take the cyclic order $(x \to y \to z \to x)$ for the *first index* and the *opposite* cyclic order for the *second index* of the derivative.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Coordinate System Used | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Coaxial cable / wave propagation along $z$ | Cylindrical | Transverse field is rotationally symmetric in $\phi$ |
| Antenna radiation pattern | Spherical | Radiation spreads radially in $r$ with angular dependence |
| Rectangular waveguide (TE/TM modes) | Cartesian | Boundaries are planes of constant $x$ and $y$ |
| Optical fibre modes | Cylindrical | Cylindrical dielectric interface |
| Electrostatic potential of a point charge | Spherical | Radial symmetry makes $\partial_\theta = \partial_\phi = 0$ |
