## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Marks are awarded for *every intermediate step* in the derivation. Skipping the limit notation or the use of the Taylor series expansion typically results in 2–3 marks being deducted. The following derivations are deliberately exhaustive.

---

### 3.1 Derivation of Divergence in Cartesian Coordinates

**Starting Point — Definition:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint_S \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$

**Step 1 — Choose an infinitesimal volume element.**
Consider a rectangular parallelepiped (a tiny box) centred at point $P(x,y,z)$ with edges $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, $\Delta z$ aligned along the coordinate axes. The unit outward normal to each face is parallel to one of the axes.

**Step 2 — Compute the flux through the pair of faces perpendicular to the $x$-axis.**

The face at $x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}$ has area $\Delta y\, \Delta z$ and outward normal $\hat{a}_x$. The flux through this face is:

$$\Phi_x^{+} \;=\; A_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2},\, y,\, z\right) \, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

The face at $x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}$ has area $\Delta y\, \Delta z$ and outward normal $-\hat{a}_x$. The flux through this face is:

$$\Phi_x^{-} \;=\; -A_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2},\, y,\, z\right) \, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Taylor series expansion to first order.**

For a small displacement $\tfrac{\Delta x}{2}$:

$$A_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right) \;\approx\; A_x(x,y,z) + \frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x}$$

$$A_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right) \;\approx\; A_x(x,y,z) - \frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4 — Net flux through the $x$-faces.**

$$\Phi_x \;=\; \Phi_x^{+} + \Phi_x^{-} \;=\; \left[ A_x + \frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} - A_x + \frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} \right] \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

$$\Phi_x \;=\; \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x}\, \Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for the $y$ and $z$ pairs (by symmetry of the procedure):**

$$\Phi_y \;=\; \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y}\, \Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

$$\Phi_z \;=\; \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}\, \Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

**Step 6 — Total outward flux and the limit.**

$$\oint_S \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{S} \;=\; \left( \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z} \right) \Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

Divide by $\Delta v = \Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$ and let $\Delta v \to 0$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Curl in Cartesian Coordinates

**Starting Point — Definition:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta S \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta S}\,\hat{a}_n \oint_C \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Strategy:** The curl is a vector. We find one component at a time by orienting the loop in the plane perpendicular to that component. We shall derive the **$x$-component**; the others follow by cyclic permutation $x \to y \to z \to x$.

**Step 1 — Orient the loop in the $yz$-plane** (perpendicular to $\hat{a}_x$). Choose a rectangular loop of sides $\Delta y$ (along $y$) and $\Delta z$ (along $z$) centred at $P$. The unit normal is $\hat{a}_n = \hat{a}_x$.

**Step 2 — Walk around the loop in the sense given by the right-hand rule with thumb along $+\hat{a}_x$.** The traversal is:

$$\text{Side 1 (bottom)}: (y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \to (y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \quad \text{along } +\hat{a}_y$$

$$\text{Side 2 (right)}: (y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \to (y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \quad \text{along } +\hat{a}_z$$

$$\text{Side 3 (top)}: (y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \to (y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \quad \text{along } -\hat{a}_y$$

$$\text{Side 4 (left)}: (y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \to (y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2},\, z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}) \quad \text{along } -\hat{a}_z$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l}$ on each side.**

Side 1: $d\vec{l} = dy\,\hat{a}_y$ evaluated at $z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$:

$$\int_{\text{Side 1}} \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; A_y\!\left(x, y, z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) \Delta y$$

Side 2: $d\vec{l} = dz\,\hat{a}_z$ evaluated at $y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}$:

$$\int_{\text{Side 2}} \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; A_z\!\left(x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}, z\right) \Delta z$$

Side 3: $d\vec{l} = -dy\,\hat{a}_y$ evaluated at $z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$:

$$\int_{\text{Side 3}} \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; -A_y\!\left(x, y, z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) \Delta y$$

Side 4: $d\vec{l} = -dz\,\hat{a}_z$ evaluated at $y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}$:

$$\int_{\text{Side 4}} \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; -A_z\!\left(x, y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}, z\right) \Delta z$$

**Step 4 — Sum the four contributions.**

$$\oint_C \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; \left[ A_y\!\left(z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) - A_y\!\left(z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) \right]\Delta y \;+\; \left[ A_z\!\left(y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) - A_z\!\left(y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) \right]\Delta z$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Taylor series expansion.**

$$A_y\!\left(z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) \;\approx\; A_y - \frac{\Delta z}{2}\frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z} \quad ; \quad A_y\!\left(z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) \;\approx\; A_y + \frac{\Delta z}{2}\frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}$$

$$A_z\!\left(y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) \;\approx\; A_z + \frac{\Delta y}{2}\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} \quad ; \quad A_z\!\left(y - \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) \;\approx\; A_z - \frac{\Delta y}{2}\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y}$$

Substituting:

$$\oint_C \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; \left(-\Delta z \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}\right)\Delta y \;+\; \left(\Delta y \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y}\right)\Delta z$$

$$\oint_C \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; \left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z} \right) \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

**Step 6 — Divide by the enclosed area and take the limit.**

$$(\nabla \times \vec{A})_x \;=\; \lim_{\Delta y, \Delta z \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta y\, \Delta z} \left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z} \right) \Delta y\, \Delta z$$

$$\boxed{(\nabla \times \vec{A})_x \;=\; \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}}$$

**Step 7 — By cyclic permutation** $x \to y \to z \to x$:

$$(\nabla \times \vec{A})_y \;=\; \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial x}, \qquad (\nabla \times \vec{A})_z \;=\; \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial y}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_x\!\left(\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}\right) + \hat{a}_y\!\left(\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial x}\right) + \hat{a}_z\!\left(\frac{\partial A_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial y}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Laplacian in Cartesian Coordinates

**Step 1 — Start from the definition** $\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient in Cartesian.**

$$\nabla V \;=\; \hat{a}_x \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} + \hat{a}_y \frac{\partial V}{\partial y} + \hat{a}_z \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

Here the components are $A_x = \partial V/\partial x$, $A_y = \partial V/\partial y$, $A_z = \partial V/\partial z$.

**Step 3 — Take the divergence of the gradient** (apply the formula from §3.1):

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V \;=\; \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Divergence in Cylindrical Coordinates

**Step 1 — Set up the differential volume element.**
A small element in cylindrical coordinates is bounded by:
- Two $\rho$-surfaces at $\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}$ and $\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}$, with areas $(\rho \pm \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2})\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$ and outward normals $\pm \hat{a}_\rho$.
- Two $\phi$-surfaces at $\phi - \tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}$ and $\phi + \tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}$, with areas $\Delta\rho\,\Delta z$ and outward normals $\pm \hat{a}_\phi$.
- Two $z$-surfaces at $z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$ and $z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$, with areas $\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta\rho$ and outward normals $\pm \hat{a}_z$.

**Step 2 — Flux through the $\rho$-pair.**
At $\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}$, the area is $\left(\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right)\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$:

$$\Phi_\rho^{+} \;=\; A_\rho\!\left(\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}, \phi, z\right)\left(\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right)\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$

At $\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}$, the area is $\left(\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right)\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$ and the outward normal is $-\hat{a}_\rho$:

$$\Phi_\rho^{-} \;=\; -A_\rho\!\left(\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}, \phi, z\right)\left(\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right)\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$

**Step 3 — Taylor-expand the product $f(\rho) = A_\rho(\rho)\,\rho$.**

$$f\!\left(\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right) \;\approx\; \rho A_\rho + \frac{\Delta\rho}{2}\frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho}$$

$$f\!\left(\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right) \;\approx\; \rho A_\rho - \frac{\Delta\rho}{2}\frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho}$$

**Step 4 — Net flux in the $\rho$-direction.**

$$\Phi_\rho \;=\; \left[ f\!\left(\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right) - f\!\left(\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}\right) \right] \Delta\phi\, \Delta z \;=\; \frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho}\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$

**Step 5 — Flux through the $\phi$-pair (areas do not change with $\phi$):**

$$\Phi_\phi \;=\; \left[ A_\phi\!\left(\phi + \tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) - A_\phi\!\left(\phi - \tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \right] \Delta\rho\, \Delta z \;=\; \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi}\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$

**Step 6 — Flux through the $z$-pair (areas do not change with $z$):**

$$\Phi_z \;=\; \left[ A_z\!\left(z + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) - A_z\!\left(z - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}\right) \right] \rho\,\Delta\phi\, \Delta\rho \;=\; \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}\,\rho\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$

**Step 7 — Total flux and limit.** Recall the volume is $\Delta v = \rho\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$.

$$\oint_S \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{S} \;=\; \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \rho\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z} \right] \Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$

Divide by $\Delta v = \rho\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Divergence in Spherical Coordinates

**Step 1 — Set up the differential volume element.**
A small cell in spherical coordinates has:
- Two $r$-surfaces at $r \pm \tfrac{\Delta r}{2}$ with areas $\left(r \pm \tfrac{\Delta r}{2}\right)^{2}\sin\theta\,\Delta\theta\,\Delta\phi$.
- Two $\theta$-surfaces at $\theta \pm \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}$ with areas $r\sin\!\left(\theta \pm \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)\Delta r\,\Delta\phi$.
- Two $\phi$-surfaces at $\phi \pm \tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}$ with areas $r\,\Delta r\,\Delta\theta$.

**Step 2 — Flux through the $r$-pair.**
The product $A_r \cdot r^2 \sin\theta$ must be Taylor-expanded:

$$\Phi_r \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2} A_r\right)\sin\theta\, \Delta r\, \Delta\theta\, \Delta\phi$$

**Step 3 — Flux through the $\theta$-pair.**
The product $A_\theta \cdot \sin\theta$ must be Taylor-expanded, with the constant area $r\,\Delta r\,\Delta\phi$:

$$\Phi_\theta \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\, A_\theta\right) r\, \Delta r\, \Delta\theta\, \Delta\phi$$

**Step 4 — Flux through the $\phi$-pair** (areas do not depend on $\phi$):

$$\Phi_\phi \;=\; \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi}\, r\, \Delta r\, \Delta\theta\, \Delta\phi$$

**Step 5 — Total flux and limit.** Divide by $\Delta v = r^{2}\sin\theta\,\Delta r\,\Delta\theta\,\Delta\phi$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial (r^{2} A_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial (\sin\theta\, A_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi}}$$

---

### 3.6 Curl in Cylindrical Coordinates (via General Curvilinear Master Formula)

**Step 1 — Identify scale factors:** $h_1 = 1,\ h_2 = \rho,\ h_3 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply the determinant form:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1)(\rho)(1)} \begin{vmatrix} (1)\hat{a}_\rho & (\rho)\hat{a}_\phi & (1)\hat{a}_z \\ \partial_\rho & \partial_\phi & \partial_z \\ (1)A_\rho & (\rho)A_\phi & (1)A_z \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Expand along the first row:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\left[ \hat{a}_\rho \begin{vmatrix} \partial_\phi & \partial_z \\ \rho A_\phi & A_z \end{vmatrix} - \rho \hat{a}_\phi \begin{vmatrix} \partial_\rho & \partial_z \\ A_\rho & A_z \end{vmatrix} + \hat{a}_z \begin{vmatrix} \partial_\rho & \partial_\phi \\ A_\rho & \rho A_\phi \end{vmatrix} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each 2×2 determinant.**

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\left[ \hat{a}_\rho\!\left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \phi} - \rho\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial z} \right) - \rho\hat{a}_\phi\!\left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial z} \right) + \hat{a}_z\!\left( \frac{\partial (\rho A_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial \phi} \right) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the coefficients:**

$$\boxed{\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_\rho\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial z}\right) + \hat{a}_\phi\!\left(\frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \rho}\right) + \hat{a}_z\,\frac{1}{\rho}\!\left(\frac{\partial (\rho A_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial \phi}\right)}$$

---

### 3.7 Laplacian in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates (via Master Formula)

**Cylindrical ($h_1=1, h_2=\rho, h_3=1$):**

$$\nabla^2 V \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\!\left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\rho\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\right) \right]$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^{2} V \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}}$$

**Spherical ($h_1=1, h_2=r, h_3=r\sin\theta$):**

$$\nabla^2 V \;=\; \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\!\left[ \sin\theta\,\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} \right]$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^{2} V \;=\; \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}}}$$

---

### 3.8 Laplacian of a Vector in Rectangular Coordinates (Bonus for EM Wave Equation)

In EM theory, the *vector* Laplacian is needed for the wave equation. In Cartesian, since the unit vectors are *constant*:

$$\nabla^{2}\vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_x\,\nabla^{2}A_x + \hat{a}_y\,\nabla^{2}A_y + \hat{a}_z\,\nabla^{2}A_z \;=\; \hat{a}_x\,\nabla^{2}A_x + \hat{a}_y\,\nabla^{2}A_y + \hat{a}_z\,\nabla^{2}A_z$$

In curved coordinates, extra terms appear because the unit vectors themselves vary with position. For example, in spherical:

$$\nabla^{2}\vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_r\!\left[\nabla^{2}A_r - \frac{2A_r}{r^{2}} - \frac{2}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial (\sin\theta\, A_\theta)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{2}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi}\right] + \cdots$$

(Expressed in full only when the question demands it; KTU 2024 typically requires only the scalar Laplacian for Module 1.)

---

### 3.9 Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** If $\vec{A} = 5\rho\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\rho + 3\rho\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + 2z\,\hat{a}_z$ in cylindrical coordinates, evaluate $\nabla \cdot \vec{A}$ at the point $\rho = 2,\ \phi = \pi/2,\ z = 1$.

**Solution:**

$$A_\rho = 5\rho\sin\phi, \quad A_\phi = 3\rho\cos\phi, \quad A_z = 2z$$

**Term 1:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (5\rho^{2}\sin\phi)}{\partial \rho} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}(10\rho\sin\phi) \;=\; 10\sin\phi$$

**Term 2:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (3\rho\cos\phi)}{\partial \phi} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}(-3\rho\sin\phi) \;=\; -3\sin\phi$$

**Term 3:**

$$\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z} \;=\; \frac{\partial (2z)}{\partial z} \;=\; 2$$

**Sum:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; 10\sin\phi - 3\sin\phi + 2 \;=\; 7\sin\phi + 2$$

At $\phi = \pi/2$, $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{A}\Big|_{P} \;=\; 7(1) + 2 \;=\; 9}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students are not required to draw physical diagrams, but a clear block-level mental model is essential to remember the formulae under exam pressure.** The following Mermaid diagrams summarize the conceptual architecture.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Construction of Vector Differential Operators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vector Differential Operator DEL] --> B[Acts on Scalar Field V]
    A --> C[Acts on Vector Field A]
    A --> D[Acts on itself DEL dot DEL]
    B --> B1[Gradient nabla V - Vector]
    C --> C1[Divergence nabla dot A - Scalar]
    C --> C2[Curl nabla cross A - Vector]
    D --> D1[Laplacian nabla squared - Scalar]
    B1 --> E[Used in: Field from potential]
    C1 --> F[Used in: Gauss Law]
    C2 --> G[Used in: Faraday Law]
    D1 --> H[Used in: Wave Equation]
    E --> I[Maxwell Equations]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Coordinate System Selection Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EM Problem Statement] --> B{Geometry of Source/Field}
    B --> C[Planar Boundaries]
    B --> D[Round/Symmetric around Axis]
    B --> E[Point Source / Spherical]
    C --> F[Cartesian x y z]
    D --> G[Cylindrical rho phi z]
    E --> H[Spherical r theta phi]
    F --> F1[Rectangular Waveguides]
    F --> F2[Parallel Plate Capacitors]
    G --> G1[Coaxial Cables]
    G --> G2[Optical Fibres]
    H --> H1[Antenna Radiation]
    H --> H2[Point Charge Potential]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Master Formula Pipeline (General Curvilinear → Specialised)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Master Curvilinear Formulae] --> M1[Divergence via h1 h2 h3]
    M --> M2[Curl via Determinant]
    M --> M3[Laplacian via h1 h2 h3]
    M1 --> P1[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,1,1]
    M1 --> P2[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,rho,1]
    M1 --> P3[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,r,rsin theta]
    M2 --> Q1[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,1,1]
    M2 --> Q2[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,rho,1]
    M2 --> Q3[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,r,rsin theta]
    M3 --> R1[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,1,1]
    M3 --> R2[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,rho,1]
    M3 --> R3[Substitute h1 h2 h3 = 1,r,rsin theta]
    P1 --> S1[Cartesian Divergence]
    P2 --> S2[Cylindrical Divergence]
    P3 --> S3[Spherical Divergence]
    Q1 --> T1[Cartesian Curl]
    Q2 --> T2[Cylindrical Curl]
    Q3 --> T3[Spherical Curl]
    R1 --> U1[Cartesian Laplacian]
    R2 --> U2[Cylindrical Laplacian]
    R3 --> U3[Spherical Laplacian]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationship Between the Three Operators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Scalar Field V] --> G[Gradient nabla V]
    G --> V1[Vector Field nabla V]
    V1 --> D[Divergence nabla dot nabla V]
    D --> L[Laplacian nabla squared V]
    S2[Scalar Field V] --> Curl2[Gradient nabla V]
    Curl2 --> Curl3[Vector Field]
    Curl3 --> Curl4[Curl nabla cross nabla V = 0]
    A[Vector Field A] --> Div[Divergence nabla dot A]
    A --> Curl[Curl nabla cross A]
    Div --> DivRes[Scalar Source Density]
    Curl --> CurlRes[Vector Circulation Density]
    L --> Wave[Used in Wave Equation]
    DivRes --> Max[Maxwell Equations]
    CurlRes --> Max
    Wave --> Max
```

### 4.5 Reference Diagram — Differential Volume Elements (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Differential Volume in 3 Coordinate Systems"
    A1[Cartesian: Box dx dy dz]
    A2[Cylindrical: Wedge rho dphi dz, thickness drho]
    A3[Spherical: Curved Cell r dtheta, r sin theta dphi, thickness dr]
    A1 --> L1[Sides: dx, dy, dz - All constant]
    A2 --> L2[Sides: drho, rho dphi, dz - Two constant one variable]
    A3 --> L3[Sides: dr, r dtheta, r sin theta dphi - All variable]
    end
    L1 --> O1[Flux product: A dot dS straightforward]
    L2 --> O2[Flux in rho: rho A rho - factor rho]
    L3 --> O3[Flux in r: r squared A r - factor r squared]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define the divergence of a vector field. State the divergence in Cartesian and spherical coordinate systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** The divergence of a vector field $\vec{A}$ at a point P is the net outward flux of $\vec{A}$ per unit volume as the volume enclosing P shrinks to zero:
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint_{S} \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$
> It is a *scalar* measure of how much the field "spreads out" from a point — a positive divergence indicates a **source**, negative a **sink**, and zero a **solenoidal** field.
>
> **Cartesian form:**
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$$
>
> **Spherical form:**
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial (r^{2} A_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial (\sin\theta\, A_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi}$$

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** State the Laplacian operator in cylindrical and spherical coordinate systems. Why is the Laplacian important in electromagnetic theory?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Cylindrical form:**
> $$\nabla^{2} V \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$$
>
> **Spherical form:**
> $$\nabla^{2} V \;=\; \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}}$$
>
> **Importance in EM Theory:** The Laplacian appears in **four of Maxwell's equations in differential form** when combined with the gradient/curl. Most importantly, the **Helmholtz wave equation** $\nabla^{2}\vec{E} + \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon\,\vec{E} = 0$ governs wave propagation, and **Laplace's / Poisson's equations** $\nabla^{2}V = -\rho/\varepsilon$ govern electrostatic potentials. Without the Laplacian, electromagnetic field theory cannot be expressed as a closed set of PDEs.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Starting from the definition, derive the expression for the **divergence of a vector field in Cartesian coordinates**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Hence derive the **divergence in cylindrical coordinates**, clearly explaining the role of the area factor $\rho$ in the radial direction. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 — State the definition:**
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint_{S} \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 2 — Choose a rectangular volume element** of sides $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ centred at $P(x,y,z)$. The flux has three pairs of contributions — one pair for each coordinate direction. (1 mark)
>
> **Step 3 — Compute the flux through the $x$-pair of faces** (areas $\Delta y \Delta z$, normals $\pm \hat{a}_x$):
> $$\Phi_x \;=\; \left[A_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) - A_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right)\right] \Delta y\, \Delta z$$
> Apply Taylor expansion: $A_x(x \pm \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}) \approx A_x \pm \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x}$.
> $$\Phi_x \;=\; \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x}\,\Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 4 — Similarly for the $y$-pair and the $z$-pair:**
> $$\Phi_y \;=\; \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y}\,\Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z, \qquad \Phi_z \;=\; \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}\,\Delta x\, \Delta y\, \Delta z$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 5 — Sum the three contributions, divide by $\Delta v = \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$, and apply the limit $\Delta v \to 0$:**
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$$
> (3 marks for clear summation, division and final boxed result)

### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 — Choose a differential volume element** in cylindrical coordinates with sides $\Delta\rho$, $\rho\Delta\phi$, $\Delta z$. The volume is $\Delta v = \rho\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$. Note that the areas of the $\rho$-faces are *not* $\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$ — they are $\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$ because the arc length $\rho\Delta\phi$ depends on $\rho$. (1 mark)
>
> **Step 2 — Flux through the $\rho$-pair.** The product $(\rho A_\rho)$ varies in the $\rho$-direction, so we Taylor-expand it:
> $$\Phi_\rho \;=\; \left[(\rho A_\rho)\Big|_{\rho + \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}} - (\rho A_\rho)\Big|_{\rho - \tfrac{\Delta\rho}{2}}\right] \Delta\phi\, \Delta z \;=\; \frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho}\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$
> (2 marks)
>
> **Step 3 — Flux through the $\phi$-pair** (constant area $\Delta\rho\,\Delta z$):
> $$\Phi_\phi \;=\; \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi}\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 4 — Flux through the $z$-pair** (constant area $\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta\rho$):
> $$\Phi_z \;=\; \rho\,\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 5 — Sum and divide by $\Delta v = \rho\,\Delta\rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$:**
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$$
> (2 marks)
>
> **Physical explanation of the factor $\rho$:** The area of a cylindrical face perpendicular to $\hat{a}_\rho$ grows linearly with $\rho$. Hence, even if $A_\rho$ were constant, the flux through the outer face would be larger than through the inner face simply because the surface is larger. The factor $\rho$ inside the derivative $\frac{\partial(\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho}$ correctly accounts for this geometric effect.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **curl of a vector field in Cartesian coordinates** starting from its definition. (7 marks)
**(b)** Using the general curvilinear (scale factor) formula, write the **curl in cylindrical and spherical coordinates**. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 — State the definition:**
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta S \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta S}\,\hat{a}_n \oint_{C} \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l}$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 2 — To find the $x$-component, choose a rectangular loop in the $yz$-plane** of sides $\Delta y$ and $\Delta z$ with unit normal $\hat{a}_n = \hat{a}_x$. Traverse the loop counter-clockwise as seen from the $+x$ direction. (1 mark)
>
> **Step 3 — Evaluate $\oint \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l}$ around the four sides.** Side 1 (along $+\hat{a}_y$ at $z-\tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$): $A_y(z-\tfrac{\Delta z}{2})\,\Delta y$. Side 2 (along $+\hat{a}_z$ at $y+\tfrac{\Delta y}{2}$): $A_z(y+\tfrac{\Delta y}{2})\,\Delta z$. Side 3 (along $-\hat{a}_y$ at $z+\tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$): $-A_y(z+\tfrac{\Delta z}{2})\,\Delta y$. Side 4 (along $-\hat{a}_z$ at $y-\tfrac{\Delta y}{2}$): $-A_z(y-\tfrac{\Delta y}{2})\,\Delta z$. (2 marks)
>
> **Step 4 — Apply Taylor expansions and collect terms:**
> $$\oint \vec{A}\cdot d\vec{l} \;=\; \left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z} \right) \Delta y\, \Delta z$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 5 — Divide by the area $\Delta y\,\Delta z$ and take the limit:**
> $$(\nabla \times \vec{A})_x \;=\; \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 6 — By cyclic permutation** $x \to y \to z \to x$ obtain the other two components and combine:
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_x\!\left(\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial A_y}{\partial z}\right) + \hat{a}_y\!\left(\frac{\partial A_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial x}\right) + \hat{a}_z\!\left(\frac{\partial A_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial A_x}{\partial y}\right)$$
> (1 mark)

### Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)

> **Step 1 — State the determinant form of the curl in general orthogonal curvilinear coordinates:**
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3} \begin{vmatrix} h_1\hat{a}_1 & h_2\hat{a}_2 & h_3\hat{a}_3 \\ \partial_1 & \partial_2 & \partial_3 \\ h_1 A_1 & h_2 A_2 & h_3 A_3 \end{vmatrix}$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 2 — For cylindrical coordinates**, $h_1=1$, $h_2=\rho$, $h_3=1$, so $h_1 h_2 h_3 = \rho$. Substitute into the determinant and expand:
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_\rho & \rho\hat{a}_\phi & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial_\rho & \partial_\phi & \partial_z \\ A_\rho & \rho A_\phi & A_z \end{vmatrix}$$
> Expanding along the first row:
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{\rho}\left[ \hat{a}_\rho \!\left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \phi} - \rho\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial z} \right) - \rho\hat{a}_\phi\!\left( \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial z} \right) + \hat{a}_z\!\left( \frac{\partial(\rho A_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial \phi} \right) \right]$$
> (2 marks)
>
> Simplify to the standard form:
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_\rho\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial z}\right) + \hat{a}_\phi\!\left(\frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \rho}\right) + \hat{a}_z\,\frac{1}{\rho}\!\left(\frac{\partial(\rho A_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial \phi}\right)$$
> (1 mark)
>
> **Step 3 — For spherical coordinates**, $h_1=1$, $h_2=r$, $h_3=r\sin\theta$, so $h_1 h_2 h_3 = r^{2}\sin\theta$. Substitute into the determinant and expand. (1 mark)
>
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_r & r\hat{a}_\theta & r\sin\theta\,\hat{a}_\phi \\ \partial_r & \partial_\theta & \partial_\phi \\ A_r & r A_\theta & r\sin\theta\, A_\phi \end{vmatrix}$$
>
> Expanding and simplifying:
> $$\nabla \times \vec{A} \;=\; \hat{a}_r\,\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\!\left[\frac{\partial (A_\phi \sin\theta)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial A_\theta}{\partial \phi}\right] + \hat{a}_\theta\,\frac{1}{r}\!\left[\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial (r A_\phi)}{\partial r}\right] + \hat{a}_\phi\,\frac{1}{r}\!\left[\frac{\partial (r A_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \theta}\right]$$
> (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\rho$ in the cylindrical divergence.** Students commonly write $\nabla \cdot \vec{A} = \frac{\partial A_\rho}{\partial \rho} + \frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial z}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct first term is $\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho A_\rho)}{\partial \rho}$. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up the $\theta$ and $\phi$ components of spherical curl.** Note the order: $\theta$ is the polar angle (from the $z$-axis) and $\phi$ is the azimuthal angle. The unit vectors $\hat{a}_\theta$ and $\hat{a}_\phi$ are *not* interchangeable. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 3. **In the Cartesian curl derivation, students often write the curl of $\vec{A}$ as a determinant *without* first stating the definition in terms of the line integral.** The determinant is the result, not the starting point. KTU examiners want to see the *limit-based* definition. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **In cylindrical curl, the term $\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho A_\phi)}{\partial \rho}$ must be present in the $\hat{a}_z$ component.** Writing $\frac{\partial A_\phi}{\partial \rho}$ alone is incomplete. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **For Laplacian, the denominator $\rho^2$ in the $\phi$-term of cylindrical and $r^2\sin^2\theta$ in the $\phi$-term of spherical are frequently missed.** (Lose 1–2 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **del operator** $\nabla$ is a *vector differential operator* — it has no value until it acts on a scalar or vector.
- [x] **Divergence** is the *flux per unit volume*; **Curl** is the *circulation per unit area*; **Laplacian** is the *divergence of the gradient*.
- [x] **Cartesian formulas are the simplest** because unit vectors are *constant* — derivatives of $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ vanish.
- [x] **Cylindrical formulas have a $\rho$ factor** in the divergence and in the $z$-component of curl because the $\rho$-faces grow with $\rho$.
- [x] **Spherical formulas have $r^2$ and $r\sin\theta$ factors** because the $r$-faces grow as $r^2$ and the $\phi$-arc length shrinks as $\sin\theta$ near the poles.
- [x] **Master formula trick (general curvilinear):** Memorise the determinant form of curl and the product form of divergence — substituting $h_1, h_2, h_3$ gives *all three* systems in one shot.
- [x] **Cyclic permutation in Cartesian curl:** The pattern is $(x,y,z) \to (y,z,x) \to (z,x,y)$ for the *first* index of the derivative, and the *opposite* direction for the *second* index.
- [x] **Physical significance:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho/\varepsilon_0$ (Gauss), $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\partial \vec{B}/\partial t$ (Faraday), $\nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2\mu\varepsilon\,\vec{E} = 0$ (Helmholtz wave equation). These are why you are studying this!
- [x] **Always show the limit notation** $\lim_{\Delta v \to 0}$ / $\lim_{\Delta S \to 0}$ in derivations — KTU marks are awarded for the *limit-based* definition.
- [x] **Taylor expansion** of the field component to first order is the key step that converts a difference into a derivative.
- [x] **Unit vector derivatives vanish in Cartesian but not in cylindrical/spherical** — relevant when computing $\nabla \times \vec{A}$ in curved systems via the determinant method (the determinant automatically accounts for this).
- [x] **Always include the area factor** when computing flux in curved coordinates: $\Delta S_\rho = \rho\,\Delta\phi\,\Delta z$ (cylindrical), $\Delta S_r = r^2\sin\theta\,\Delta\theta\,\Delta\phi$ (spherical).
- [x] **The divergence, curl and Laplacian are invariant** — they give the *same* physical result regardless of the coordinate system used. Only the *form* of the expression changes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Field and Magnetic Field: Review of Coulomb's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Field and Magnetic Field: Review of Coulomb's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Coulomb's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coulomb's Law (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *"The electrostatic force between two stationary, point charges is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the two charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. The force acts along the line joining the two charges."*

This is a **fundamental law of electrostatics** discovered by French physicist **Charles-Augustin de Coulomb in 1785**, through his famous torsion balance experiment. It is the electrostatic analogue of Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Key Parameters

The standard constants involved are:

- **Coulomb's Constant (k):** $k = 8.9875 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2 \approx 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$
- **Permittivity of Free Space (ε₀):** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2/(\text{N·m}^2) \approx \frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9} \text{ F/m}$
- **Relationship:** $k = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$
- **Elementary Charge (e):** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two constants?** The factor $4\pi$ appears when we use the symmetric **SI (rationalized) form**, simplifying equations involving spherical and cylindrical geometries (e.g., Gauss's Law). This is why KTU 2024 problems often use $\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$ directly.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Spring" Model

Imagine two magnets on a frictionless table:
- **Like poles repel** (pushing each other away, like a compressed spring releasing)
- **Unlike poles attract** (pulling each other together, like a stretched spring snapping back)

Coulomb's Law is **electricity's version of this magnetic dance**, except now the "magnets" are **electrical charges**. The further apart you move them, the weaker the push or pull becomes — but the decrease is **quadratic** (inverse-square), not linear.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** Think of a Wi-Fi signal. As you double the distance from the router, the signal strength drops to **one-fourth**, not one-half. Coulomb's Law behaves the same way — double the distance, force becomes **one-quarter**.

## 1.4 Electric Field — The Companion Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Field Definition (KTU Module 1 Core Concept):**
> *"The electric field at a point in space is the force experienced by a unit positive test charge placed at that point, assuming the test charge is so small that it does not disturb the original charge distribution."*

Mathematically:
$$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$

The unit of electric field is **Volts per meter (V/m)** or equivalently **Newtons per Coulomb (N/C)**.

## 1.5 Magnetic Field — Brief Introduction

While Coulomb's Law deals with **static charges**, the magnetic field arises from **moving charges (currents)**. A magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is defined as the force per unit current element:

$$\vec{F} = I \vec{dl} \times \vec{B}$$

The unit is **Tesla (T)** or **Weber per square meter (Wb/m²)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vector Form of Coulomb's Law

For two point charges $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ separated by a distance $r$, the force on $Q_1$ due to $Q_2$ is:

$$\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2} \cdot \hat{a}_{21}$$

Where:
- $\hat{a}_{21}$ is the **unit vector** directed **from charge 2 to charge 1**
- The sign of $Q_1 Q_2$ determines the **direction** (attractive if negative, repulsive if positive)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the source charge and test charge**
- The **source charge** creates the field
- The **test charge** experiences the force

**Step 2 — Calculate the magnitude using the scalar law**
$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\vert Q_1 \vert \cdot \vert Q_2 \vert}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the direction**
- If charges have **same sign** → force is **repulsive** (pushes charges apart)
- If charges have **opposite signs** → force is **attractive** (pulls charges together)

**Step 4 — Apply vector notation for complete description**
- Use position vectors $\vec{r}_1$ and $\vec{r}_2$ for the charges
- The separation vector is $\vec{r} = \vec{r}_1 - \vec{r}_2$
- The magnitude is $r = \vert \vec{r} \vert$
- The unit vector is $\hat{a}_r = \frac{\vec{r}}{r}$

## 2.3 The Superposition Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superposition Principle (KTU High-Yield Topic):**
> *"The total force on a charge due to a system of charges is the vector sum of the forces exerted on it by each individual charge, acting independently."*

For $N$ charges acting on charge $Q$:
$$\vec{F}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{F}_i = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{r_i^2} \hat{a}_i$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **SI Unit** | **Description** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Coulomb's Law (Magnitude) | $F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \frac{\vert Q_1 Q_2 \vert}{r^2}$ | Newton (N) | Force between two point charges |
| Coulomb's Constant | $k = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \approx 9 \times 10^9$ | N·m²/C² | Proportionality constant |
| Permittivity of Free Space | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m | Electric property of vacuum |
| Vector Force | $\vec{F}_{12} = k \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2} \hat{a}_{21}$ | N | Force with direction |
| Electric Field (Point Charge) | $\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{a}_r$ | V/m or N/C | Field from single charge |
| Electric Field (Multiple Charges) | $\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{r_i^2} \hat{a}_i$ | V/m | Field using superposition |
| Force on Charge in Field | $\vec{F} = Q\vec{E}$ | N | Lorentz force (electric part) |
| Magnetic Force | $\vec{F} = Q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ | N | Force on moving charge |
| Lorentz Total Force | $\vec{F} = Q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | N | Complete electromagnetic force |
| Energy in Electric Field | $U = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2$ | J/m³ | Energy density |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** In the formula table above, notice that the absolute value $\vert Q_1 Q_2 \vert$ is used for **magnitude calculations**, while the **sign of the product** $Q_1 Q_2$ is used for **direction determination** in vector form.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Capacitor Design** — Coulomb's Law underlies the capacitance of parallel-plate, cylindrical, and spherical capacitors used in **every electronic circuit**.
2. **High-Voltage Transmission Lines** — Engineers use Coulomb's Law to calculate the **electric field strength** around power lines to prevent corona discharge.
3. **Electrostatic Precipitators** — Used in **pollution control** in thermal power plants and cement factories to remove particulate matter from exhaust gases.
4. **Photocopiers and Laser Printers** — Toner particles are charged and manipulated using electrostatic forces derived from Coulomb's Law.
5. **Scanning Electron Microscopes (SEM)** — Electron beams are controlled using **electric and magnetic fields**, both fundamentally rooted in Coulomb's force law.

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electric Field from Coulomb's Law

**Starting Point:** We know that Coulomb's Law gives the force between two charges. We want to derive the **electric field** produced by a single point charge $Q$ at a distance $r$.

**Given Data:**
- Source charge: $Q$ (at origin)
- Test charge: $q_0$ (at distance $r$)
- Vacuum medium: $\varepsilon_0$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1: Write the force experienced by test charge $q_0$**
$$\vec{F} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q q_0}{r^2} \hat{a}_r$$

*Explanation:* This is the direct application of Coulomb's Law in vector form, where $\hat{a}_r$ is the radial unit vector pointing from $Q$ to $q_0$.

**Step 2: Apply the definition of electric field**
$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$

*Explanation:* By definition, the electric field is the force per unit positive test charge. We substitute the force expression.

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify**
$$\vec{E} = \frac{1}{q_0} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q q_0}{r^2} \hat{a}_r \right]$$

**Step 4: Cancel the test charge $q_0$ from numerator and denominator**
$$\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{a}_r$$

*Explanation:* This is the final form of the electric field produced by a point charge. Note that the field is **independent of the test charge**, confirming that it is a property of space created by the source charge.

## 3.2 Derivation: Force on a Test Charge in a System of N Charges

**Given:** A system of $N$ point charges $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_N$ located at positions $\vec{r}_1, \vec{r}_2, \ldots, \vec{r}_N$. A test charge $Q$ is located at position $\vec{r}$.

**Step 1: Force on $Q$ due to $Q_1$ alone**
$$\vec{F}_1 = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q Q_1}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_1 \vert^2} \hat{a}_{1}$$

Where $\hat{a}_{1}$ is the unit vector from $Q_1$ to $Q$.

**Step 2: Force on $Q$ due to $Q_2$ alone**
$$\vec{F}_2 = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q Q_2}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_2 \vert^2} \hat{a}_{2}$$

**Step 3: Generalize for all $N$ charges**
$$\vec{F}_i = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q Q_i}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_i \vert^2} \hat{a}_{i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

**Step 4: Apply Superposition Principle — Add all forces vectorially**
$$\vec{F}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{F}_i = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_i \vert^2} \hat{a}_{i}$$

**Step 5: Factor out common terms for elegance**
$$\vec{F}_{total} = Q \vec{E}_{total}$$

Where:
$$\vec{E}_{total} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}_i \vert^2} \hat{a}_{i}$$

*Explanation:* This proves the **consistency** of the superposition principle with Coulomb's Law. The total electric field is simply the **vector sum** of individual fields.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Three-Charge Configuration

**Problem:** Three charges are placed at the vertices of a right triangle:
- $Q_1 = +5 \, \mu\text{C}$ at point A(0, 0)
- $Q_2 = -3 \, \mu\text{C}$ at point B(4, 0)
- $Q_3 = +2 \, \mu\text{C}$ at point C(0, 3)

Find the **net force** on $Q_1$ due to $Q_2$ and $Q_3$.

**Step 1: Calculate distance $r_{12}$ between $Q_1$ and $Q_2$**
$$r_{12} = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate distance $r_{13}$ between $Q_1$ and $Q_3$**
$$r_{13} = \sqrt{(0-0)^2 + (3-0)^2} = 3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate force $F_{12}$ (force on $Q_1$ due to $Q_2$)**
$$F_{12} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\vert Q_1 Q_2 \vert}{r_{12}^2}$$
$$F_{12} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(3 \times 10^{-6})}{(4)^2}$$
$$F_{12} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{15 \times 10^{-12}}{16}$$
$$F_{12} = \frac{135 \times 10^{-3}}{16} = 8.4375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} \approx 8.44 \text{ mN}$$

**Step 4: Determine direction of $F_{12}$**
Since $Q_1$ is **positive** and $Q_2$ is **negative**, the force is **attractive**. So $Q_1$ is pulled **towards** $Q_2$, i.e., in the **+x direction**.

$$\vec{F}_{12} = +8.44 \, \hat{a}_x \text{ mN}$$

**Step 5: Calculate force $F_{13}$ (force on $Q_1$ due to $Q_3$)**
$$F_{13} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{(3)^2}$$
$$F_{13} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{9}$$
$$F_{13} = 10 \times 10^{-3} = 10 \text{ mN}$$

**Step 6: Determine direction of $F_{13}$**
Since both $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ are **positive**, the force is **repulsive**. So $Q_1$ is pushed **away** from $Q_3$, i.e., in the **-y direction**.

$$\vec{F}_{13} = -10 \, \hat{a}_y \text{ mN}$$

**Step 7: Apply Superposition — Add the forces**
$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{12} + \vec{F}_{13} = 8.44 \hat{a}_x - 10 \hat{a}_y \text{ mN}$$

**Step 8: Calculate magnitude of total force**
$$\vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert = \sqrt{(8.44)^2 + (10)^2} = \sqrt{71.23 + 100} = \sqrt{171.23}$$
$$\vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert \approx 13.09 \text{ mN}$$

**Step 9: Calculate direction (angle with +x axis)**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-10}{8.44}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-1.185) \approx -49.8°$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\vec{F}_{total} \approx 13.09 \text{ mN at } -49.8° \text{ from the +x axis}}$$

## 4.1 Functional Flow: From Coulomb's Law to Electric Field

The following block diagram illustrates the **logical flow** from fundamental charge interaction to measurable electric field quantities:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Charge Q] --> B[Apply Coulomb Law]
    B --> C[Calculate Force on Test Charge]
    C --> D[Divide by Test Charge Magnitude]
    D --> E[Define Electric Field E]
    E --> F[Apply Superposition for N Charges]
    F --> G[Total Electric Field Etotal]
    G --> H[Calculate Force on Any Charge]
    H --> I[Engineering Applications]
    I --> I1[Capacitor Design]
    I --> I2[Transmission Line Analysis]
    I --> I3[Electrostatic Precipitators]
    I --> I4[Semiconductor Devices]
```

## 4.2 Vector Force Resolution: Three-Charge System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs [Input Charges]
        Q1[Q1 at Position r1]
        Q2[Q2 at Position r2]
        Q3[Q3 at Position rN]
    end
    inputs --> calc[Distance Calculation r sub i]
    calc --> coulomb[Coulomb Force Computation]
    coulomb --> vecsum[Vector Sum by Superposition]
    vecsum --> netforce[Net Force on Test Charge]
    netforce --> field[Electric Field E = F slash q]

```

## 4.3 Process Architecture: Solving KTU Coulomb's Law Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Problem Statement]) --> identify[Identify Given Charges and Positions]
    identify --> dist[Calculate Distances r12, r13, ...]
    dist --> mag[Compute Force Magnitudes]
    mag --> dir[Determine Force Directions from Signs]
    dir --> decomp[Resolve Forces into x and y Components]
    decomp --> sum[Apply Vector Superposition]
    sum --> result[Net Force and Direction]
    result --> verify[Verify Units and Physical Reasonableness]
    verify --> end([Final Answer])
```

## 4.4 Comparative Map: Electric Field vs. Magnetic Field

| **Property** | **Electric Field E** | **Magnetic Field B** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Source | Stationary or time-varying charges | Moving charges (currents) or time-varying E |
| Affects | Stationary and moving charges | **Only moving** charges |
| Force equation | $\vec{F}_E = Q\vec{E}$ | $\vec{F}_B = Q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ |
| Units | V/m or N/C | Tesla T or Wb/m² |
| Field lines | Start on +, end on - (open or closed) | Always **closed loops** (no monopoles) |
| Medium dependence | Permittivity $\varepsilon$ | Permeability $\mu$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Although this module focuses primarily on Coulomb's Law (electrostatics), the **Lorentz force equation** $\vec{F} = Q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ unifies both fields. This equation is the **foundation of Module 2 and Module 3** in your PEEVT411 syllabus.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: State Coulomb's Law in vector form and explain the significance of the inverse-square nature of the law.**

**Model Answer:**

Coulomb's Law in vector form states that the electrostatic force $\vec{F}_{12}$ experienced by charge $Q_1$ due to charge $Q_2$ separated by distance $r$ is given by:

$$\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2} \hat{a}_{21}$$

where $\hat{a}_{21}$ is the unit vector from $Q_2$ to $Q_1$.

**Significance of inverse-square law:**

1. The force decreases **rapidly** with distance — doubling the distance reduces the force to **one-fourth**.
2. It implies that electric field lines **spread out** in three-dimensional space, distributing the influence over a spherical surface of area $4\pi r^2$.
3. It is the fundamental reason for **Gauss's Law** in integral form: $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$.
4. The inverse-square law enables **shielding** — a charge inside a closed conductor experiences no net force from external charges.

**[3 Marks: 1 for statement, 2 for significance]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define electric field intensity. Derive the electric field due to a point charge.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ at a point is defined as the electrostatic force experienced by a unit positive test charge placed at that point, with the test charge being infinitesimally small so as not to disturb the source charge distribution.

$$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$

**Derivation:** Consider a source charge $Q$ at the origin and a test charge $q_0$ at distance $r$. By Coulomb's Law:

$$\vec{F} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q q_0}{r^2} \hat{a}_r$$

Dividing both sides by $q_0$:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{a}_r \text{ V/m}$$

This shows that the electric field is **radial** (along $\hat{a}_r$), points **outward** for positive $Q$, and depends only on the source charge — not on the test charge.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]

**Q: (a)** State and explain Coulomb's Law in both scalar and vector forms. Discuss the limitations of Coulomb's Law. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Four point charges of magnitudes $+2 \, \mu\text{C}$, $-3 \, \mu\text{C}$, $+4 \, \mu\text{C}$, and $-1 \, \mu\text{C}$ are placed at the corners of a square of side $0.5$ m. Find the net force on the $+2 \, \mu\text{C}$ charge. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Statement of Coulomb's Law:** The electrostatic force between two stationary point charges is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

**Scalar Form (Magnitude):**
$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\vert Q_1 Q_2 \vert}{r^2}$$

**Vector Form:**
$$\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2} \hat{a}_{21}$$

**Explanation of components:**
- The factor $\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$ is Coulomb's constant ($9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C²)
- $Q_1 Q_2$ is the product of charges (sign indicates direction)
- $r^2$ is the squared distance
- $\hat{a}_{21}$ is the unit vector from $Q_2$ to $Q_1$

**Limitations of Coulomb's Law:**

1. **Applicable only to stationary (static) charges** — fails for charges in rapid motion
2. **Valid only for point charges** — cannot be directly applied to continuous charge distributions without integration
3. **Holds strictly only in vacuum** — in a medium, the force is reduced by the relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$
4. **Cannot be applied at very small distances** ($< 10^{-15}$ m) where nuclear forces dominate
5. **Assumes classical (non-relativistic) conditions** — fails at speeds approaching the speed of light

**[7 Marks: 2 for statement, 2 for vector form, 3 for limitations]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Setup:** Place the square with corners at:
- A(0, 0): $Q_A = +2 \, \mu\text{C}$ (test charge)
- B(0.5, 0): $Q_B = -3 \, \mu\text{C}$
- C(0.5, 0.5): $Q_C = +4 \, \mu\text{C}$
- D(0, 0.5): $Q_D = -1 \, \mu\text{C}$

**Distances from A:**
- $r_{AB} = 0.5$ m
- $r_{AC} = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.5^2} = 0.5\sqrt{2}$ m
- $r_{AD} = 0.5$ m

**Step 1: Force $\vec{F}_{AB}$ (A to B, along +x)**
$$F_{AB} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(3 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.5)^2}$$
$$F_{AB} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{6 \times 10^{-12}}{0.25} = 0.216 \text{ N}$$
Direction: Attractive (unlike signs) → A pulled towards B → **+x direction**
$$\vec{F}_{AB} = +0.216 \, \hat{a}_x \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Force $\vec{F}_{AD}$ (A to D, along +y)**
$$F_{AD} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.5)^2}$$
$$F_{AD} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-12}}{0.25} = 0.072 \text{ N}$$
Direction: Attractive → A pulled towards D → **+y direction**
$$\vec{F}_{AD} = +0.072 \, \hat{a}_y \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Force $\vec{F}_{AC}$ (A to C, along diagonal)**
$$F_{AC} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(4 \times 10^{-12})}{(0.5\sqrt{2})^2}$$
$$F_{AC} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{8 \times 10^{-12}}{0.5} = 0.144 \text{ N}$$
Direction: Repulsive (like signs) → A pushed away from C → along $-\hat{a}_x - \hat{a}_y$ direction

Unit vector from C to A: $\frac{(0-0.5, 0-0.5)}{0.5\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-\hat{a}_x - \hat{a}_y)$

$$\vec{F}_{AC} = 0.144 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-\hat{a}_x - \hat{a}_y) = -0.1018 \, \hat{a}_x - 0.1018 \, \hat{a}_y \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Apply Superposition**
$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{AB} + \vec{F}_{AD} + \vec{F}_{AC}$$
$$\vec{F}_{total} = (0.216 - 0.1018)\hat{a}_x + (0.072 - 0.1018)\hat{a}_y$$
$$\vec{F}_{total} = 0.1142 \, \hat{a}_x - 0.0298 \, \hat{a}_y \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Magnitude**
$$\vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert = \sqrt{(0.1142)^2 + (0.0298)^2} = \sqrt{0.01304 + 0.000888} = \sqrt{0.01393}$$
$$\vert \vec{F}_{total} \vert \approx 0.118 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Direction**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-0.0298}{0.1142}\right) \approx -14.6° \text{ from +x axis}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\vec{F}_{total} \approx 0.118 \text{ N at } -14.6° \text{ from +x axis}}$$

**[7 Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for force calculations, 2 for components, 1 for superposition, 1 for magnitude/direction]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]

**Q: (a)** Define electric field intensity and electric flux. Derive the relationship between electric field and electric potential. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A charge $Q = 8 \, \mu\text{C}$ is placed at the origin. Find the electric field at point P(3, 4, 0) m. If a test charge of $+1 \, \mu\text{C}$ is placed at P, calculate the force on it and the electric potential at P. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Electric Field Intensity ($\vec{E}$):** The force experienced by a unit positive test charge placed at a point in an electric field, with the test charge being infinitesimally small.

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0} \quad \text{Units: V/m or N/C}$$

**Electric Flux ($\Psi_E$):** The total number of electric field lines passing through a given surface. Mathematically, it is the surface integral of the electric field over the area:

$$\Psi_E = \oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} \quad \text{Units: V·m or N·m}^2/\text{C}$$

**Relationship between $\vec{E}$ and Potential $V$:**

Electric potential $V$ at a point is defined as the work done per unit positive charge in bringing the charge from infinity to that point:

$$V = -\int_{\infty}^{r} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

For a point charge at the origin:

$$V = -\int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} dr = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$$

**The relationship is:**
$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z\right)$$

This shows that the electric field is the **negative gradient** of the potential. The negative sign indicates that $\vec{E}$ points from **high potential to low potential**.

**[7 Marks: 2 for definitions, 3 for derivation, 2 for gradient relationship]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $Q = 8 \, \mu\text{C} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ C at origin O(0, 0, 0), test point P(3, 4, 0) m.

**Step 1: Calculate distance $r$ from O to P**
$$r = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate magnitude of electric field at P**
$$E = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^2} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{(5)^2}$$
$$E = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{25} = \frac{72 \times 10^3}{25} = 2880 \text{ V/m}$$

**Step 3: Find unit vector from O to P**
$$\hat{a}_r = \frac{\vec{OP}}{r} = \frac{3\hat{a}_x + 4\hat{a}_y}{5} = 0.6\hat{a}_x + 0.8\hat{a}_y$$

**Step 4: Vector form of electric field**
$$\vec{E} = E \hat{a}_r = 2880(0.6\hat{a}_x + 0.8\hat{a}_y)$$
$$\vec{E} = 1728\hat{a}_x + 2304\hat{a}_y \text{ V/m}$$

**Step 5: Calculate force on test charge $q_0 = 1 \, \mu\text{C}$**
$$\vec{F} = q_0 \vec{E} = (1 \times 10^{-6})(1728\hat{a}_x + 2304\hat{a}_y)$$
$$\vec{F} = 1.728 \times 10^{-3}\hat{a}_x + 2.304 \times 10^{-3}\hat{a}_y \text{ N}$$
$$\vec{F} \approx 1.728\hat{a}_x + 2.304\hat{a}_y \text{ mN}$$

**Step 6: Calculate electric potential at P**
$$V = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{r} = 9 \times 10^9 \cdot \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{5}$$
$$V = \frac{72 \times 10^3}{5} = 14{,}400 \text{ V} = 14.4 \text{ kV}$$

**Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{\vec{E} = 1728\hat{a}_x + 2304\hat{a}_y \text{ V/m}, \quad \vec{F} = 1.728\hat{a}_x + 2.304\hat{a}_y \text{ mN}, \quad V = 14.4 \text{ kV}}$$

**[7 Marks: 1 for distance, 2 for E field, 1 for force, 1 for potential, 2 for vector components]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the distance** — Students often write $r$ instead of $r^2$ in Coulomb's Law. This is a **3-mark deduction** in part (a) and **1-2 marks** in numerical problems.
>
> 2. **Sign confusion in vector form** — Using $\hat{a}_{12}$ instead of $\hat{a}_{21}$ reverses the direction. Always verify: the unit vector points **from source to test charge**.
>
> 3. **Not applying Superposition correctly** — When multiple charges exist, students often **add magnitudes** instead of **vector components**. Always decompose into $F_x$ and $F_y$ first.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing $\mu\text{C}$ with m, or nC with cm. **Convert all charges to Coulombs and all distances to meters** before substituting.
>
> 5. **Confusing $\varepsilon_0$ with $\varepsilon_r$** — The permittivity of free space is $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. In a medium, use $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$. The problem must specify the medium.
>
> 6. **Skipping direction in final answer** — A force with **magnitude only** is incomplete. Always state both magnitude AND direction (angle from a reference axis).
>
> 7. **Calculator errors with powers of 10** — $9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-12} = 9 \times 10^{-3}$, not $9 \times 10^{21}$. Practice your scientific notation!

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

✅ **Coulomb's Law (Scalar):** $F = k\frac{\vert Q_1 Q_2 \vert}{r^2}$ where $k = 9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C²

✅ **Coulomb's Law (Vector):** $\vec{F} = k\frac{Q_1 Q_2}{r^2}\hat{a}$ where $\hat{a}$ is from source to test charge

✅ **Electric Field:** $\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0} = k\frac{Q}{r^2}\hat{a}_r$ — points **away from** positive charges, **toward** negative charges

✅ **Superposition:** Total field/force is the **vector sum** of individual contributions — never add magnitudes directly

✅ **Permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $k = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$

✅ **Force Direction Rules:**
   - Like charges → **Repulsion**
   - Unlike charges → **Attraction**

✅ **Electric Field vs. Magnetic Field:**
   - $\vec{E}$ acts on **all** charges (stationary or moving)
   - $\vec{B}$ acts **only on moving** charges, given by $\vec{F} = Q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$

✅ **Lorentz Force:** $\vec{F} = Q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ — the unified electromagnetic force law

✅ **Inverse-Square Law:** Doubling distance → 1/4 force; Tripling distance → 1/9 force

✅ **Engineering Relevance:** Capacitors, transmission lines, electrostatic precipitators, electron beam control, semiconductor devices

✅ **Exam Strategy:** Always draw a diagram, label coordinates, identify charge signs, compute magnitudes, resolve into components, then apply superposition

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gauss's Law and Ampere's Current Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gauss's Law and Ampere's Current Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Gauss's Law (Gauss's Theorem for Electric Field)

### Formal Academic Definition

**Gauss's Law** states that the total electric flux passing through any closed hypothetical surface (called a **Gaussian surface**) in free space is equal to **1/ε₀** times the total net charge enclosed within that surface. Mathematically expressed in integral form:

$$\oint_{S} \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc}$$

where $\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E}$ is the **electric flux density** (or electric displacement vector), $d\vec{S}$ is the infinitesimal outward area vector of the closed surface, and $Q_{enc}$ is the total charge enclosed.

In free space (vacuum), this reduces to the most commonly tested KTU form:

$$\oint_{S} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Gauss's Law is one of Maxwell's four foundational equations. The integral form is tested almost every semester in Module 1 questions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blowing air uniformly into a closed balloon. The total air flowing **out** through the balloon's surface depends only on **how much air you are pumping in** — it does *not* depend on the shape of the balloon. In the same way, Gauss's Law tells us that the total electric field "flow" (flux) through any closed surface depends *only* on the charge trapped *inside*, not on the geometry of the surface we draw. This is the basis of the **"fluidity" of field lines** — field lines originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges, so what enters minus what leaves equals the enclosed charge.

### Key Physical Constants

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m} \approx \frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9} \text{ F/m}$
- **Permeability of free space (relevant for Ampere):** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

---

## 1.2 Ampere's Current Law (Ampere's Circuital Law)

### Formal Academic Definition

**Ampere's Current Law** (originally proposed by André-Marie Ampère in 1826) states that the line integral of the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ around any **closed path (Amperian loop)** is exactly equal to the total current enclosed by that path:

$$\oint_{C} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$$

The equivalent form using magnetic flux density $\vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{H}$ in free space is:

$$\oint_{C} \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Maxwell's Correction (KTU Advanced Note):** Maxwell later added the **displacement current** term $\frac{d\vec{D}}{dt}$ on the right-hand side to make the law consistent with the continuity equation, giving the full Maxwell–Ampere equation. However, for Module 1, the conduction-current form above is the standard KTU-tested version.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Picture a vertical pole standing in a flowing river. The faster the water flows past the pole, the stronger the circular whirlpool (vortex) around it. Similarly, an electric current acts like the "flowing water," and the magnetic field forms **concentric circles** around the wire. Ampere's Law simply says: *walk around the wire in a closed loop, sum up the magnetic field component along your path, and you will always get a number proportional to the current piercing the loop.*

### Key Physical Constants

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Speed of light relation:** $c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}$

> [!TIP]
> **Why these laws matter in engineering:** Gauss's Law is the foundation for analyzing **capacitors, transmission lines, and electrostatic shielding**, while Ampere's Law underpins the design of **inductors, transformers, motors, solenoids, and electromagnets**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gauss's Law — Operational Logic

The derivation of Gauss's Law from Coulomb's Law rests on the **inverse-square nature** of the electrostatic force. The key operational logic is:

1. **Step 1 — Flux Definition:** Define electric flux $\Phi_E$ through a surface as $\Phi_E = \int_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S}$. The dot product captures only the component of $\vec{E}$ perpendicular to the surface.

2. **Step 2 — Closed Surface Convention:** For a closed surface, $d\vec{S}$ is conventionally taken as the **outward normal**, making flux leaving the surface positive.

3. **Step 3 — Inverse-Square Cancellation:** The $1/r^2$ decay of the Coulomb field from a point charge is exactly cancelled by the $r^2$ growth of the surface area of an enclosing sphere. The net flux becomes independent of the radius of the Gaussian sphere.

4. **Step 4 — Superposition Principle:** Since electric fields obey linear superposition, the flux due to multiple charges is the algebraic sum of fluxes from individual charges, leaving only those *inside* the surface as contributors.

5. **Step 5 — Surface Independence:** Any arbitrary closed surface enclosing the same charge gives the same flux — this is the **divergence theorem** in integral form.

### Engineering Utility of Gauss's Law

- Calculating electric fields of **highly symmetric charge distributions** (point, line, plane, sphere) without integration.
- Designing **Faraday cages** and electrostatic shields.
- Analyzing **breakdown voltages** in high-voltage equipment.
- Computing **capacitance** of parallel-plate, cylindrical, and spherical geometries.

---

## 2.2 Ampere's Current Law — Operational Logic

1. **Step 1 — Magnetic Field Circulation:** Define the line integral $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$ as the **circulation** of the magnetic field around a closed contour $C$. Only the tangential component of $\vec{H}$ contributes.

2. **Step 2 — Enclosed Current Sign Convention:** Currents piercing the loop in the direction of the right-hand rule (curl fingers in direction of $C$, thumb points in direction of $I$) are **positive**; opposite currents are **negative**.

3. **Step 3 — Path Independence (for symmetric cases):** When $\vec{H}$ is constant in magnitude and tangential to a chosen symmetric path, it can be pulled out of the integral, giving $H \cdot (2\pi r) = I_{enc}$ for cylindrical symmetry.

4. **Step 4 — Current Continuity Assumption:** In steady-state (DC) conditions, the original Ampere's Law is fully consistent with the continuity equation. For time-varying fields, the displacement current correction is required.

5. **Step 5 — Symmetry Requirement:** Ampere's Law is *only* practically useful when the chosen Amperian loop aligns with the natural symmetry of the magnetic field (e.g., circular loop around a wire, rectangular loop around a current sheet).

### Engineering Utility of Ampere's Current Law

- Calculating magnetic fields of **infinitely long wires, solenoids, toroids, coaxial cables, and infinite current sheets**.
- Designing **MRI machines, inductors, relays, and switching circuits**.
- Analyzing **eddy currents** in transformer cores and conducting plates.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Law / Concept | Integral Form | Differential Form | SI Units of Key Quantity |
|---|---------------|---------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Gauss's Law (Electric) | $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon_0}$ | Flux: V·m; $\varepsilon_0$: F/m |
| 2 | Gauss's Law (Electric Flux Density) | $\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ | $\vec{D}$: C/m² |
| 3 | Ampere's Law (Original) | $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$ | $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$ | $\vec{H}$: A/m; $I$: A |
| 4 | Ampere's Law (Free space) | $\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$ | $\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J}$ | $\vec{B}$: T (Tesla) |
| 5 | Field of infinite wire | $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$ | — | T |
| 6 | Field inside solenoid | $B = \mu_0 n I$ | — | T |
| 7 | Field inside toroid | $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2\pi r}$ | — | T |
| 8 | Field of infinite sheet | $B = \frac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ | — | T (K = surface current density, A/m) |
| 9 | Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | — | F/m |
| 10 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | — | H/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In the differential form, recall the key vector identity — Gauss's Law gives the **divergence** of $\vec{E}$, while Ampere's Law gives the **curl** of $\vec{H}$. These are two of Maxwell's four equations. The other two (Faraday's Law and $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$) belong to later modules.

### Boundary Conditions (Quick Reference)

| Condition | Electric Field | Magnetic Field |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Tangential $\vec{E}$ | Continuous across boundary (no surface current) | — |
| Normal $\vec{D}$ | Discontinuous by $\rho_s$ (surface charge) | — |
| Tangential $\vec{H}$ | — | Discontinuous by surface current density $\vec{K}$ |
| Normal $\vec{B}$ | — | Continuous across boundary |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Gauss's Law from Coulomb's Law

**Starting point:** Coulomb's Law for the electric field due to a point charge $Q$ located at the origin:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{a}_r$$

**Step 1 — Choose a Gaussian Surface:** Take a sphere of radius $r$ centered on the charge. The outward area element on this sphere is:

$$d\vec{S} = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_r$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Dot Product:** At every point on the sphere, $\vec{E}$ is purely radial and $d\vec{S}$ is also radial, so $\hat{a}_r \cdot \hat{a}_r = 1$:

$$\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \cdot r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \frac{Q \sin\theta}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over the Closed Sphere:** The limits of $\theta$ are $0$ to $\pi$ and $\phi$ are $0$ to $2\pi$:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Angular Integrals:**

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta = \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{0}^{\pi} = -(-1) - (-1) = 2$$

**Step 5 — Combine Results:**

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot (2\pi) \cdot (2) = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$$

This proves Gauss's Law for a single point charge. By the **principle of superposition**, the result extends to any distribution of charges.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Ampere's Current Law from Biot–Savart Law

**Starting point:** Biot–Savart Law gives the magnetic flux density at a point due to a current element $I \, d\vec{l}$:

$$d\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I \, d\vec{l} \times \hat{a}_r}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 1 — Express $\vec{B}$ at Distance $r$ from a Long Straight Wire:** By symmetry, the field is purely tangential and constant in magnitude on a circle of radius $r$ around the wire:

$$\vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} \hat{a}_\phi$$

**Step 2 — Choose an Amperian Loop:** Pick the circular path of radius $r$ concentric with the wire. The path element is:

$$d\vec{l} = r \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_\phi$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Dot Product:** Since $\hat{a}_\phi \cdot \hat{a}_\phi = 1$:

$$\vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} \cdot r \, d\phi = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} d\phi$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Around the Full Loop (0 to $2\pi$):**

$$\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi = \mu_0 I$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$$

This is the integral form of Ampere's Law, rigorously derived from the Biot–Savart Law for the symmetric case. The result generalizes via **Stokes' Theorem** to the differential form $\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Application via Symmetry

| Charge / Current Distribution | Best Gaussian / Amperian Surface | Resulting Field Magnitude |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Point charge $Q$ | Sphere of radius $r$ | $E = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ |
| Infinite line charge $\rho_L$ | Coaxial cylinder of radius $r$, length $L$ | $E = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$ |
| Infinite sheet of charge $\rho_S$ | Pill-box (rectangular Gaussian cylinder) | $E = \frac{\rho_S}{2\varepsilon_0}$ |
| Uniformly charged sphere (volume) | Sphere of radius $r$ (inside / outside) | Inside: $E = \frac{\rho r}{3\varepsilon_0}$; Outside: $E = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$ |
| Long straight current-carrying wire | Circle of radius $r$ around wire | $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$ |
| Solenoid ($n$ turns/unit length) | Rectangle (one side inside, one outside) | $B = \mu_0 n I$ (inside); $\approx 0$ (outside) |
| Toroid ($N$ turns, mean radius $r$) | Circle of radius $r$ inside the toroid | $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{2\pi r}$ |
| Infinite current sheet (surface current $K$) | Rectangle straddling the sheet | $B = \frac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In derivations, always state the **symmetry argument first** *before* writing the integral. Examiners award 1–2 marks just for the symmetry justification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gauss's Law — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Charges Distributed in Space] --> B[Construct Hypothetical Closed Gaussian Surface]
    B --> C{Symmetry Identified?}
    C -- Spherical --> D1[Use Spherical Gaussian Surface]
    C -- Cylindrical --> D2[Use Coaxial Cylindrical Surface]
    C -- Planar --> D3[Use Pillbox Gaussian Surface]
    D1 --> E[Evaluate Surface Integral of E dot dS]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Gauss Law: Integral equals Qenc by epsilon0]
    F --> G[Extract Electric Field E]
    G --> H[Result: Closed Form Expression for E]
```

## 4.2 Ampere's Current Law — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Steady Current in Conductor] --> B[Identify Symmetry of B Field]
    B --> C{Symmetry Type?}
    C -- Cylindrical --> D1[Choose Circular Amperian Loop of radius r]
    C -- Solenoidal --> D2[Choose Rectangular Loop Straddling Solenoid]
    C -- Toroidal --> D3[Choose Circular Loop Inside Toroid]
    D1 --> E[Evaluate Line Integral of B dot dl around closed path]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Ampere Law: Integral equals mu0 times Ienc]
    F --> G[Extract Magnetic Field B]
    G --> H[Result: Closed Form Expression for B]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Maxwell's Equations (Module 1 Context)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Maxwell Four Equations] --> B[Gauss Law for Electric Field]
```
