```mermaid
    A --> C[Gauss Law for Magnetic Field]
    A --> D[Ampere Maxwell Law]
    A --> E[Faraday Law]
    B --> B1[Integral Form: Closed Surface Integral of E dot dS equals Q by eps0]
    B --> B2[Differential Form: Divergence of E equals rho by eps0]
    D --> D1[Integral Form: Closed Line Integral of H dot dl equals Ienc plus dD by dt]
    D --> D2[Differential Form: Curl of H equals J plus dD by dt]
    D1 --> F[Used in this Module 1]
    B1 --> F
```

## 4.4 Visual Schematic — Field Line Behaviour

| Phenomenon | Visual Description |
|------------|--------------------|
| **Positive Point Charge** | Field lines radiate **outward** symmetrically. Any closed surface around it has a net **outward** flux. |
| **Negative Point Charge** | Field lines point **inward**. Net flux through a closed surface is **negative** (toward the charge). |
| **Dipole (+q and –q)** | Equal numbers of lines **enter and exit** a far-away surface; net flux is **zero**. |
| **Current-Carrying Wire** | Magnetic field lines form **concentric circles** in planes perpendicular to the wire. Direction given by the **right-hand rule** (thumb = current, fingers = $\vec{B}$). |
| **Solenoid** | Magnetic field lines are **parallel and dense inside** the coil, nearly **zero outside**. Behaves like a bar magnet. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in exams:** Mermaid diagrams are presented here for **conceptual understanding**. In KTU answer sheets, examiners expect a **hand-drawn 2D figure** with the Gaussian surface / Amperian loop clearly marked and the field direction indicated by arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State and explain Gauss's Law in electrostatics. Mention the significance of $\varepsilon_0$ in the equation.**

**Model Answer:**

Gauss's Law states that the total electric flux passing through any closed surface in free space is equal to the total charge enclosed divided by the permittivity of free space:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Significance of $\varepsilon_0$:** It is the **permittivity of free space**, with value $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. It quantifies how easily the vacuum permits the formation of electric flux lines for a given enclosed charge. A larger $\varepsilon_0$ means the same charge produces a *smaller* electric field.

**Physical interpretation:** The law is valid for *any* closed surface, regardless of shape. It is most useful when applied to charge distributions with high symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, planar), allowing $\vec{E}$ to be extracted without performing direct Coulomb integration.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**State Ampere's Current Law. Why is it not valid for time-varying currents without Maxwell's correction?**

**Model Answer:**

Ampere's Current Law in its original form states that the line integral of the magnetic field intensity around any closed path equals the conduction current enclosed by that path:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$$

**Why it fails for time-varying currents:** The original law is inconsistent with the **continuity equation** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t}$) under non-steady conditions. Taking the divergence of both sides leads to a contradiction: the left side gives $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ while the continuity equation requires $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} \neq 0$ when charge density varies. Maxwell resolved this by adding the **displacement current** term $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ on the right-hand side, giving the complete **Maxwell–Ampere equation**:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc} + \frac{d}{dt}\int_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1 Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electric field intensity at a distance $r$ from an infinite line charge of uniform linear charge density $\rho_L$ C/m, using Gauss's Law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical conductor of radius $a$ carries a uniformly distributed current $I$ A. Using Ampere's Law, find the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ at (i) $r < a$ and (ii) $r > a$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Symmetry Argument:** By the infinite extent of the line charge, the electric field must be purely radial, point **outward** (for $\rho_L > 0$), and depend only on the radial distance $r$. There is no $\phi$ or $z$ dependence.

**Step 2 — Choose Gaussian Surface:** A coaxial cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$ is the natural choice. It has three surfaces: curved surface $S_1$, and two flat end caps $S_2$ and $S_3$.

**Step 3 — Flux Through the End Caps:** Since $\vec{E}$ is purely radial and the end-cap normals are along $\hat{a}_z$:

$$\int_{S_2} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{S_3} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Flux Through the Curved Surface:** On the curved surface, $\vec{E}$ is parallel to $d\vec{S} = r \, d\phi \, dz \, \hat{a}_r$:

$$\int_{S_1} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = E_r \cdot (2\pi r L)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Gauss's Law:**

$$E_r \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{\rho_L L}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $E_r$:**

$$E_r = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 r}$$

This is the final expression for the electric field of an infinite line charge. The direction is $\hat{a}_r$ (radially outward for positive $\rho_L$).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Symmetry Argument:** The current is uniformly distributed, so by cylindrical symmetry, $\vec{H}$ is purely in the $\hat{a}_\phi$ direction and depends only on $r$.

**Step 2 — Choose Amperian Loop:** A circle of radius $r$ concentric with the conductor.

**Case (i): $r < a$ (inside the conductor)**

**Step 3 — Enclosed Current:** The uniform current density is $J = \frac{I}{\pi a^2}$. The current enclosed by a loop of radius $r$ is:

$$I_{enc} = J \cdot \pi r^2 = \frac{I r^2}{a^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ampere's Law:**

$$H_\phi \cdot (2\pi r) = I_{enc} = \frac{I r^2}{a^2}$$

$$H_\phi = \frac{I r}{2\pi a^2} \quad (r < a)$$

**Case (ii): $r > a$ (outside the conductor)**

**Step 5 — Enclosed Current:** The full current $I$ is enclosed:

$$I_{enc} = I$$

**Step 6 — Apply Ampere's Law:**

$$H_\phi \cdot (2\pi r) = I$$

$$H_\phi = \frac{I}{2\pi r} \quad (r > a)$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\vec{H} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{I r}{2\pi a^2} \hat{a}_\phi, & r < a \\[2mm] \dfrac{I}{2\pi r} \hat{a}_\phi, & r > a \end{cases}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Module 1 Choice B]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, derive the magnetic field intensity inside a long solenoid of $n$ turns per unit length carrying a current $I$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** State and prove Gauss's Law using Coulomb's Law. Explain why a Gaussian surface outside a charge distribution gives zero net flux even when field lines pass through it. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Diagram Description:** A long solenoid is a helical coil wound tightly on a cylindrical form. By the right-hand rule, when current $I$ flows in the windings, the magnetic field inside the solenoid is **along the axis** and nearly uniform. Outside, the field is approximately zero (for an ideal infinite solenoid).

**Step 2 — Choose Amperian Loop:** Take a rectangular closed path $ABCD$ with one side $AB$ of length $l$ parallel to the axis **inside** the solenoid, the opposite side $CD$ **outside**, and the two perpendicular sides $BC$ and $DA$ radial.

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Side:**

- **Side $AB$ (inside, parallel to axis):** $\vec{H}$ is along the axis, parallel to $d\vec{l}$:

$$\int_A^B \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \cdot l$$

- **Side $CD$ (outside):** $\vec{H} \approx 0$ outside, so this contribution is zero.

- **Sides $BC$ and $DA$ (perpendicular):** $\vec{H}$ inside is parallel to the axis while $d\vec{l}$ is radial, so $\vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$. Outside contribution is also zero.

**Step 4 — Total Line Integral:**

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \cdot l$$

**Step 5 — Enclosed Current:** The Amperian loop encloses $n \cdot l$ turns, each carrying current $I$:

$$I_{enc} = n l I$$

**Step 6 — Apply Ampere's Law:**

$$H \cdot l = n l I$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $H$ and Convert to $B$:**

$$H = n I \quad \text{and} \quad B = \mu_0 n I$$

**Conclusion:** The magnetic field inside an ideal infinite solenoid is uniform and depends only on the turn density $n$ and current $I$, *not* on the position along the axis.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Statement:** Gauss's Law states that the total electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by $\varepsilon_0$:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Proof from Coulomb's Law:** *(See the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

**Recap of proof steps:**

1. Coulomb's Law: $\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{a}_r$
2. Spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ with $d\vec{S} = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{a}_r$.
3. $\vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$
4. Integrate: $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$.
5. By superposition, this extends to any charge distribution.

**Why a Gaussian surface *outside* a charge distribution gives zero net flux:**

A closed surface that encloses **no net charge** has $Q_{enc} = 0$. Therefore, by Gauss's Law:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{0}{\varepsilon_0} = 0$$

Physically, every field line that **enters** the surface (negative flux contribution) must **exit** the surface (positive flux contribution), because field lines are continuous and have no sources or sinks in charge-free regions. The net inward flux exactly cancels the net outward flux. This is true even if individual field lines pass through the surface — what matters is the **net signed flux**, which integrates to zero.

For example, enclosing a positive charge $+Q$ together with an equal negative charge $-Q$ (a dipole) inside a Gaussian surface gives $Q_{enc} = 0$ and thus zero net flux, even though field lines from $+Q$ do thread the surface before terminating on $-Q$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetry argument** (lose 1–2 marks): Always justify *why* a particular Gaussian surface or Amperian loop is chosen *before* writing integrals.
> 2. **Sign errors in enclosed current/charge**: Currents opposite to the right-hand-rule direction are **negative**. Charges outside the Gaussian surface **do not** contribute, even if their field lines cross the surface.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion of $\varepsilon_0$**: Use $\varepsilon_0 = \frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9}$ F/m for clean calculations; avoid mixing $\varepsilon_0$ and $\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$ incorrectly.
> 4. **Mixing up $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{D}$**: $\vec{E}$ is the electric field intensity (V/m), $\vec{D}$ is electric flux density (C/m²). They differ by the factor $\varepsilon_0$ (in free space) or $\varepsilon$ (in a medium).
> 5. **Forgetting the displacement current term** in time-varying problems: Original Ampere's Law is valid only for *steady* (DC) currents.
> 6. **Neglecting end-cap flux** in cylindrical Gaussian surface problems: Confirm that $\vec{E} \perp d\vec{S}$ on the end caps before setting them to zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gauss's Law (Integral):** $\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$ — relates **electric flux** through a **closed surface** to enclosed charge.
- **Gauss's Law (Differential):** $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon_0}$ — states that charge density is the source of electric field divergence.
- **Ampere's Law (Integral, original):** $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$ — relates **magnetic field circulation** around a closed path to enclosed current.
- **Ampere's Law (Differential):** $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$ — current density is the source of magnetic field curl.
- **Key insight:** Gauss's Law involves the **divergence** of $\vec{E}$ (scalar), while Ampere's Law involves the **curl** of $\vec{H}$ (vector). They are two distinct Maxwell equations, applicable to different symmetries.
- **Symmetry is the gateway:** Gauss's Law and Ampere's Law are only *practically* useful when high symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, planar) allows the field to be pulled out of the integral.
- **Permittivity** ($\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m) and **permeability** ($\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m) are fundamental constants of free space, related by $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$.
- **Right-hand rule** is the standard sign convention for both current direction in Ampere's Law and outward normal in Gauss's Law.
- **Application checklist for problems:** (1) Identify symmetry, (2) Draw Gaussian/Amperian surface aligned with symmetry, (3) Write flux/line integral, (4) Identify enclosed charge/current, (5) Solve for field, (6) State direction with unit vector.
- **Limitation to remember:** Original Ampere's Law fails for time-varying currents — Maxwell's displacement current correction is essential for EM wave propagation, which is the central theme of this entire course.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson and Laplace Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson and Laplace Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Poisson Equation** and the **Laplace Equation** are second-order partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern the behavior of the **scalar electric potential** $V$ in a static (time-invariant) electromagnetic field. They are derived directly from the point form of **Gauss's Law for Electric Fields** combined with the gradient relationship between the electric field $\vec{E}$ and the potential $V$.

The two equations are mathematically stated as:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{(Poisson's Equation)} $$

$$ \nabla^{2} V = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace's Equation)} $$

where:
- $\nabla^{2}$ is the **Laplacian operator** (del-squared), a scalar differential operator.
- $V$ is the **electric scalar potential**, measured in **Volts (V)**.
- $\rho_{v}$ is the **volume charge density**, measured in **Coulombs per cubic meter ($\text{C/m}^3$)**.
- $\varepsilon$ is the **permittivity of the medium**, measured in **Farads per meter ($\text{F/m}$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Laplace's Equation is a special case of Poisson's Equation.** It applies strictly to **charge-free regions** of space where $\rho_{v} = 0$. In any region containing charges, Poisson's equation must be used.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the top of a hill, and the hill represents the **electric potential** $V$ in space.

- The **slope** of the hill at any point is the **electric field** $\vec{E}$ (a steeper slope means a stronger field pushing charges downhill).
- The **Laplacian** $\nabla^{2} V$ acts like a measure of how the "curvature" or "concavity" of the hill behaves at a point.
- If the region is **empty of charges** (a "valley of nothingness"), the landscape must be **perfectly smooth** (no bumps, no dips created by charges). This smoothness is **Laplace's Equation**: $\nabla^{2} V = 0$.
- If there are **charges** (positive charges are like underground "pushers" lifting the terrain up, negative charges are "sinkholes" pulling it down), the landscape gets curved by them. This forced curvature is **Poisson's Equation**: $\nabla^{2} V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** You must be able to **state, derive, and apply** both equations. Additionally, you are expected to know the **Laplacian in all three standard coordinate systems** (Cartesian, Cylindrical, and Spherical), as the choice of coordinates depends on the geometry of the problem (e.g., cylindrical for coaxial cables, spherical for antennas).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Typical Value (Free Space) |
|--------|----------|---------|---------------------------|
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | $\text{F/m}$ | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \approx \dfrac{1}{36\pi \times 10^{9}}}$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Permittivity of medium | $\text{F/m}$ | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | Relative permittivity | dimensionless | $\mathbf{1}$ (air), $\mathbf{2.2}$ (plexiglass), $\mathbf{10}$ (water) |
| $\rho_v$ | Volume charge density | $\text{C/m}^3$ | Problem-specific |
| $V$ | Electric potential | $\text{V}$ | Problem-specific |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation (from Maxwell's Equations to Poisson/Laplace)

**Step 1: Start with Gauss's Law in Point (Differential) Form**
The fundamental governing equation for static electric fields in matter is:

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{v} $$

**Step 2: Introduce the Constitutive Relation**
For linear, isotropic, and homogeneous media, the electric flux density $\vec{D}$ is related to the electric field $\vec{E}$ by:

$$ \vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E} $$

Substituting this into Gauss's law:

$$ \nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \rho_{v} $$

**Step 3: Homogeneity Simplification**
If $\varepsilon$ is a **constant** throughout the region (homogeneous medium), it can be pulled out of the divergence operator:

$$ \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) = \rho_{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 4: Relate $\vec{E}$ to the Scalar Potential $V$**
Because the electrostatic field is **conservative** (irrotational, $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$), it can be expressed as the gradient of a scalar potential:

$$ \vec{E} = -\nabla V $$

The negative sign indicates that $\vec{E}$ points from **higher potential to lower potential**.

**Step 5: Substitute into the Divergence Equation**
Plugging $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ into $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho_v/\varepsilon$:

$$ \nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 6: Recognize the Laplacian Operator**
By definition, the **divergence of a gradient** is the **Laplacian**: $\nabla \cdot (\nabla V) = \nabla^{2} V$. Thus:

$$ -\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

Rearranging yields **Poisson's Equation**:

$$ \boxed{\nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon}} $$

**Step 7: The Charge-Free Special Case**
In a region where there is **no charge** ($\rho_{v} = 0$), the right-hand side vanishes, leaving **Laplace's Equation**:

$$ \boxed{\nabla^{2} V = 0} $$

### 2.2 Why and How — The Physical "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the Laplacian:** The Laplacian $\nabla^{2} V$ at a point measures the **net outflow of the gradient of $V$** from an infinitesimal volume around that point. Physically, it tells you how the potential "curves" due to nearby charges. Positive charges cause the potential to "bowl" upward; negative charges cause a "saddle" shape.

- **Why $\nabla^{2} V$ (and not $\nabla V$)?** Because a point charge affects the potential in a 3D neighborhood around it, not just in one direction. The Laplacian accounts for **second-order spatial variation** in **all directions** simultaneously.
- **How does geometry change things?** In Cartesian coordinates, the Laplacian is the simple sum $\partial^{2}/\partial x^{2} + \partial^{2}/\partial y^{2} + \partial^{2}/\partial z^{2}$. In cylindrical or spherical systems, it has additional weighting factors ($1/r$, $1/r^2 \sin\theta$) to account for the geometry of the coordinate surfaces.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Poisson and Laplace equations are the **backbone of electrostatic analysis** in virtually every electrical engineering domain:

1. **Capacitor Design:** Computing capacitance of parallel-plate, cylindrical, and spherical capacitors.
2. **VLSI / Semiconductor Device Physics:** Modeling potential profiles in p-n junctions, MOSFETs, and bipolar transistors.
3. **High-Voltage Engineering:** Designing insulators and bushings to avoid dielectric breakdown.
4. **Antenna Theory (Spherical Coordinates):** Computing radiated fields and antenna input impedance.
5. **Transmission Lines (Cylindrical Coordinates):** Modeling potential inside coaxial cables.
6. **Cathode Ray Tubes & Electron Beams:** Determining the focusing and deflection potentials.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

**Table 1: Laplacian Operator in the Three Standard Coordinate Systems**

| Coordinate System | Full Laplacian Expansion |
|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Cartesian** $(x, y, z)$ | $\nabla^{2} V = \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$ |
| **Cylindrical** $(\rho, \phi, z)$ | $\nabla^{2} V = \dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}}$ |
| **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ | $\nabla^{2} V = \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^{2} \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^{2} \sin^{2}\theta}\dfrac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on KTU notation:** In some KTU textbooks, the cylindrical radial coordinate is written as $r$ or $\rho$. The standard convention is $\rho$ for the radial cylindrical distance, while $r$ is used for the spherical radial distance. Be careful not to confuse the two in your answers.

**Table 2: Master Summary of Key Equations and Boundary Conditions**

| Concept | Equation / Statement | SI Unit |
|---------|----------------------|---------|
| Poisson's Equation | $\nabla^{2} V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$ | $\text{V/m}^2$ |
| Laplace's Equation | $\nabla^{2} V = 0$ | $\text{V/m}^2$ |
| Electric field from potential | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | $\text{V/m}$ |
| Dirichlet BC (specifies $V$) | $V \vert_{\text{boundary}} = f(\text{boundary})$ | $\text{V}$ |
| Neumann BC (specifies $\partial V / \partial n$) | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial n}\vert_{\text{boundary}} = g(\text{boundary})$ | $\text{V/m}$ |
| Uniqueness condition | Solution is unique if $V$ is specified on the **entire** closed boundary | — |
| Permittivity relation | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | $\text{F/m}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Trap:** If a problem gives a volume charge density $\rho_v$ in $\mu\text{C/m}^3$ (microcoulombs), you **must** convert it to $\text{C/m}^3$ before substituting. Forgetting this unit conversion is one of the most common ways students lose 1–2 marks in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Gauss's Law to Poisson's Equation

We will carry out the **complete algebraic derivation** with **every transition shown explicitly** so that you can reproduce it in the KTU examination step-by-step.

**Starting Point:** Gauss's Law in differential (point) form.

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{v} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} $$

This equation states that the **divergence of the electric flux density** at any point in space equals the **volume charge density** at that point.

**Step 1: Apply the Constitutive Relation $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$**

$$ \nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \rho_{v} $$

We expand this using the **product rule for divergence**: $\nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) + \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \varepsilon)$.

$$ \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) + \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \varepsilon) = \rho_{v} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)} $$

**Step 2: Assume a Homogeneous Medium**

In a **homogeneous** medium, the permittivity $\varepsilon$ is **constant** everywhere (it does not vary with position). Therefore, the gradient of $\varepsilon$ is zero:

$$ \nabla \varepsilon = 0 \quad \text{(in a homogeneous medium)} $$

Substituting this into Equation 2, the second term vanishes:

$$ \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) + \vec{E} \cdot (0) = \rho_{v} $$

$$ \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) = \rho_{v} $$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{...(Equation 3)} $$

**Step 3: Substitute the Definition of Electric Field from a Potential**

By definition, the electrostatic field is the **negative gradient** of the scalar potential $V$:

$$ \vec{E} = -\nabla V \quad \text{...(Equation 4)} $$

Substituting Equation 4 into Equation 3:

$$ \nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 4: Apply the Distributive Property of the Divergence Operator**

The minus sign can be factored out:

$$ -(\nabla \cdot \nabla V) = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 5: Recognize the Definition of the Laplacian Operator**

The **Laplacian operator** is defined as the **divergence of the gradient**:

$$ \nabla \cdot \nabla V = \nabla^{2} V $$

Therefore:

$$ -\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 6: Final Rearrangement**

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

This is the **final form of Poisson's Equation** in a homogeneous medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for the Examiner:** Always state your assumptions explicitly when deriving Poisson's equation in an exam. The two most important assumptions are: (1) **electrostatics** (fields are time-invariant, $\partial/\partial t = 0$), and (2) **homogeneous medium** ($\varepsilon$ is constant). If either is missing, your derivation is incomplete and you will lose marks.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Reduction to Laplace's Equation

**Starting from Poisson's Equation:**

$$ \nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Condition:** In a region of space that is **free of any charges**, the volume charge density is zero everywhere in that region:

$$ \rho_{v} = 0 $$

**Substitution:** Setting $\rho_v = 0$ on the right-hand side:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = -\frac{0}{\varepsilon} $$

$$ \nabla^{2} V = 0 $$

This is **Laplace's Equation**. The implication is profound: in a charge-free region, the potential varies **linearly** (in 1D) or in a **harmonic** manner (in 2D/3D). There are no local "bends" caused by charges; the function is as smooth as the boundary conditions allow.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: A Generic 1D Solution of Laplace's Equation (Worked Analytical Method)

The **standard KTU-style approach** to solving Laplace's equation involves three stages: (1) reduce the PDE to an ODE, (2) integrate twice, (3) apply boundary conditions.

**General 1D Problem:** Suppose the potential $V$ depends only on $x$ in a region with no charge. Then:

$$ \frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{...(Equation A)} $$

**Stage 1 — First Integration:** Integrate both sides with respect to $x$:

$$ \int \frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} \, dx = \int 0 \, dx $$

$$ \frac{dV}{dx} = A $$

where $A$ is the **first constant of integration**.

**Stage 2 — Second Integration:** Integrate again with respect to $x$:

$$ \int \frac{dV}{dx} \, dx = \int A \, dx $$

$$ V(x) = A x + B $$

where $B$ is the **second constant of integration**.

**Stage 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
Two boundary conditions are required to determine $A$ and $B$. For example, in a parallel-plate capacitor with $V(0) = 0$ and $V(d) = V_0$:

- Apply $V(0) = 0$: $\quad 0 = A(0) + B \Rightarrow B = 0$
- Apply $V(d) = V_0$: $\quad V_0 = A(d) + 0 \Rightarrow A = V_0 / d$

**Final Solution:**

$$ V(x) = \frac{V_0}{d} x $$

This shows the potential varies **linearly** between the plates, and the electric field is constant:

$$ E = -\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{V_0}{d} $$

The negative sign indicates the field points in the $-x$ direction (from the higher-potential plate to the lower-potential plate), exactly as expected physically.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: A Generic 1D Solution of Poisson's Equation (Worked Analytical Method)

**General 1D Problem:** In a region between two parallel plates separated by distance $d$, the volume charge density is uniform: $\rho_v = \rho_0$. The plates are at $V(0) = 0$ and $V(d) = 0$.

**Step 1: Reduce to an ODE**

$$ \frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho_0}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 2: First Integration**

$$ \frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{\rho_0}{\varepsilon} x + A $$

**Step 3: Second Integration**

$$ V(x) = -\frac{\rho_0}{2\varepsilon} x^{2} + A x + B $$

**Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions**

- At $x = 0$, $V = 0$: $\quad 0 = 0 + 0 + B \Rightarrow B = 0$
- At $x = d$, $V = 0$: $\quad 0 = -\dfrac{\rho_0 d^{2}}{2\varepsilon} + A d \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{\rho_0 d}{2\varepsilon}$

**Final Solution:**

$$ V(x) = -\frac{\rho_0}{2\varepsilon} x^{2} + \frac{\rho_0 d}{2\varepsilon} x = \frac{\rho_0}{2\varepsilon} x (d - x) $$

The **parabolic** shape of $V(x)$ is the signature of a uniform volume charge — the potential curves due to the constant "push" of charges, exactly as Poisson's equation predicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Solving Poisson/Laplace Problems

The diagram below maps the **decision-making process** you must follow when tackling a Poisson or Laplace problem in the KTU examination. This is the **exact thought process** a top-scorer uses to maximize marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read the Problem Statement] --> B{Is the region charge-free?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Laplace Equation: nabla-squared V equals 0]
    B -- No --> D[Use Poisson Equation: nabla-squared V equals minus rho-v over epsilon]
    C --> E{What is the geometry?}
    D --> E
    E -- Parallel plates / rectangular --> F[Use Cartesian Coordinates]
    E -- Coaxial cable / cylindrical rod --> G[Use Cylindrical Coordinates]
    E -- Sphere / antenna --> H[Use Spherical Coordinates]
    F --> I{Does V depend on more than one variable?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- No, 1D only --> J[Reduce PDE to ODE by dropping zero derivatives]
    I -- Yes, 2D or 3D --> K[Apply Separation of Variables: V equals X times Y times Z]
    J --> L[Integrate Twice to get general solution with constants]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Apply Boundary Conditions to find constants]
    M --> N[Write Final Expression for V]
    N --> O[If required, compute E equals minus gradient of V]
    O --> P[End: Substitute numerical values for final numerical answer]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Maxwell's Equations and Poisson/Laplace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Maxwell Equation 1: Gauss Law for E] --> M2[Constitutive Relation: D equals epsilon times E]
    M2 --> M3[Homogeneous Medium: epsilon is constant]
    M3 --> M4[Define Scalar Potential: E equals minus gradient of V]
    M4 --> M5[Combine to form Poisson Equation]
    M5 --> M6{Set rho-v equals 0 for charge-free region}
    M6 -- Yes --> M7[Laplace Equation: nabla-squared V equals 0]
    M6 -- No --> M8[Poisson Equation remains: nabla-squared V equals minus rho-v over epsilon]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Boundary Condition Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC[Boundary Conditions for Unique Solution] --> BC1[Dirichlet: V specified on entire boundary]
    BC --> BC2[Neumann: Normal derivative of V specified on boundary]
    BC --> BC3[Mixed Cauchy: V on part of boundary and dV by dn on remaining part]
    BC1 --> UC[Uniqueness Theorem Guarantees Single Solution]
    BC2 --> UC
    BC3 --> UC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **Uniqueness Theorem** is what makes the entire boundary-value problem approach to electromagnetics valid. It guarantees that if you find **any** function $V$ that satisfies both the differential equation (Poisson or Laplace) **and** the boundary conditions, that function is the **only** correct answer. This is why you can confidently submit your solution in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Poisson's equation and Laplace's equation. Mention the condition under which Poisson's equation reduces to Laplace's equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Poisson's Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that relates the scalar electric potential $V$ to the volume charge density $\rho_v$ in a homogeneous medium:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Laplace's Equation** is the special form of Poisson's equation that applies in a region with **no charge**:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = 0 $$

**Condition for reduction:** Poisson's equation reduces to Laplace's equation when the region under consideration is **charge-free**, i.e., the volume charge density $\rho_v = 0$ everywhere in that region. Substituting $\rho_v = 0$ into Poisson's equation yields $0/-\varepsilon = 0$, giving $\nabla^{2} V = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Hint:** 1 mark for stating Poisson's equation, 1 mark for stating Laplace's equation, 1 mark for the reduction condition.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Write the Laplacian operator $\nabla^{2} V$ in Cartesian, cylindrical, and spherical coordinate systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

In **Cartesian coordinates** $(x, y, z)$:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}} $$

In **cylindrical coordinates** $(\rho, \phi, z)$:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial z^{2}} $$

In **spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}\frac{\partial^{2} V}{\partial \phi^{2}} $$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Solving Laplace's Equation in 1D

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive Poisson's equation for a homogeneous medium starting from Gauss's law in point form. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two infinite parallel conducting plates are located at $x = 0$ and $x = d$ in free space. The plate at $x = 0$ is grounded (i.e., $V = 0$) and the plate at $x = d$ is maintained at a potential $V = V_0$. The region between the plates is **charge-free**. Find the potential distribution $V(x)$ between the plates using Laplace's equation, and hence determine the electric field $\vec{E}$ in the region. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Poisson's Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the starting equation — Gauss's law in differential form:

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_{v} $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the constitutive relation $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$:

$$ \nabla \cdot (\varepsilon \vec{E}) = \rho_{v} $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use the product rule for divergence to expand:

$$ \varepsilon (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) + \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \varepsilon) = \rho_{v} $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State Assumption 1 — the medium is **homogeneous**, so $\nabla \varepsilon = 0$. This simplifies to:

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State Assumption 2 — the field is **electrostatic** (time-invariant), so we can define a scalar potential: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$:

$$ \nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

$$ -\nabla^{2} V = \frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Rearrange to get the final Poisson's equation:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = -\frac{\rho_{v}}{\varepsilon} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions to be stated explicitly (for full 7 marks):**
> 1. The medium is **homogeneous, linear, and isotropic** (so $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ with $\varepsilon$ a constant scalar).
> 2. The field is **electrostatic** (no time variation, $\partial/\partial t = 0$).
> 3. The field is in a **source-free region outside the charges** for the potential to be well-defined.

**Part (b) — Solving Laplace's Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the governing equation. Since the region between the plates is **charge-free** ($\rho_v = 0$), we use **Laplace's equation**:

$$ \nabla^{2} V = 0 $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Choose coordinates. Since the plates are infinite and parallel to the $y$-$z$ plane, the potential depends only on $x$ (no $y$ or $z$ dependence). In Cartesian 1D, Laplace's equation becomes:

$$ \frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = 0 $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate once:

$$ \frac{dV}{dx} = A $$

where $A$ is the first constant of integration.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integrate again:

$$ V(x) = A x + B $$

where $B$ is the second constant of integration.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the first boundary condition $V(0) = 0$:

$$ 0 = A(0) + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0 $$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply the second boundary condition $V(d) = V_0$:

$$ V_0 = A d + 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{V_0}{d} $$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Write the final potential distribution and compute the electric field:

$$ V(x) = \frac{V_0}{d} x $$

$$ \vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\frac{dV}{dx} \hat{a}_x = -\frac{V_0}{d} \hat{a}_x $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The potential varies **linearly** between the plates, and the electric field is **uniform** (constant in both magnitude and direction) with magnitude $\vert E \vert = V_0 / d$, directed from the high-potential plate at $x = d$ to the grounded plate at $x = 0$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Solving Poisson's Equation in 1D with Charge

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Uniqueness Theorem** for the solution of Poisson's and Laplace's equations. Why is this theorem important in electromagnetic field analysis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In the region between two parallel plates separated by $d = 2\,\text{cm}$ in free space, there is a uniform volume charge density of $\rho_v = 10\,\mu\text{C/m}^3$. The plates are at potentials $V(0) = 0$ and $V(d) = 0$ (both grounded). Using Poisson's equation, find the potential distribution $V(x)$ and the electric field $\vec{E}$ between the plates. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Uniqueness Theorem (7 marks):**

**Statement (3 marks):** The Uniqueness Theorem states that **a solution to Poisson's (or Laplace's) equation in a given region is unique** if the potential $V$ is specified on the **entire closed boundary** of that region (this is called the **Dirichlet condition**). Alternatively, the solution is also unique if the **normal derivative of the potential**, $\partial V / \partial n$, is specified on the entire boundary (**Neumann condition**), or a combination of both (mixed Cauchy condition).

**Explanation (2 marks):** Mathematically, the theorem guarantees that if you have found any function $V$ that satisfies both the differential equation (Poisson or Laplace) **and** the prescribed boundary conditions, then that function is the **only physically correct** answer. The proof is by contradiction: if two different solutions $V_1$ and $V_2$ both satisfied the same equation and boundary conditions, then their difference $W = V_1 - V_2$ would satisfy $\nabla^2 W = 0$ with zero boundary values, and using Green's identity one can show that $W$ must be identically zero, forcing $V_1 = V_2$.

**Importance (2 marks):**
1. It allows engineers to use **trial-and-error methods** (separation of variables, Fourier series, numerical methods) and be confident that the first correct solution found is the only one.
2. It underpins the use of **numerical techniques** like Finite Element Method (FEM) and Finite Difference Method (FDM), since these methods are essentially seeking the unique solution that satisfies the boundary conditions.

**Part (b) — Solving Poisson's Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the governing equation. With a uniform charge density, we use **Poisson's equation**:

$$ \frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $\rho_v = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{C/m}^3$ and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$:

$$ \frac{d^{2} V}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}} = -1.1295 \times 10^{6}\,\text{V/m}^2 $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate once:

$$ \frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{\rho_v}{\varepsilon} x + A = -1.1295 \times 10^{6} \, x + A $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integrate again:

$$ V(x) = -\frac{\rho_v}{2\varepsilon} x^{2} + A x + B = -5.6475 \times 10^{5} \, x^{2} + A x + B $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the first boundary condition $V(0) = 0$:

$$ 0 = 0 + 0 + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0 $$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply the second boundary condition $V(d) = V(0.02) = 0$:

$$ 0 = -5.6475 \times 10^{5} \times (0.02)^{2} + A \times 0.02 $$

$$ 0 = -5.6475 \times 10^{5} \times 4 \times 10^{-4} + 0.02 A $$

$$ 0 = -225.9 + 0.02 A $$

$$ A = \frac{225.9}{0.02} = 11295\,\text{V/m} $$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Write the final solution and compute the electric field:

$$ V(x) = -5.6475 \times 10^{5} \, x^{2} + 11295 \, x \quad \text{(V, with } x \text{ in meters)} $$

$$ \vec{E} = -\frac{dV}{dx} \hat{a}_x = \left(1.1295 \times 10^{6} \, x - 11295\right) \hat{a}_x \quad \text{V/m} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification by Physical Intuition:** The potential $V(x)$ is a downward-opening parabola (since the coefficient of $x^2$ is negative) with zeros at $x = 0$ and $x = d = 0.02$ m, and a maximum somewhere in between. This makes physical sense: positive charges "push" the potential up in the middle, but the grounded plates pull it back to zero at the boundaries.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (lose 1 mark):** Always convert $\mu\text{C/m}^3$ to $\text{C/m}^3$ before substituting into formulas. The difference between $10\,\mu\text{C/m}^3$ and $10\,\text{C/m}^3$ is a factor of $10^6$, which will give wildly wrong numerical answers.
> 2. **Missing Constants of Integration (lose 1 mark):** When integrating twice, you must include **two arbitrary constants** ($A$ and $B$). Forgetting either leads to an underdetermined system.
> 3. **Confusing Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates (lose 1–2 marks):** In cylindrical coordinates, the radial coordinate is typically $\rho$ (or $r$ in some textbooks), and there is a $1/\rho$ factor. In spherical coordinates, the radial coordinate is $r$, and the angular terms have $1/r^2$ factors. **Always draw the geometry first** and choose coordinates accordingly.
> 4. **Forgetting to State Assumptions in Derivations (lose 1–2 marks):** Derivations of Poisson's equation are **only valid** under specific assumptions. Always state: (i) electrostatics, (ii) homogeneous medium, (iii) isotropic medium.
> 5. **Forgetting the Negative Sign in $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ (lose 0.5 mark):** The electric field points from **high to low** potential, hence the negative sign. Skipping it gives the wrong direction in the answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Poisson and Laplace Equations**

- ☐ **Poisson's Equation:** $\nabla^{2} V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon$ — applies wherever there is charge.
- ☐ **Laplace's Equation:** $\nabla^{2} V = 0$ — applies only in **charge-free** regions.
- ☐ **Derivation Path:** Gauss's law ($\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$) → constitutive relation ($\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$) → homogeneous simplification → potential definition ($\vec{E} = -\nabla V$) → final Poisson equation.
- ☐ **Two Critical Assumptions:** (1) **Electrostatics** (no time variation), (2) **Homogeneous medium** (constant $\varepsilon$).
- ☐ **Three Coordinate Systems:** Cartesian (rectangular geometries), Cylindrical (coaxial cables), Spherical (antennas, point charges).
- ☐ **Laplacian in 1D:** Reduces to $\dfrac{d^2 V}{dx^2}$; integrate twice → linear solution.
- ☐ **Boundary Conditions Matter:** Two conditions (e.g., $V$ at two points) are needed to fix the two constants of integration.
- ☐ **Uniqueness Theorem:** Solution is unique if $V$ (or $\partial V/\partial n$) is specified on the **entire** closed boundary.
- ☐ **Electric Field from Potential:** Always remember the negative sign: $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.
- ☐ **Unit Conversions:** $\mu \rightarrow 10^{-6}$, $\text{n} \rightarrow 10^{-9}$, $\text{p} \rightarrow 10^{-12}$. Always convert before calculating.
- ☐ **Physical Interpretation:** Linear potential → uniform field (no charge). Parabolic potential → non-uniform field (with charge).
- ☐ **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m} \approx 1/(36\pi \times 10^{9})\,\text{F/m}$.
- ☐ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** For cylindrical coordinates, the radial operator is $\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right)$, **not** simply $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \rho^2}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of E and V Using Laplace Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determination of E and V Using Laplace Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In electrostatics, when a region of space is **free of any charge distribution** (i.e., the volume charge density $\rho_v = 0$), the electrostatic potential $V$ at every point inside that source-free region satisfies the **Laplace Equation**, named after the French mathematician **Pierre-Simon Laplace**.

Formally, the Laplace equation in electrostatics is the homogeneous form of **Poisson's equation** when $\rho_v = 0$:

$$\nabla^2 V = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must be able to derive and solve the Laplace equation in all three principal coordinate systems — **Cartesian**, **Cylindrical (Polar)**, and **Spherical** — and then recover the electric field intensity $\mathbf{E}$ using the gradient relation $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$.

The operator $\nabla^2$ (called the **Laplacian**) expands differently in each coordinate system:

$$
\nabla^2 V = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} & \text{Cartesian} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} & \text{Cylindrical} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{R^2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial R}\!\left(R^2\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial R}\right) + \dfrac{1}{R^2 \sin\theta}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \dfrac{1}{R^2 \sin^2\theta}\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} & \text{Spherical} \end{cases}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pouring water onto a **flat, dry concrete surface**. The water flows in all directions trying to find the lowest point. Similarly, the potential $V$ in a charge-free region behaves like a "smooth" function — it has no peaks or valleys caused by local sources (charges). 

- **Where there is a charge** → the surface has a "bump" (or "dip") — this is governed by **Poisson's equation** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v/\varepsilon_0$.
- **Where there is no charge** → the surface is mathematically "saddle-shaped" and smooth — this is governed by **Laplace's equation** $\nabla^2 V = 0$.

The smoothness constraint is so powerful that, if you specify the potential values on the *boundaries* of a region, the potential *everywhere inside* is uniquely determined. This is **Uniqueness Theorem**, a favorite KTU question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants to Remember (Bold for emphasis):**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m}$
> - The Laplacian operator $\nabla^2$ is a **second-order scalar differential operator**.
> - The solution to Laplace's equation is called a **harmonic function**.

### 1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solving Laplace's equation is the backbone of:
1. **Capacitor design** — finding $V$ between parallel plates.
2. **Coaxial cable field analysis** — finding $V$ and $\mathbf{E}$ between inner conductor and outer sheath.
3. **Shielded enclosures** — EMC/EMI analysis inside Faraday cages.
4. **High-voltage insulator design** — predicting breakdown regions.
5. **Semiconductor device modeling** — potential inside p-n junctions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain — Why and How

The methodology for determining $\mathbf{E}$ and $V$ using Laplace's equation follows a strict 5-step recipe that KTU examiners love to test:

1. **Identify the geometry** — Is the problem 1-D, 2-D, or 3-D? Which coordinate system is best suited (Cartesian for rectangular, Cylindrical for wires/cables, Spherical for spheres)?
2. **Simplify the Laplacian** — Drop terms that do not depend on coordinates that vary. For example, if $V$ depends only on $x$ in Cartesian, all partial derivatives with respect to $y$ and $z$ are zero.
3. **Solve the ODE** — Integrate the simplified second-order ODE twice. Two constants of integration $A$ and $B$ will appear.
4. **Apply Boundary Conditions (BCs)** — Use the given potential or field values on the physical boundaries to evaluate $A$ and $B$.
5. **Recover $\mathbf{E}$** — Apply the gradient operation: $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two integrations?** The equation $\nabla^2 V = 0$ is a **second-order** differential equation. Each integration reduces the order by one, leaving **two constants** to be fixed by **two boundary conditions**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Coordinate Choice

- **Cartesian** is natural when the equipotential surfaces are **flat planes** (e.g., parallel-plate capacitor).
- **Cylindrical (polar in 2-D)** is natural when the equipotentials are **concentric cylinders** (e.g., coaxial cable).
- **Spherical** is natural when the equipotentials are **concentric spheres** (e.g., charged conducting sphere).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\nabla^2 V = 0$ | Laplace equation (source-free region, $\rho_v = 0$) |
| 2 | $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v / \varepsilon_0$ | Poisson equation (with charge density) |
| 3 | $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$ | Gradient relation between field and potential |
| 4 | $\nabla^2 V_{\text{Cart}} = \partial^2 V/\partial x^2 + \partial^2 V/\partial y^2 + \partial^2 V/\partial z^2$ | Laplacian in Cartesian |
| 5 | $\nabla^2 V_{\text{Cyl}} = (1/r)\,\partial(r\,\partial V/\partial r)/\partial r + (1/r^2)\,\partial^2 V/\partial \phi^2 + \partial^2 V/\partial z^2$ | Laplacian in Cylindrical |
| 6 | $\nabla^2 V_{\text{Sph}} = (1/R^2)\,\partial(R^2\,\partial V/\partial R)/\partial R + (1/R^2\sin\theta)\,\partial(\sin\theta\,\partial V/\partial \theta)/\partial\theta + (1/R^2\sin^2\theta)\,\partial^2 V/\partial\phi^2$ | Laplacian in Spherical |
| 7 | $V(x) = -Ex + C$ | 1-D Cartesian potential (uniform $\mathbf{E}$) |
| 8 | $V(r) = A \ln(r) + B$ | 2-D cylindrical / line-charge potential |
| 9 | $V(R) = A/R + B$ | Spherical / point-charge-like potential |
| 10 | $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x\,\partial V/\partial x - \hat{a}_y\,\partial V/\partial y - \hat{a}_z\,\partial V/\partial z$ | Cartesian $\mathbf{E}$ from $V$ |
| 11 | $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_r\,\partial V/\partial r - \hat{a}_\phi\,(1/r)\,\partial V/\partial\phi - \hat{a}_z\,\partial V/\partial z$ | Cylindrical $\mathbf{E}$ from $V$ |
| 12 | $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_R\,\partial V/\partial R - \hat{a}_\theta\,(1/R)\,\partial V/\partial\theta - \hat{a}_\phi\,(1/R\sin\theta)\,\partial V/\partial\phi$ | Spherical $\mathbf{E}$ from $V$ |
| 13 | $V = \int \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$ | Potential from field (line integral) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Laplace's equation is the foundation of **numerical methods** like Finite Difference Method (FDM) and Finite Element Method (FEM), which engineers use in software like ANSYS Maxwell and COMSOL to solve for $V$ in complex real-world geometries where analytical solutions are impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step derivations of the **three classic KTU problems** that examiners frequently set. Every algebraic step is written out — no shortcuts.

---

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Potential Between Two Infinite Parallel Plates (Cartesian, 1-D)

**Problem Setup:** Two infinite parallel plates are located at $x = 0$ and $x = d$. The plate at $x = 0$ is held at $V = V_0$ and the plate at $x = d$ is at $V = 0$. The region between the plates is charge-free. Find $V(x)$ and $\mathbf{E}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
By symmetry, the potential depends only on $x$ (not on $y$ or $z$). So $V = V(x)$.

**Step 2 — Simplify the Laplacian.**
Since $V$ is independent of $y$ and $z$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = 0$$

Therefore, the Laplace equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — First integration (with respect to $x$).**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = \int 0 \, dx = A$$

where $A$ is the first constant of integration.

**Step 4 — Second integration (with respect to $x$).**

$$V(x) = \int A \, dx = Ax + B$$

where $B$ is the second constant of integration.

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions.**

BC 1: At $x = 0$, $V = V_0$:
$$V_0 = A(0) + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = V_0$$

BC 2: At $x = d$, $V = 0$:
$$0 = A(d) + V_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -\frac{V_0}{d}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression for $V(x)$.**

$$\boxed{V(x) = V_0\left(1 - \frac{x}{d}\right) = -\frac{V_0}{d}\,x + V_0}$$

**Step 7 — Recover $\mathbf{E}$ using $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$.**

$$\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -\hat{a}_x \cdot \left(-\frac{V_0}{d}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = \hat{a}_x \frac{V_0}{d}}$$

**Interpretation:** The electric field is uniform, pointing in the $+x$ direction (from high potential $V_0$ to low potential $0$).

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Potential in a Coaxial Cable (Cylindrical, 1-D)

**Problem Setup:** An infinitely long coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius $a$ at potential $V_0$ and an outer conductor of radius $b$ at potential $V = 0$. The region $a < r < b$ is vacuum (no charge). Find $V(r)$ and $\mathbf{E}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
By cylindrical symmetry (no $\phi$ or $z$ dependence), $V = V(r)$.

**Step 2 — Simplify the Laplacian.**
Since $V$ depends only on $r$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = 0$$

The cylindrical Laplacian reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dV}{dr}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the term in parentheses must equal a constant $C_1$.**

$$r\frac{dV}{dr} = C_1$$

**Step 4 — First integration (separation).**

$$\frac{dV}{dr} = \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Second integration.**

$$V(r) = C_1 \ln(r) + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions.**

BC 1: At $r = a$, $V = V_0$:
$$V_0 = C_1 \ln(a) + C_2$$

BC 2: At $r = b$, $V = 0$:
$$0 = C_1 \ln(b) + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -C_1 \ln(b)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $C_1$.**
Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$$V_0 = C_1 \ln(a) - C_1 \ln(b) = C_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)$$

$$C_1 = \frac{V_0}{\ln(a/b)}$$

**Step 8 — Final expression for $V(r)$.**

$$\boxed{V(r) = V_0\,\frac{\ln(r/b)}{\ln(a/b)}}$$

**Step 9 — Recover $\mathbf{E}$.**

$$\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_r \frac{dV}{dr} = -\hat{a}_r \cdot \frac{V_0}{\ln(a/b)} \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = \hat{a}_r \,\frac{V_0}{r\,\ln(b/a)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The field points radially outward (since $V_0 > 0$ and $b > a$, $\ln(b/a) > 0$). The $\mathbf{E}$ falls off as $1/r$, characteristic of cylindrical geometry.

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Potential in a Spherical Shell (Spherical, 1-D)

**Problem Setup:** The region $a < R < b$ is charge-free. The inner sphere ($R = a$) is at $V = V_0$ and the outer sphere ($R = b$) is at $V = 0$. Find $V(R)$ and $\mathbf{E}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
By spherical symmetry, $V = V(R)$ only.

**Step 2 — Simplify the Laplacian.**
The $\theta$ and $\phi$ derivatives vanish:

$$\frac{1}{R^2}\frac{d}{dR}\!\left(R^2 \frac{dV}{dR}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Set the inner derivative to a constant $C_1$.**

$$R^2 \frac{dV}{dR} = C_1$$

$$\frac{dV}{dR} = \frac{C_1}{R^2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to find $V(R)$.**

$$V(R) = -\frac{C_1}{R} + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply BCs.**

BC 1: $V(a) = V_0$:
$$V_0 = -\frac{C_1}{a} + C_2$$

BC 2: $V(b) = 0$:
$$0 = -\frac{C_1}{b} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{C_1}{b}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $C_1$.**
Subtracting:

$$V_0 = -\frac{C_1}{a} + \frac{C_1}{b} = C_1\left(\frac{1}{b} - \frac{1}{a}\right) = C_1 \cdot \frac{a-b}{ab}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{V_0 \cdot a b}{a - b}$$

**Step 7 — Final expression for $V(R)$.**

$$V(R) = -\frac{V_0\,ab}{R(a-b)} + \frac{V_0\,ab}{b(a-b)} = \frac{V_0\,ab}{a-b}\left(\frac{1}{b} - \frac{1}{R}\right)$$

$$\boxed{V(R) = \frac{V_0\,a}{(b-a)}\left(\frac{b}{R} - 1\right)}$$

**Step 8 — Recover $\mathbf{E}$.**

$$\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_R \frac{dV}{dR} = -\hat{a}_R \cdot \frac{V_0\,ab}{R^2(a-b)}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = \hat{a}_R \,\frac{V_0\,ab}{R^2(b-a)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The field falls off as $1/R^2$, just like a point charge — the hallmark of spherical symmetry.

---

### 3.4 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX

| Geometry | Coordinate | Form of $V$ | Form of $\mathbf{E}$ | Decay |
|----------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|-------|
| Parallel plates | Cartesian ($x$) | $Ax + B$ (linear) | Constant ($1/R^0$) | None |
| Coaxial cable | Cylindrical ($r$) | $A\ln(r) + B$ | $A/r$ | $1/r$ |
| Spherical shell | Spherical ($R$) | $A/R + B$ | $A/R^2$ | $1/R^2$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** The number of integration constants is always 2. The integration produces a logarithmic function in 2-D cylindrical geometry and a $1/r$ function in 3-D spherical geometry — these come from the extra "directional" terms in the Laplacian.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The 5-Step Solution Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Geometry and Boundary Conditions] --> B[Step 1: Choose Coordinate System]
    B --> B1[Cartesian if plates/box]
    B --> B2[Cylindrical if cable/wire]
    B --> B3[Spherical if sphere/orbit]
    B1 --> C[Step 2: Simplify Laplacian by Symmetry]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Step 3: Solve Second-Order ODE]
    D --> D1[First integration: get dV/dr or dV/dx]
    D1 --> D2[Second integration: get V with two constants A and B]
    D2 --> E[Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions]
    E --> E1[BC1 gives one constant]
    E --> E2[BC2 gives the other constant]
    E2 --> F[Step 5: Compute E using E = -gradient V]
    F --> G[Final Answer: V and E in Closed Form]
    G --> H[Verify using Laplace equation: del-square V must equal 0]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of the Laplace Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Block]
        I1[Geometry Description]
        I2[Boundary Conditions V at surfaces]
        I3[Source-free check rho_v equals 0]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[Equation Solver Block]
        S1[Laplacian Operator Selection]
        S2[Coordinate System Adapter]
        S3[ODE Integrator Module]
        S4[Boundary Condition Matcher]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block]
        O1[Potential V x y z]
        O2[Electric Field E x y z]
        O3[Equipotential Plot Generator]
    end

    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S4
    I3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S4 --> S3
    S3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Coordinate-System Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the shape of the boundary?}
    Q1 -->|Flat planes| R1[Use Cartesian Coordinates]
    Q1 -->|Cylinders or wires| R2[Use Cylindrical Coordinates]
    Q1 -->|Spheres or orbits| R3[Use Spherical Coordinates]

    R1 --> R1a[Laplacian: d2V/dx2 plus d2V/dy2 plus d2V/dz2]
    R2 --> R2a[Laplacian: 1/r times d/dr of r dV/dr plus 1/r2 d2V/dphi2 plus d2V/dz2]
    R3 --> R3a[Laplacian: 1/R2 d/dR of R2 dV/dR plus angular terms]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Energy Flow from BC to E-field

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Geometry
    participant Laplacian
    participant Integrator
    participant EField
    User->>Geometry: Provide boundary surfaces and V values
    Geometry->>Laplacian: Select appropriate coordinate system
    Laplacian->>Integrator: Simplified ODE form passed
    Integrator->>Integrator: First integration: gives dV
    Integrator->>Integrator: Second integration: gives V plus constants
    Integrator->>EField: Final V x y z expression
    EField->>User: E x y z via negative gradient of V
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State and explain Laplace's equation. Why is it important in electrostatics?**

**Model Answer:**
Laplace's equation is a second-order partial differential equation that governs the electrostatic potential $V$ in a charge-free region ($\rho_v = 0$). It is given by:

$$\nabla^2 V = 0$$

Expanding in Cartesian coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Importance:**
1. It is the **homogeneous** form of Poisson's equation.
2. By the **Uniqueness Theorem**, specifying the potential on closed boundaries uniquely determines $V$ everywhere inside.
3. It is the **mathematical foundation** for numerical field solvers (FDM, FEM) used in capacitor, transmission line, and insulator design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] What is the relationship between potential $V$ and electric field $\mathbf{E}$? How is it used after solving Laplace's equation?**

**Model Answer:**
The electric field intensity $\mathbf{E}$ is the **negative gradient** of the scalar electric potential $V$:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$$

In Cartesian form:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} - \hat{a}_y\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} - \hat{a}_z\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

**Usage after Laplace's equation:**
Once $V$ is found by solving $\nabla^2 V = 0$ and applying boundary conditions, the spatial derivative of $V$ with respect to the appropriate coordinate gives $\mathbf{E}$ directly. This is much simpler than computing $\mathbf{E}$ from $\rho_v$ via Coulomb's law in complex geometries.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the potential $V(x)$ between two infinite parallel plates separated by a distance $d$, given $V(0) = V_0$ and $V(d) = 0$, in a charge-free region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An infinitely long coaxial cable has inner radius $a = 1$ mm and outer radius $b = 5$ mm. The inner conductor is at $V_0 = 100$ V and the outer at $V = 0$. Using Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates, find $V(r)$ and $\mathbf{E}(r)$. Compute $|\mathbf{E}|$ at $r = 2$ mm. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

1. By symmetry, $V = V(x)$ only.
2. Laplace equation reduces to: $\dfrac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = 0$
3. Integrate twice: $V(x) = Ax + B$
4. Apply BCs:
   - $V(0) = V_0 \Rightarrow B = V_0$
   - $V(d) = 0 \Rightarrow A = -V_0/d$
5. Result: $\boxed{V(x) = V_0\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{d}\right)}$
6. Field: $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_x\,dV/dx = \hat{a}_x\,V_0/d$ (uniform field).

##### Model Solution to (b):

1. Geometry: $V = V(r)$ only.
2. Simplified Laplacian: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dV}{dr}\right) = 0$
3. Integrate: $r\dfrac{dV}{dr} = C_1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dV}{dr} = \dfrac{C_1}{r}$
4. Second integration: $V(r) = C_1 \ln(r) + C_2$
5. Apply BCs:
   - $V(a) = V_0$: $V_0 = C_1 \ln(a) + C_2$
   - $V(b) = 0$: $0 = C_1 \ln(b) + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = -C_1 \ln(b)$
6. Solving: $C_1 = \dfrac{V_0}{\ln(a/b)}$
7. Final potential: $\boxed{V(r) = V_0\,\dfrac{\ln(r/b)}{\ln(a/b)}}$
8. Field: $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_r\,dV/dr = \hat{a}_r\,\dfrac{V_0}{r\,\ln(b/a)}$
9. At $r = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m, $a = 0.001$ m, $b = 0.005$ m, $V_0 = 100$ V:

$$\ln(b/a) = \ln(0.005/0.001) = \ln(5) \approx 1.6094$$

$$|\mathbf{E}(r=2\,\text{mm})| = \frac{100}{0.002 \times 1.6094} \approx 31{,}062 \ \text{V/m}$$

$$\boxed{|\mathbf{E}| \approx 31.06 \ \text{kV/m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the potential $V(R)$ in the charge-free region $a < R < b$ between two concentric spherical shells, given $V(a) = V_0$ and $V(b) = 0$, using Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the **Uniqueness Theorem** for Laplace's equation. Two different solutions cannot exist for the same boundary value problem. Briefly justify. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

1. By spherical symmetry, $V = V(R)$ only.
2. Laplace equation: $\dfrac{1}{R^2}\dfrac{d}{dR}\!\left(R^2\dfrac{dV}{dR}\right) = 0$
3. First integration: $R^2\dfrac{dV}{dR} = C_1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dV}{dR} = \dfrac{C_1}{R^2}$
4. Second integration: $V(R) = -\dfrac{C_1}{R} + C_2$
5. Apply BCs:
   - $V(a) = V_0$: $V_0 = -C_1/a + C_2$
   - $V(b) = 0$: $0 = -C_1/b + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = C_1/b$
6. Solving: $C_1 = \dfrac{V_0\,ab}{a-b}$
7. Final potential: $\boxed{V(R) = \dfrac{V_0\,a}{b-a}\left(\dfrac{b}{R} - 1\right)}$
8. Field: $\mathbf{E} = -\hat{a}_R\,dV/dR = \hat{a}_R\,\dfrac{V_0\,ab}{R^2(b-a)}$

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Uniqueness Theorem Statement:** *If a solution to Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 V = 0$ exists in a volume $V$ enclosed by a surface $S$, and the potential $V$ is specified on $S$ (Dirichlet condition) or its normal derivative $\partial V/\partial n$ is specified on $S$ (Neumann condition), then the solution $V$ inside $V$ is unique.*

**Justification (proof sketch):**
Suppose two solutions $V_1$ and $V_2$ both satisfy $\nabla^2 V = 0$ with the same boundary values. Define $V_d = V_1 - V_2$. Then:
- $\nabla^2 V_d = \nabla^2 V_1 - \nabla^2 V_2 = 0$ (linearity).
- $V_d = 0$ on the boundary $S$ (or $\partial V_d/\partial n = 0$).

Apply **Green's first identity**:

$$\int_V (V_d \,\nabla^2 V_d + \vert \nabla V_d \vert^2)\, dV = \oint_S V_d\,\frac{\partial V_d}{\partial n}\,dS$$

Since $V_d = 0$ on $S$ and $\nabla^2 V_d = 0$ in $V$:

$$\int_V \vert \nabla V_d \vert^2\, dV = 0$$

This implies $\nabla V_d = 0$ everywhere, hence $V_d = $ constant, hence $V_1 = V_2$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second integration constant.** A common error is writing $V(x) = Ax + V_0$ and skipping the boundary at $x = d$. Always apply **both** BCs.
> 2. **Wrong sign in $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$.** Field points from **high** potential to **low** potential. A missing minus sign reverses the direction and costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using Poisson's equation in a charge-free region.** Students sometimes write $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v/\varepsilon_0$ and substitute $\rho_v = 0$ *after* setting up. Always confirm $\rho_v = 0$ first, *then* use Laplace.
> 4. **Mixing up cylindrical and spherical operators.** The spherical Laplacian has a $\sin\theta$ factor; the cylindrical one does not. Don't blindly copy formulas.
> 5. **In spherical problems, computing $\mathbf{E}$ as $-\partial V/\partial R$ only.** In full 3-D, $\mathbf{E}$ also has $\theta$ and $\phi$ components. With pure spherical symmetry they vanish, but the **method** of gradient must be applied carefully.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Laplace equation:** $\nabla^2 V = 0$ is the **only** equation in source-free electrostatics.
- **Always pick the right coordinate system** based on geometry (plates → Cartesian, cables → Cylindrical, spheres → Spherical).
- **Always two constants of integration** because the equation is **second-order** — **two boundary conditions** are mandatory.
- **Recovery of $\mathbf{E}$** is always via $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$ in the appropriate coordinate system.
- **Decay pattern:** Parallel plate $\Rightarrow$ constant $\mathbf{E}$; Coaxial cable $\Rightarrow \mathbf{E} \propto 1/r$; Spherical shell $\Rightarrow \mathbf{E} \propto 1/R^2$.
- **Uniqueness Theorem** guarantees that **one set of boundary values = one unique potential** inside.
- **Direction of $\mathbf{E}$:** From high to low potential — a fact often tested in KTU 1-mark MCQs.
- **Poisson vs. Laplace:** $\rho_v = 0 \Rightarrow$ Laplace; $\rho_v \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ Poisson.
- **Key constants to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $k = 1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0) = 9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C².
- **Verifying your answer:** Always substitute $V$ back into $\nabla^2 V$ and confirm it gives 0. KTU examiners award "verification marks."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Capacitance and Inductance of Two-Wire Transmission Line and Coaxial Cable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Derivation of Capacitance and Inductance of Two-Wire Transmission Line and Coaxial Cable # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Transmission Line Parameters — The Distributed Perspective In high-frequency engineering, a transmission line is **never** treated as a simple pair of ideal wires. Instead, it is modeled as a **distributed parameter network** where voltage and current vary continuously with both position and time. For a two-conductor TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic) guiding system, the four fundamental primary constants per unit length are: | Parameter | Symbol | Unit (per unit length) | |---|---|---| | Resistance | $R$ | $\Omega/\text{m}$ | | **Inductance** | $L$ | $\text{H/m}$ | | **Capacitance** | $C$ | $\text{F/m}$ | | Conductance | $G$ | $\text{S/m}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 2 demands rigorous **derivation** (not just memorization) of $L$ and $C$ for the two canonical geometries — the **two-wire line** and the **coaxial cable**. These derivations are board-favorite 14-mark questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Pipe Intuition Imagine a long, thin water pipe carrying an oscillating flow. - The **water inertia** resisting sudden changes in flow rate is analogous to **Inductance ($L$)** — it stores energy in the magnetic field surrounding the conductor. - The **elastic stretch of the pipe walls** that resists pressure buildup is analogous to **Capacitance ($C$)** — it stores energy in the electric field between the conductors. - The **friction of water with the pipe** is **Resistance ($R$)**, and **small leaks** are **Conductance ($G$)**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Capacitance per unit length ($C$):** The amount of charge (in Coulombs) that must be placed on the conductor per unit length to raise its potential by one Volt with respect to the return conductor. Mathematically, $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ (F/m). > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Inductance per unit length ($L$):** The total magnetic flux linkages (in Weber-turns) per unit length produced per Ampere of current flowing through the conductor. Mathematically, $L = \dfrac{\Lambda}{I}$ (H/m), where $\Lambda$ is the flux linkage per unit length. ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering The values of $L$ and $C$ directly determine the **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ and the **phase velocity** $v_p$ of the line: $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \qquad v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$ For a lossless line in free space, $Z_0 \approx 377\,\Omega$. In practice, TV antenna twin-lead is designed for $300\,\Omega$, and Ethernet coaxial cable for $50\,\Omega$ — every one of these standard values comes from the derivations that follow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ > **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ > **Speed of light:** $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — TEM Mode Assumption In both geometries (two-wire and coaxial), we assume: 1. **Quasi-static / TEM propagation** — the fields are transverse to the direction of propagation, and $\vec{E}$ lies entirely in the cross-sectional plane. 2. **Perfectly conducting conductors** (no penetration of field into the metal). 3. **Uniform charge and current distribution** around the periphery (valid when the conductor spacing $D$ is much larger than the radius $a$). Under TEM conditions, Laplace's equation governs the potential in the cross-section: $$\nabla^2 V = 0$$ and the fields are derivable from a scalar potential $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ and a vector potential $\vec{H} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$. ## 2.2 Two-Wire Line — Geometry and Setup Consider two infinitely long, parallel cylindrical conductors of radius $a$ each, separated by a center-to-center distance $D$ (where $D \gg a$), suspended in a homogeneous dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$. - Conductor 1 carries linear charge density $+\rho_L$ (C/m) and current $+I$. - Conductor 2 carries linear charge density $-\rho_L$ (C/m) and current $-I$ (return path). The total capacitance per unit length is found by computing the potential difference between the two surfaces and using $C = \rho_L / V$. ## 2.3 Coaxial Cable — Geometry and Setup A coaxial cable consists of: - An **inner conductor** of radius $a$. - A **concentric outer conductor** of inner radius $b$ (and outer radius $c$, though for inductance/capacitance the outer radius is irrelevant if current returns on the inner surface of the outer conductor). - A dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$ filling the region $a < \rho < b$. Because of cylindrical symmetry, fields depend only on the radial coordinate $\rho$ (using $\rho$ instead of $r$ to avoid confusion with the line charge density), making this geometry substantially simpler than the two-wire case. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Two-Wire Line (per unit length) | Coaxial Cable (per unit length) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Electric Field $\vec{E}$ | From Gauss's Law: $E_\rho = \dfrac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon \rho}\left(\hat{a}_{\rho 1} - \hat{a}_{\rho 2}\right)$ | $E_\rho = \dfrac{V}{\rho \ln(b/a)}\,\hat{a}_\rho$ | | 2 | Magnetic Field $\vec{H}$ | $H_\phi = \dfrac{I}{2\pi}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_1} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}\right)\hat{a}_\phi$ | $H_\phi = \dfrac{I}{2\pi \rho}\,\hat{a}_\phi$ | | 3 | **Capacitance $C$** | $\dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)} \approx \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ | | 4 | **External Inductance $L_{ext}$** | $\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\cosh^{-1}(D/2a) \approx \dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a)$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ | | 5 | **Internal Inductance $L_{int}$** | $\dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ (two conductors total) | $\dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ (two conductors total) | | 6 | **Total Inductance $L$** | $\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}$ | | 7 | Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ | $\approx \dfrac{120}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(D/a)\ \Omega$ | $\approx \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(b/a)\ \Omega$ | | 8 | Phase Velocity $v_p$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r\varepsilon_r}}$ | Same as two-wire | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Approximation Note:** For two-wire line, $\cosh^{-1}(D/2a) \approx \ln(D/a)$ holds when $D \gg a$. This is the **standard KTU 2024 working approximation**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Antenna feed lines (twin-lead):** The $300\,\Omega$ TV antenna feed uses two-wire geometry. - **Cable TV / Ethernet (coaxial):** Standard $75\,\Omega$ and $50\,\Omega$ impedances are designed using the coaxial formula. - **High-voltage power transmission:** The capacitance per unit length determines the charging current of long lines. - **RF/Microwave circuits:** Coaxial stubs, filters, and resonators are designed using $L$ and $C$ values. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Capacitance of a Two-Wire Transmission Line ### Step 1: Geometry and Charge Assumption Consider two parallel cylindrical conductors of radius $a$, separated by distance $D$ (center-to-center), embedded in a homogeneous dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. Let conductor 1 be at the origin and conductor 2 at $x = D$ on the $x$-axis. Assume the conductors carry equal and opposite line charge densities $+\rho_L$ and $-\rho_L$ (C/m). ### Step 2: Electric Field of a Single Infinite Line Charge For an isolated infinite line charge of density $\rho_L$ along the $z$-axis, applying Gauss's law to a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $\rho$ and length $\ell$: $$\oint \vec{D}\cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{enc} \Rightarrow D_\rho (2\pi\rho \ell) = \rho_L \ell$$ Therefore: $$\vec{E}_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon \rho_1}\hat{a}_{\rho_1}$$ where $\rho_1$ is the perpendicular distance from the line to the field point. > **Logic:** Gauss's law converts a 3-D surface integral into an algebraic equation by exploiting the cylindrical symmetry of an infinite line charge. ### Step 3: Superposition of Fields The total electric field at any point $P$ (located at distances $\rho_1$ from wire 1 and $\rho_2$ from wire 2) is: $$\vec{E} = \vec{E}_1 + \vec{E}_2 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left(\frac{\hat{a}_{\rho 1}}{\rho_1} - \frac{\hat{a}_{\rho 2}}{\rho_2}\right)$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Potential Difference The potential difference $V$ between the two conductors is obtained by integrating $\vec{E}$ along any path from the surface of conductor 2 to the surface of conductor 1.
