We choose a path along the $x$-axis (the line joining the centers), where $\hat{a}_{\rho 1} = \hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_{\rho 2} = -\hat{a}_x$ (since we move from wire 2 toward wire 1, the radial unit vector from wire 2 toward us points in $-\hat{a}_x$ direction). $$V = V_1 - V_2 = -\int_{D-a}^{D+a} \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{\ell} = \int_{D-a}^{D+a} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left(\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{D-x}\right)(-dx)$$ Wait — let us be careful with signs. Integrating from the surface of wire 2 ($x = D-a$) to the surface of wire 1 ($x = a$), the differential element is $d\vec{\ell} = dx\,\hat{a}_x$, and on this path $\hat{a}_{\rho 1} = -\hat{a}_x$, $\hat{a}_{\rho 2} = +\hat{a}_x$. So: $$V = -\int_{D-a}^{a} \left[\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left(\frac{-\hat{a}_x}{x} - \frac{\hat{a}_x}{D-x}\right)\right]\cdot \hat{a}_x\,dx$$ $$V = \int_{D-a}^{a} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left(\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{D-x}\right)dx$$ $$V = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\Big[\ln x - \ln(D-x)\Big]_{D-a}^{a}$$ Evaluating the limits: $$V = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left[\Big(\ln a - \ln(D-a)\Big) - \Big(\ln(D-a) - \ln a\Big)\right]$$ $$V = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left[\ln a - \ln(D-a) - \ln(D-a) + \ln a\right]$$ $$V = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\left[2\ln a - 2\ln(D-a)\right] = \frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{a}{D-a}\right)$$ Since $D \gg a$, we have $D - a \approx D$, so: $$\boxed{V \approx \frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{a}{D}\right) = -\frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)}$$ ### Step 5: Capacitance per Unit Length $$C = \frac{\rho_L}{|V|} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}\quad \text{F/m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Generalized (Exact) Form:** Without the $D \gg a$ approximation, using the exact integral, the result is: > $$C = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)} \quad \text{F/m}$$ > The KTU board accepts the approximated form when the condition $D \gg a$ is stated explicitly. --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Inductance of a Two-Wire Transmission Line ### Step 1: Magnetic Field from Ampere's Law For an isolated long straight wire carrying current $I$ along the $z$-axis, Ampere's law on a circular Amperian path of radius $\rho$ gives: $$\oint \vec{H}\cdot d\vec{\ell} = I_{enc} \Rightarrow H_\phi (2\pi\rho) = I$$ $$\vec{H} = \frac{I}{2\pi\rho}\hat{a}_\phi$$ ### Step 2: Magnetic Field Between Two Wires (External Region) For two parallel wires carrying equal and opposite currents (wire 1 at $x=0$ with $+I$, wire 2 at $x=D$ with $-I$), superposition gives: $$\vec{H}_{ext} = \frac{I}{2\pi}\left(\frac{\hat{a}_{\phi 1}}{\rho_1} + \frac{\hat{a}_{\phi 2}}{\rho_2}\right)$$ ### Step 3: External Flux Linkage per Unit Length Consider a rectangular surface of unit length in the $z$-direction, bounded by $x = a$ (surface of wire 1) and $x = D - a$ (surface of wire 2). The flux through this surface is: $$\Phi_{ext} = \int_{a}^{D-a} B_x \, dx \quad \text{(per unit length in z)}$$ But it is easier to compute the flux per unit length by integrating only the $x$-component of $\vec{B}$ on the $x$-axis. By symmetry, on the line joining the centers, both contributions of $\vec{B}$ point in the $+\hat{a}_y$ direction (tangent to circles around each wire). So: $$B_y = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{D-x}\right)$$ The flux per unit length through the strip from $a$ to $D-a$: $$\Phi_{ext} = \int_a^{D-a} B_y\,dx = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\int_a^{D-a}\left(\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{D-x}\right)dx$$ $$\Phi_{ext} = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\Big[\ln x - \ln(D-x)\Big]_a^{D-a}$$ $$\Phi_{ext} = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\left[\Big(\ln(D-a) - \ln a\Big) - \Big(\ln a - \ln(D-a)\Big)\right]$$ $$\Phi_{ext} = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\cdot 2\ln\left(\frac{D-a}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu I}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D-a}{a}\right)$$ Using $D \gg a$: $$\Phi_{ext} \approx \frac{\mu I}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)$$ ### Step 4: Internal Flux Linkage per Conductor Inside a solid cylindrical conductor of radius $a$, the current is distributed uniformly (skin effect neglected for DC/low frequency). The enclosed current at radius $\rho$ is: $$I_{enc} = I\cdot\frac{\rho^2}{a^2}$$ The magnetic field inside is: $$H_{int} = \frac{I\,\rho}{2\pi a^2}$$ The internal flux per unit length, per conductor: $$\Phi_{int} = \int_0^a \mu H_{int}\,d\rho = \int_0^a \frac{\mu I \rho}{2\pi a^2}\,d\rho = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi a^2}\cdot\frac{a^2}{2} = \frac{\mu I}{4\pi}$$ ### Step 5: Total Inductance per Unit Length For both conductors (each carrying current $I$ in opposite directions, each contributing its own internal flux): $$L_{total} = L_{ext} + 2L_{int,one} = \frac{\Phi_{ext}}{I} + 2\cdot\frac{\mu}{4\pi}$$ $$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\quad \text{H/m}}$$ > [!NOTE] > At high frequencies where skin effect dominates, the internal inductance term $\mu/(4\pi)$ becomes negligible ($\approx 0$), and only the external term remains. --- ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Capacitance of a Coaxial Cable ### Step 1: Apply Gauss's Law in Cylindrical Symmetry Place a line charge $+\rho_L$ on the inner conductor and $-\rho_L$ on the inner surface of the outer conductor. By cylindrical symmetry, the electric field points radially and depends only on $\rho$. For $a < \rho < b$, Gauss's law: $$D_\rho (2\pi \rho \ell) = \rho_L \ell \Rightarrow \vec{D} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$$ $$\vec{E} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon \rho}\hat{a}_\rho$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Potential Difference The potential difference between the inner and outer conductors is: $$V = -\int_b^a \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{\rho} = -\int_b^a \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon\rho}\,d\rho$$ $$V = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}[\ln\rho]_b^a = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}(\ln a - \ln b) = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Capacitance per Unit Length $$\boxed{C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\quad \text{F/m}}$$ --- ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Inductance of a Coaxial Cable ### Step 1: Magnetic Field Between Conductors For the inner conductor carrying current $+I$ in the $+\hat{a}_z$ direction (and returning on the outer conductor as $-I$): For $a < \rho < b$, Ampere's law gives: $$\vec{H} = \frac{I}{2\pi\rho}\hat{a}_\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{B} = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}\hat{a}_\phi$$ ### Step 2: External Flux Linkage Consider a rectangular area in the $\rho$–$z$ plane, of width $(b - a)$ in the radial direction and unit length in the $z$ direction. The flux through it per unit length: $$\Phi_{ext} = \int_a^b B_\phi\,d\rho = \int_a^b \frac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}\,d\rho = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Internal Flux Linkage of Both Conductors As derived in Section 3.2 Step 4, the internal flux per conductor (per unit length) is $\mu I / 4\pi$. Both the inner and outer conductors contribute, giving a total internal flux: $$\Phi_{int} = 2 \times \frac{\mu I}{4\pi} = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{\mu I}{4\pi}$$ ### Step 4: Total Inductance per Unit Length $$L = \frac{\Phi_{ext} + \Phi_{int}}{I} = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}$$ $$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\quad \text{H/m}}$$ --- ## 3.5 The Beautiful Duality — $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ For **both** the two-wire line and the coaxial cable, multiplying the capacitance and inductance per unit length: $$LC = \left(\frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\right)\left(\frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)\right) = \mu\varepsilon$$ This is **not a coincidence** — it is a fundamental property of any TEM-mode transmission line in a homogeneous medium. It guarantees that the phase velocity: $$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r\varepsilon_r}}$$ depends only on the medium, **not on the geometry** — a profound result that the KTU examiner loves to test. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram — Two-Wire Line ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TwoWire["TWO-WIRE TRANSMISSION LINE — CROSS SECTION"] direction TB WIRE1["CONDUCTOR 1
Radius = a
Charge = +rho_L
Current = +I"] WIRE2["CONDUCTOR 2
Radius = a
Charge = -rho_L
Current = -I"] FIELD1["Electric Field Lines
Radial from Wire 1"] FIELD2["Magnetic Field Lines
Circles around each wire"] SEP["Separation D between centers
Assumption: D >> a"] DIELECTRIC["DIELECTRIC MEDIUM
permittivity = epsilon
permeability = mu"] end WIRE1 ---|"rho_1 distance"| FIELD1 WIRE2 ---|"rho_2 distance"| FIELD2 FIELD1 --- DIELECTRIC FIELD2 --- DIELECTRIC WIRE1 ===|"D"| WIRE2 SEP -.- WIRE1 SEP -.- WIRE2 ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram — Coaxial Cable ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Coax["COAXIAL CABLE — CROSS SECTIONAL VIEW"] direction TB INNER["INNER CONDUCTOR
Radius = a
Charge = +rho_L per unit length
Current = +I in +z direction"] OUTER["OUTER CONDUCTOR
Inner radius = b
Charge = -rho_L per unit length
Current = -I in -z direction"] GAP["DIELECTRIC REGION
a less than rho less than b
epsilon and mu"] EFIELD["Electric Field E points radially outward
E = rho_L divided by 2 pi epsilon rho"] HFIELD["Magnetic Field H is circumferential
H = I divided by 2 pi rho"] end INNER ===|"gap filled with dielectric"| GAP GAP ===|"inner surface of outer conductor"| OUTER EFIELD -.- GAP HFIELD -.- GAP ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Derivation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Geometry and Symmetry Assumption"] --> B["Apply Gauss Law to find D field"] A2["START: Geometry and Current Path"] --> C["Apply Ampere Law to find H field"] B --> D["Integrate E to find V between conductors"] D --> E["C = rho_L divided by V = capacitance per unit length"] C --> F["Integrate B over cross section to find flux"] F --> G["Add internal flux contribution mu over 4 pi"] G --> H["L = flux divided by I = inductance per unit length"] E --> I["VERIFICATION: L times C equals mu times epsilon"] H --> I I --> J["Compute characteristic impedance Z0 = sqrt L by C"] J --> K["Compute phase velocity vp = 1 by sqrt LC"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Two-Wire vs Coaxial ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TW["TWO WIRE LINE"] TW1["Capacitance: pi epsilon over ln D by a"] TW2["Inductance: mu over pi ln D by a plus mu over 4 pi"] TW3["Geometry: Open, asymmetric"] TW4["Application: TV antenna feed 300 ohm"] end subgraph CX["COAXIAL CABLE"] CX1["Capacitance: 2 pi epsilon over ln b by a"] CX2["Inductance: mu over 2 pi ln b by a plus mu over 4 pi"] CX3["Geometry: Closed, symmetric"] CX4["Application: Ethernet RF 50 ohm or 75 ohm"] end TW1 --- CX1 TW2 --- CX2 TW3 --- CX3 TW4 --- CX4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Observation from the diagrams:** The coaxial cable has **2×** the capacitance and **half** the inductance of an equivalent two-wire line with the same logarithmic ratio. This is why $Z_0$ for coax is $\approx 60\ln(b/a)/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$, exactly half of the two-wire value. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2] **Define inductance per unit length of a transmission line. State its expression for a coaxial cable.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Inductance per unit length of a transmission line is defined as the total magnetic flux linkages per unit length produced per ampere of current flowing through the conductor. It is denoted by $L$ and measured in $\text{H/m}$. $$L = \frac{\Lambda}{I}\quad \text{(H/m)}$$ - **Expression for coaxial cable (2 marks):** $$L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\quad \text{H/m}$$ where $a$ = inner conductor radius, $b$ = inner radius of outer conductor, $\mu$ = permeability of the dielectric. The first term is the **external inductance** and $\mu/(4\pi)$ is the **internal inductance** of both conductors combined. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2] **Why is the characteristic impedance of a two-wire transmission line higher than that of a coaxial cable for the same dielectric and same conductor spacing ratio?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - For two-wire line: $Z_0 = \dfrac{120}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(D/a)\ \Omega$ - For coaxial cable: $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(b/a)\ \Omega$ - The factor of **2 difference** arises because the coaxial cable has **twice the capacitance per unit length** (due to the closed, fully enclosing geometry) and **half the external inductance per unit length** (because the magnetic field exists only in the region $a < \rho < b$, not extending to infinity). - Therefore, $Z_0^{\text{coax}} = \sqrt{L/C} = \sqrt{(\text{half of }L_{\text{ext}})/(2C_{\text{ext}})} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{L_{\text{ext}}/C_{\text{ext}}}$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **capacitance per unit length of a two-wire transmission line** consisting of two parallel cylindrical conductors of radius $a$ each, separated by a distance $D$ between centers, embedded in a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. State the assumption used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **inductance per unit length of a coaxial cable** with inner conductor radius $a$ and outer conductor inner radius $b$, in a medium of permeability $\mu$. Hence find the characteristic impedance. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Place conductor 1 at origin and conductor 2 at $x = D$ along the $x$-axis. Assume uniform line charge densities $+\rho_L$ and $-\rho_L$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Gauss's law to find the electric field from one wire: $$\vec{E}_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon\rho_1}\hat{a}_{\rho 1}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Superpose and integrate to get potential difference: $$V = \frac{\rho_L}{\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)\quad \text{(using } D \gg a\text{)}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Capacitance per unit length: $$C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}\ \text{F/m}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** State assumption: $D \gg a$ and uniform charge distribution. #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** For $a < \rho < b$, use Ampere's law: $$\vec{H} = \frac{I}{2\pi\rho}\hat{a}_\phi,\quad \vec{B} = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}\hat{a}_\phi$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute external flux per unit length: $$\Phi_{ext} = \int_a^b \frac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}d\rho = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Add internal flux from both conductors: $$\Phi_{int} = \frac{\mu I}{4\pi}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Total inductance: $$L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\ \text{H/m}$$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Use $C = 2\pi\varepsilon/\ln(b/a)$ to get: $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) + \text{(small correction)}$$ For $D \gg a$ / high-frequency approximation (internal inductance negligible): $$Z_0 \approx \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\ \Omega$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style — Alternative Choice] **(a)** Starting from Gauss's law, derive the **capacitance per unit length of a coaxial cable** with inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a **two-wire transmission line** with conductor radius $a$ and separation $D$, derive the **inductance per unit length**, including both external and internal contributions. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** By cylindrical symmetry, apply Gauss's law on a cylinder of radius $\rho$ for $a < \rho < b$: $$D_\rho(2\pi\rho\ell) = \rho_L\ell \Rightarrow E_\rho = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon\rho}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Integrate to get potential difference: $$V = \int_a^b E_\rho\,d\rho = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Capacitance per unit length: $$C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\ \text{F/m}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** For free-space dielectric, $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0}{\ln(b/a)} \approx \dfrac{55.6}{\ln(b/a)}\ \text{pF/m}$ #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply Ampere's law for current in one wire: $H_\phi = I/(2\pi\rho_1)$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** External flux per unit length between surfaces: $$\Phi_{ext} = \int_a^{D-a}\frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{D-x}\right)dx = \frac{\mu I}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Internal flux for one conductor: $\Phi_{int}^{(1)} = \mu I/(4\pi)$. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Total internal flux for both conductors: $\Phi_{int} = \mu I/(4\pi)$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Total inductance per unit length: $$L = \frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) + \frac{\mu}{4\pi}\ \text{H/m}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign confusion (–1 mark):** Many students forget that the two conductors carry **opposite** charges/currents. The superposition must use $+\rho_L$ and $-\rho_L$, **not** $+\rho_L$ and $+\rho_L$. > 2. **Missing internal inductance (–1 mark):** When asked for "total" or "complete" inductance, students often write only the external term. Always add $\mu/(4\pi)$ for two-wire and $\mu/(4\pi)$ for coax. > 3. **Forgetting the approximation (–1 mark):** For two-wire line, the formula $\pi\varepsilon/\ln(D/a)$ is valid only when $D \gg a$. **Always state this assumption explicitly** at the start of your solution. > 4. **Wrong units (–1 mark):** Capacitance is in F/m, inductance in H/m. Writing just F or H loses 1 mark. > 5. **Path of integration (–1 mark):** For potential difference, integrate from one conductor surface to the other — **not** from center to center. > 6. **Mixing up $\rho_L$ and $r$:** Use $\rho_L$ (or $\lambda$) for linear charge density, and $\rho$ (or $r$) for radial distance. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Capacitance of two-wire line:** $C = \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}\ \text{F/m}$ *(valid for $D \gg a$)* - ✅ **Inductance of two-wire line:** $L = \dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}\ \text{H/m}$ - ✅ **Capacitance of coaxial cable:** $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\ \text{F/m}$ - ✅ **Inductance of coaxial cable:** $L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) + \dfrac{\mu}{4\pi}\ \text{H/m}$ - ✅ **Universal TEM identity:** $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ for **any** TEM-mode two-conductor line. - ✅ **Phase velocity:** $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC} = c/\sqrt{\mu_r\varepsilon_r}$ — independent of geometry. - ✅ **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C} \approx 120\ln(D/a)/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ for two-wire, $\approx 60\ln(b/a)/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ for coax. - ✅ **Internal inductance of one solid round conductor:** $L_{int} = \mu/(8\pi)\ \text{H/m}$ → total for the line (two conductors) = $\mu/(4\pi)$. - ✅ **Assumption:** Uniform charge/current distribution (valid at DC and for $D \gg a$). - ✅ **Free-space values:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$. - ✅ **At high frequencies** (skin effect), the internal inductance term becomes negligible. - ✅ **Geometric meaning of $\ln$:** It comes from integrating $1/\rho$ — the inverse-distance dependence of field from a line/cylinder. - ✅ **For exam:** Always start with the geometry figure, state assumptions, then apply Gauss's law / Ampere's law with clear integral limits.

topics.title: Energy Stored in Electric and Magnetic Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Stored in Electric and Magnetic Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In electromagnetic theory, the concept of **Energy Stored in Electric and Magnetic Field** refers to the total work done by external sources in establishing the static electric field **E** and the magnetostatic field **H** (or **B**) within a given region of space. This energy is recoverable; the field acts as a reservoir of energy that can be returned to the source when the field collapses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> Energy density expressions are central to deriving **Poynting's Theorem** and understanding the energy transport mechanism in **Time-Varying Fields** (covered later in Module 3). Mastery of these static-field energy expressions is a mandatory prerequisite.

The energy is expressed in two equivalent forms:

1. **Circuit Parameter Form (Localized):** Energy is associated with circuit elements like capacitors ($W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$) and inductors ($W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$).
2. **Field Theory Form (Distributed):** Energy resides in the field itself, distributed in space with a volume density $w = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ (electric) and $w = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ (magnetic).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Stretched Spring and the Spinning Flywheel**
> 
> **Electric Field ↔ Stretched Mechanical Spring:** Imagine a spring attached to a wall. To stretch it, you do work. That work is stored as *potential energy* in the spring. If you release it, you get the energy back. Similarly, building up an electric field (say, by charging a capacitor) requires work done against the Coulomb repulsion between like charges. This work is stored in the electric field between the capacitor plates. The stronger the field (analogous to a stiffer stretch), the more energy it holds.
> 
> **Magnetic Field ↔ Spinning Flywheel:** A spinning flywheel has *kinetic energy* stored in its motion — energy you can extract later (e.g., to keep a clock ticking). Similarly, a magnetic field (built up by current flowing in an inductor) contains energy stored in the motion of charges. The faster the current (or the stronger the **H** field), the more rotational "inertia" (magnetic energy) is stored. When the current stops, the collapsing magnetic field returns this energy to the circuit (often producing a high-voltage spark — a familiar example in car ignition systems).

### 1.3 Physical Constants Used

- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** (Permittivity of free space)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** (Permeability of free space)
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (Speed of light in vacuum)
- $\eta_0 = 376.73$ **Ω** (Intrinsic impedance of free space)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Energy Storage Mechanisms — A Logical Breakdown

#### A. Electric Field Energy ($W_E$)
- **Why does energy exist?** To assemble a system of charges, an external agent must do work against the mutual Coulomb repulsion (or attraction). This work is stored as electric potential energy in the field.
- **How is it computed?** Two routes:
  1. **Circuit form:** $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ (energy of a charged capacitor)
  2. **Field form:** $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_V \varepsilon E^2 \, dV$ (energy distributed in space)
- **Volume energy density:** $w_E = \tfrac{dW_E}{dV} = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D}$ **J/m³**

#### B. Magnetic Field Energy ($W_M$)
- **Why does energy exist?** To establish a current in an inductor, the source must do work against the induced back-EMF (Faraday's law). This work is stored as magnetic energy.
- **How is it computed?** Two routes:
  1. **Circuit form:** $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$ (energy of a current-carrying inductor)
  2. **Field form:** $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_V \mu H^2 \, dV$ (energy distributed in space)
- **Volume energy density:** $w_M = \tfrac{dW_M}{dV} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}$ **J/m³**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Energy in capacitor (circuit) | $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}QV = \tfrac{Q^2}{2C}$ | Joules (J) | $C$ in F, $V$ in V |
| 2 | Energy in inductor (circuit) | $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\Phi I = \tfrac{\Phi^2}{2L}$ | Joules (J) | $L$ in H, $I$ in A |
| 3 | Electric energy density | $w_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D}$ | J/m³ | Linear medium: $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon\mathbf{E}$ |
| 4 | Magnetic energy density | $w_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}$ | J/m³ | Linear medium: $\mathbf{B} = \mu\mathbf{H}$ |
| 5 | Total electric field energy | $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \varepsilon E^2 \, dV = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \, dV$ | J | Volume integral over field region |
| 6 | Total magnetic field energy | $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \mu H^2 \, dV = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV$ | J | Volume integral over field region |
| 7 | Field energy in terms of potentials | $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \rho V \, dV$ | J | $\rho$ = volume charge density, $V$ = scalar potential |
| 8 | Field energy in terms of vector potential | $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV$ | J | $\mathbf{A}$ = vector potential, $\mathbf{J}$ = current density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The transition from **circuit form** to **field form** is achieved by using $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}$ (parallel plate) and relating $E = V/d$, or by using $L = \dfrac{\mu N^2 A}{\ell}$ (solenoid) and relating $H = NI/\ell$. The energy density is a **local** property of the field — it does not depend on the geometry of the source.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Transmission Lines:** The electric field around high-voltage AC lines stores reactive energy, and the magnetic field around the conductors stores additional reactive energy. Power engineers must manage these to maintain voltage stability.
- **MRI Machines:** The superconducting coils in MRI systems store massive magnetic energy (megajoules). The controlled release of this energy during a quench (loss of superconductivity) is a critical safety concern.
- **Wireless Power Transfer (IPT):** Resonant inductive coupling relies on the magnetic energy stored in coils to transfer power without physical contact — used in electric vehicle charging and biomedical implants.
- **Capacitor Energy Banks:** Pulsed-power applications (railguns, defibrillators) store joules to megajoules in capacitor banks and release them in microseconds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in an Electric Field (Starting from Capacitor)

**Goal:** Derive the energy density $w_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Work done to charge a capacitor**

Consider charging a capacitor of capacitance $C$ from $0$ to a final charge $Q$ at voltage $V$. At an intermediate stage, let the charge be $q$ and the voltage be $v = q/C$. The work done to add an infinitesimal charge $dq$ against the existing voltage $v$ is:

$$
dW = v \, dq = \frac{q}{C} \, dq
$$

**Step 2: Integrate from 0 to Q**

$$
W_E = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{2C}\left[q^2\right]_0^Q = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2}CV^2
$$

This is the well-known circuit-level expression.

**Step 3: Substitute the geometry of a parallel-plate capacitor**

For a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$, separation $d$, and dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon$:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}, \quad V = E \cdot d \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^2 = E^2 d^2
$$

Substituting into the energy expression:

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} \cdot E^2 d^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 (Ad)
$$

**Step 4: Recognize the volume of the field region**

The product $A \cdot d$ is exactly the volume between the plates, $V_{\text{vol}}$ (using $V$ for volume here to avoid confusion with potential):

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 \cdot V_{\text{vol}}
$$

**Step 5: Define energy density**

$$
w_E = \frac{W_E}{V_{\text{vol}}} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 \quad \text{[J/m}^3\text{]}
$$

In vector form, since $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$ in a linear isotropic medium:

$$
w_E = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D}
$$

For a general (non-uniform) field, the total energy is:

$$
\boxed{W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \, dV = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \varepsilon E^2 \, dV}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Stored in a Magnetic Field (Starting from Inductor)

**Goal:** Derive the energy density $w_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Work done to establish a current in an inductor**

An inductor of self-inductance $L$ has a back-EMF given by Faraday's law:

$$
e = -L\frac{di}{dt}
$$

To drive a current $i$ against this back-EMF, the source must supply power $p = e \cdot i$ (magnitude), so the work done in time $dt$ is:

$$
dW = p \, dt = L \, i \, \frac{di}{dt} \, dt = L \, i \, di
$$

**Step 2: Integrate from 0 to I**

$$
W_M = \int_0^I L \, i \, di = \frac{1}{2}LI^2
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the geometry of a long solenoid**

For a long solenoid of length $\ell$, cross-sectional area $A$, $N$ turns, and core permeability $\mu$:

$$
L = \frac{\mu N^2 A}{\ell}, \quad H = \frac{NI}{\ell} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac{H\ell}{N}
$$

Squaring the current:

$$
I^2 = \frac{H^2 \ell^2}{N^2}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the energy expression**

$$
W_M = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N^2 A}{\ell} \cdot \frac{H^2 \ell^2}{N^2} = \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 (A\ell)
$$

**Step 5: Recognize the volume of the field region**

The product $A \cdot \ell$ is the volume enclosed by the solenoid, $V_{\text{vol}}$:

$$
W_M = \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 \cdot V_{\text{vol}}
$$

**Step 6: Define magnetic energy density**

$$
\boxed{w_M = \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \quad \text{[J/m}^3\text{]}}
$$

For a general (non-uniform) field:

$$
W_M = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mu H^2 \, dV
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy in Terms of Sources (Potential / Vector Potential)

#### A. Electric field energy in terms of $\rho$ and $V$

We start from Gauss's law and the gradient relation:

$$
\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E} = -\varepsilon \nabla V
$$

Using Green's first identity (a vector calculus identity), one can show:

$$
\int_V \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \, dV = \int_V \rho V \, dV
$$

Therefore, the total electric field energy can be written as:

$$
\boxed{W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \rho V \, dV}
$$

This is a powerful result — it shows that the energy can be computed purely from the source distribution $\rho$ and the resulting potential $V$, without explicitly finding the field.

#### B. Magnetic field energy in terms of $\mathbf{J}$ and $\mathbf{A}$

We start from Ampère's law in magnetostatics:

$$
\nabla \times \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}
$$

Using a similar vector identity, one can show:

$$
\int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV = \int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{W_M = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor ½?** The factor ½ in both energy expressions arises from the fact that we are computing the work to *assemble* the system against its own field. Without the ½, we would double-count the work. This is analogous to the factor ½ in $W = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ in mechanics, which comes from integrating $v \, dv$ from 0 to $v$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Example (KTU-Style):** A parallel-plate capacitor has circular plates of radius $a = 5$ cm separated by $d = 2$ mm of air. It is charged to $V = 1000$ V. Find: (a) the energy stored, (b) the energy density.

**Solution:**

**(a) Energy stored:**

Capacitance:
$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \pi a^2}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(\pi)(0.05)^2}{0.002}
$$

$$
C = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(7.854 \times 10^{-3})}{0.002} = 3.476 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} \approx 34.76 \text{ pF}
$$

Energy:
$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2}(3.476 \times 10^{-11})(1000)^2 = 1.738 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} \approx 17.38 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

**(b) Energy density:**

Electric field between the plates:
$$
E = \frac{V}{d} = \frac{1000}{0.002} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ V/m}
$$

Energy density:
$$
w_E = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2 = \frac{1}{2}(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(5 \times 10^5)^2 = 1.107 \text{ J/m}^3
$$

**Verification:** Total energy = density × volume:
$$
W_E = w_E \cdot V_{\text{vol}} = (1.107)(\pi)(0.05)^2(0.002) = 1.738 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} \quad \checkmark
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Electromagnetic Energy Storage

The following block diagram illustrates how energy storage relates to the source, the field, and the resulting circuit parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SOURCE OF ENERGY"] --> B["WORK DONE BY EXTERNAL AGENT"]
    B --> C1["Charging a Capacitor q against V"]
    B --> C2["Driving Current i against back-EMF"]
    C1 --> D1["STATIC ELECTRIC FIELD E in Dielectric"]
    C2 --> D2["STATIC MAGNETIC FIELD H around Conductor"]
    D1 --> E1["Circuit Form: WE = 0.5 C V squared"]
    D1 --> E2["Field Form: WE = 0.5 integral epsilon E squared dV"]
    D2 --> F1["Circuit Form: WM = 0.5 L I squared"]
    D2 --> F2["Field Form: WM = 0.5 integral mu H squared dV"]
    E1 --> G["TOTAL FIELD ENERGY W = WE plus WM"]
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H["Energy Density w = 0.5 E dot D plus 0.5 B dot H"]
    H --> I["Time-Varying Case: Poynting Vector S = E cross H"]
    I --> J["Energy Flow and Radiation Module 3"]
```

### 4.2 Mapping Circuit Quantities to Field Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "CAPACITIVE SIDE"
        A1["Capacitance C"] --> A2["Voltage V"]
        A2 --> A3["Electric Field E equals V over d"]
        A3 --> A4["Energy Density wE equals 0.5 epsilon E squared"]
    end
    subgraph "INDUCTIVE SIDE"
        B1["Inductance L"] --> B2["Current I"]
        B2 --> B3["Magnetic Field H equals NI over l"]
        B3 --> B4["Energy Density wM equals 0.5 mu H squared"]
    end
    A4 --> C["TOTAL EM ENERGY IN VOLUME V"]
    B4 --> C
```

### 4.3 Detailed Concept Map: Electric Field Energy Derivation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0["START: Assembling charges on a capacitor"] --> N1["Work dW equals v dq equals q by C dq"]
    N1 --> N2["Integrate from q=0 to q=Q"]
    N2 --> N3["WE equals 0.5 Q squared over C equals 0.5 C V squared"]
    N3 --> N4["Substitute C equals epsilon A over d and V equals E d"]
    N4 --> N5["WE equals 0.5 epsilon E squared times A d"]
    N5 --> N6["Recognize A d as Volume Vvol"]
    N6 --> N7["wE equals WE over Vvol equals 0.5 epsilon E squared"]
    N7 --> N8["General form: WE equals 0.5 integral E dot D dV"]
    N8 --> N9["Source form: WE equals 0.5 integral rho V dV"]
    N9 --> END["RESULT: Energy is local, resides in field, not in charges"]
```

### 4.4 Detailed Concept Map: Magnetic Field Energy Derivation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["START: Establishing current I in an inductor"] --> M1["Back-EMF e equals minus L di by dt"]
    M1 --> M2["Power supplied p equals L i di by dt"]
    M2 --> M3["Work dW equals L i di"]
    M3 --> M4["Integrate from i=0 to i=I"]
    M4 --> M5["WM equals 0.5 L I squared"]
    M5 --> M6["Substitute L equals mu N squared A over l"]
    M6 --> M7["Substitute H equals NI over l, so I equals Hl over N"]
    M7 --> M8["WM equals 0.5 mu H squared times A l"]
    M8 --> M9["Recognize A l as Volume Vvol"]
    M9 --> M10["wM equals WM over Vvol equals 0.5 mu H squared"]
    M10 --> M11["General form: WM equals 0.5 integral B dot H dV"]
    M11 --> M12["Source form: WM equals 0.5 integral A dot J dV"]
    M12 -> END2["RESULT: Magnetic energy is local, stored in B and H fields"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each "node" represents a logical step. The arrows indicate derivation flow. In your KTU answer sheet, you may reproduce a simplified version of any of these flowcharts to gain easy 1-2 marks on presentation, especially in 14-mark derivations.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define energy density in an electric field. Derive the expression for the energy stored in a parallel-plate capacitor in terms of the electric field.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The electric energy density is defined as the energy stored per unit volume of the electric field region:

$$
w_E = \frac{dW_E}{dV} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 \quad \text{J/m}^3
$$

For a parallel-plate capacitor of plate area $A$ and separation $d$ with dielectric $\varepsilon$, the capacitance is $C = \varepsilon A / d$ and the field between the plates is $E = V/d$. Hence $V = Ed$ and $C = \varepsilon A/d$, giving:

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} \cdot (Ed)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 (Ad)
$$

Since $Ad$ is the volume between the plates, the energy density is $w_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between electric field energy and magnetic field energy. Write the expression for magnetic energy density in a linear medium.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Electric Field Energy | Magnetic Field Energy |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Source | Stationary charges ($\rho$) | Steady currents ($\mathbf{J}$) |
| Field quantity | $\mathbf{E}$ or $\mathbf{D}$ | $\mathbf{H}$ or $\mathbf{B}$ |
| Energy density | $w_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ | $w_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2$ |
| Circuit analogue | Capacitor: $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Inductor: $W = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$ |
| Recovery | Released on discharge | Released on current decay |

The magnetic energy density in a linear medium ($\mathbf{B} = \mu\mathbf{H}$) is:

$$
w_M = \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \quad \text{J/m}^3
$$

**(3 Marks)**

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ factor** is the #1 mistake. Students write $W = CV^2$ or $W = LI^2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ and $\tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$. This costs **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $\varepsilon$ (in F/m) with $E$ (in V/m) is correct, but if you substitute numerical values without checking, expect unit-mark deductions.
> 3. **In vector form, write $\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D}$ and $\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}$**, not just $ED$ or $BH$. Examiners look for vector notation in 7+ mark derivations.
> 4. **For 14-mark questions, the "starting point" carries weight.** If you just state the formula without deriving it from the circuit element, you lose up to 4 marks.
> 5. **Always state "J/m³"** for energy density and "J" for total energy. Units in the final answer are graded.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Energy Derivation with Numerical Application**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a static electric field. Starting from the work done in charging a capacitor, obtain the total electric field energy in a region containing a linear dielectric. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A long solenoid of length $\ell = 50$ cm, radius $a = 2$ cm, and $N = 1000$ turns carries a current $I = 5$ A. The core has relative permeability $\mu_r = 500$. Calculate: (i) the magnetic field energy stored, (ii) the magnetic energy density inside the solenoid, and (iii) the self-inductance of the solenoid. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Electric Field Energy (7 Marks):**

Consider charging a capacitor of capacitance $C$ from $0$ to final charge $Q$. At an intermediate charge $q$, the voltage across the capacitor is $v = q/C$. The infinitesimal work done by the source to add a charge $dq$ is:

$$
dW = v \, dq = \frac{q}{C} \, dq
$$

Total work done in charging from $q = 0$ to $q = Q$:

$$
W_E = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C} \, dq = \frac{1}{2C}\left[q^2\right]_0^Q = \frac{Q^2}{2C}
$$

Using $Q = CV$:

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2
$$

For a parallel-plate capacitor, $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}$ and $V = Ed$, so:

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} \cdot (E d)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 (Ad)
$$

The quantity $Ad$ is the volume of the dielectric between the plates. Therefore, the energy per unit volume (energy density) is:

$$
\boxed{w_E = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \quad \text{J/m}^3}
$$

For a general (non-uniform) field in a linear dielectric:

$$
\boxed{W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \, dV = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \varepsilon E^2 \, dV \quad \text{J}}
$$

**(7 Marks – Award 1 mark for the integral form, 2 marks for the charging-work integral, 2 marks for the parallel-plate substitution, 2 marks for the final boxed result.)**

---

**(b) Solenoid Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $\ell = 0.5$ m, $a = 0.02$ m, $N = 1000$, $I = 5$ A, $\mu_r = 500$, so $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(500) = 6.283 \times 10^{-4}$ H/m.

**(i) Magnetic field intensity inside the solenoid:**

$$
H = \frac{NI}{\ell} = \frac{(1000)(5)}{0.5} = 10000 \text{ A/m} = 10^4 \text{ A/m}
$$

**Magnetic flux density:**

$$
B = \mu H = (6.283 \times 10^{-4})(10^4) = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \text{ T}
$$

**Energy density:**

$$
w_M = \frac{1}{2}\mu H^2 = \frac{1}{2}(6.283 \times 10^{-4})(10^4)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(6.283 \times 10^{-4})(10^8)
$$

$$
w_M = \frac{1}{2}(6.283 \times 10^4) = 3.142 \times 10^4 \text{ J/m}^3
$$

**(ii) Total magnetic field energy:**

Volume of the solenoid: $V = \pi a^2 \ell = \pi (0.02)^2 (0.5) = 6.283 \times 10^{-4}$ m³

$$
W_M = w_M \cdot V = (3.142 \times 10^4)(6.283 \times 10^{-4}) = 19.74 \text{ J}
$$

**(iii) Self-inductance:**

Using $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$:

$$
L = \frac{2 W_M}{I^2} = \frac{2(19.74)}{(5)^2} = \frac{39.48}{25} = 1.579 \text{ H}
$$

**Verification using $L = \mu N^2 A / \ell$:**

$$
L = \frac{\mu N^2 \pi a^2}{\ell} = \frac{(6.283 \times 10^{-4})(10^6)(\pi)(4 \times 10^{-4})}{0.5}
$$

$$
L = \frac{(6.283 \times 10^{-4})(10^6)(1.2566 \times 10^{-3})}{0.5} = \frac{0.7896}{0.5} = 1.579 \text{ H} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(7 Marks – Award 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), and 2 marks for (iii).)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Energy in Terms of Sources**

**(a)** Starting from the scalar potential formulation, derive the expression $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \rho V \, dV$ for the total electrostatic energy in a linear, isotropic medium. Explain why this expression involves the factor ½. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coaxial cable has inner radius $a = 1$ mm, outer radius $b = 4$ mm, length $\ell = 10$ m, and is filled with a dielectric of $\varepsilon_r = 4$. The cable is charged to a potential difference $V_0 = 200$ V between the inner and outer conductors. Calculate: (i) the electric field as a function of radius, (ii) the energy stored per unit length, and (iii) the capacitance per unit length. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \rho V \, dV$ (7 Marks):**

We start from the field-form expression:

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \, dV
$$

In a linear, isotropic, homogeneous medium: $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$, and the field can be expressed via the scalar potential: $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V$.

Substituting:

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{D} \cdot (-\nabla V) \, dV = -\frac{1}{2}\int_V \mathbf{D} \cdot \nabla V \, dV
$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (V\mathbf{D}) = V(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D}) + \mathbf{D} \cdot \nabla V$:

$$
\mathbf{D} \cdot \nabla V = \nabla \cdot (V\mathbf{D}) - V(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D})
$$

From Gauss's law: $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho$. So:

$$
\mathbf{D} \cdot \nabla V = \nabla \cdot (V\mathbf{D}) - V\rho
$$

Substituting back:

$$
W_E = -\frac{1}{2}\int_V \left[\nabla \cdot (V\mathbf{D}) - V\rho\right] dV
$$

$$
W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V V\rho \, dV - \frac{1}{2}\int_V \nabla \cdot (V\mathbf{D}) \, dV
$$

Applying the divergence theorem to the second integral:

$$
\int_V \nabla \cdot (V\mathbf{D}) \, dV = \oint_S V\mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S}
$$

For a localized charge distribution, the potential $V \sim 1/r$ and $|\mathbf{D}| \sim 1/r^2$, so the surface area $\sim r^2$, making the surface integral vanish as $r \to \infty$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{W_E = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \rho V \, dV}
$$

**Why the factor ½?** The factor ½ accounts for the fact that we are computing the work done to *assemble* the system, not to maintain it. When charge is brought from infinity, the potential $V$ at a point depends on the *final* charge distribution, but the work done in bringing the *last* element of charge is computed against the *existing* field. The integration of the product naturally produces a factor of ½, just as in mechanics, $W = \int F \, dx = \int mv \, dv = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Coaxial Cable Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $a = 10^{-3}$ m, $b = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\ell = 10$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $V_0 = 200$ V.

The permittivity is $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r = 4\varepsilon_0$.

**(i) Electric field as a function of radius:**

Using Gauss's law on a cylindrical surface of radius $r$ (where $a \le r \le b$) and length $\ell$, with charge $+\lambda$ per unit length on the inner conductor:

$$
\oint \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = Q_{\text{enc}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D(2\pi r \ell) = \lambda \ell
$$

$$
D = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{D}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon r}
$$

The potential difference is:

$$
V_0 = \int_a^b E \, dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\int_a^b \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi \varepsilon}\ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

So:

$$
\lambda = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon V_0}{\ln(b/a)} = \frac{2\pi (4\varepsilon_0)(200)}{\ln(4)} = \frac{1600\pi \varepsilon_0}{1.3863} = 1154\pi \varepsilon_0
$$

Numerically: $\lambda = 1154\pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) = 3.211 \times 10^{-8}$ C/m.

The electric field is:

$$
\boxed{E(r) = \frac{V_0}{r \ln(b/a)} = \frac{200}{r \ln(4)} = \frac{288.4}{r} \text{ V/m}, \quad a \le r \le b}
$$

**(ii) Energy stored per unit length:**

The energy density is:

$$
w_E = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon \left(\frac{V_0}{r\ln(b/a)}\right)^2 = \frac{\varepsilon V_0^2}{2r^2[\ln(b/a)]^2}
$$

For a cylindrical shell of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and length $\ell$, the volume is $dV = 2\pi r \ell \, dr$. The total energy is:

$$
W_E = \int_a^b w_E \cdot 2\pi r \ell \, dr = \frac{\pi \varepsilon \ell V_0^2}{[\ln(b/a)]^2}\int_a^b \frac{dr}{r}
$$

$$
W_E = \frac{\pi \varepsilon \ell V_0^2}{[\ln(b/a)]^2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{\pi \varepsilon \ell V_0^2}{\ln(b/a)}
$$

Energy per unit length:

$$
\frac{W_E}{\ell} = \frac{\pi \varepsilon V_0^2}{\ln(b/a)} = \frac{\pi (4\varepsilon_0)(200)^2}{\ln(4)} = \frac{160000\pi \varepsilon_0}{1.3863}
$$

$$
\frac{W_E}{\ell} = \frac{1.600 \times 10^{5} \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.3863} = 3.211 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J/m} = 3.21 \, \mu\text{J/m}
$$

**(iii) Capacitance per unit length:**

Using $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}C V_0^2$ and the energy per unit length:

$$
\frac{C}{\ell} = \frac{2 W_E / \ell}{V_0^2} = \frac{2 \times 3.211 \times 10^{-6}}{(200)^2} = \frac{6.422 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^4} = 1.606 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}
$$

**Verification using standard formula $C/\ell = 2\pi\varepsilon/\ln(b/a)$:**

$$
\frac{C}{\ell} = \frac{2\pi (4\varepsilon_0)}{\ln(4)} = \frac{8\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.3863} = \frac{2.225 \times 10^{-10}}{1.3863} = 1.606 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(7 Marks – Award 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), and 2 marks for (iii).)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Stored in Electric and Magnetic Field**

- ⚡ **Electric Field Energy (Circuit):** $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}QV = \tfrac{Q^2}{2C}$
- ⚡ **Electric Energy Density:** $w_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D}$ **J/m³**
- ⚡ **Electric Field Energy (Volume Integral):** $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_V \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D} \, dV$
- ⚡ **Source Form:** $W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_V \rho V \, dV$
- 🧲 **Magnetic Field Energy (Circuit):** $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\Phi I = \tfrac{\Phi^2}{2L}$
- 🧲 **Magnetic Energy Density:** $w_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H}$ **J/m³**
- 🧲 **Magnetic Field Energy (Volume Integral):** $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_V \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{H} \, dV$
- 🧲 **Source Form:** $W_M = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_V \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{J} \, dV$

**Critical Don'ts:**
- ❌ Never omit the factor ½ — it accounts for the assembly work.
- ❌ Never confuse $\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{D}$ with $ED \cos\theta$ unless the angle between them is non-zero (in linear media, they are parallel, so it's just $ED$).
- ❌ Never apply circuit formulas ($W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$) to distributed systems — use the volume integral.

**Critical Do's:**
- ✅ Always work in SI units: $\varepsilon_0$ in F/m, $\mu_0$ in H/m, $E$ in V/m, $H$ in A/m, $B$ in T.
- ✅ For numerical problems, verify by computing $W$ in *two different ways* (e.g., field density and circuit form).
- ✅ Memorize the parallel-plate ($C = \varepsilon A/d$, $E = V/d$) and solenoid ($L = \mu N^2 A/\ell$, $H = NI/\ell$) geometries — they are essential for substitutions.
- ✅ Connect this topic forward to **Poynting's Theorem** (Module 3): the rate of energy flow out of a region is $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}$ (Poynting vector), and the energy densities derived here are the time-averaged storage terms.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Displacement Current Density

**Displacement current density** is the time rate of change of the electric flux density (electric displacement field) $\vec{D}$ at a point in a medium. It was introduced by **James Clerk Maxwell** (1861–1865) to resolve the inconsistency in Ampère's circuital law when applied to time-varying fields, most famously across the dielectric gap of a charging capacitor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> $$\vec{J}_d = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$
> For a linear, homogeneous, isotropic dielectric, since $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$, this reduces to:
> $$\vec{J}_d = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$
> Units: **A/m²** (same as conduction current density $\vec{J}_c$).

The corresponding scalar **displacement current** through any surface $S$ is:
$$I_d = \int_S \vec{J}_d \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_S \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Leaky Bucket and a Refilling Pipe:**
> Imagine water flowing through a pipe (conduction current). When the pipe empties into a tank, the water level in the tank rises. The rising water level represents an *effective flow* even though no water is physically crossing the tank's interior in the same way as through the pipe. This "effective flow" (rising water) is analogous to **displacement current** — it is not a real movement of charges across the dielectric gap, but the *changing electric field* between capacitor plates plays the role of a current that "completes the circuit". Without it, Ampère's law would say magnetic fields mysteriously vanish inside the capacitor — a physical impossibility!

In free space, the physical constant is **$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** (permittivity of free space), and in a general linear medium $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$, where $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity.

---

## 1.2 Continuity Equation

**The Continuity Equation** is a mathematical statement of the **principle of conservation of electric charge**. It expresses that electric charge can neither be created nor destroyed — only moved from one location to another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement (Point/Differential Form):**
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0$$
> where $\vec{J}$ is the total current density (A/m²) and $\rho_v$ is the volume charge density (C/m³).
>
> **Equivalent Integral Form:**
> $$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho_v \, dV$$

The term $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$ physically represents the **net outflow of current per unit volume**, while $\dfrac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t}$ represents the **rate of change of charge density** at that point.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Compressible Fluid Analogy:**
> Think of charge as an incompressible fluid flowing through a flexible pipe. If more fluid flows *into* a region than flows *out*, the region's "charge density" must *increase* over time. Conversely, if more fluid flows *out* than in, charge density *decreases*. The continuity equation simply says: **"What goes in minus what goes out equals what accumulates inside."** Charge is never created or destroyed — it is merely redistributed.

The continuity equation is one of the most fundamental *pillars* of electromagnetism and is mathematically consistent with all four of **Maxwell's equations** taken together.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Maxwell's Correction Was Needed — The Logical Build-Up

The story of displacement current begins with a logical inconsistency in Ampère's original law. Let us trace Maxwell's reasoning step by step:

### Step 1: Ampère's Original Circuital Law (Steady Currents Only)

For steady (DC) currents, Ampère's law in integral form is:
$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc} = \int_S \vec{J}_c \cdot d\vec{S}$$

This worked perfectly for steady currents because $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c = 0$ (current lines form closed loops).

### Step 2: The Capacitor Paradox (Failure Under Time-Varying Fields)

Consider a parallel-plate capacitor being charged by a time-varying current $i(t)$. Take a closed loop $C$ encircling the connecting wire, and two different surfaces bounded by $C$:

- **Surface $S_1$**: Sliced through the wire → encloses conduction current $I_c$.
- **Surface $S_2$**: Sliced between the capacitor plates (in the dielectric) → encloses **no conduction current** ($I_c = 0$), even though $C$ is the same loop.

Both surfaces must give the same answer for $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$ — this is **CONTRADICTORY**.

### Step 3: Maxwell's Resolution (1865)

Maxwell realized that the **time-varying electric field** between the plates acts as an effective current. He added a second term to Ampère's law:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \left( \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

This makes $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$ independent of the choice of surface — it now has the same value whether calculated using $S_1$ or $S_2$.

### Step 4: Conservation of Charge — Source of Continuity Equation

From Gauss's law: $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$. Taking the divergence of the Ampère-Maxwell equation and using the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0$ yields:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0$$

This is the **continuity equation**, demonstrating that displacement current is mathematically essential for charge conservation.

---

| 8 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ | Gauss's law (electric) | Used in continuity derivation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Why Does This Matter?**
> - **Antenna Design**: Time-varying fields and displacement currents are the foundation of electromagnetic wave radiation.
> - **Capacitor Selection in High-Frequency Circuits**: At RF/microwave frequencies, displacement current through dielectrics becomes dominant and dictates heating losses.
> - **Optical Fiber Communication**: Total internal reflection and Snell's law at fiber boundaries rely on Maxwell's displacement current term.
> - **Medical Imaging (MRI)**: Time-varying magnetic fields produced by displacement currents are core to gradient coil design.

---

## 2.3 Special Cases Worth Remembering

1. **For steady currents**: $\dfrac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0$, hence $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ (current lines form closed loops — Kirchhoff's Current Law generalized).
2. **For static charge distributions**: No current flows, so $\vec{J} = 0$ and $\rho_v$ is time-invariant.
3. **In a perfect conductor**: $\vec{E} = 0$ inside, so $\vec{J}_d = 0$; only $\vec{J}_c$ exists.
4. **In a perfect dielectric**: $\vec{J}_c = 0$, but $\vec{J}_d = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ can be non-zero — this is the situation between capacitor plates.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, examiner-ready derivations for both the **Continuity Equation** and the **Displacement Current Density**. Every algebraic step is written out explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (from Conservation of Charge)

### Given:
A closed surface $S$ enclosing a volume $V$. Current density $\vec{J}$ flows outward.

### Step 1: Set Up the Physical Principle

By the **principle of conservation of electric charge**, the net current flowing *out* of the closed surface per unit time must equal the *rate of decrease* of the charge enclosed within the volume.

$$\text{Current flowing outward per second} = -\frac{dQ_{enc}}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Express the Outward Current as a Surface Integral

The total current $I_{out}$ flowing outward through the closed surface $S$ is given by the surface integral of current density:

$$I_{out} = \oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

### Step 3: Express the Enclosed Charge as a Volume Integral

The total charge enclosed in volume $V$ is the volume integral of charge density $\rho_v$:

$$Q_{enc} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV$$

### Step 4: Combine the Two Expressions

Substituting into the conservation statement:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho_v \, dV$$

### Step 5: Bring the Time Derivative Inside the Volume Integral

Since the volume $V$ is fixed (surface $S$ does not deform with time), we can swap differentiation and integration:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} \, dV$$

### Step 6: Apply the Divergence Theorem

The **Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem)** converts the surface integral on the left-hand side into a volume integral:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV$$

### Step 7: Equate the Two Volume Integrals

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} \, dV$$

$$\int_V \left( \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} \right) dV = 0$$

### Step 8: Argue for Arbitrary Volume $V$

Since this must hold for *any arbitrary* volume $V$ (the integral vanishes for every possible choice of $V$), the integrand itself must vanish identically at every point:

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0}$$

This is the **Continuity Equation in point (differential) form**. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Displacement Current Density (from Ampère's Law and Capacitor Physics)

### Step 1: State Ampère's Original Circuital Law

For a steady current, Ampère's law relates the line integral of magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ around a closed loop $C$ to the conduction current $I_{enc}$ enclosed:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc} = \int_S \vec{J}_c \cdot d\vec{S}$$

### Step 2: Apply to a Charging Parallel-Plate Capacitor

Consider the figure: a circular loop $C$ of radius $r$ encircles the wire carrying charging current $i(t)$ to the capacitor. We pick **two different surfaces** $S_1$ and $S_2$ both bounded by the same loop $C$:

- **Surface $S_1$**: Disk-shaped, intersecting the wire → $I_{enc} = I_c$ (conduction current)
- **Surface $S_2$**: Bag-shaped, passing *between* the capacitor plates → $I_{enc} = 0$ (no conduction current through dielectric)

Since $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$ must be independent of surface choice, we have a contradiction: $I_c = 0$ is wrong.

### Step 3: Recognize the Missing Term

Between the plates, although no charges physically cross, there is a **time-varying electric field** $\vec{E}(t)$. By Gauss's law, this field corresponds to surface charge $\pm Q$ on the plates:

$$Q = \int_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \varepsilon E \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the plate area.

### Step 4: Compute the Time Derivative of the Electric Flux

Differentiating both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = \int_S \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

The left-hand side $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = I_c$ (the conduction current in the wire). The right-hand side has the form of a *current-like* term — Maxwell identified this as the **displacement current** $I_d$.

### Step 5: Define the Displacement Current Density

To make the units consistent (current per unit area), define:

$$\boxed{\vec{J}_d = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}}$$

Then the total displacement current through any surface $S$ is:

$$I_d = \int_S \vec{J}_d \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_S \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} = \frac{dQ}{dt} = I_c$$

### Step 6: Write the Generalized (Ampère-Maxwell) Law

Adding $\vec{J}_d$ to the original Ampère's law:

$$\boxed{\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \left( \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) \cdot d\vec{S}}$$

In point form:
$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

Now both surfaces $S_1$ and $S_2$ yield the same value of $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$, and the contradiction is resolved. ∎

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Showing $I_c = I_d$ for a Parallel-Plate Capacitor

A common KTU numerical/theoretical question asks you to show that the conduction current equals the displacement current. Below is a clean derivation suitable for 7-mark sub-parts.

| Step | Equation / Statement | Physical Justification |
|------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Capacitance $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}$ | Standard formula for parallel-plate capacitor |
| 2 | Charge on plate $Q = CV = \dfrac{\varepsilon A V}{d}$ | Definition of capacitance |
| 3 | Conduction current $I_c = \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Time derivative of charge |
| 4 | Electric field $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Uniform field between plates |
| 5 | Displacement current density $J_d = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{d}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Definition of $\vec{J}_d$ |
| 6 | Total displacement current $I_d = J_d \cdot A = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Multiply by area |
| 7 | $\therefore I_c = I_d$ | Comparison of Steps 3 and 6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The conduction current in the wire equals the displacement current between the capacitor plates. This is *exactly* what allows current to behave as a continuous flow in time-varying circuits, satisfying Kirchhoff's Current Law even at the capacitor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Continuity Equation from Ampère-Maxwell Law

This reverse derivation is also frequently asked in KTU exams. It demonstrates the internal consistency of Maxwell's equations.

### Step 1: Start with the Ampère-Maxwell Law

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

### Step 2: Take the Divergence of Both Sides

$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

### Step 3: Apply Vector Identity

The divergence of any curl is identically zero:
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) \equiv 0$$

Therefore:
$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

### Step 4: Substitute Gauss's Law

By Gauss's law for the electric field:
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$$

So:
$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0}$$

This is exactly the continuity equation. Hence, **charge conservation is built into Maxwell's equations** when displacement current is included. ∎

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section provides **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions modeled on actual KTU 2024 ESE patterns, with full model solutions and a recap checklist.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT411]`
**Define displacement current density. Why was this concept introduced by Maxwell?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Displacement current density is the time rate of change of electric flux density $\vec{D}$:
> $$\vec{J}_d = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$
> Its SI unit is **A/m²**.

> **Why introduced (2 marks):** Maxwell introduced this concept to resolve the inconsistency in Ampère's circuital law when applied to time-varying fields. When a capacitor is being charged, applying Ampère's original law to a loop encircling the wire gives the conduction current $I_c$ for one surface, but zero for another surface passing between the plates — a contradiction. By adding the displacement current term $\int_S \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S}$, the line integral $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$ becomes independent of surface choice, and the two surfaces give the same answer. This also ensures consistency with charge conservation (continuity equation).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT411]`
**State the continuity equation in differential form. What does each term represent physically?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement (2 marks):** The continuity equation in differential (point) form is:
> $$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0$$
> where $\vec{J}$ is the total current density in A/m² and $\rho_v$ is the volume charge density in C/m³.

> **Physical meaning (1 mark):**
> - $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$ — net outflow of current per unit volume (current leaving minus current entering).
> - $\dfrac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t}$ — rate of change of charge density at that point.
> - The equation states that any net outflow of current from a region must be accompanied by a corresponding decrease in the enclosed charge density — i.e., **charge is conserved**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and provides internal choice (typically two alternative questions, you answer one). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEEVT411]`

**(a) Derive the continuity equation in differential form starting from the principle of conservation of electric charge.** (7 Marks)

**(b) With reference to a parallel-plate capacitor being charged, define displacement current density and show that the displacement current between the plates equals the conduction current in the connecting wire.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Continuity Equation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the conservation principle: the net current flowing outward through any closed surface $S$ per unit time equals the rate of decrease of charge enclosed within volume $V$.

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{dQ_{enc}}{dt}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the enclosed charge as a volume integral:
$$Q_{enc} = \int_V \rho_v \, dV$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute and bring the time derivative inside the integral (since $V$ is fixed):
$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} \, dV$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:
$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{J}) \, dV = -\int_V \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} \, dV$$

$$\int_V \left( \nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} \right) dV = 0$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Since the volume $V$ is *arbitrary*, the integrand must vanish pointwise:
$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0}$$

This is the **continuity equation** in point form, expressing conservation of charge.

---

#### Part (b) — Displacement Current Density in a Parallel-Plate Capacitor (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$, separation $d$, dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon$, and instantaneous voltage $V(t)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The capacitance is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}$, and charge on the plate is $Q = CV$. The conduction current in the connecting wire is:
$$I_c = \frac{dQ}{dt} = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The uniform electric field between the plates is $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$. Differentiating:
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{d} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Displacement current density between the plates (in the dielectric) is:
$$\vec{J}_d = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} = \frac{\varepsilon}{d} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} \, \hat{n}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** The total displacement current through the surface area $A$ is:
$$I_d = J_d \cdot A = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$

Comparing with Step 2:
$$\boxed{I_d = I_c}$$

Hence, the **displacement current between the plates equals the conduction current in the wire**, ensuring current continuity in the time-varying circuit.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PEEVT411]`

**(a) Starting from Ampère's circuital law, explain how Maxwell modified it. State the resulting Ampère-Maxwell law in both integral and point forms.** (7 Marks)

**(b) By taking the divergence of the Ampère-Maxwell law and using Gauss's law, derive the continuity equation. Hence show that charge conservation is automatically satisfied by Maxwell's equations.** (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Maxwell's Modification of Ampère's Law (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write Ampère's original circuital law (valid only for steady currents):
$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc} = \int_S \vec{J}_c \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify the inconsistency: applied to a charging capacitor, this gives different answers for surfaces passing through the wire (giving $I_c$) versus surfaces passing between the plates (giving 0), though both share the same bounding loop $C$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Maxwell's correction: add a displacement current density term $\dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ to make the law independent of surface choice. The corrected Ampère-Maxwell law is:

$$\text{Integral form: } \oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \left( \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

$$\text{Point form: } \nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Physical justification: the displacement current density $\vec{J}_d = \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ arises from the time-varying electric field in the capacitor gap, which acts as an effective current.

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Continuity from Ampère-Maxwell Law (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the Ampère-Maxwell law in point form:
$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take the divergence of both sides:
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use the vector identity that the divergence of any curl is zero:
$$\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = 0$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Swap the order of space and time derivatives:
$$\nabla \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \vec{D})$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply Gauss's law $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$:
$$0 = \nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Rearrange to obtain the continuity equation:
$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0}$$

This proves that **charge conservation is built into Maxwell's equations** — they are mathematically consistent with the continuity equation only because the displacement current term was added.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** Students often write displacement current in *Amperes* but displacement current *density* in *A/m²*. Both are correct, but the question wording dictates which is required — read carefully.
> 2. **Missing the volume-integral argument:** When deriving the continuity equation from the divergence theorem, the final step (volume is *arbitrary*, so integrand must vanish) is worth 1–2 marks but often skipped.
> 3. **Swapping $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{D}$:** Displacement current density involves $\vec{D}$, not $\vec{E}$. Use $\vec{J}_d = \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ in general, only reducing to $\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ in linear media.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify $I_c = I_d$:** Many students derive $J_d$ but forget the final numerical equality proof — KTU examiners specifically look for this conclusion.
> 5. **Direction of $\hat{n}$:** When writing $I_c = I_d$ for a capacitor, the displacement current "flows" in the same direction as would conduction current — be careful with vector orientations.
> 6. **Missing Gauss's law substitution:** In the reverse derivation (continuity from Ampère-Maxwell), students forget to substitute $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ at the critical step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Displacement current density** is defined as $\vec{J}_d = \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$, with units **A/m²**.
- [x] For **linear, isotropic, homogeneous** media: $\vec{J}_d = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$.
- [x] For **free space**: $\vec{J}_d = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- [x] **Scalar displacement current** through surface $S$: $I_d = \int_S \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \cdot d\vec{S} = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$.
- [x] **Continuity equation (point form)**: $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \dfrac{\partial \rho_v}{\partial t} = 0$.
- [x] **Continuity equation (integral form)**: $\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho_v \, dV$.
- [x] For **steady currents**: $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0$ (current lines form closed loops — basis of KCL).
- [x] **Ampère-Maxwell law**: $\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S \left( \vec{J}_c + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right) \cdot d\vec{S}$.
- [x] **Point form**: $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_c + \dfrac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$.
- [x] **Capacitor identity**: $I_c = I_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$.
- [x] **The capacitor paradox** is resolved *only* by including $\vec{J}_d$ in Ampère's law.
- [x] **Charge conservation** follows from (i) Gauss's law + (ii) Ampère-Maxwell law, *only* because displacement current is present.
- [x] **Derivation sequence to remember**:
  1. Conservation of charge → continuity equation (direct derivation).
  2. Ampère-Maxwell law + Gauss's law → continuity equation (reverse derivation).
  3. Continuity equation + Gauss's law → Ampère-Maxwell law (round-trip).
- [x] **Engineering applications**: antenna radiation, RF capacitor design, optical fibers, MRI coils, transmission line theory.
- [x] **Examiner's favorite 7-mark question**: "Show that $I_c = I_d$ for a parallel-plate capacitor" — practice the full table from Section 3.3.
- [x] **Examiner's favorite 7-mark question (alt.)**: "Derive the continuity equation from Ampère-Maxwell law" — practice the divergence + Gauss substitution.
- [x] **Mnemonic**: "**C**harge **C**ontinuity comes from **D**ivergence" — $\nabla \cdot \vec{J}$ measures outflow.
- [x] **Mnemonic**: "**D**isplacement current **D**epends on **dD/dt**" — don't write $\vec{E}$ directly without justification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:**
> In the 2024 scheme, emphasis is on **conceptual clarity** and **derivation steps**, not just final answers. Always show intermediate steps, justify each transformation, and conclude with a boxed final result. For numerical problems, retain units throughout — examiners allocate at least 1 mark for correct units and final boxed answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Vector Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Vector Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic Vector Potential (A):** A vector field defined in magnetostatics such that the magnetic flux density **B** at any point is equal to the **curl** of **A**.
> 
> $$\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$$

The unit of magnetic vector potential **A** is **Weber per meter (Wb/m)** or equivalently **Tesla·meter (T·m)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is A introduced?** Because **∇·B = 0** (Gauss's Law for Magnetism, one of Maxwell's equations), the magnetic flux density **B** is always solenoidal (divergence-free). Any solenoidal field can be expressed as the curl of some other vector field — that vector field is what we call the **magnetic vector potential A**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of it this way:

| Electric Field E | Magnetic Field B |
|---|---|
| **Scalar potential V** (a single number at every point) | **Vector potential A** (a vector at every point) |
| $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ |
| Easy to visualize as contour lines (equipotentials) | Harder to visualize — has circulation around currents |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Picture — The "Whirlpool" Analogy:** Imagine dropping a leaf into water above an open drain. The leaf traces a circle, even though no one pushed it in a straight line. The water's motion is **curl-like** (circulating), and so is magnetic field **B** around a current-carrying wire. To describe this circulating motion mathematically, we don't track the leaf — we define a vector field **A** whose *curl* produces the swirling **B** field. A is a kind of "momentum map" of the swirl.

In real engineering: **A** is essential when dealing with **inductance calculations**, **antenna radiation theory**, and **quantum mechanics** (where the canonical momentum is $\vec{p} - q\vec{A}$).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants Referenced

- **$\mu_0$** = Permeability of free space = **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$**
- **$\epsilon_0$** = Permittivity of free space ≈ **$8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
