$$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\delta} - 1\right]^{1/2}$$

$$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times \sqrt{\frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(2.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12})}{2}} \times \left[\sqrt{1 + 0.719^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}$$

$$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times \sqrt{1.3906 \times 10^{-17}} \times \left[\sqrt{1.517} - 1\right]^{1/2}$$

$$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times 1.179 \times 10^{-8.5} \times [1.232 - 1]^{0.5}$$

$$\alpha = 7.41 \times 1.179 \times 10^{-8.5} \times 0.481 \approx 2.0 \; \text{Np/m}$$

**Step (c) — Phase constant $\beta$:**

$$\beta = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times 1.179 \times 10^{-8.5} \times [1.232 + 1]^{0.5}$$

$$\beta \approx 7.41 \times 1.179 \times 10^{-8.5} \times 1.497 \approx 6.23 \; \text{rad/m}$$

**Step (d) — Intrinsic impedance $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$$

The magnitude and phase can be computed as:

$$\vert\eta\vert = \frac{\sqrt{\omega\mu}}{\left( \sigma^2 + \omega^2\varepsilon^2 \right)^{1/4}} = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi \times 10^8 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}}{\left( 0.01^2 + 0.0139^2 \right)^{1/4}}$$

$$\vert\eta\vert = \frac{1.253}{\left( 2.93 \times 10^{-4} \right)^{1/4}} = \frac{1.253}{0.4135} \approx 219 \; \Omega$$

Phase: $\theta_\eta = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}(0.719) \approx 17.9°$

**Step (e) — Skin depth $\delta$:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2.0} = 0.5 \; \text{m}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EM Wave Propagation Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Plane EM Wave in Source-Free Medium"] --> B["Apply Maxwell Curl Equations in Phasor Form"]
    B --> C["Take Curl of Faraday's Law and Substitute Ampere's Law"]
    C --> D["Derive Helmholtz Wave Equation: nabla-squared E equals gamma-squared E"]
    D --> E["Identify Complex Propagation Constant gamma equals alpha plus j beta"]
    E --> F{"Classify Medium by Loss Tangent: sigma divided by omega epsilon"}
    F -->|sigma = 0| G["PERFECT DIELECTRIC BRANCH: gamma is purely imaginary; alpha equals 0; beta equals omega sqrt mu epsilon"]
    F -->|0 less than sigma less than infinity| H["LOSSY DIELECTRIC BRANCH: general alpha and beta formulas apply; intrinsic impedance is complex"]
    F -->|sigma very large| I["GOOD CONDUCTOR BRANCH: alpha equals beta equals sqrt pi f mu sigma; eta equals one plus j times alpha divided by sigma"]
    G --> J["Compute Phase Velocity and Wavelength in Lossless Medium"]
    H --> K["Compute Magnitude and Phase of Intrinsic Impedance"]
    I --> L["Compute Skin Depth: delta equals 1 divided by alpha"]
    J --> M["FINAL OUTPUT: Field Expressions E_z_t and H_y_t"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Field Orientation and Decay in a Good Conductor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Wave Incident at Surface z equals 0"] --> B["Surface Field E_0 and H_0"]
    B --> C["Penetration into Conductor: z increases"]
    C --> D["Skin Depth delta: amplitude falls to 1 over e"]
    D --> E["Amplitude at z equals 3 delta: about 5 percent of surface value"]
    E --> F["Amplitude at z equals 5 delta: about 0.7 percent of surface value - effectively zero"]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Flow of Attenuation Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Three Medium Types"] --> B["Perfect Dielectric: alpha equals 0, constant amplitude indefinitely"]
    A --> C["Lossy Medium: alpha greater than 0, gradual exponential decay"]
    A --> D["Good Conductor: alpha large, very rapid decay within thin skin depth delta"]
    B --> E["E field at z: E_0 cos omega t minus beta z"]
    C --> F["E field at z: E_0 exp minus alpha z cos omega t minus beta z"]
    D --> G["E field at z: E_0 exp minus z over delta cos omega t minus beta z"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Statement:** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `step1`) and contain no reserved keywords, bold/italic markers, or markdown tables inside the label quotes. The diagrams render the analytical decision flow rather than attempting to draw physical 3D wave fields, which is the recommended KTU note-taking approach.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term *intrinsic impedance* of a medium. What is its value for free space, and what does its complex nature in a lossy medium indicate?**

**Model Answer:**

The intrinsic impedance $\eta$ of a medium is defined as the ratio of the phasor electric field intensity to the phasor magnetic field intensity of a uniform plane wave traveling in the medium:

$$\eta = \frac{\vert\vec{E}_s\vert}{\vert\vec{H}_s\vert}$$

For free space: $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi \approx 377 \; \Omega$.

A complex $\eta$ (with both magnitude and phase $\theta_\eta \neq 0$) in a lossy medium indicates that the $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields are **out of phase** in time, which is the direct signature of energy dissipation in the medium. **(3 marks)**

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is *skin depth*? Write its expression for a good conductor and state how it varies with frequency and conductivity.**

**Model Answer:**

Skin depth $\delta$ is defined as the distance into a conducting medium at which the amplitude of an electromagnetic wave decays to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its value at the surface.

For a good conductor:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu\sigma}}$$

**Variations:**
- $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$ — *decreases* as frequency increases
- $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{\sigma}$ — *decreases* as conductivity increases

Hence, high-frequency signals in highly conductive materials (e.g., copper at GHz) are confined to a microscopically thin surface layer. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students are given an internal choice and must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the **Helmholtz wave equation** for the electric field in a source-free conducting medium. Define the complex propagation constant and identify its real and imaginary parts. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the phasor form of Maxwell's curl equations in a source-free, linear, isotropic, homogeneous medium with parameters $\mu$, $\varepsilon$, $\sigma$:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E}_s = -j\omega\mu\vec{H}_s \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H}_s = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}_s \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2:** Take the curl of equation (1):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}_s) = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H}_s)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{A}) - \nabla^2 \vec{A}$. For source-free media, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E}_s = 0$:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E}_s = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H}_s)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (2):

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E}_s = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}_s$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $-1$ and define the complex propagation constant $\gamma$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E}_s = \gamma^2 \vec{E}_s \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)} = \alpha + j\beta$$

Here, $\alpha$ is the **attenuation constant** (Np/m) governing exponential amplitude decay, and $\beta$ is the **phase constant** (rad/m) governing the spatial rate of phase variation. **(7 marks — full derivation as above)**

**(b)** For a plane wave propagating in a **lossless dielectric** with $\mu_r = 1$ and $\varepsilon_r = 4$, determine: (i) the phase velocity, (ii) the wavelength at 50 MHz, and (iii) the intrinsic impedance of the medium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $f = 50 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\sigma = 0$.

**Step (i) — Phase velocity:**

In a lossless dielectric, the phase velocity is:

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1 \times 4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step (ii) — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{50 \times 10^6} = 3 \; \text{m}$$

**Step (iii) — Intrinsic impedance:**

$$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0\mu_r}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{\eta_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{120\pi}{\sqrt{4}} = 60\pi \approx 188.5 \; \Omega$$

**(7 marks — clear labeled steps with correct numerical substitution)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **attenuation constant $\alpha$** and **phase constant $\beta$** for a plane wave propagating in a lossy dielectric medium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Begin with $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ and the squared form:

$$\gamma^2 = (\alpha + j\beta)^2 = \alpha^2 - \beta^2 + j2\alpha\beta$$

**Step 2:** Also express $\gamma^2$ from the definition:

$$\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon) = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon + j\omega\mu\sigma$$

**Step 3:** Equate real and imaginary parts:

$$\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon \quad \text{...(A)}$$

$$2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma \quad \text{...(B)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ using the identity:

$$\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \sqrt{(\alpha^2 - \beta^2)^2 + (2\alpha\beta)^2} = \omega\mu\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2}$$

**Step 5:** Solve the system: add and subtract (A) from the magnitude result:

$$2\alpha^2 = \omega\mu\left[\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2} - \omega\varepsilon\right]$$

$$2\beta^2 = \omega\mu\left[\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2} + \omega\varepsilon\right]$$

**Step 6:** Take square roots and factor out $\omega^2\varepsilon^2$ to obtain the final closed-form expressions:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}}$$

**(7 marks — derivation as above with each step explicitly justified)**

**(b)** A plane wave at 1 GHz propagates in **seawater** with $\mu_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_r = 80$, $\sigma = 4$ S/m. Calculate: (i) the loss tangent and identify the medium type, (ii) the skin depth, and (iii) the intrinsic impedance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 10^9$ Hz, $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^9$ rad/s, $\varepsilon_r = 80$, $\mu_r = 1$, $\sigma = 4$ S/m.

**Step (i) — Loss tangent:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 10^9 \times 80 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\tan\delta = \frac{4}{4.4506} \approx 0.899$$

Since $\tan\delta \approx 0.9$ (not very small, not very large), the medium is a **lossy dielectric** — use the general formulas.

**Step (ii) — Skin depth:**

Compute $\alpha$ first:

$$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\delta} - 1\right]^{1/2}$$

$$\sqrt{1 + 0.899^2} = \sqrt{1.808} = 1.345$$

$$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{\frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(80 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12})}{2}} \times \sqrt{1.345 - 1}$$

$$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{1.7795 \times 10^{-16}} \times \sqrt{0.345}$$

$$\alpha = 6.283 \times 10^9 \times 1.334 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5874 \approx 49.2 \; \text{Np/m}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{49.2} \approx 0.0203 \; \text{m} \approx 2.03 \; \text{cm}$$

**Step (iii) — Intrinsic impedance:**

$$\vert\eta\vert = \frac{\sqrt{\omega\mu}}{(\sigma^2 + \omega^2\varepsilon^2)^{1/4}} = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}}{(16 + 19.81)^{1/4}}$$

$$\vert\eta\vert = \frac{\sqrt{7895.7}}{(35.81)^{0.25}} = \frac{88.86}{2.446} \approx 36.3 \; \Omega$$

Phase: $\theta_\eta = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}(0.899) \approx 20.95°$

So $\eta \approx 36.3 \angle 21° \; \Omega$.

**(7 marks — substitution and reasoning as shown)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$ formulas:** The "minus" sign goes with $\alpha$ (attenuation), the "plus" sign with $\beta$ (phase). Reversing these is the **#1 most common error** in KTU answer scripts.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\omega$ correctly:** Always verify $\omega = 2\pi f$ and not just $f$. Many students lose 2-3 marks by writing $\omega = f$ in a numerical problem.
> 3. **Using the wrong approximation:** Students often blindly apply the *good conductor* formulas ($\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$) to a lossy dielectric. **Always compute the loss tangent first** to classify the medium.
> 4. **Confusing intrinsic impedance with characteristic impedance of a transmission line:** The intrinsic impedance $\eta$ is a *medium property*; the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ depends on the line geometry.
> 5. **Missing the $\pi$ factor:** In skin depth, it is $\pi f \mu \sigma$ under the square root, **not** $f \mu \sigma$ alone. Marks are deducted for the missing $\pi$.
> 6. **Skipping the time-domain conversion:** A complete answer should ideally present the final time-domain fields $E_x(z,t)$ and $H_y(z,t)$, not just the phasor forms. Examiners often award a "presentation" mark for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- [x] **Three medium classes** are defined by the loss tangent $\tan\delta = \sigma/\omega\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Perfect dielectric:** $\sigma = 0$, $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$, $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ (real).
- [x] **Lossy dielectric:** General formulas apply; $\eta$ becomes complex with phase $\theta_\eta = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)$.
- [x] **Good conductor:** $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$, $\eta = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$, $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$.
- [x] **The Helmholtz equation** $\nabla^2 \vec{E}_s = \gamma^2 \vec{E}_s$ is the **master equation** for all wave propagation in linear media.
- [x] **The intrinsic impedance of free space** is exactly $120\pi \approx 377 \; \Omega$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and **medium phase velocity** $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.
- [x] **Skin depth varies as $1/\sqrt{f}$ and $1/\sqrt{\sigma}$** — this is the key to understanding RF shielding, transformer losses, and microwave heating.
- [x] **In a good conductor, $\alpha = \beta$** — this equality is a "signature" of the good-conductor regime.
- [x] **Wavelength in any medium:** $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta = v_p/f$.
- [x] **Poynting vector** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ — the direction of energy flow; for a $+z$ propagating wave with $\vec{E} = E_x\hat{x}$ and $\vec{H} = H_y\hat{y}$, $\vec{S}$ points in $+\hat{z}$.
- [x] **Time-domain vs. phasor form:** Always remember the conversion $E(z,t) = \text{Re}\{E_s e^{j\omega t}\}$.
- [x] **Units to remember:** $\alpha$ in Np/m, $\beta$ in rad/m, $\eta$ in $\Omega$, $\delta$ in m, $\gamma$ in m$^{-1}$.
- [x] **The conductor/dielectric boundary** is conventionally $\tan\delta = 1$, i.e., $\sigma = \omega\varepsilon$.
- [x] **For numerical problems**, always (1) compute $\tan\delta$, (2) classify the medium, (3) choose the correct formula set, (4) substitute carefully, (5) present results with proper units.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Propagation of Plane EM Wave in Perfect Dielectric, Lossy Medium, and Good Conductor.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Media Attenuation, Phase Velocity, Group Velocity, and Skin Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Media Attenuation, Phase Velocity, Group Velocity, and Skin Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Media Attenuation (Attenuation Constant, $\alpha$)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Media attenuation refers to the progressive reduction in the amplitude (and hence intensity) of an electromagnetic wave as it propagates through a real (lossy) material medium. Mathematically, for a lossy medium characterized by conductivity $\sigma$, permittivity $\varepsilon$, and permeability $\mu$, the wave's electric field decays exponentially as:

$$E(z, t) = E_0 \, e^{-\alpha z} \, \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$$

where the **attenuation constant $\alpha$** (measured in **Np/m**, Nepers per meter) quantifies the rate of exponential decay of the field amplitude per unit distance traveled by the wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, students must master the distinction between the **attenuation constant ($\alpha$)** and the **phase constant ($\beta$)**. They emerge as the real and imaginary parts of the complex propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$, which is the cornerstone of wave propagation in lossy media.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine shouting across a foggy football stadium. The further your voice travels, the more the fog particles (water droplets) absorb and scatter your sound energy, making it quieter at greater distances. Media attenuation in EM waves is exactly the same: the "fog" is the lossy medium (free electrons, dipoles, or polar molecules) that absorbs energy from the propagating wave. A perfect vacuum (or lossless dielectric with $\sigma = 0$) is like a perfectly silent, clear night — your voice carries forever. A highly conductive metal (e.g., copper) is like dense, thick fog — the wave dies out in microns.

---

### 1.2 Phase Velocity ($v_p$)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Phase velocity is the speed at which a surface of constant phase (a wavefront) of a monochromatic sinusoidal wave propagates through a given medium. It is defined as:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} \quad \text{(in lossless media)}$$

In free space, $v_p = c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$, where $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a perfectly synchronized marching band in a parade. Every band member takes one step per second (frequency $f$). The "crest" of the band's height pattern (the tall people) moves down the street at a certain speed — that is the **phase velocity**. It is the speed at which an *individual ripple* of the wave appears to travel, not the speed of physical energy transfer.

---

### 1.3 Group Velocity ($v_g$)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Group velocity is the speed at which the overall envelope (or "shape") of a wave packet — a localized superposition of many frequencies — propagates through a medium. It is formally defined as the derivative of angular frequency with respect to the phase constant:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta}$$

In a non-dispersive medium, $v_p = v_g = c/n$, where $n$ is the refractive index. In a **dispersive medium** (where $\beta$ is a nonlinear function of $\omega$), $v_p \neq v_g$, and it is $v_g$ that governs the speed of *energy* and *information* transport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Examiners:** Signals and energy travel at the **group velocity**, not the phase velocity. A classic KTU trick question asks: "If $v_p > c$, does this violate relativity?" — The answer is **No**, because in such cases $v_g \leq c$ is what preserves causality.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine throwing a small stone into a pond. The "splash" (the wave packet envelope) travels outward at one speed — that is the **group velocity**. But the individual ripples within the splash (the phases) move slightly faster or slower relative to the splash itself — those are the phase velocities. In water, the group is the recognizable "bump," and it moves slower than the internal ripples. The bump is what a small floating leaf bobs up on; that is the *energy* transfer speed.

---

### 1.4 Skin Depth ($\delta$)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
The **skin depth** (or penetration depth) of a medium is the distance measured from the boundary (surface) into the conductor at which the amplitude of an electromagnetic wave has decayed to $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ of its value at the surface. Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of the attenuation constant:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

For a **good conductor** (where $\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$), this simplifies to the well-known expression:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine dropping a sponge into a pool of dark ink: only the outermost layer of the sponge gets deeply stained, while the inner core remains relatively clean. Skin depth is the "ink penetration depth" of an EM wave into a conductor. That is why, at high frequencies, current effectively flows only in a very thin outer shell of a wire — the interior is largely unused, which is why hollow copper tubes are just as effective as solid copper rods for high-frequency applications like waveguides and RF transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** At $\mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$ mains frequency, the skin depth in copper is roughly **9.3 mm**, but at **5 GHz** (Wi-Fi/microwave), it shrinks to about **0.93 μm** — almost 10,000× thinner. This single fact underlies the design of every antenna, microwave oven cavity, and RFID tag in existence.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8}$ m/s |
| Free-space permeability | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| Free-space permittivity | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta_0$ | $376.73 \approx 120\pi \, \Omega$ |
| Conductivity of copper | $\sigma_{Cu}$ | $5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m |
| Conductivity of seawater | $\sigma_{sw}$ | $\approx 4$ S/m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Complex Propagation Constant — The Foundation

When an EM wave propagates through a homogeneous, isotropic, lossy medium (with $\sigma \neq 0$, $\mu$, $\varepsilon$), Maxwell's curl equations lead to the **Helmholtz wave equation** for the phasor electric field $\mathbf{E}$:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \, \mathbf{E}$$

where the **complex propagation constant** is:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{j \omega \mu (\sigma + j \omega \varepsilon)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation for Module 3:** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$

This single equation governs **all** lossy media — from perfect dielectrics to perfect conductors. The two limiting cases below are what examiners love to test.

---

### 2.2 Case Analysis: Good Dielectric vs. Good Conductor

**Case A — Lossless Dielectric ($\sigma = 0$):**

$$\alpha = 0, \quad \beta = \omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}, \quad v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$$

No attenuation. The wave propagates undamped.

**Case B — Good Dielectric (Low-Loss, $\sigma \ll \omega \varepsilon$):**

$$\alpha \approx \frac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}, \quad \beta \approx \omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}$$

Attenuation is small but non-zero. This is the regime of typical insulating materials at microwave frequencies (e.g., dry plastics, low-loss ceramics like alumina, FR-4 substrate at low GHz).

**Case C — Good Conductor (High-Loss, $\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$):**

$$\alpha \approx \beta \approx \sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

Attenuation is huge. This is the regime of all metals (copper, aluminum, silver, gold) at radio and microwave frequencies.

---

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Parameter

- **Why does $\alpha$ depend on $\sigma$?** Because free charges in the medium act as energy sinks — they absorb wave energy and dissipate it as heat (Joule heating, $I^2R$ losses). Higher conductivity = more free charges = more attenuation.
- **Why does $\beta$ depend on $\omega$ and $\mu\varepsilon$?** Because $\beta$ governs how rapidly the wave oscillates in space, which depends on the wave's frequency and the medium's refractive properties.
- **Why is $\alpha = \beta$ in a good conductor?** Physically, this is because the conduction current dominates so strongly that the displacement current becomes negligible, forcing the in-phase and quadrature components of the field to decay at the same rate.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorize units carefully. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value to avoid table-breaking pipes.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Applies To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Complex propagation constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$ | 1/m | All lossy media |
| 2 | Attenuation const. (lossless) | $\alpha = 0$ | Np/m | $\sigma = 0$ |
| 3 | Phase const. (lossless) | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | rad/m | $\sigma = 0$ |
| 4 | Attenuation const. (good dielectric) | $\alpha \approx \frac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ | Np/m | $\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon$ |
| 5 | Attenuation const. (good conductor) | $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Np/m | $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$ |
| 6 | Phase const. (good conductor) | $\beta \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | rad/m | $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$ |
| 7 | Phase velocity (general) | $v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta}$ | m/s | Any medium |
| 8 | Group velocity (general) | $v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta}$ | m/s | Any medium |
| 9 | Skin depth (general) | $\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ | m | Any medium |
| 10 | Skin depth (good conductor) | $\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | m | $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$ |
| 11 | Refractive index | $n = \frac{c}{v_p} = \sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$ | dimensionless | Lossless |
| 12 | Product relation (non-dispersive) | $v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c^2}{n^2}$ | m²/s² | Non-dispersive media |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Phenomena Used |
|---|---|
| **Coaxial cable design** | $\alpha$ sets signal loss per meter; skin depth limits current to outer conductor at high $f$ |
| **Antenna radiation efficiency** | Skin depth determines how much RF current flows in antenna metal |
| **Microwave oven** | Penetration depth in food (~1.5 cm at 2.45 GHz) determines heating uniformity |
| **RFID / NFC tags** | Skin effect at 13.56 MHz / 900 MHz limits tag thickness and read range |
| **Optical fiber communications** | Group velocity dispersion (GVD) limits bandwidth-distance product |
| **Underwater / underground radar** | Attenuation in seawater/soil dictates usable frequency window |
| **EMI shielding** | Skin depth of shield material at the interference frequency must be < shield thickness |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in a Lossy Medium

**Starting Point:** Helmholtz equation for the phasor electric field $\mathbf{E}$ in a lossy, source-free, homogeneous medium:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{E} = \gamma^2 \mathbf{E}, \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma^2 = j \omega \mu (\sigma + j \omega \varepsilon)$$

Let us expand $\gamma$:

$$\gamma^2 = j \omega \mu \sigma + j^2 \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon + j \omega \mu \sigma$$

We now set $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ and square it:

$$(\alpha + j\beta)^2 = \alpha^2 - \beta^2 + j(2\alpha\beta)$$

Equating real and imaginary parts of $\gamma^2$:

$$\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$2 \alpha \beta = \omega \mu \sigma \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Solving the system:** Square both equations and add them:

$$(\alpha^2 - \beta^2)^2 + (2\alpha\beta)^2 = \omega^4 \mu^2 \varepsilon^2 + \omega^2 \mu^2 \sigma^2$$

$$(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2 = \omega^2 \mu^2 (\omega^2 \varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2)$$

$$\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \omega \mu \sqrt{\omega^2 \varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2} \quad \text{...(III)}$$

Subtract (I) from (III):

$$2\beta^2 = \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon + \omega \mu \sqrt{\omega^2 \varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2}$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{2}} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1 \right]^{1/2}}$$

Similarly, add (I) to (III):

$$2\alpha^2 = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon + \omega \mu \sqrt{\omega^2 \varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{2}} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1 \right]^{1/2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two boxed equations are the **general, exact** expressions for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in **any** homogeneous lossy medium. The "good dielectric" and "good conductor" cases are approximations derived by applying the appropriate limit to $\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Good-Conductor Approximation

In a good conductor, $\frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon} \gg 1$. Therefore:

$$\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}\right)^2} \approx \frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}$$

Substituting into the general expression for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{2}} \left[ \frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon} - 1 \right]^{1/2} \approx \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}}$$

$$\alpha \approx \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

A nearly identical calculation gives $\beta \approx \alpha$:

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx \beta \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Skin Depth

By definition, the skin depth $\delta$ is the distance over which the field amplitude decays by a factor of $\frac{1}{e}$:

$$E(\delta) = E_0 \, e^{-\alpha \delta} = \frac{E_0}{e} \implies e^{-\alpha \delta} = e^{-1} \implies \alpha \delta = 1$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}}$$

For a good conductor:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Skin Depth of Copper at 1 MHz

**Given:** $f = 1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, $\mu \approx \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m.

**Step 1:** Compute the product $f \mu \sigma$:

$$f \mu \sigma = (10^6)(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(5.8 \times 10^7) = 4\pi \times 5.8 \times 10^6$$

$$= 22.4 \pi \times 10^6 \approx 7.286 \times 10^7$$

**Step 2:** Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = \sqrt{\pi \times 7.286 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{2.288 \times 10^8} \approx 1.512 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Invert to get $\delta$:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{1.512 \times 10^4} \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = \mathbf{66.1 \, \mu m}$$

So at 1 MHz, an EM wave penetrates copper only about **66 micrometers** — roughly the thickness of a human hair. This is why high-frequency circuits use **plated** rather than solid copper.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Regimes

| Property | Lossless Dielectric ($\sigma = 0$) | Good Dielectric ($\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon$) | Good Conductor ($\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | $0$ | $\frac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ | $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ |
| $\beta$ | $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ |
| $v_p$ | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{2\omega}{\mu\sigma}}$ |
| $\eta$ (intrinsic) | $\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$ (approx.) | $(1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu}{2\sigma}}$ |
| Skin depth $\delta$ | $\infty$ | $\frac{2}{\sigma}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon}{\mu}}$ | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ |
| Example | Air, vacuum, perfect insulator | Dry wood, dry plastic, FR-4 | Copper, silver, aluminum, gold |

---

### 3.6 Mathematical Connection Between $v_p$ and $v_g$

Given $v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta}$, we have $\beta = \frac{\omega}{v_p}$, and therefore:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta} = \frac{1}{\frac{d\beta}{d\omega}} = \frac{1}{\frac{d}{d\omega}\left(\frac{\omega}{v_p}\right)} = \frac{v_p}{1 - \frac{\omega}{v_p}\frac{dv_p}{d\omega}}$$

This is the **fundamental dispersion relation**:

$$\boxed{v_g = \frac{v_p}{1 - \frac{\omega}{v_p}\frac{dv_p}{d\omega}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **If $v_p$ is constant w.r.t. $\omega$** (non-dispersive medium), then $\frac{dv_p}{d\omega} = 0$ and $v_g = v_p$. This is the case in vacuum and lossless dielectrics at low frequencies.
>
> **If $v_p$ depends on $\omega$** (dispersive medium), then $v_g \neq v_p$. This is the case in optical fibers, plasmas, and at frequencies near material resonances.

For a lossless dielectric where $v_p = \frac{c}{n}$ with $n = \sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$, the relation $v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c^2}{n^2}$ follows directly. In free space, $n = 1$, so $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$. Since $v_p = c$, we recover $v_g = c$ — as expected.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Signal Propagation in a Lossy Medium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source EM Wave E0 cos wt] --> B[Couples Into Lossy Medium]
    B --> C[Complex Propagation Constant gamma equals alpha plus j beta]
    C --> D1[Real Part alpha = Attenuation Constant Np per m]
    C --> D2[Imag Part beta = Phase Constant rad per m]
    D1 --> E[Exponential Decay envelope E0 exp -alpha z]
    D2 --> F[Sinusoidal Phase Rotation cos wt minus beta z]
    E --> G[Combined Field E z t]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Sigma compared to omega epsilon}
    H -->|sigma much greater| I1[Good Conductor: alpha ~ sqrt pi f mu sigma]
    H -->|sigma much less| I2[Good Dielectric: alpha ~ sigma over 2 sqrt mu over epsilon]
    I1 --> J1[Skin Depth delta equals 1 over alpha ~ 1 to 100 microns]
    I2 --> J2[Long Range Propagation: delta in meters or km]
    G --> K[Phase Velocity vp = omega over beta]
    G --> L[Group Velocity vg = d omega over d beta]
    K --> M{Dispersive Medium?}
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N1[vp not equal to vg: signal distortion]
    M -->|No| N2[vp equals vg: undistorted signal]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture: How Attenuation, Phase, and Group Velocities Interact

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Wave Packet]
        W1[Localized EM Pulse]
    end

    subgraph MEDIUM[Lossy Dispersive Medium]
        ENV[Envelope travels at vg]
        PHASE[Internal phase rotates at vp]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Attenuated Output]
        OUT1[Reduced amplitude exp -alpha z]
        OUT2[Phase-shifted waveform]
    end

    W1 --> ENV
    W1 --> PHASE
    ENV --> OUT1
    PHASE --> OUT2
    PHASE -.->|Modulates| ENV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this diagram:** The original input is a localized wave packet. As it enters the lossy dispersive medium, two distinct things happen: the *envelope* (overall shape) travels at group velocity $v_g$ and decays exponentially via $\alpha$, while the *internal carrier phase* propagates at $v_p$. The phase component continuously modulates the envelope. The output is therefore both attenuated and phase-shifted relative to the input.

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: Skin Depth Across Frequencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Frequency f] --> F1{Conductivity of Material}
    F1 -->|Copper sigma = 5.8e7 S/m| S1[At 50 Hz: delta ~ 9.3 mm]
    F1 -->|Copper sigma = 5.8e7 S/m| S2[At 1 MHz: delta ~ 66 microns]
    F1 -->|Copper sigma = 5.8e7 S/m| S3[At 5 GHz: delta ~ 0.93 microns]
    F1 -->|Seawater sigma = 4 S/m| S4[At 1 kHz: delta ~ 250 m]
    F1 -->|Seawater sigma = 4 S/m| S5[At 1 MHz: delta ~ 8 m]
    F1 -->|Seawater sigma = 4 S/m| S6[At 1 GHz: delta ~ 0.25 m]
    S1 --> USE1[Used in Power Line Design]
    S2 --> USE2[Used in AM Radio Coils]
    S3 --> USE3[Used in Microwave Cavities]
    S4 --> USE4[Used in Submarine Communication]
    S5 --> USE5[Used in Ocean Radar]
    S6 --> USE6[Used in Marine Microwave Links]
```

---

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Maxwell's Equations to Skin Depth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Maxwell Curl Equations] --> M2[Apply to Lossy Medium: J = sigma E]
    M2 --> M3[Helmholtz Wave Equation: del squared E = gamma squared E]
    M3 --> M4[Define gamma squared = j omega mu sigma + j squared omega squared mu epsilon]
    M4 --> M5[Set gamma = alpha + j beta]
    M5 --> M6[Equate Real and Imaginary Parts]
    M6 --> M7[General alpha and beta Formulas]
    M7 --> M8[Apply Good Conductor Limit sigma >> omega epsilon]
    M8 --> M9[Skin Depth delta = 1 over alpha = 1 over sqrt pi f mu sigma]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the terms (i) attenuation constant, (ii) phase constant, and (iii) skin depth. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Attenuation constant ($\alpha$):** It is the real part of the complex propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ in a lossy medium. It measures the rate at which the amplitude of an EM wave decays per unit distance of propagation. **SI unit: Neper per meter (Np/m).**

(ii) **Phase constant ($\beta$):** It is the imaginary part of the complex propagation constant. It measures the rate of change of phase of the wave per unit distance. **SI unit: radian per meter (rad/m).**

(iii) **Skin depth ($\delta$):** It is defined as the depth into a conductor at which the amplitude of an EM wave decays to $\frac{1}{e}$ of its value at the surface. Mathematically, $\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$. **SI unit: meter (m).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Distinguish between phase velocity and group velocity. In which medium do they become equal?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Phase Velocity $v_p$ | Group Velocity $v_g$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Speed of a constant-phase point (wavefront) of a monochromatic wave | Speed of the envelope of a wave packet (group of frequencies) |
| Formula | $v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta}$ | $v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta}$ |
| Physical meaning | Speed of an individual ripple inside the wave | Speed of the overall wave shape, i.e., energy & information transport |
| Equal to? | Equal to $v_g$ in **non-dispersive** media | Equal to $v_p$ in **non-dispersive** media |

In a **non-dispersive medium** such as free space (vacuum) or a perfect lossless dielectric (e.g., air at low frequencies), the phase velocity and group velocity are **equal** because $\beta$ is linearly proportional to $\omega$ (i.e., $\frac{d\beta}{d\omega}$ is constant). In dispersive media (e.g., optical fibers, plasmas, lossy media), $v_p \neq v_g$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]

**(a)** Starting from the complex propagation constant $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$, derive the expressions for the attenuation constant $\alpha$ and phase constant $\beta$ for a lossy dielectric medium. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a good conductor, show that the skin depth is given by $\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$. A flat copper plate ($\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0$) is to be used as an EMI shield for a 1 GHz circuit. Calculate the minimum thickness of the plate required to provide at least 60 dB of attenuation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Starting from:

$$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$

We write $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$. Squaring:

$$\gamma^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon\left(1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right) = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon + j\omega\mu\sigma$$

Equating with $(\alpha + j\beta)^2 = \alpha^2 - \beta^2 + 2j\alpha\beta$:

**Real part:** $\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = -\omega^2\mu\varepsilon$ … (I)

**Imaginary part:** $2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma$ … (II)

Squaring and adding (I) and (II):

$$(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)^2 = \omega^4\mu^2\varepsilon^2 + \omega^2\mu^2\sigma^2$$

$$\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \omega\mu\sqrt{\omega^2\varepsilon^2 + \sigma^2}$$

Solving the system (adding/subtracting with (I)):

$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]^{1/2}$$

$$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]^{1/2}$$

These are the **general, exact** expressions valid for any lossy medium. **(7 Marks — full marks for deriving both expressions explicitly and identifying the limiting conditions.)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

For a good conductor, $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$, so $\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} \approx \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$, and:

$$\alpha \approx \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

Skin depth: $\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$. **(3 Marks derived)**

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $f = 10^9$ Hz, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

$$\pi f \mu \sigma = \pi \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7$$

$$= 4\pi^2 \times 5.8 \times 10^9 = 4 \times (9.8696) \times 5.8 \times 10^9$$

$$= 22.9 \times 10^{10} = 2.29 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.29 \times 10^{11}}} = \frac{1}{4.78 \times 10^5} \approx 2.09 \, \mu m$$

**60 dB attenuation** means power reduces by factor of $10^6$, which corresponds to field amplitude reducing by factor of $10^3 = 1000$.

Required: $e^{-\alpha t} = \frac{1}{1000} \implies \alpha t = \ln(1000) = 3 \ln 10 \approx 6.908$.

$$t = \frac{6.908}{\alpha} = 6.908 \times \delta = 6.908 \times 2.09 \, \mu m$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 14.4 \, \mu m}$$

So a copper plate of just **~15 μm thickness** is sufficient to provide 60 dB shielding at 1 GHz — a striking demonstration of skin effect. **(4 Marks for numerical answer.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between phase velocity and group velocity. Show that for a non-dispersive medium, $v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c^2}{n^2}$, where $n$ is the refractive index. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A radar system operating at 10 GHz uses a wave propagating through a humid atmosphere modeled as a lossy dielectric with $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$, and loss tangent $\tan\delta = 0.05$. Calculate: (i) the attenuation constant $\alpha$, (ii) the phase velocity $v_p$, and (iii) the distance at which the field strength drops to 1% of its value at the transmitter. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Given $v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta}$, we have $\beta = \frac{\omega}{v_p}$. Differentiating $v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta}$:

$$v_g = \frac{1}{\frac{d\beta}{d\omega}} = \frac{v_p}{1 - \frac{\omega}{v_p}\frac{dv_p}{d\omega}} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

In a non-dispersive medium, $v_p$ is independent of $\omega$, so $\frac{dv_p}{d\omega} = 0$ and $v_g = v_p = \frac{c}{n}$. Therefore:

$$v_p \cdot v_g = v_p^2 = \left(\frac{c}{n}\right)^2 = \frac{c^2}{n^2}$$

This proves the required relation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $f = 10^{10}$ Hz, $\varepsilon_r = 4$, $\mu_r = 1$, $\tan\delta = 0.05$.

First, check regime: $\omega\varepsilon = 2\pi \times 10^{10} \times 4 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{0}$ S/m, and $\sigma = \omega\varepsilon\tan\delta \approx 0.111$ S/m. So $\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon$ — **good dielectric** approximation is valid. **(1 Mark for regime identification)**

**(i) Attenuation constant:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} = \frac{\sigma}{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\mu_r}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\cdot\frac{1}{\eta_0}$$

Using $\eta_0 = 120\pi \, \Omega \approx 377 \, \Omega$:

$$\alpha = \frac{0.111}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{377} = \frac{0.111}{1508} \approx 7.36 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Np/m}$$

**(ii) Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Distance for 1% field strength:**

$$E(z) = E_0 e^{-\alpha z} = 0.01 E_0 \implies \alpha z = \ln(100) = 4.605$$

$$z = \frac{4.605}{7.36 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 6.26 \times 10^4 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{62.6 \text{ km}}$$

**(3 Marks for calculations — full method marks as long as correct approach is shown.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 3 Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check the regime** ($\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon$ vs. $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$) before applying the good-dielectric or good-conductor formula. **Always compute the loss tangent first.** (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing Np/m with dB/m** for attenuation. Conversion: $1 \text{ Np} = 8.686 \text{ dB}$. If a question states attenuation in dB, students must convert to Np/m *before* computing $\alpha$ in SI units. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Writing $\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ for all media.** This is true in general, but the *useful* formula $\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ is valid **only** in good conductors. In good dielectrics, $\delta = \frac{2}{\sigma}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon}{\mu}}$. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Saying $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ unconditionally.** This holds **only in free space**. In a dielectric, it is $\frac{c^2}{n^2}$. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing up phase velocity with signal velocity.** Examiners specifically test whether students understand that $v_g$ (not $v_p$) is the velocity of energy/information transport. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 6. **Forgetting to square $\mu$ correctly when computing $\delta$ from a partially given $\mu_r$.** Students often write $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ in the wrong place, leading to errors of orders of magnitude. (-2 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Year KTU 2024 Module 3 Rapid-Fire Revision List:**

- [ ] **Attenuation constant $\alpha$** is the real part of $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$, and its units are **Np/m**.
- [ ] **Phase constant $\beta$** is the imaginary part of $\gamma$, units **rad/m**.
- [ ] General formula: $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- [ ] For **lossless dielectric** ($\sigma = 0$): $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.
- [ ] For **good dielectric** ($\sigma \ll \omega\varepsilon$): $\alpha \approx \frac{\sigma}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}$, $\beta \approx \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.
- [ ] For **good conductor** ($\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$): $\alpha \approx \beta \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$.
- [ ] **Phase velocity** $v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta}$ — speed of wavefront (constant-phase point).
- [ ] **Group velocity** $v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta}$ — speed of wave envelope (energy/information).
- [ ] In **non-dispersive media**, $v_p = v_g$.
- [ ] In **dispersive media** (e.g., lossy dielectrics, optical fibers), $v_p \neq v_g$.
- [ ] For lossless dielectrics: $v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c^2}{n^2}$. For free space: $v_p = v_g = c$.
- [ ] **Skin depth** $\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ (general); $\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ (good conductor).
- [ ] Skin depth **decreases** with increasing frequency as $\delta \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$.
- [ ] Skin depth **decreases** with increasing conductivity as $\delta \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma}}$.
- [ ] At **high frequencies**, current flows only in a thin outer shell of a conductor — this is the **skin effect**.
- [ ] **Conversion factor**: 1 Np = 8.686 dB.
- [ ] Attenuation in **dB/m** $= 8.686 \alpha$ (where $\alpha$ is in Np/m).
- [ ] Always **check the regime** by computing $\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$ **first** before applying any formula.
- [ ] **Refractive index** $n = \frac{c}{v_p} = \sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$ (lossless case).
- [ ] **Real-world examples to remember:** Copper at 1 MHz → $\delta \approx 66 \, \mu m$; Copper at 5 GHz → $\delta \approx 0.93 \, \mu m$; Seawater at 1 kHz → $\delta \approx 250 \, m$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection and Refraction of Plane Electromagnetic Waves at Boundaries for Normal and Oblique Incidence (Parallel and Perpendicular Polarization)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reflection and Refraction of Plane Electromagnetic Waves at Boundaries for Normal and Oblique Incidence (Parallel and Perpendicular Polarization) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition When a **plane electromagnetic wave** propagating through one medium encounters a planar boundary with a second medium, the wave generally splits into two components: 1. A **reflected wave** that bounces back into the original medium. 2. A **refracted (transmitted) wave** that penetrates the second medium, with a changed direction of propagation. The mathematical characterization of this phenomenon is governed by the **boundary conditions** for the electric and magnetic field vectors at the interface, resulting in the **Fresnel Equations**. For oblique incidence, the behavior of the wave depends critically on the **orientation of the electric field vector (E)** relative to the **plane of incidence** (the plane containing the incident wave vector and the surface normal). This gives rise to two canonical polarization states: - **Perpendicular Polarization (TE / s-polarization):** The electric field is **perpendicular** to the plane of incidence (parallel to the boundary surface). - **Parallel Polarization (TM / p-polarization):** The electric field is **parallel** to the plane of incidence (lies within it). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plane of Incidence Definition:** The plane formed by the **incident wave propagation vector** (k_i) and the **surface normal vector** (n̂) at the point of incidence. All relevant vectors (incident, reflected, refracted k-vectors) lie in this plane. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a light beam (a small "wave packet" of EM energy) travelling through air and hitting the surface of a swimming pool at an angle. - Some of the light **bounces back** like a ball off a wall (reflection). - Some of the light **enters the water**, but its path **bends** because water is "denser" optically (refraction). Now, imagine that the light is not just a single beam, but a vibrating electric field pointing in some direction. Depending on whether this vibration is **sideways** or **up-down** relative to the water's surface, the boundary "responds" differently: - The **sideways-vibrating** wave (perpendicular polarization) has one set of bounce/penetration rules. - The **up-down-vibrating** wave (parallel polarization) follows a different set of rules, and at one special angle (**Brewster's angle**), it can pass through with **zero reflection** — like magic sunglasses that eliminate glare! > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?:** Polarized sunglasses exploit the **Brewster's angle** of reflected glare from water/roads. The reflected light is strongly polarized parallel to the surface (perpendicular polarization), so the sunglasses block this glare. Radar systems, optical fibers, and antenna radomes all depend on these reflection/refraction laws. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | **≈ 377 Ω (ohms)** | | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ | Intrinsic impedance of a medium | ohms (Ω) | | $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | **≈ 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** | | $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** | | $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | **≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s** | > [!IMPORTANT] > For a **lossless dielectric medium**, the intrinsic impedance is purely real: $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon} = \eta_0 \sqrt{\mu_r/\varepsilon_r}$, and the wave number is $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometry of Wave Interaction at a Boundary Consider two lossless dielectric media: - **Medium 1** (region $z < 0$): permittivity $\varepsilon_1$, permeability $\mu_1$, intrinsic impedance $\eta_1$, refractive index $n_1$. - **Medium 2** (region $z > 0$): permittivity $\varepsilon_2$, permeability $\mu_2$, intrinsic impedance $\eta_2$, refractive index $n_2$. A plane wave in Medium 1 strikes the planar boundary at angle $\theta_i$ (measured from the **normal**). The reflected wave departs at angle $\theta_r$, and the transmitted (refracted) wave continues at angle $\theta_t$. ### 2.1.1 Laws of Reflection and Refraction (Snell's Law) - **Law of Reflection:** $\theta_i = \theta_r$ - **Snell's Law:** $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$, equivalently $k_1 \sin\theta_i = k_2 \sin\theta_t$. ## 2.2 Normal Incidence Analysis For normal incidence ($\theta_i = 0$): $$ \Gamma = \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1} \quad \text{(Reflection Coefficient)} $$ $$ \tau = \frac{2\eta_2}{\eta_2 + \eta_1} \quad \text{(Transmission Coefficient for E-field)} $$ The reflection coefficient for the magnetic field is $-\Gamma$, and the magnetic transmission coefficient is $\tau_m = \frac{2\eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}$. **Power Reflection and Transmission Coefficients:** $$ R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \left(\frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_2 + \eta_1}\right)^2 $$ $$ T = \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \vert \tau \vert^2 = \frac{4\eta_1 \eta_2}{(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2} $$ **Energy Conservation:** $R + T = 1$. ## 2.3 Oblique Incidence — Perpendicular Polarization (TE / s-polarization) The electric field is perpendicular to the plane of incidence. Applying the boundary conditions (continuity of tangential E and tangential H at $z=0$) yields the **Fresnel reflection and transmission coefficients for perpendicular polarization**: $$ \Gamma_\perp = \frac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t} $$ $$ \tau_\perp = \frac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t} $$ **Special case (non-magnetic media, $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_0$):** Using $n = \sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$ and $n \cos\theta = \eta_0 \cos\theta/\eta$ (substituting Snell's law): $$ \Gamma_\perp = \frac{\cos\theta_i - \sqrt{(\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1) - \sin^2\theta_i}}{\cos\theta_i + \sqrt{(\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1) - \sin^2\theta_i}} $$ ## 2.4 Oblique Incidence — Parallel Polarization (TM / p-polarization) The electric field lies in the plane of incidence. The corresponding Fresnel coefficients are: $$ \Gamma_\parallel = \frac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i} $$ $$ \tau_\parallel = \frac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i} $$ **Special case (non-magnetic media):** $$ \Gamma_\parallel = \frac{(\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1)\cos\theta_i - \sqrt{(\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1) - \sin^2\theta_i}}{(\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1)\cos\theta_i + \sqrt{(\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1) - \sin^2\theta_i}} $$ ## 2.5 Brewster's Angle (Polarization Angle) The angle of incidence at which the reflection coefficient becomes **zero** for a specific polarization. - **For parallel polarization (TM):** $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1}\right) = \tan^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$. - **For perpendicular polarization (TE):** Brewster's angle does **not** exist in the lossless dielectric case (no real-angle solution). > [!NOTE] > At Brewster's angle for TM polarization, the **reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular** to each other ($\theta_t + \theta_B = 90°$). This is the basis of laser windows, optical isolators, and glare-reducing lenses. ## 2.6 Total Internal Reflection (TIR) Occurs when the wave travels from a **denser** to a **rarer** medium ($n_1 > n_2$) and the angle of incidence exceeds the **critical angle**: $$ \theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1) = \sin^{-1}\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1} \quad \text{(for non-magnetic media)} $$ For $\theta_i > \theta_c$, $\sin\theta_t > 1$, leading to an **evanescent wave** in Medium 2 that decays exponentially with distance from the interface. The reflection coefficient magnitude becomes **unity** ($\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$), and the wave is **completely reflected** back into Medium 1. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k_1 \sin\theta_i = k_2 \sin\theta_t$ | Snell's Law (vector form) | Any two media | | 2 | $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$ | Snell's Law (refractive index) | Non-magnetic ($\mu_1 = \mu_2$) | | 3 | $\Gamma = (\eta_2 - \eta_1)/(\eta_2 + \eta_1)$ | Normal incidence reflection coeff. | $E$-field parallel to boundary | | 4 | $\tau = 2\eta_2/(\eta_2 + \eta_1)$ | Normal incidence transmission coeff. (E) | $E$-field parallel to boundary | | 5 | $R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ | Power reflection coefficient | Energy ratio | | 6 | $T = (4\eta_1 \eta_2)/(\eta_1 + \eta_2)^2$ | Power transmission coefficient (normal) | $R + T = 1$ | | 7 | $\Gamma_\perp = (\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t)/(\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t)$ | TE/Perpendicular pol. Γ | Oblique incidence | | 8 | $\tau_\perp = (2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i)/(\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t)$ | TE/Perpendicular pol. τ | Oblique incidence | | 9 | $\Gamma_\parallel = (\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i)/(\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i)$ | TM/Parallel pol. Γ | Oblique incidence | | 10 | $\tau_\parallel = (2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i)/(\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i)$ | TM/Parallel pol. τ | Oblique incidence | | 11 | $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1}$ | Brewster's angle (TM) | Non-magnetic dielectrics | | 12 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1}$ | Critical angle | $n_1 > n_2$, non-magnetic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are central to the design of **optical fiber communication systems** (TIR for guiding light), **anti-reflection coatings** on lenses (destructive interference in thin films), **laser cavities** (Brewster windows for polarized output), and **radar-absorbing materials** (matched impedance for $R \to 0$). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Fresnel Equations for Perpendicular Polarization (TE) ### Setup Let the plane of incidence be the **xz-plane**. The boundary is the $z = 0$ plane. Medium 1 occupies $z < 0$, and Medium 2 occupies $z > 0$. For **perpendicular polarization**, $\mathbf{E}$ is in the **y-direction** (perpendicular to the plane of incidence): **Incident wave** (travelling in +x, +z direction): $$ \mathbf{E}_i = \hat{y} E_{i0} e^{-j(k_{1x}x + k_{1z}z)} $$ $$ \mathbf{H}_i = \frac{E_{i0}}{\eta_1} \left(-\hat{x}\cos\theta_i + \hat{z}\sin\theta_i\right) e^{-j(k_{1x}x + k_{1z}z)} $$ **Reflected wave** (travelling in +x, –z direction): $$ \mathbf{E}_r = \hat{y} E_{r0} e^{-j(k_{1x}x - k_{1z}z)} $$ $$ \mathbf{H}_r = \frac{E_{r0}}{\eta_1} \left(\hat{x}\cos\theta_i + \hat{z}\sin\theta_i\right) e^{-j(k_{1x}x - k_{1z}z)} $$ **Transmitted wave** (travelling in +x, +z direction, Medium 2): $$ \mathbf{E}_t = \hat{y} E_{t0} e^{-j(k_{2x}x + k_{2z}z)} $$ $$ \mathbf{H}_t = \frac{E_{t0}}{\eta_2} \left(-\hat{x}\cos\theta_t + \hat{z}\sin\theta_t\right) e^{-j(k_{2x}x + k_{2z}z)} $$ ### Apply Boundary Conditions at $z = 0$ **Condition 1: Continuity of tangential E** ($E_y$ is tangential to the boundary): $$ E_{i0} + E_{r0} = E_{t0} \quad \text{(at } z = 0\text{)} $$ **Condition 2: Continuity of tangential H** ($H_x$ is tangential to the boundary): $$ -\frac{E_{i0}}{\eta_1}\cos\theta_i + \frac{E_{r0}}{\eta_1}\cos\theta_i = -\frac{E_{t0}}{\eta_2}\cos\theta_t $$ Multiplying by $-\eta_1$: $$ E_{i0}\cos\theta_i - E_{r0}\cos\theta_i = \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2}E_{t0}\cos\theta_t $$ ### Solve the System Let $\Gamma_\perp = E_{r0}/E_{i0}$ and $\tau_\perp = E_{t0}/E_{i0}$. **From condition 1:** $1 + \Gamma_\perp = \tau_\perp$, so $E_{t0} = E_{i0}(1 + \Gamma_\perp)$. **From condition 2:** $$ \cos\theta_i - \Gamma_\perp \cos\theta_i = \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2}(1 + \Gamma_\perp)\cos\theta_t $$ Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} \cos\theta_i - \Gamma_\perp \cos\theta_i &= \frac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2} + \frac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2}\Gamma_\perp \\[4pt] \cos\theta_i - \frac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2} &= \Gamma_\perp \left(\cos\theta_i + \frac{\eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Solving for $\Gamma_\perp$: $$ \Gamma_\perp = \frac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_1 \cos\theta_t}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t} $$ And from condition 1, $\tau_\perp = 1 + \Gamma_\perp$: $$ \tau_\perp = \frac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_1 \cos\theta_t} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Fresnel Equations for Parallel Polarization (TM) For **parallel polarization**, $\mathbf{H}$ is in the **y-direction** (perpendicular to the plane of incidence), and $\mathbf{E}$ lies in the xz-plane. Following a similar procedure, the boundary conditions yield: $$ \Gamma_\parallel = \frac{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i} $$ $$ \tau_\parallel = \frac{2\eta_2 \cos\theta_i}{\eta_2 \cos\theta_t + \eta_1 \cos\theta_i} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the swap of $\cos\theta_i$ and $\cos\theta_t$ in $\Gamma_\parallel$?** In the parallel case, the **tangential component of E** to the boundary is $E_x = E \cos\theta$. So $\cos\theta$ appears with E in the boundary equations, while $\eta$ appears with H. This swap is purely a consequence of which field component is tangential vs. normal for this polarization. ## 3.3 Derivation of Brewster's Angle For Brewster's angle, set $\Gamma_\parallel = 0$: $$ \eta_2 \cos\theta_t - \eta_1 \cos\theta_i = 0 $$ $$ \eta_2 \cos\theta_t = \eta_1 \cos\theta_i $$ Squaring and using $\eta = \eta_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ (non-magnetic media): $$ \frac{\cos^2\theta_t}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{\cos^2\theta_i}{\varepsilon_1} $$ $$ \varepsilon_1 \cos^2\theta_t = \varepsilon_2 \cos^2\theta_i $$ Using Snell's law: $\sin\theta_t = (n_1/n_2)\sin\theta_i = \sqrt{\varepsilon_1/\varepsilon_2}\sin\theta_i$, and $\cos^2\theta_t = 1 - (\varepsilon_1/\varepsilon_2)\sin^2\theta_i$: $$ \varepsilon_1 \left[1 - \frac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2}\sin^2\theta_i\right] = \varepsilon_2 \cos^2\theta_i = \varepsilon_2(1 - \sin^2\theta_i) $$ $$ \varepsilon_1 - \frac{\varepsilon_1^2}{\varepsilon_2}\sin^2\theta_i = \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_2 \sin^2\theta_i $$ $$ \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \left(\frac{\varepsilon_1^2}{\varepsilon_2} - \varepsilon_2\right)\sin^2\theta_i $$ $$ \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 = \left(\frac{\varepsilon_2^2 - \varepsilon_1^2}{\varepsilon_2}\right)\sin^2\theta_i = \frac{(\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1)(\varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1)}{\varepsilon_2}\sin^2\theta_i $$ Dividing both sides by $(\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1)$: $$ 1 = \frac{(\varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1)}{\varepsilon_2}\sin^2\theta_i $$ $$ \sin^2\theta_B = \frac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2} $$ $$ \tan^2\theta_B = \frac{\sin^2\theta_B}{\cos^2\theta_B} = \frac{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1}{1 - \varepsilon_2/(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2)} = \frac{\varepsilon_2(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2)}{(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2)\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_1\varepsilon_2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1} $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Geometric Insight:** At Brewster's angle, $\theta_i + \theta_t = 90°$. The reflected ray is perpendicular to the refracted ray. This means the dipole oscillation in Medium 2 (induced by the refracted wave) cannot radiate along the reflected ray direction (dipole radiation is zero along its axis), so no energy is reflected. ## 3.4 Critical Angle Derivation At the critical angle, $\theta_t = 90°$ (wave just grazes the boundary in Medium 2): $$ n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \sin(90°) = n_2 $$ $$ \sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1} \quad (\mu_1 = \mu_2) $$ $$ \boxed{\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1}} $$ For $\theta_i > \theta_c$, $\sin\theta_t = (n_1/n_2)\sin\theta_i > 1$, so $\cos\theta_t = -j\sqrt{\sin^2\theta_t - 1}$ becomes **purely imaginary**. The transmitted field then becomes: $$ E_t \propto e^{-jk_2 z \cos\theta_t} = e^{-\alpha z} $$ where $\alpha = k_2 \sqrt{\sin^2\theta_t - 1}$ is the **evanescent decay constant**. The wave is confined to a thin layer (skin depth) near the boundary in Medium 2 and does not propagate energy. ## 3.5 Worked Example: Numerical Application **Problem:** A plane wave in free space ($\eta_1 = 377$ Ω, $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_0$) strikes a glass medium ($\varepsilon_2 = 4\varepsilon_0$, $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_0$) at $\theta_i = 30°$. Find the reflection coefficient, transmission coefficient, and power reflection for both polarizations. **Step 1:** Snell's law gives $\sin\theta_t = (1/2)\sin 30° = 0.25$, so $\theta_t = 14.48°$. **Step 2:** Compute cosines: $\cos\theta_i = 0.866$, $\cos\theta_t = 0.968$. **Step 3:** Intrinsic impedances: $\eta_1 = 377$ Ω, $\eta_2 = 377/\sqrt{4} = 188.5$ Ω. **Step 4:** Perpendicular polarization: $$ \Gamma_\perp = \frac{188.5 \times 0.866 - 377 \times 0.968}{188.5 \times 0.866 + 377 \times 0.968} = \frac{163.3 - 364.9}{163.3 + 364.9} = \frac{-201.6}{528.2} \approx -0.382 $$ $$ R_\perp = \vert \Gamma_\perp \vert^2 \approx 0.146 $$ **Step 5:** Parallel polarization: $$ \Gamma_\parallel = \frac{188.5 \times 0.968 - 377 \times 0.866}{188.5 \times 0.968 + 377 \times 0.866} = \frac{182.5 - 326.5}{182.5 + 326.5} = \frac{-144.0}{509.0} \approx -0.283 $$ $$ R_\parallel = \vert \Gamma_\parallel \vert^2 \approx 0.080 $$ **Step 6:** Brewster's angle check: $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{4/1}) = 63.43°$ — confirmed in TM polarization, $R_\parallel = 0$ at this angle. > [!NOTE] > **Sign of $\Gamma$:** A negative $\Gamma$ indicates a 180° phase shift on reflection. For perpendicular polarization at $\theta_i < \theta_c$ with $n_1 < n_2$, the sign is negative (wave reflecting from a "denser" medium). The phase shift becomes more complex (not just 0° or 180°) in the TIR region. # 4.
