<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]**

**Q1.** Define Brewster's angle. Show that at this angle, the reflected and refracted rays are mutually perpendicular.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Brewster's angle $\theta_B$ is the angle of incidence for which the Fresnel reflection coefficient for p-polarized light ($r_p$) becomes zero — i.e., the p-polarized component is **completely transmitted** with **zero reflection**.

**Proof (1 mark):** From the Brewster condition $\tan \theta_B = n_2/n_1$ and Snell's law $n_1 \sin \theta_B = n_2 \sin \theta_t$, substituting $\sin \theta_t = (n_1/n_2)\sin \theta_B$ and using $\cos \theta_B = 1/\sqrt{1+\tan^2 \theta_B}$:

$$\sin \theta_t = \frac{n_1}{n_2} \cdot \frac{n_2/n_1}{\sqrt{1+(n_2/n_1)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(n_2/n_1)^2}} = \cos \theta_B$$

$$\Rightarrow \theta_t = 90^\circ - \theta_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_B + \theta_t = 90^\circ$$

The reflected ray is on the same side of the normal as the incident ray (angle $\theta_B$ from normal), while the refracted ray is at $\theta_t = 90^\circ - \theta_B$ on the other side. The angle between them is $180^\circ - \theta_B - \theta_t = 90^\circ$. **Q.E.D.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** A light ray traveling in air is incident on a glass surface of refractive index 1.5 at the Brewster angle. Calculate the angle of incidence and the angle of refraction.

**Model Answer:**

Given: $n_1 = 1.0$, $n_2 = 1.5$.

$$\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31^\circ \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\theta_t = 90^\circ - \theta_B = 90^\circ - 56.31^\circ = 33.69^\circ \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Main Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Derivation Path**

**(a)** Starting from the Fresnel equations, derive an expression for the Brewster angle for a p-polarized electromagnetic wave incident from medium 1 (refractive index $n_1$) onto medium 2 (refractive index $n_2$). State clearly the condition that defines this angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For light incident from air onto a glass surface ($n = 1.5$) at the Brewster angle, compute:
  (i) The Brewster angle and the corresponding refraction angle
  (ii) The reflectance for s-polarized light
  (iii) The transmittance for p-polarized light **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Fresnel reflection coefficient for p-polarized (TM) wave:
$$r_p = \frac{n_2 \cos \theta_i - n_1 \cos \theta_t}{n_2 \cos \theta_i + n_1 \cos \theta_t}$$

> **Step 2 (1 mark):** State the **Brewster condition** explicitly: $r_p = 0$.

> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Set numerator to zero:
$$n_2 \cos \theta_i = n_1 \cos \theta_t \quad \text{...(1)}$$

> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Invoke Snell's law:
$$n_1 \sin \theta_i = n_2 \sin \theta_t \quad \text{...(2)}$$

> **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Solve by squaring and adding (or dividing). The final result:
$$\tan \theta_B = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_B = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

> **Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude with the orthogonality statement:
$$\theta_B + \theta_t = 90^\circ \quad \text{(reflected ray} \perp \text{refracted ray)}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Brewster angle & refraction angle (2.5 marks):**

$$\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31^\circ \quad \text{...(1.25 marks)}$$

$$\theta_t = 90^\circ - 56.31^\circ = 33.69^\circ \quad \text{...(1.25 marks)}$$

**(ii) Reflectance of s-polarized light (2.5 marks):**

Using $\theta_i = 56.31^\circ$, $\theta_t = 33.69^\circ$:

$$r_s = \frac{n_1 \cos \theta_i - n_2 \cos \theta_t}{n_1 \cos \theta_i + n_2 \cos \theta_t} = \frac{(1)(0.5547) - (1.5)(0.8321)}{(1)(0.5547) + (1.5)(0.8321)}$$

$$r_s = \frac{0.5547 - 1.2482}{0.5547 + 1.2482} = \frac{-0.6935}{1.8029} = -0.3847 \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\rho_s = r_s^2 = 0.1480 \;\;\; (14.80\%) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**(iii) Transmittance of p-polarized light (2 marks):**

Since $r_p = 0$ at $\theta_B$, the **entire p-polarized wave is transmitted**:

$$T_p = 1 - \rho_p = 1 - 0 = 1 \quad \text{(or 100\%)}$$

$$\boxed{T_p = 100\%}$$

> Energy conservation: $T_p + R_p = 1$ where $R_p = \rho_p = 0$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is the physical significance of Brewster's angle? Explain with a neat diagram why no p-polarized light is reflected at this angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A beam of unpolarized light is incident on a glass plate ($n = 1.65$) at the Brewster angle. Calculate:
  (i) The angle of incidence
  (ii) The angle between the reflected and refracted rays
  (iii) The percentage of incident s-polarized light reflected **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Physical Significance (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (2 marks):** The Brewster angle is the angle at which **the dipole radiation pattern** of the induced oscillations in the second medium **fails to emit** in the direction of the reflected ray. Specifically, when a light wave hits the interface, it forces the bound electrons in medium 2 to oscillate. These oscillating dipoles **cannot radiate along their own oscillation direction**.

> **Step 2 (2 marks):** The dipoles oscillate **parallel to the E-field of the refracted wave**, i.e., perpendicular to the refracted ray. At $\theta_B$, the reflected ray is **perpendicular to the refracted ray** — i.e., the reflected direction **coincides with the dipole axis**. Since no radiation occurs along the dipole axis, **no reflected wave can exist** for p-polarized light.

> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Diagram expectation (textual description):**
> - Draw the interface horizontal.
> - Draw the normal vertical.
> - Draw the incident ray at $\theta_B$ from the normal.
> - Draw the refracted ray at $\theta_t = 90^\circ - \theta_B$.
> - Show the reflected ray on the same side as incident, also at $\theta_B$ from the normal — and emphasize that this reflected ray is **perpendicular to the refracted ray**.

> **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Engineering applications:** Brewster windows in lasers, polarizing beam splitters, glare-reducing sunglasses, ellipsometry.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 1.65$, incident medium is air.

**(i) Angle of incidence (2 marks):**

$$\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(1.65) = 58.78^\circ \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Angle between reflected and refracted rays (2 marks):**

By the orthogonality property:

$$\text{Angle} = 90^\circ \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Reflectance of s-polarized light (3 marks):**

$\theta_t = 90^\circ - 58.78^\circ = 31.22^\circ$

$$\cos \theta_B = \cos(58.78^\circ) = 0.5183$$
$$\cos \theta_t = \cos(31.22^\circ) = 0.8554$$

$$r_s = \frac{n_1 \cos \theta_i - n_2 \cos \theta_t}{n_1 \cos \theta_i + n_2 \cos \theta_t} = \frac{(1)(0.5183) - (1.65)(0.8554)}{(1)(0.5183) + (1.65)(0.8554)}$$

$$r_s = \frac{0.5183 - 1.4114}{0.5183 + 1.4114} = \frac{-0.8931}{1.9297} = -0.4629 \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

$$\rho_s = r_s^2 = 0.2143 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{21.43\%} \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> - **Mixing up polarizations:** Writing $r_s = 0$ at Brewster's angle — **WRONG**. Only $r_p = 0$.
> - **Forgetting the angle:** Reporting only $\theta_B$ but **not specifying that it applies to p-polarized light** — costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Sign error in $r_s$:** Students often drop the negative sign in $r_s$ — but for **reflectance** ($\rho = r^2$), the sign is irrelevant; however, for **field calculations**, the sign matters.
> - **Missing the orthogonality condition:** $\theta_B + \theta_t = 90^\circ$ is a **mandatory** statement in derivations.
> - **In Snell's law:** Confusing $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ with the Brewster formula.
> - **No unit check:** Reporting Brewster angle without degree symbol.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Brewster's Angle**

- ✅ **Definition:** Angle of incidence for which **p-polarized reflection is zero**: $r_p = 0$.
- ✅ **Formula:** $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$.
- ✅ **Special case (air-to-medium):** $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(n)$.
- ✅ **Geometric rule:** $\theta_B + \theta_t = 90^\circ$ — **reflected $\perp$ refracted**.
- ✅ **Applies to p-polarization only** (TM wave). **No Brewster angle for s-polarization.**
- ✅ **Physical origin:** Oscillating dipoles in medium 2 cannot radiate along their axis; at $\theta_B$ the reflected direction is parallel to the dipole axis.
- ✅ **At $\theta_B$:** $\rho_p = 0$, $T_p = 100\%$.
- ✅ **At $\theta_B$ for s-pol:** $r_s \neq 0$; typically $\rho_s \approx 10$–$20\%$ (material-dependent).
- ✅ **Engineering uses:** Brewster windows in gas lasers, polarizing beam splitters, glare-reducing filters, ellipsometry.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** For incidence from denser to rarer medium, $\theta_B$ still defined but the formula is unchanged: $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$ — using indices correctly is crucial.
- ✅ **Spectral dependence:** Since $n$ depends on wavelength (dispersion), $\theta_B$ is **wavelength-dependent** — different colors have slightly different Brewster angles (used in spectroscopic ellipsometry).
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"B-P-zero"** — **B**rewster angle, **P**olarized p-wave reflection, **zero** reflectance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Density of EM Wave and Poynting Vector Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Density of EM Wave and Poynting Vector Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Power Density of an Electromagnetic Wave

The **Power Density** of an electromagnetic (EM) wave is defined as the instantaneous amount of energy carried per unit area per unit time by the wave as it propagates through a given medium. It is a scalar quantity measured in **watts per square meter (W/m²)**, and it quantifies the *intensity* or *energy flux* of the EM wave at a point in space.

Mathematically, the instantaneous power density is given by the magnitude of the **Poynting Vector** $\vec{S}$:

$$P_d = \vert \vec{S} \vert = \vert \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \vert$$

Where:
- $\vec{E}$ is the instantaneous electric field intensity (V/m)
- $\vec{H}$ is the instantaneous magnetic field intensity (A/m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The direction of the Poynting vector is always perpendicular to the plane containing $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$, and it indicates the **direction of wave propagation**. For a uniform plane wave, $\vec{S}$ is parallel to the wave vector $\vec{k}$.

### 1.2 Poynting Vector Theorem

The **Poynting Theorem** is essentially the work-energy theorem applied to electromagnetic fields. It is a statement of the conservation of energy for electromagnetic fields, mathematically derived from Maxwell's curl equations. It states that the net power flowing out of a closed surface is equal to the rate of decrease of energy stored within the volume plus the ohmic (conduction) losses.

In differential form, the Poynting theorem is expressed as:

$$-\nabla \cdot \vec{S} = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left( \frac{1}{2} \epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2 + \frac{1}{2} \mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2 \right)$$

In integral form:

$$-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_V \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} \, dV + \frac{d}{dt}\int_V \left( \frac{1}{2} \epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2 + \frac{1}{2} \mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2 \right) dV$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The River of Energy:**
> Imagine a river flowing steadily across a flat plain. The water carries energy (kinetic) from upstream to downstream. Now, picture a *cross-sectional slice* of that river: the amount of water crossing that slice per second is the *flow rate* (analogous to power). The *power density* is simply the flow rate **per unit cross-sectional area** — how much "energy" crosses each square meter of that slice every second.
> 
> In an EM wave, the river is the electromagnetic field, and the *Poynting Vector* $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ tells you both **how much energy is flowing per unit area** (magnitude) and **which way it is flowing** (direction). The perpendicular electric ($\vec{E}$) and magnetic ($\vec{H}$) fields act like two hands pushing the energy forward in the direction perpendicular to both.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants

The two critical constants governing energy storage in EM fields are:
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0 / \epsilon_0} \approx 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Poynting vector is the backbone of wireless power transfer analysis, antenna radiation pattern computation, satellite link budget design, optical fiber power calculations, and microwave oven heating analysis. Every radio, Wi-Fi, and 5G antenna in existence is characterized by its radiated Poynting vector field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Concept

To understand power flow in EM waves, we follow this logical chain:

- **Step 1 — The Two Faces of EM Energy:**
  An EM wave stores energy in two distinct forms:
  - Electric energy density: $w_e = \frac{1}{2} \epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2$ (J/m³)
  - Magnetic energy density: $w_m = \frac{1}{2} \mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2$ (J/m³)
  - Total energy density: $w = w_e + w_m$

- **Step 2 — Instantaneous Power Density (Poynting Vector):**
  The vector that quantifies directional power flow is defined as the cross product of the instantaneous $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$:
  $$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$
  - Direction: Given by right-hand rule, perpendicular to both $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$.
  - Magnitude: $\vert \vec{S} \vert = E H \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$.

- **Step 3 — Why "Average" Power Density Matters in Practice:**
  In sinusoidal steady-state, $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ are time-varying (e.g., $E_0 \cos\omega t$). Instantaneous power oscillates at $2\omega$. Engineers care about the **time-averaged power density**:
  $$\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \vec{S} \, dt$$
  For phasor fields $\vec{E_s}$ and $\vec{H_s}$:
  $$\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\left( \vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^* \right)$$

- **Step 4 — Poynting Theorem as Energy Conservation Law:**
  It is essentially an accounting identity for electromagnetic energy:
  - **Power leaving a volume** (outflow) = **Power dissipated as heat** (ohmic loss) + **Rate of change of stored energy** (in E and H fields).
  - This mirrors Kirchhoff's laws but in field form.

- **Step 5 — Physical Interpretation of Each Term in Poynting Theorem:**
  | Term | Physical Meaning |
  | :--- | :--- |
  | $-\nabla \cdot \vec{S}$ | Power density leaving a point per unit volume (W/m³) |
  | $\vec{J} \cdot \vec{E}$ | Ohmic power dissipated per unit volume (W/m³) |
  | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2\right)$ | Rate of change of electric energy density |
  | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2\right)$ | Rate of change of magnetic energy density |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ | Instantaneous Poynting vector | W/m² |
| 2 | $S_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}(\vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^*)$ | Time-average Poynting vector (phasor form) | W/m² |
| 3 | $S_{avg} = \frac{\vert E_s \vert \vert H_s \vert}{2}$ | Magnitude for plane wave in free space | W/m² |
| 4 | $S_{avg} = \frac{\vert E_s \vert^2}{2\eta_0}$ | Power density in terms of E-field (free space) | W/m² |
| 5 | $S_{avg} = \frac{\eta_0 \vert H_s \vert^2}{2}$ | Power density in terms of H-field (free space) | W/m² |
| 6 | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\epsilon}$ | Intrinsic impedance of medium | Ω |
| 7 | $w_e = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon \vert E \vert^2$ | Electric energy density | J/m³ |
| 8 | $w_m = \frac{1}{2}\mu \vert H \vert^2$ | Magnetic energy density | J/m³ |
| 9 | $-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A} = P_{diss} + \frac{dW_{stored}}{dt}$ | Integral form of Poynting theorem | W |
| 10 | $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | Ω |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Engineering:** The radiated power of an antenna is computed by integrating the average Poynting vector over a closed spherical surface surrounding the antenna (the *radiation sphere*). This gives the *Total Radiated Power (TRP)*.
- **Microwave Engineering:** The power delivered to a load through a transmission line is given by the integration of the Poynting vector across the cross-section of the line.
- **Optical Fiber Communication:** Signal power loss along a fiber is determined by the attenuation of the Poynting vector magnitude due to absorption and scattering.
- **Solar Energy:** The solar constant ($\approx 1361$ W/m²) is the time-averaged Poynting vector magnitude of sunlight at Earth's distance from the sun.
- **RFID / Wireless Charging:** Power density calculations determine safe exposure limits (e.g., FCC's 10 W/m² limit at 2.4 GHz).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Poynting Theorem from Maxwell's Equations

We begin with Maxwell's two curl equations (in phasor-free, time-domain form):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \epsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 1:** Take the dot product of Equation (1) with $\vec{H}$:

$$\vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\mu \vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2:** Take the dot product of Equation (2) with $\vec{E}$:

$$\vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} + \epsilon \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract Step 1 from Step 2:

$$\vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H}) - \vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} + \epsilon \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} + \mu \vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \cdot (\vec{A} \times \vec{B}) = \vec{B} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) - \vec{A} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{B})$ on the left-hand side. With $\vec{A} = \vec{E}$ and $\vec{B} = \vec{H}$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

Therefore, our equation becomes:

$$-\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J} + \epsilon \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} + \mu \vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the product rule identities:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2\right) = \epsilon \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{1}{2}\mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2\right) = \mu \vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the **differential (point) form** of Poynting's Theorem:

$$-\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left( \frac{1}{2}\epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2 + \frac{1}{2}\mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2 \right)$$

**Step 7:** Define the **Poynting vector** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$. Then:

$$-\nabla \cdot \vec{S} = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}$$

where $w = w_e + w_m$ is the total electromagnetic energy density.

**Step 8:** Integrate over an arbitrary volume $V$ bounded by a closed surface $S$, and apply the Divergence Theorem ($\int_V \nabla \cdot \vec{S} \, dV = \oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A}$):

$$-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_V \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} \, dV + \frac{d}{dt}\int_V w \, dV$$

This is the **integral form of Poynting's theorem**, which physically states:
**Power flowing INTO the volume** = **Power dissipated as heat (ohmic loss)** + **Rate of increase of stored EM energy**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Time-Average Power Density for a Uniform Plane Wave

Consider a uniform plane wave propagating in the $+z$ direction in free space with:

$$\vec{E} = \hat{x} E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz)$$

$$\vec{H} = \hat{y} \frac{E_0}{\eta_0} \cos(\omega t - kz)$$

**Step 1:** Compute the instantaneous Poynting vector:

$$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} = \hat{x} \times \hat{y} \cdot E_0 \cdot \frac{E_0}{\eta_0} \cos^2(\omega t - kz)$$

**Step 2:** Use $\hat{x} \times \hat{y} = \hat{z}$:

$$\vec{S} = \hat{z} \frac{E_0^2}{\eta_0} \cos^2(\omega t - kz)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the time average over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$. Recall that $\langle \cos^2(\omega t - kz) \rangle = 1/2$:

$$\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \hat{z} \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0}$$

**Step 4:** In terms of peak values, this is the **average power density** (radiation intensity) of a plane wave:

$$S_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{E_0 H_0}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of 1/2?** In sinusoidal steady-state, the fields oscillate. The instantaneous power oscillates at twice the frequency, so its average over one full cycle is exactly half the peak instantaneous power. This 1/2 factor is the *single most common source of errors* in KTU exam solutions — students often forget it.

### 3.3 Phasor Form of Average Power Density

For time-harmonic fields with phasor representations $\vec{E_s}$ and $\vec{H_s}$:

$$\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re}\left[ \vec{E_s} e^{j\omega t} \right], \quad \vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}\left[ \vec{H_s} e^{j\omega t} \right]$$

The instantaneous Poynting vector is:

$$\vec{S} = \text{Re}\left[ \vec{E_s} e^{j\omega t} \right] \times \text{Re}\left[ \vec{H_s} e^{j\omega t} \right]$$

Using the identity $\text{Re}(A)\times \text{Re}(B) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(A \times B^*) + \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(A \times B \, e^{j2\omega t})$:

$$\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left( \vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^* \right)$$

The $e^{j2\omega t}$ term averages to zero over a full period.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in an Electromagnetic Wave

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maxwell Curl Equations] --> B[Dot with E and H]
    B --> C[Apply Vector Identity]
    C --> D[Poynting Vector S = E × H]
    D --> E[Differential Poynting Theorem]
    E --> F[Integrate Over Volume V]
    F --> G[Apply Divergence Theorem]
    G --> H[Integral Poynting Theorem]
    D --> I[Instantaneous Power Density]
    I --> J[Time Average over Period T]
    J --> K[Average Power Density S_avg = 1/2 Re E × H*]
    H --> L[Power In = Ohmic Loss + dW_stored/dt]
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style K fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Schematic — E, H, and S Vector Orientation in a Plane Wave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlaneWave[Uniform Plane Wave Propagation]
        direction LR
        Ex["E field along +x axis"] -->|Cross product| Svec["Poynting Vector S along +z axis"]
        Hy["H field along +y axis"] -->|Cross product| Svec
        Svec -->|Power flow| Prop["Energy propagates in +z direction"]
    end
    style Ex fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Hy fill:#e65100,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Svec fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Prop fill:#f57f17,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Poynting Theorem Components Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[EM Fields E and H in Volume V] --> P1[Poynting Vector S at Surface]
    Input --> P2[Volume Integral of J.E]
    Input --> P3[Time Derivative of Stored Energy]
    P1 --> Out1[Net Power Outflow across Surface]
    P2 --> Out2[Ohmic Power Dissipated]
    P3 --> Out3[Rate of Change of Field Energy]
    Out1 --> Eq[Energy Balance: Out1 = Out2 + Out3]
    Out2 --> Eq
    Out3 --> Eq
    style Input fill:#37474f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Eq fill:#ad1457,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Out1 fill:#00838f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Out2 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Out3 fill:#6d4c41,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block diagrams above map the *functional flow* of the Poynting theorem — from Maxwell's equations, through the Poynting vector, to the final integral energy-balance statement. The color coding is for clarity only and is not part of the formal notation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Define the Poynting vector. What is its physical significance and what are its SI units?**

**Model Answer:**

The Poynting vector $\vec{S}$ is defined as the cross product of the instantaneous electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ and the instantaneous magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$:

$$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

**Physical Significance:**
- The **magnitude** of $\vec{S}$ represents the *instantaneous power density* (power per unit area) flowing through a surface perpendicular to $\vec{S}$.
- The **direction** of $\vec{S}$ indicates the *direction of propagation* of electromagnetic energy.

**SI Unit:** Watt per square meter (W/m²), or equivalently, J/(s·m²).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**State and explain the Poynting theorem in words. Write its differential form.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The Poynting theorem states that the *net power flowing out of a closed surface* surrounding a volume is equal to the *rate of decrease of electromagnetic energy stored* within the volume *plus* the *ohmic power dissipated* within the volume.

In other words, energy can neither be created nor destroyed in electromagnetic processes — it is either dissipated as heat, stored in fields, or radiated across boundaries.

**Differential Form:**

$$-\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left( \frac{1}{2}\epsilon \vert \vec{E} \vert^2 + \frac{1}{2}\mu \vert \vec{H} \vert^2 \right)$$

Where $\vec{J} \cdot \vec{E}$ is the ohmic power dissipated per unit volume, and the bracketed term is the total electromagnetic energy density.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Part B Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **Poynting theorem** starting from Maxwell's curl equations. Clearly state the physical meaning of each term in the final expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A uniform plane wave propagates in free space with an electric field given by $\vec{E} = \hat{y} \, 10 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ V/m. If the frequency is 1 GHz, calculate:
  - (i) The time-average power density in W/m².
  - (ii) The peak electric and magnetic field magnitudes.
  - (iii) The total energy density (electric + magnetic). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

*See Section 3.1 above for the complete step-by-step derivation.* The final integral form is:

$$-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_V \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} \, dV + \frac{d}{dt}\int_V w \, dV$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $-\oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A}$ : Total electromagnetic power flowing out of the closed surface.
- $\int_V \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} \, dV$ : Total power dissipated as heat (Joule heating) within the volume.
- $\frac{d}{dt}\int_V w \, dV$ : Rate of change of total EM energy stored inside the volume.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $\vec{E} = \hat{y} \, 10 \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ V/m, $f = 1$ GHz, free space.

**Step 1 — Identify peak E-field:** $E_0 = 10$ V/m, $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^9$ rad/s.

**Step 2 — Compute intrinsic impedance of free space:**

$$\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\epsilon_0}} = 120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute peak H-field:**

$$H_0 = \frac{E_0}{\eta_0} = \frac{10}{120\pi} \approx 26.5 \, \text{mA/m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute time-average power density:**

$$S_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{(10)^2}{2 \times 120\pi} = \frac{100}{240\pi} \approx 0.1326 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute total energy density:**

Electric energy density (time-averaged):

$$\langle w_e \rangle = \frac{1}{4}\epsilon_0 E_0^2 = \frac{1}{4} \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \approx 2.21 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{J/m}^3$$

Magnetic energy density (time-averaged):

$$\langle w_m \rangle = \frac{1}{4}\mu_0 H_0^2 = \frac{1}{4} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (26.5 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

Computing $H_0^2 = (26.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \approx 7.02 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$\langle w_m \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 7.02 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.21 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{J/m}^3$$

**Total energy density:**

$$\langle w \rangle = \langle w_e \rangle + \langle w_m \rangle = 2 \times 2.21 \times 10^{-10} = 4.42 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{J/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** For a plane wave in free space, $\langle w_e \rangle = \langle w_m \rangle$. The energy is shared equally between the electric and magnetic fields.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Part B Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **time-average Poynting vector** for a time-harmonic electromagnetic wave using phasor representation. Show that the instantaneous power density has both a DC and a 2ω component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The magnetic field of a uniform plane wave in free space is given by $\vec{H} = \hat{z} \, 2 \sin(\pi \times 10^8 t - \beta y)$ A/m. Calculate:
  - (i) The wavelength and the value of $\beta$.
  - (ii) The instantaneous power density vector $\vec{S}$.
  - (iii) The time-average power density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Let $\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re}[\vec{E_s} e^{j\omega t}]$ and $\vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}[\vec{H_s} e^{j\omega t}]$.

**Step 1:** Compute the instantaneous Poynting vector:

$$\vec{S}(t) = \vec{E}(t) \times \vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}[\vec{E_s} e^{j\omega t}] \times \text{Re}[\vec{H_s} e^{j\omega t}]$$

**Step 2:** Use the identity $\text{Re}(A)\text{Re}(B) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(AB^*) + \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(AB)$:

$$\vec{S}(t) = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left(\vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^*\right) + \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left(\vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s} \, e^{j2\omega t}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The first term is **time-independent (DC component)**:

$$\vec{S}_{DC} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left(\vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^*\right)$$

**Step 4:** The second term **oscillates at $2\omega$** and averages to zero over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$\langle \vec{S}_{2\omega} \rangle = 0$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the time-average Poynting vector is:

$$\boxed{\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left(\vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^*\right)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $\vec{H} = \hat{z} \, 2 \sin(\pi \times 10^8 t - \beta y)$ A/m.

**Step 1 — Identify angular frequency:**

$$\omega = \pi \times 10^8 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute frequency and wavelength:**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{\pi \times 10^8}{2\pi} = 5 \times 10^7 \, \text{Hz} = 50 \, \text{MHz}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^7} = 6 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute propagation constant $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.047 \, \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the electric field:** Since $\vec{H}$ is along $\hat{z}$ and the wave propagates in $+y$ direction, $\vec{E}$ must be along $\hat{x}$ (perpendicular to both). The magnitude is $E_0 = \eta_0 H_0$:

$$E_0 = 120\pi \times 2 = 240\pi \approx 754 \, \text{V/m}$$

$$\vec{E} = \hat{x} \, 240\pi \sin(\pi \times 10^8 t - \beta y) \, \text{V/m}$$

**Step 5 — Instantaneous Poynting vector:**

$$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H} = \hat{x} \times \hat{z} \cdot (240\pi)(2) \sin^2(\pi \times 10^8 t - \beta y)$$

Using $\hat{x} \times \hat{z} = -\hat{y}$:

$$\vec{S} = -\hat{y} \, 480\pi \sin^2(\pi \times 10^8 t - \beta y) \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Time-average power density:** Using $\langle \sin^2 \rangle = 1/2$:

$$S_{avg} = \frac{480\pi}{2} = 240\pi \approx 754 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of propagation direction:** The negative sign in $-\hat{y}$ indicates the wave actually propagates in the $-y$ direction, *not* $+y$. This is a classic trap — students often assume propagation is in the $+y$ direction without checking the sign of $\hat{x} \times \hat{z}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1/2 in time-average:** A 7-mark problem can be reduced to 3–4 marks if the 1/2 factor is missed in the time-average calculation. Always write $\langle \sin^2 \rangle = 1/2$ explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up intrinsic impedance:** Use $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$ for free space, NOT $50 \, \Omega$ (which is a common transmission-line impedance, not a free-space parameter).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Poynting Vector Definition:** $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ — instantaneous, units W/m².
- ✅ **Time-Average Poynting Vector:** $\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E_s} \times \vec{H_s}^*)$ — for sinusoidal steady-state.
- ✅ **Power Density for Plane Wave:** $S_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{E_0 H_0}{2} = \frac{\eta_0 H_0^2}{2}$.
- ✅ **Intrinsic Impedance of Free Space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$. (Memorize this!)
- ✅ **Poynting Theorem (Differential Form):** $-\nabla \cdot \vec{S} = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial t}$.
- ✅ **Poynting Theorem (Integral Form):** Power out = Ohmic loss + d(Stored energy)/dt.
- ✅ **Energy Density Equipartition:** For a plane wave, $\langle w_e \rangle = \langle w_m \rangle$, so $\langle w \rangle = \epsilon_0 E_0^2 / 2 = 2 \langle w_e \rangle$.
- ✅ **Direction Rule:** $\vec{S}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$, given by the right-hand rule.
- ✅ **The 1/2 Factor:** ALWAYS present in time-average calculations for sinusoidal fields.
- ✅ **Cross-product identities to remember:** $\hat{x} \times \hat{y} = \hat{z}$, $\hat{y} \times \hat{z} = \hat{x}$, $\hat{z} \times \hat{x} = \hat{y}$ (cyclic), and the *anti-cyclic* orderings give negative signs.
- ✅ **Power vs. Energy Density Relation:** $S_{avg} = w_{avg} \cdot v_p$, where $v_p$ is the phase velocity.
- ✅ **Vector Identity to Memorize:** $\nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) = \vec{H} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{E}) - \vec{E} \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{H})$.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** When given a $\sin$ field, do not convert to $\cos$ unnecessarily — just use $\langle \sin^2 \rangle = 1/2$ directly.
- ✅ **Units to remember:** $\vec{S}$ in W/m², $\vec{E}$ in V/m, $\vec{H}$ in A/m, $\eta_0$ in Ω, $w$ in J/m³.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polarization of Electromagnetic Wave: Linear, Circular, and Elliptical Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polarization of Electromagnetic Wave: Linear, Circular, and Elliptical Polarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarization of an Electromagnetic Wave** is defined as the geometric locus traced by the tip of the time-varying electric field vector $\vec{E}$ of the electromagnetic wave, when observed in a plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation ($\vec{k}$), at a fixed point in space.

For a uniform plane wave propagating in the $+z$ direction, the electric field vector generally lies in the transverse $xy$-plane and can be resolved into two orthogonal components:
$$\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{x}E_x(z,t) + \hat{y}E_y(z,t)$$

The relative phase difference $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x$ and the amplitude ratio $E_{0y}/E_{0x}$ between these two orthogonal components uniquely determine the **state of polarization** of the wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polarization is a *transverse wave phenomenon*. Longitudinal waves (e.g., sound waves in air) do not exhibit polarization. This property is exclusively tied to the transverse nature of EM waves, a direct consequence of Maxwell's equations ($\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ in free space).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a long rope tied to a wall. You shake the free end:
- If you shake it **only up-and-down**, a wave travels along the rope with oscillations confined to a single vertical plane. This is analogous to **linearly polarized light** (a *Polaroid* sunglasses lens works on this principle — it blocks all light vibrating in directions other than its transmission axis).

- If you shake the rope by moving your hand in a **circular motion**, the wave still travels forward, but the rope's displacement traces a helix (and from the side, a circle). This is analogous to **circularly polarized light** — used in 3D movie glasses and satellite communication.

- If your hand moves in an **elliptical (squashed circle) path**, you get **elliptically polarized light** — the most general case found in nature, including most reflected sunlight.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Polarization tells us *the direction in which the electric field is vibrating* as the wave moves through space. Human eyes cannot detect polarization, but bees, ants, and some birds can — they use it for navigation.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ |
| Impedance of free space | $\eta_0$ | $\mathbf{377 \; \Omega}$ (approx.) |
| Angular frequency | $\omega$ | $\mathbf{rad/s}$ |
| Wave number | $k$ | $\mathbf{rad/m}$ |
| Phase constant | $\beta$ | $\mathbf{rad/m}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Framework

Consider a uniform plane wave propagating in the $+z$ direction. The two orthogonal components of the electric field can be expressed as:

$$E_x(z,t) = E_{0x} \cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x)$$

$$E_y(z,t) = E_{0y} \cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y)$$

where:
- $E_{0x}, E_{0y}$ = amplitudes of the $x$ and $y$ components
- $\phi_x, \phi_y$ = initial phase angles
- $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x$ = relative phase difference
- $k = \omega/c$ = wave number in free space

### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Classification of Polarization States

**Step 1 — Identify the General Condition:**
At any fixed point in space (say $z=0$), the locus of the tip of $\vec{E}$ as time varies is determined by the values of $E_{0x}$, $E_{0y}$, and $\Delta\phi$.

**Step 2 — Eliminate the Time Variable:**
Using the trigonometric identity to eliminate the time parameter, the general equation of the polarization ellipse is obtained:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 - \frac{2 E_x E_y}{E_{0x} E_{0y}} \cos(\Delta\phi) = \sin^2(\Delta\phi)$$

This is the **Polarization Ellipse Equation** — the most general result.

**Step 3 — Classify Based on Phase Difference:**

- **Case 1: $\Delta\phi = n\pi$ (where $n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$)**
  - $\sin^2(\Delta\phi) = 0$
  - The equation reduces to a straight line → **Linear Polarization**

- **Case 2: $\Delta\phi = \pm\pi/2$ AND $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$**
  - $\sin^2(\Delta\phi) = 1$
  - The equation reduces to a circle → **Circular Polarization**

- **Case 3: All Other Conditions**
  - General ellipse → **Elliptical Polarization**

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why the ellipse?** The two components $E_x$ and $E_y$ are sinusoidal with the same frequency but possibly different phases/amplitudes. Combining them traces a Lissajous figure — a closed curve in the $xy$-plane. The specific shape (line, circle, ellipse) depends on the phase relationship.

- **How is polarization detected?** Using a **polarizing filter (analyzer)**. By rotating the filter's transmission axis, the transmitted intensity varies as $I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$ (**Malus's Law**), where $\theta$ is the angle between the polarization direction and the analyzer axis.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Condition / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General E-field (z-propagation) | $\vec{E} = \hat{x}E_{0x}\cos(\omega t - kz) + \hat{y}E_{0y}\cos(\omega t - kz + \Delta\phi)$ | Starting point for all polarization analysis |
| 2 | Polarization Ellipse Equation | $\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 - \frac{2 E_x E_y}{E_{0x}E_{0y}}\cos\Delta\phi = \sin^2\Delta\phi$ | Most general polarization state |
| 3 | Linear Polarization | $\Delta\phi = 0$ or $\pi$ | Vibration along a fixed straight line |
| 4 | Circular Polarization | $\Delta\phi = +\pi/2$ (RHC) or $-\pi/2$ (LHC), $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$ | Tip of $\vec{E}$ traces a circle |
| 5 | Elliptical Polarization | Any other $\Delta\phi$ with $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ | Tip of $\vec{E}$ traces an ellipse |
| 6 | Axial Ratio (AR) | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Major Axis}}{\text{Minor Axis}}$ | $AR = 1$ for circular; $AR = \infty$ for linear |
| 7 | Malus's Law | $I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$ | Intensity through an ideal polarizer |
| 8 | Degree of Polarization | $p = \dfrac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} + I_{\min}}$ | $p = 1$: fully polarized; $p = 0$: unpolarized |
| 9 | Stokes Parameters | $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$ | Complete polarization state description |
| 10 | Brewster's Angle | $\tan\theta_B = n_2/n_1$ | Angle for complete $p$-polarization reflection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Polarization is exploited in **LCD displays** (twisted nematic crystals rotate polarization), **optical fiber communication** (preserves polarization to reduce modal dispersion), **radar cross-section reduction** (uses RAM to scatter/absorb polarized signals), and **remote sensing** (different materials reflect different polarizations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Polarization Ellipse Equation

**Starting Point:**
At a fixed point in space, choose $z = 0$ (without loss of generality). The electric field components are:

$$E_x(t) = E_{0x} \cos(\omega t + \phi_x)$$

$$E_y(t) = E_{0y} \cos(\omega t + \phi_y)$$

**Step 1 — Define the phase difference.**
Let $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x$. We can absorb $\phi_x$ into the time origin, so:

$$E_x = E_{0x} \cos(\omega t)$$

$$E_y = E_{0y} \cos(\omega t + \Delta\phi)$$

**Step 2 — Express the time derivative.**
Since both are sinusoids of the same frequency, we can write:

$$E_y = E_{0y}[\cos(\omega t)\cos(\Delta\phi) - \sin(\omega t)\sin(\Delta\phi)]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$.**
From $E_x$:
$$\cos(\omega t) = \frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}$$

Substitute into $E_y$:
$$E_y = E_{0y}\left[\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\cos(\Delta\phi) - \sin(\omega t)\sin(\Delta\phi)\right]$$

Solving for $\sin(\omega t)$:
$$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{1}{\sin(\Delta\phi)}\left[\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\cos(\Delta\phi) - \frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Pythagorean Identity.**
Using $\sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t) = 1$:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{\sin^2(\Delta\phi)}\left[\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\cos(\Delta\phi) - \frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right]^2 = 1$$

**Step 5 — Expand and simplify.**
Multiplying through and rearranging yields the **Polarization Ellipse Equation**:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 - \frac{2 E_x E_y}{E_{0x}E_{0y}}\cos(\Delta\phi) = \sin^2(\Delta\phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation fully characterizes linear, circular, and elliptical polarization as special cases.

---

### 3.2 Special Case 1: Linear Polarization Derivation

**Condition:** $\Delta\phi = 0$ or $\pi$, so $\cos(\Delta\phi) = \pm 1$ and $\sin(\Delta\phi) = 0$.

Substituting into the ellipse equation:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 \mp \frac{2 E_x E_y}{E_{0x}E_{0y}} = 0$$

This factors as a perfect square:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}} \mp \frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow E_y = \pm\left(\frac{E_{0y}}{E_{0x}}\right) E_x$$

**Conclusion:** The locus is a **straight line** through the origin with slope $\pm E_{0y}/E_{0x}$. The sign depends on whether the components are in phase ($\Delta\phi = 0$, positive slope) or out of phase ($\Delta\phi = \pi$, negative slope).

---

### 3.3 Special Case 2: Circular Polarization Derivation

**Condition:** $\Delta\phi = +\pi/2$ (Right-Hand Circular) and $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$.

With these:
- $\cos(\Delta\phi) = \cos(\pi/2) = 0$
- $\sin(\Delta\phi) = \sin(\pi/2) = 1$

Substituting into the ellipse equation:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_0}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_0}\right)^2 = 1$$

$$E_x^2 + E_y^2 = E_0^2$$

**Conclusion:** The locus is a **circle** of radius $E_0$. The sense of rotation:
- $\Delta\phi = +\pi/2$ → **Right-Hand Circular (RHC)**: rotates clockwise when viewed from the direction the wave is coming from (against propagation).
- $\Delta\phi = -\pi/2$ → **Left-Hand Circular (LHC)**: rotates counter-clockwise.

> [!TIP]
> **Right-Hand Rule for KTU Exams:** Point your right thumb in the direction of propagation. If your fingers curl in the direction of $\vec{E}$ rotation, it is RHC polarization.

---

### 3.4 Special Case 3: Elliptical Polarization (Most General)

**Condition:** Any arbitrary $\Delta\phi$ (excluding $0, \pi, \pm\pi/2$) and/or $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$.

The general ellipse equation with tilted orientation can be written in the form:

$$A E_x^2 + B E_x E_y + C E_y^2 = 1$$

where $A = 1/E_{0x}^2$, $B = -2\cos(\Delta\phi)/(E_{0x}E_{0y})$, $C = 1/E_{0y}^2$.

The **tilt angle $\tau$** of the major axis with respect to the $x$-axis is:

$$\tan(2\tau) = \frac{2 E_{0x} E_{0y} \cos(\Delta\phi)}{E_{0x}^2 - E_{0y}^2}$$

The **axial ratio (ellipticity)** is determined by the eigenvalues of the matrix representation.

**Example Numerical Case:**
Let $E_{0x} = 3$ V/m, $E_{0y} = 2$ V/m, $\Delta\phi = 45°$.

$$9 \cdot \cos^2(\omega t) + 4 \cdot \cos^2(\omega t + 45°) - 2 \cdot \frac{12 \cos(45°)}{6} \cdot \cos(\omega t)\cos(\omega t + 45°) = \sin^2(45°)$$

The resulting locus is an **ellipse tilted at an angle** $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\left(\frac{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot \cos 45°}{9 - 4}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\left(\frac{12 \cdot 0.707}{5}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\arctan(1.697) \approx 29.74°$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Polarization Type | Phase Condition $\Delta\phi$ | Amplitude Condition | Locus Shape | Handedness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $0$ or $\pi$ | Any | Straight line | N/A |
| Circular | $+\pi/2$ | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$ | Circle | Right-Hand (RHC) |
| Circular | $-\pi/2$ | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$ | Circle | Left-Hand (LHC) |
| Elliptical | $0 < \Delta\phi < \pi/2$ | $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ | Tilted ellipse | Right-Hand |
| Elliptical | $-\pi/2 < \Delta\phi < 0$ | $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ | Tilted ellipse | Left-Hand |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Polarization State Determination Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: EM Wave Propagating in Z direction] --> compA[Resolve E field into Ex and Ey components]
    compA --> compB{Ex and Ey in phase or 180 deg out of phase?}
    compB -- YES --> linearA[LINEAR POLARIZATION]
    linearA --> linearB[Locus is a straight line through origin]
    compB -- NO --> compC{Is phase difference exactly 90 deg?}
    compC -- NO --> ellipA[ELLIPTICAL POLARIZATION]
    ellipA --> ellipB[General case: tilted ellipse]
    compC -- YES --> compD{Ex amplitude equals Ey amplitude?}
    compD -- NO --> ellipA
    compD -- YES --> compE{Phase of Ey leads or lags Ex by 90 deg?}
    compE -- LEADS --> rhcA[RIGHT HAND CIRCULAR POLARIZATION]
    compE -- LAGS --> lhcA[LEFT HAND CIRCULAR POLARIZATION]
    rhcA --> rhcB[Circle traced clockwise against propagation]
    lhcA --> lhcB[Circle traced counterclockwise against propagation]
    classDef resultStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class linearA,ellipA,rhcA,lhcA resultStyle
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Polarization Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input: Ex t and Ey t signals] --> procA[Step 1: Extract Amplitudes E0x and E0y]
    inputA --> procB[Step 2: Extract Phase Difference Delta phi]
    procA --> procC[Step 3: Apply Polarization Ellipse Equation]
    procB --> procC
    procC --> decA{Determine Locus Shape}
    decA --> outA[Output: Type of Polarization]
    outA --> outB[Linear OR Circular OR Elliptical]
    outA --> outC[Handedness: RHC or LHC]
    outA --> outD[Tilt Angle tau and Axial Ratio AR]
    classDef ioStyle fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procStyle fill:#E8F8F5,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class inputA,outB,outC,outD ioStyle
    class procA,procB,procC,decA procStyle
```

### 4.3 Conceptual State Machine: Polarization Classification

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UNPOLARIZED
    UNPOLARIZED --> PARTIALLY_POLARIZED : Random phase mixing
    PARTIALLY_POLARIZED --> FULLY_POLARIZED : Coherent source
    FULLY_POLARIZED --> LINEAR : Delta phi equals 0 or pi
    FULLY_POLARIZED --> ELLIPTICAL : Arbitrary Delta phi
    ELLIPTICAL --> CIRCULAR : Delta phi equals 90 deg and E0x equals E0y
    LINEAR --> ELLIPTICAL : Introduce phase difference
    CIRCULAR --> ELLIPTICAL : Change amplitude ratio
    state LINEAR {
        stateA1[Horizontal]
        stateA2[Vertical]
        stateA3[Tilted at angle theta]
    }
    state CIRCULAR {
        stateB1[Right Hand RHC]
        stateB2[Left Hand LHC]
    }
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (For Mental Visualization)

**Diagram 1 — Linear Polarization:**
Show the $z$-axis as the propagation direction. Draw the $x$-axis horizontally. The $\vec{E}$ vector oscillates as a double-headed arrow along a single line in the $xy$-plane (e.g., along $x$ for horizontal linear polarization). The locus is a line segment of length $2E_{0x}$.

**Diagram 2 — Circular Polarization:**
Draw the $z$-axis. In the $xy$-plane, draw a circle of radius $E_0$. Mark points on the circle at $0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$ to show how the $\vec{E}$ vector tip rotates as time progresses. Add a helical trace on a cylinder of radius $E_0$ along the $z$-axis to show the screw-like 3D structure of the wavefront.

**Diagram 3 — Elliptical Polarization:**
Draw the $z$-axis. In the $xy$-plane, draw a tilted ellipse with major axis $2a$ at tilt angle $\tau$ and minor axis $2b$. The vector $\vec{E}$ rotates tracing this ellipse, with handedness determined by $\Delta\phi$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define polarization of an electromagnetic wave. Why is polarization not exhibited by sound waves in air?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Polarization** is the phenomenon of restricting the vibrations of the electric field vector of an electromagnetic wave to a specific direction perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

Sound waves are **longitudinal waves** — the medium particles vibrate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Since the vibration is along one fixed axis (the propagation axis), there is no transverse component to define a polarization plane. Only **transverse waves** (with vibrations perpendicular to propagation) can exhibit polarization. EM waves are transverse by nature (a consequence of Maxwell's equations), hence they are polarizable, while sound waves in air are not.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the longitudinal wave explanation with reference to Maxwell's equations for EM waves.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between linear, circular, and elliptical polarization with suitable diagrams.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Linear | Circular | Elliptical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase difference $\Delta\phi$ | $0$ or $\pi$ | $\pm\pi/2$ | Any other value |
| Amplitude ratio | Any | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$ | $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ |
| Locus of $\vec{E}$ tip | Straight line | Circle | Tilted ellipse |
| Axial ratio | $\infty$ | $1$ | Finite ($> 1$) |
| Special case of | Elliptical | Elliptical | Most general |

*Diagrams:* Show $\vec{E}$ oscillating along a single line (linear); tracing a circle (circular); tracing a tilted ellipse (elliptical), all in the plane perpendicular to the propagation direction $\hat{z}$.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for the table/differences, 1 mark for the diagrams.)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each question is split into two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** An electromagnetic wave propagating in free space along the $+z$ direction has its electric field given by:
$$\vec{E}(z,t) = 5\cos(\omega t - kz)\hat{x} + 5\cos\left(\omega t - kz - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\hat{y} \;\; \text{V/m}$$

Identify the type of polarization. Justify your answer mathematically.

**(b)** Derive the general polarization ellipse equation. Hence, show that circular polarization is a special case of elliptical polarization.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Identify the components.**
$$E_x(z,t) = 5\cos(\omega t - kz)$$
$$E_y(z,t) = 5\cos\left(\omega t - kz - \frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 5\sin(\omega t - kz)$$

**Step 2: Compare amplitudes and phases.**
- Amplitudes: $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = 5$ V/m — **Equal**
- Phase difference: $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x = -\pi/2$ — The $y$-component **lags** the $x$-component by $\pi/2$.

**Step 3: Determine the polarization type.**
Since the amplitudes are equal and the phase difference is exactly $\pm\pi/2$, the wave is **Circularly Polarized**.

**Step 4: Determine the handedness.**
$\Delta\phi = -\pi/2$ means $E_y$ lags $E_x$ by $90°$. Using the right-hand rule (point thumb along $+z$, fingers curl from $+x$ to $-y$ via $+y$ to $-x$), the rotation appears **counter-clockwise when viewed against the direction of propagation** (i.e., looking toward the source).

Therefore, the wave is **Left-Hand Circularly Polarized (LHC)**.

**Step 5: Verification via ellipse equation.**
At $z=0$:
$$E_x^2 + E_y^2 = 25\cos^2(\omega t) + 25\sin^2(\omega t) = 25$$
The locus is a circle of radius $5$ V/m — confirms circular polarization.

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for amplitude and phase identification, 2 marks for type identification, 2 marks for handedness, 1 mark for verification.)*

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: State the field components.**
At $z = 0$, for the general case:
$$E_x = E_{0x}\cos(\omega t)$$
$$E_y = E_{0y}\cos(\omega t + \Delta\phi)$$

**Step 2: Apply the expansion.**
$$E_y = E_{0y}[\cos(\omega t)\cos(\Delta\phi) - \sin(\omega t)\sin(\Delta\phi)]$$

**Step 3: Substitute and eliminate $\omega t$.**
Substituting $\cos(\omega t) = E_x/E_{0x}$ and solving for $\sin(\omega t)$:
$$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{1}{\sin(\Delta\phi)}\left[\frac{E_x \cos(\Delta\phi)}{E_{0x}} - \frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right]$$

**Step 4: Use the identity $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$.**
After expansion and simplification (as derived in Section 3.1):
$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 - \frac{2E_x E_y}{E_{0x}E_{0y}}\cos(\Delta\phi) = \sin^2(\Delta\phi) \quad \text{...(General Ellipse)}$$

**Step 5: Specialize to circular polarization.**
For circular polarization, set $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$ and $\Delta\phi = \pm\pi/2$:
- $\cos(\Delta\phi) = 0$
- $\sin(\Delta\phi) = \pm 1$, so $\sin^2(\Delta\phi) = 1$

Substituting:
$$\frac{E_x^2}{E_0^2} + \frac{E_y^2}{E_0^2} = 1 \Rightarrow E_x^2 + E_y^2 = E_0^2$$

This is the equation of a **circle** of radius $E_0$ in the $E_x$-$E_y$ plane, confirming that circular polarization is a special case of the general ellipse (with equal amplitudes and $90°$ phase difference).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for specialization to circular case.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Two linearly polarized waves are combined. Wave 1 is polarized along $\hat{x}$ with amplitude $E_0$ and zero initial phase. Wave 2 is polarized along $\hat{y}$ with amplitude $2E_0$ and a phase lead of $60°$ relative to Wave 1. Both propagate in the $+z$ direction. Determine the type of polarization and calculate the tilt angle of the resulting polarization ellipse.

**(b)** State and explain Brewster's Law. A light ray traveling in air ($n_1 = 1.0$) is incident on a glass surface ($n_2 = 1.5$) at Brewster's angle. Calculate (i) the Brewster angle, and (ii) the angle of refraction at this condition.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Write the field expressions.**
$$E_x = E_0\cos(\omega t - kz)$$
$$E_y = 2E_0\cos(\omega t - kz + 60°)$$

**Step 2: Identify the parameters.**
- $E_{0x} = E_0$, $E_{0y} = 2E_0$
- $\Delta\phi = 60°$
- Amplitudes are **unequal**, phase is **not** $0°, 180°$, or $90°$

**Step 3: Conclude the polarization type.**
Since $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$ and $\Delta\phi$ is an arbitrary non-extreme value, the polarization is **Elliptical**.

**Step 4: Calculate the tilt angle.**
$$\tan(2\tau) = \frac{2 E_{0x} E_{0y} \cos(\Delta\phi)}{E_{0x}^2 - E_{0y}^2}$$

$$\tan(2\tau) = \frac{2 \cdot E_0 \cdot 2E_0 \cdot \cos(60°)}{E_0^2 - (2E_0)^2} = \frac{4 E_0^2 \cdot 0.5}{E_0^2 - 4E_0^2} = \frac{2 E_0^2}{-3 E_0^2} = -\frac{2}{3}$$

$$2\tau = \arctan(-2/3) = -33.69°$$

$$\tau = -16.85°$$

The negative sign indicates the major axis is tilted in the clockwise direction from the $+x$-axis. The magnitude of the tilt angle is approximately **16.85°**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for type identification, 3 marks for tilt angle calculation.)*

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Brewster's Law Statement:**
> When unpolarized light is incident on a dielectric surface at a specific angle called the **Brewster angle** $\theta_B$, the reflected light becomes **completely linearly polarized** with its electric field perpendicular to the plane of incidence. At this angle, the reflected and refracted rays are mutually perpendicular.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\tan(\theta_B) = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the two media.

**Explanation:** At $\theta_B$, the dipoles oscillating in the refracted medium cannot radiate along the direction of the reflected ray (perpendicular to their oscillation direction). Thus, no $p$-polarized (parallel to plane of incidence) component is reflected — only $s$-polarized (perpendicular to plane of incidence) light remains in the reflected beam.

**Calculation:**

(i) **Brewster's Angle:**
$$\tan(\theta_B) = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.5}{1.0} = 1.5$$

$$\theta_B = \arctan(1.5) \approx 56.31°$$

(ii) **Angle of Refraction at Brewster's Condition:**
At Brewster's angle, $\theta_r + \theta_B = 90°$ (since reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular).

$$\theta_r = 90° - 56.31° = 33.69°$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for statement and explanation, 2 marks for formula, 1 mark for $\theta_B$ calculation, 2 marks for $\theta_r$ deduction.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of phase difference** (marks lost: ~2): Students often state "phase difference = 90°" without specifying whether $E_y$ leads or lags $E_x$. This determines the **handedness** (RHC vs LHC) — a key marking criterion in KTU exams.
> 2. **Confusing degrees and radians** (marks lost: ~1): Trigonometric functions in KTU problems are frequently given in degrees ($\Delta\phi = 60°$), but students may incorrectly compute $\cos$ or $\sin$ treating the input as radians. Always convert: $60° = \pi/3$ rad.
> 3. **Missing the generality of the ellipse equation** (marks lost: ~2): When asked to "show that circular polarization is a special case," students must explicitly substitute the **two conditions** (equal amplitudes + 90° phase) into the **general** ellipse equation — not start fresh from the components.
> 4. **Skipping the handedness determination**: KTU examiners specifically check if the student can identify RHC vs LHC. Use the right-hand rule clearly: thumb along $\hat{k}$, fingers curl in direction of $\vec{E}$ rotation.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers**: Brewster's angle should be reported in degrees (or radians with units specified). The amplitude should be in V/m.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Polarization** describes the **time-varying orientation of the electric field vector** in a plane transverse to the direction of propagation.
- ✅ It is a property **exclusive to transverse waves** (EM waves), not longitudinal waves (sound).
- ✅ The general polarization state is an **ellipse** described by: $\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{0x}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2 - \frac{2E_x E_y}{E_{0x}E_{0y}}\cos(\Delta\phi) = \sin^2(\Delta\phi)$.
- ✅ **Linear Polarization** → $\Delta\phi = 0$ or $\pi$ (any amplitude ratio).
- ✅ **Circular Polarization** → $\Delta\phi = \pm\pi/2$ **AND** $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$.
- ✅ **Elliptical Polarization** → Most general case (any other $\Delta\phi$ or unequal amplitudes).
- ✅ **Right-Hand Circular (RHC)**: $\Delta\phi = +\pi/2$ (use right-hand rule).
- ✅ **Left-Hand Circular (LHC)**: $\Delta\phi = -\pi/2$ (use right-hand rule).
- ✅ **Malus's Law**: $I = I_0\cos^2\theta$ — intensity of polarized light through an analyzer.
- ✅ **Brewster's Law**: $\tan(\theta_B) = n_2/n_1$ — angle for total $p$-polarization in reflection.
- ✅ **Axial Ratio (AR)**: 1 for circular, $\infty$ for linear, finite for elliptical.
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: LCDs, optical fibers, 3D movies, radar, remote sensing, polaroid sunglasses.
- ✅ **Common KTU exam trap**: Always check both amplitude condition AND phase condition when classifying polarization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uniform Lossless Transmission Line and Line Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uniform Lossless Transmission Line and Line Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Transmission Line** is a waveguide structure specifically designed to transmit electrical energy (in the form of time-varying voltages and currents, or equivalently, time-varying electric and magnetic fields) from one point to another with maximum efficiency and minimum distortion. Common physical realizations include two-wire lines, coaxial cables, strip lines, microstrip lines, and waveguides.

A **Uniform Transmission Line** is one whose cross-sectional geometry, material properties, and hence its electrical parameters remain **constant along its entire length**. This uniformity ensures that the wave propagation characteristics do not change as the wave travels down the line. Mathematically, this means the per-unit-length parameters $R$, $L$, $G$, and $C$ are independent of the spatial coordinate $z$.

A **Lossless Transmission Line** is the idealized case of a uniform transmission line in which all forms of power dissipation (ohmic losses in the conductors and dielectric losses in the insulating medium) are assumed to be zero. The defining conditions are:

$$R = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad G = 0$$

where $R$ is the per-unit-length series resistance (in $\Omega/\text{m}$) and $G$ is the per-unit-length shunt conductance (in $\text{S/m}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on the **lossless** case. The full lossy line treatment appears in Module 5. However, a strong conceptual understanding of why $R=0$ and $G=0$ is essential for deriving the propagation equations you will see in the exam.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Perfect Water Hose"

Imagine a perfectly smooth, frictionless rubber hose carrying water from a pump (source) to a nozzle (load). 

- A **uniform** hose has the same diameter, wall thickness, and material along its entire length. Water experiences the same push or pull no matter where along the hose you observe it.
- A **lossless** hose has no friction between the water and the inner walls (no resistance) and no leaks through the walls (no conductance). Therefore, all the energy you pump in at one end arrives perfectly at the other end — minus none.

In an electrical transmission line, the **inductance $L$** represents the magnetic field energy storage (like the inertia/momentum of the water column), and the **capacitance $C$** represents the electric field energy storage (like the elasticity of the hose wall). The "lossy" elements $R$ (wall friction) and $G$ (leaky walls) are mathematically removed in the lossless case.

### 1.3 The Four Primary Line Parameters

Every transmission line, regardless of type, is fully characterized by **four distributed parameters** defined per unit length:

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | Physical Origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R$ | Series resistance per unit length | $\Omega/\text{m}$ | Finite conductivity ($\sigma_c$) of the metallic conductors |
| $L$ | Series inductance per unit length | $\text{H/m}$ | Time-varying magnetic field surrounding the conductors |
| $G$ | Shunt conductance per unit length | $\text{S/m}$ | Finite conductivity ($\sigma_d$) of the dielectric insulator |
| $C$ | Shunt capacitance per unit length | $\text{F/m}$ | Time-varying electric field between the two conductors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** $R, L$ are **series** elements (along the line), while $G, C$ are **shunt** elements (between the two conductors). This series-shunt topology is precisely why telegrapher's equations are coupled first-order PDEs.

### 1.4 When is the "Lossless" Approximation Valid?

The lossless assumption is **highly accurate** when:

- The conductors are made of highly conductive metals such as **silver ($\sigma \approx 6.14 \times 10^7 \text{ S/m}$)**, **copper ($\sigma \approx 5.8 \times 10^7 \text{ S/m}$)**, or **gold**.
- The dielectric insulator is a near-perfect insulator (e.g., polyethylene, Teflon, dry air) with $\sigma_d \to 0$.
- The operating frequency is **high** (typically RF and microwave ranges), where skin effect makes $R$ grow as $\sqrt{f}$, but the dominant reactance $\omega L$ grows linearly with $f$, making $R \ll \omega L$.

Standard physical constants used throughout this module:

$$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}, \quad \varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}, \quad c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Distributed Parameter Model — The Building Block

A transmission line of length $\ell$ is conceptually constructed from an **infinite cascade** of identical infinitesimal sections, each of length $\Delta z \to 0$. Each infinitesimal section contains a series impedance $z\Delta z$ and a shunt admittance $y\Delta z$:

$$z = R + j\omega L \quad \text{(series impedance per unit length, in } \Omega/\text{m)}$$

$$y = G + j\omega C \quad \text{(shunt admittance per unit length, in } \text{S/m)}$$

This distributed model captures the fact that voltage and current can vary **continuously** with both position $z$ and time $t$ — unlike circuit theory where components are lumped at discrete nodes.

### 2.2 The Telegrapher's Equations (Foundation of All Transmission Line Theory)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) to an infinitesimal section of length $\Delta z$ and taking the limit $\Delta z \to 0$ yields the **general telegrapher's equations**:

$$\frac{\partial V(z,t)}{\partial z} = -L\frac{\partial I(z,t)}{\partial t} - RI(z,t)$$

$$\frac{\partial I(z,t)}{\partial z} = -C\frac{\partial V(z,t)}{\partial t} - GV(z,t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two coupled first-order PDEs are universally true for **all** uniform transmission lines (lossy and lossless). All the formulas that follow are derived from them.

### 2.3 Substituting the Lossless Conditions ($R = 0$, $G = 0$)

When $R = 0$ and $G = 0$, the telegrapher's equations simplify dramatically to:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -L\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = -C\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

Assuming **time-harmonic (sinusoidal steady-state)** operation of the form $V(z,t) = \text{Re}\{V_s(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$ and $I(z,t) = \text{Re}\{I_s(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$, the time derivatives are replaced by multiplication by $j\omega$. The phasor (frequency-domain) form becomes:

$$\frac{dV_s}{dz} = -j\omega L \, I_s$$

$$\frac{dI_s}{dz} = -j\omega C \, V_s$$

### 2.4 The Wave Equation for Lossless Lines

Differentiating the first equation with respect to $z$ and substituting the second gives the **one-dimensional Helmholtz wave equation** in phasor form:

$$\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} = -\omega^2 L C \, V_s \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} + \omega^2 L C \, V_s = 0$$

Similarly for current:

$$\frac{d^2 I_s}{dz^2} + \omega^2 L C \, I_s = 0$$

The general solutions are linear combinations of forward and backward traveling waves.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every essential formula** you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Propagation constant (lossless) | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ | $\text{rad/m}$ — purely imaginary |
| 2 | Phase constant | $\beta$ | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | $\text{rad/m}$ |
| 3 | Phase velocity | $v_p$ | $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 4 | Wavelength on the line | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta} = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $\Omega$ — purely real for lossless line |
| 6 | General voltage solution | $V_s(z)$ | $V_s(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$ | Forward + backward wave |
| 7 | General current solution | $I_s(z)$ | $I_s(z) = \dfrac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \dfrac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$ | Sign difference at reflection |
| 8 | Forward-wave ratio | $V_0^+/I_0^+$ | $V_0^+ / I_0^+ = Z_0$ | Impedance of forward wave |
| 9 | Reflection coefficient (voltage) | $\Gamma$ | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Input impedance (terminated) | $Z_{in}(z)$ | $Z_{in}(z) = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta z')}$ | $\Omega$ — $z'$ is distance from load |
| 11 | Standing wave ratio | $\text{SWR}$ | $\text{SWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | $\geq 1$, dimensionless |
| 12 | Power delivered to matched load | $P_L$ | $P_L = \dfrac{\vert V_0^+ \vert^2}{2 Z_0}$ | Watts — average power |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding lossless transmission line theory is **non-negotiable** in modern engineering:

- **RF/Microwave Engineering:** Antenna feed lines, radar systems, satellite communication, 5G/6G base stations.
- **Digital High-Speed Design:** PCB trace design for DDR memory, USB 3.0, HDMI, and PCI Express — at gigabit speeds, even short traces behave as transmission lines.
- **Power Engineering:** EHV (Extra High Voltage) power transmission line design, where losses must be minimized over hundreds of kilometers.
- **Impedance Matching Networks:** Quarter-wave transformers, single-stub and double-stub tuners — all based on lossless line theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the lossless assumption is exam-relevant:** At KTU board exams, questions on input impedance, SWR, and standing wave patterns almost always assume $R=0$ and $G=0$ because the algebra becomes tractable. Any time you see a numerical problem with $R$ and $G$ not given, **assume lossless**.
