## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Telegrapher's Equations to the Wave Equation

**Starting Point:** The lossless phasor telegrapher's equations:

$$\frac{dV_s}{dz} = -j\omega L \, I_s \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

$$\frac{dI_s}{dz} = -j\omega C \, V_s \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate Eq. (1) with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{dV_s}{dz}\right) = -j\omega L \, \frac{dI_s}{dz}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute Eq. (2) into the right-hand side:

$$\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} = -j\omega L \, \left(-j\omega C \, V_s\right)$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the product $j\omega L \cdot j\omega C = (j\omega)^2 LC = -\omega^2 LC$:

$$\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} = -\omega^2 L C \, V_s$$

**Step 4:** Bring all terms to one side to obtain the standard wave equation:

$$\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} + \omega^2 L C \, V_s = 0$$

This is a linear, second-order, homogeneous, constant-coefficient ODE. Its characteristic equation is $m^2 + \omega^2 LC = 0$, giving $m = \pm j\beta$ where $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: General Solution for Voltage on a Lossless Line

The general solution of the wave equation is a linear combination of two exponentials:

$$V_s(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- The term $V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z}$ represents a **forward-traveling wave** (incident wave, moving from source toward load in the $+z$ direction).
- The term $V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$ represents a **backward-traveling wave** (reflected wave, moving from load toward source in the $-z$ direction).
- $V_0^+$ and $V_0^-$ are complex constants determined by boundary conditions (source voltage and load impedance).

To verify these are indeed traveling waves, restore the time dependence $e^{j\omega t}$:

$$V^+(z,t) = \text{Re}\{V_0^+ e^{j(\omega t - \beta z)}\}$$

This is a sinusoid that maintains constant amplitude and travels in the $+z$ direction with phase $\omega t - \beta z$ held constant: $\frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = v_p$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Current Solution and Characteristic Impedance

Substitute the voltage solution back into Eq. (1):

$$\frac{dV_s}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left(V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}\right) = -j\beta V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + j\beta V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

Setting this equal to $-j\omega L \, I_s$:

$$-j\omega L \, I_s = -j\beta V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + j\beta V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

Divide by $-j\omega L$:

$$I_s(z) = \frac{\beta}{\omega L} V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{\beta}{\omega L} V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

Using $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$:

$$\frac{\beta}{\omega L} = \frac{\omega\sqrt{LC}}{\omega L} = \frac{\sqrt{LC}}{L} = \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} = \frac{1}{Z_0}$$

Therefore:

$$I_s(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

This confirms the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ as the ratio of forward-wave voltage to forward-wave current:

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_0^+}{I_0^+} = -\frac{V_0^-}{I_0^-} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;\; \Omega$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Phase Velocity

From $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ and the universal definition of phase velocity $v_p = \omega/\beta$:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

For an air-filled line (or a line in vacuum), $L$ and $C$ are such that $v_p$ approaches the speed of light $c$. For example, a lossless coaxial cable in vacuum has:

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

For a line with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, the effective phase velocity reduces to:

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Input Impedance of a Terminated Lossless Line

Let the load impedance $Z_L$ be located at $z = 0$ and let the observation point be at distance $z = -\ell$ (i.e., a length $\ell$ away from the load toward the source). The boundary condition at the load is:

$$V_s(0) = Z_L \, I_s(0)$$

Substituting the general solutions:

$$V_0^+ + V_0^- = Z_L \cdot \frac{V_0^+ - V_0^-}{Z_0}$$

Solving for the reflection coefficient $\Gamma = V_0^-/V_0^+$:

$$V_0^+ + \Gamma V_0^+ = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0}(V_0^+ - \Gamma V_0^+)$$

$$1 + \Gamma = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0}(1 - \Gamma)$$

$$Z_0(1 + \Gamma) = Z_L(1 - \Gamma)$$

$$Z_0 + Z_0 \Gamma = Z_L - Z_L \Gamma$$

$$\Gamma(Z_0 + Z_L) = Z_L - Z_0$$

$$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}}$$

Now compute the input impedance at $z = -\ell$:

$$Z_{in}(-\ell) = \frac{V_s(-\ell)}{I_s(-\ell)} = \frac{V_0^+ e^{+j\beta\ell} + V_0^- e^{-j\beta\ell}}{\frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta\ell} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta\ell}}$$

Factor out $V_0^+ e^{+j\beta\ell}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$Z_{in}(-\ell) = Z_0 \cdot \frac{1 + \Gamma e^{-2j\beta\ell}}{1 - \Gamma e^{-2j\beta\ell}}$$

Substitute $\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ and use $z' = \ell$ as the distance from the load:

$$Z_{in}(z') = Z_0 \cdot \frac{(Z_L + Z_0) + (Z_L - Z_0)e^{-2j\beta z'}}{(Z_L + Z_0) - (Z_L - Z_0)e^{-2j\beta z'}}$$

Apply Euler's identity $e^{-2j\beta z'} = \cos(2\beta z') - j\sin(2\beta z')$. After algebraic expansion and using half-angle identities, this reduces to the **canonical lossless line input impedance formula**:

$$\boxed{Z_{in}(z') = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta z')}}$$

This is the single most important formula in transmission line theory and is **guaranteed to appear** in your KTU exam.

### 3.6 Special Cases from the Input Impedance Formula

**Case 1: Short-circuited load** ($Z_L = 0$):

$$Z_{in}^{SC}(z') = jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')$$

This is a **purely reactive** input impedance — it can be inductive (positive reactance) or capacitive (negative reactance) depending on the electrical length $\beta z'$. For $\beta z' = \pi/4$ (line length $\lambda/8$), $Z_{in} = jZ_0$.

**Case 2: Open-circuited load** ($Z_L \to \infty$):

$$Z_{in}^{OC}(z') = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta z')$$

**Case 3: Matched load** ($Z_L = Z_0$, hence $\Gamma = 0$):

$$Z_{in}(z') = Z_0 \quad \text{(independent of } z'\text{)}$$

The line appears as a **pure resistance $Z_0$** at any point, no reflections, full power transfer.

**Case 4: Quarter-wave transformer** ($z' = \lambda/4$, so $\beta z' = \pi/2$ and $\tan(\beta z') \to \infty$):

$$Z_{in}(\lambda/4) = \frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$$

This non-intuitive result is the basis of the **quarter-wave matching transformer**, used to match two different impedances by inserting a transmission line segment of length $\lambda/4$ with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_S Z_L}$.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Distributed Parameter Lumped Section Equivalent Circuit

The following block diagram represents one infinitesimal section ($\Delta z$) of a uniform lossless transmission line. An actual line of finite length is built by cascading infinitely many such sections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["V(z, t)"] -->|Series Inductance L dz| nodeB["Node at z+dz"]
    nodeA --- nodeC["I(z, t) line current"]
    nodeB -->|Shunt Capacitance C dz| nodeD["Return conductor ground reference"]
    nodeB --- nodeE["I(z+dz, t) line current"]
    nodeC -->|enters series L| nodeA
    nodeE -->|leaves section| nodeF["Continues to next section"]
    style nodeA fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8
    style nodeB fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025
    style nodeC fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038
    style nodeE fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038
    style nodeD fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **series branch** contains only the inductance $L \, dz$ (no resistor, since $R = 0$ in lossless case).
- The **shunt branch** contains only the capacitance $C \, dz$ (no conductance, since $G = 0$).
- The voltage drop across $L \, dz$ is the spatial derivative $\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} dz$.
- The current diverted into $C \, dz$ is the spatial derivative $\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} dz$.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Forward and Backward Wave Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSource["Source Vg with Zg"] -->|Drives| nodeLine["Uniform Lossless Transmission Line"]
    nodeLine -->|Forward wave V0+ e to the power of j beta z| nodeLoad["Load ZL"]
    nodeLoad -->|Reflection coefficient Gamma| nodeRefl["Reflected wave V0- e to the power of j beta z"]
    nodeRefl -->|Travels back| nodeLine
    nodeLine -->|Standing wave V_s z| nodeInput["Input Impedance Zin at source end"]
    nodeZ0["Characteristic Impedance Z0 = sqrt of L over C"] -.->|Defines| nodeLine
    nodeBeta["Phase constant beta = omega sqrt of LC"] -.->|Defines| nodeLine
    nodeMatch{"ZL equals Z0 ?"} -->|YES| nodeNoRefl["No reflection, SWR = 1, full power transfer"]
    nodeMatch -->|NO| nodeRefl
    style nodeSource fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8

    style nodeZ0 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038
```


## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define characteristic impedance of a uniform lossless transmission line. For an air-filled coaxial cable with per-unit-length parameters $L = 0.4 \, \mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 100 \, \text{pF/m}$, calculate the characteristic impedance and the phase velocity.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a uniform lossless transmission line is defined as the ratio of the voltage to the current of a single forward-traveling wave (or equivalently, the impedance presented by an infinitely long line). It is a real, frequency-independent quantity for a lossless line.

**Calculation:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{3}} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.25 \; \Omega$$

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-17}}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-8.5}} \approx 1.58 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Result:** $Z_0 \approx 63.25 \; \Omega$, $v_p \approx 1.58 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What are the conditions under which a transmission line is considered lossless? State the expressions for phase velocity and wavelength in such a line.

**Model Answer:**

A transmission line is considered **lossless** when the per-unit-length series resistance $R = 0$ (no ohmic conductor loss) and the per-unit-length shunt conductance $G = 0$ (no dielectric leakage loss). Under these conditions, all four primary parameters reduce to two, $L$ and $C$, and the propagation constant becomes purely imaginary ($\gamma = j\beta$).

**Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Wavelength on the line:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} \;\; \text{m}$$

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Examination) carry **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must attempt either **Question A** or **Question B** as an internal choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the general telegrapher's equations, derive the wave equation for voltage on a uniform lossless transmission line in phasor form. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lossless transmission line has $L = 0.5 \, \mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 200 \, \text{pF/m}$. Find: (i) the phase velocity, (ii) the characteristic impedance, (iii) the phase constant at $f = 100 \, \text{MHz}$, and (iv) the wavelength at this frequency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Assumptions: (1) The line is uniform — $L$ and $C$ are independent of $z$. (2) The line is lossless — $R = 0$ and $G = 0$. (3) Sinusoidal steady-state (time-harmonic) operation at angular frequency $\omega$.

The general telegrapher's equations are:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -L\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} - RI$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = -C\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} - GV$$

Substituting $R = 0$, $G = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = -L\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = -C\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Assuming time-harmonic phasors $V(z,t) = \text{Re}\{V_s(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$ and $I(z,t) = \text{Re}\{I_s(z) e^{j\omega t}\}$, the time derivatives become multiplication by $j\omega$:

$$\frac{dV_s}{dz} = -j\omega L \, I_s \quad \text{...(3)}$$

$$\frac{dI_s}{dz} = -j\omega C \, V_s \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Differentiate Eq. (3) with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} = -j\omega L \frac{dI_s}{dz}$$

Substitute Eq. (4) into the right-hand side:

$$\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} = -j\omega L \cdot (-j\omega C \, V_s) = -\omega^2 L C \, V_s$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{d^2 V_s}{dz^2} + \omega^2 L C \, V_s = 0}$$

This is the one-dimensional Helmholtz wave equation. Its general solution is $V_s(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$ where $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$. (7 marks — full marks for complete derivation)

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

Given: $L = 0.5 \, \mu\text{H/m} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{H/m}$, $C = 200 \, \text{pF/m} = 200 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$, $f = 100 \, \text{MHz} = 10^8 \, \text{Hz}$, so $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^8 \, \text{rad/s}$.

**(i) Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-16}}} = \frac{1}{10^{-8}} = 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Characteristic impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \; \Omega$$

**(iii) Phase constant:**

$$\beta = \frac{\omega}{v_p} = \frac{2\pi \times 10^8}{10^8} = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \; \text{rad/m}$$

Alternatively, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} = 2\pi \times 10^8 \times 10^{-8} = 2\pi$ rad/m. (Confirms the same answer.)

**(iv) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{10^8}{10^8} = 1 \; \text{m}$$

**Result:** $v_p = 10^8$ m/s, $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$, $\beta = 2\pi$ rad/m, $\lambda = 1$ m. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$ is terminated by a load $Z_L = 75 + j25 \, \Omega$. Find: (i) the voltage reflection coefficient, (ii) the standing wave ratio (SWR), and (iii) the input impedance at a distance of $\lambda/8$ from the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the input impedance of a lossless transmission line terminated by a load $Z_L$. Using this result, show that a quarter-wavelength line acts as an impedance inverter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Problem:**

Given: $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$, $Z_L = 75 + j25 \, \Omega$, distance from load $z' = \lambda/8$.

**(i) Voltage reflection coefficient:**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25}$$

Rationalize the denominator by multiplying by the complex conjugate:

$$\Gamma = \frac{(25 + j25)(125 - j25)}{(125 + j25)(125 - j25)} = \frac{25 \cdot 125 - 25 \cdot j25 + j25 \cdot 125 - j^2 \cdot 625}{125^2 + 25^2}$$

$$= \frac{3125 - j625 + j3125 + 625}{15625 + 625} = \frac{3750 + j2500}{16250}$$

$$\Gamma = \frac{3750}{16250} + j\frac{2500}{16250} = 0.2308 + j0.1538$$

Magnitude:

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \sqrt{0.2308^2 + 0.1538^2} = \sqrt{0.0533 + 0.0237} = \sqrt{0.0770} \approx 0.2775$$

**(ii) Standing wave ratio:**

$$\text{SWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 0.2775}{1 - 0.2775} = \frac{1.2775}{0.7225} \approx 1.768$$

**(iii) Input impedance at $z' = \lambda/8$:**

At $z' = \lambda/8$: $\beta z' = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{8} = \frac{\pi}{4}$, so $\tan(\beta z') = \tan(\pi/4) = 1$.

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta z')}$$

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{(75 + j25) + j(50)(1)}{50 + j(75 + j25)(1)} = 50 \cdot \frac{75 + j25 + j50}{50 + j75 - 25}$$

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{75 + j75}{25 + j75} = 50 \cdot \frac{3 + j3}{1 + j3}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - j3)$:

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{(3 + j3)(1 - j3)}{(1 + j3)(1 - j3)} = 50 \cdot \frac{3 - j9 + j3 - j^2 9}{1 + 9} = 50 \cdot \frac{3 + 9 + j(-6)}{10}$$

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{12 - j6}{10} = 50 \cdot (1.2 - j0.6) = 60 - j30 \; \Omega$$

**Result:** $\Gamma \approx 0.231 + j0.154$ with $\vert \Gamma \vert \approx 0.278$, $\text{SWR} \approx 1.77$, $Z_{in} = 60 - j30 \, \Omega$. (7 marks)

**(b) Derivation of Input Impedance and Quarter-Wave Inverter:**

Starting from the general voltage and current solutions on a lossless line with the load at $z = 0$:

$$V_s(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$$

$$I_s(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

At the load ($z = 0$): $V_s(0) = Z_L \, I_s(0)$ gives the reflection coefficient $\Gamma = V_0^-/V_0^+ = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$.

The input impedance at distance $z'$ from the load (i.e., at $z = -z'$) is:

$$Z_{in}(z') = \frac{V_s(-z')}{I_s(-z')} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{1 + \Gamma e^{-2j\beta z'}}{1 - \Gamma e^{-2j\beta z'}}$$

Substituting $\Gamma$ and simplifying using $\cos(2\theta) = 1 - 2\sin^2\theta$ and $\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$:

$$Z_{in}(z') = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta z')}$$

**Quarter-Wave Impedance Inverter:**

For a quarter-wavelength line, $z' = \lambda/4$, so $\beta z' = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} = \pi/2$. Since $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$, the input impedance formula reduces to:

$$Z_{in}(\lambda/4) = Z_0 \cdot \frac{jZ_0 \tan(\pi/2)}{jZ_L \tan(\pi/2)} = \frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$$

This result shows that a quarter-wave line transforms the load impedance according to $Z_{in} \cdot Z_L = Z_0^2$ — a product relationship. Choosing $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_S Z_L}$ allows a quarter-wave segment to match a source impedance $Z_S$ to a load $Z_L$. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning — Most Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units correctly.** $L$ in $\mu\text{H/m}$ must be converted to $\text{H/m}$ (multiply by $10^{-6}$) before plugging into formulas. $C$ in $\text{pF/m}$ must be converted to $\text{F/m}$ (multiply by $10^{-12}$).
> 2. **Confusing distance-from-load vs. distance-from-source.** The variable $z'$ in the input impedance formula is the distance **from the load**, not from the source. Drawing a small diagram of the line with the load on the right and the observation point on the left is highly recommended.
> 3. **Forgetting the sign in the current wave.** The backward current wave has a **minus sign**: $I_s = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$. This arises because the backward wave propagates in the $-z$ direction.
> 4. **Using $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ without checking lossless assumption.** This expression for $\beta$ is valid only when $R = 0$ and $G = 0$. In a lossy line, $\beta$ is the imaginary part of the more complex propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$.
> 5. **Leaving the answer in non-decimal form.** KTU examiners expect numerical answers in decimal form (e.g., $50 \, \Omega$ not $\sqrt{2500} \, \Omega$). The final numerical value must be clearly boxed or underlined.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lossless condition:** $R = 0$, $G = 0$. Only $L$ and $C$ remain.
- **Propagation constant:** $\gamma = j\beta$ (purely imaginary), where $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ rad/m.
- **Phase velocity:** $v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ m/s. For a dielectric-filled line, $v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$.
- **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ ohms — **real, frequency-independent, positive**.
- **General voltage:** $V_s(z) = V_0^+ e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^- e^{+j\beta z}$ (forward + backward).
- **General current:** $I_s(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$.
- **SWR:** $\frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} \geq 1$. SWR = 1 for matched load.
- **Input impedance formula (most important):** $Z_{in}(z') = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta z')}$.
- **Short-circuited line:** $Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta z')$.
- **Open-circuited line:** $Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta z')$.
- **Matched load:** $Z_{in} = Z_0$ at any distance; no reflections, no standing waves.
- **Quarter-wave transformer:** $Z_{in}(\lambda/4) = \frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$ — use $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_S Z_L}$ to match source to load.
- **Half-wave line:** $Z_{in}(\lambda/2) = Z_L$ — impedance repeats every half-wavelength.
- **Standing waves arise from the interference of forward and backward traveling waves.** Maxima of $\vert V \vert$ occur at voltage antinodes, minima at voltage nodes. The distance between successive maxima is $\lambda/2$.
- **Time-harmonic assumption:** $e^{j\omega t}$ convention is used throughout. In KTU solutions, always state this assumption explicitly when deriving phasor equations.
- **Energy storage (lossless):** Energy oscillates between magnetic field storage in $L$ and electric field storage in $C$. No net average power is dissipated in the line itself; the only power loss is in the load.
- **Exam mantra:** When in doubt, draw the line, label the load at $z = 0$, mark the observation point at $z = -z'$, write down $V_s$ and $I_s$, apply the boundary condition $V_s(0) = Z_L I_s(0)$, solve for $\Gamma$, and substitute into the input impedance formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line Equations, Voltage and Current Distribution of a Line Terminated with Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line Equations, Voltage and Current Distribution of a Line Terminated with Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Transmission Line** is a guided medium that transports electromagnetic energy or signals from one point to another. Unlike a simple wire (assumed to have lumped behavior), a transmission line is treated as a **distributed parameter network** because its physical length is comparable to (or a significant fraction of) the wavelength of the propagating signal. It is fully characterised by four primary line constants per unit length:

- **R** (Ω/m) — Series resistance of the conductors (accounts for ohmic/conductor loss).
- **L** (H/m) — Series inductance (accounts for the magnetic field around the conductors).
- **G** (S/m) — Shunt conductance of the dielectric (accounts for dielectric leakage loss).
- **C** (F/m) — Shunt capacitance (accounts for the electric field between the conductors).

The **Transmission Line Equations** (popularly known as the **Telegrapher's Equations**) are a coupled pair of first-order partial differential equations that govern the spatial and temporal variation of voltage $v(x,t)$ and current $i(x,t)$ along such a distributed line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The derivation of the Telegrapher's equations from the distributed model, their conversion into wave equations, and the resulting general solution for voltage and current are **guaranteed high-yield questions** in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). Mastery of the boundary conditions and the reflection coefficient is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Model

Imagine a long, flexible water hose connected to a tap. The water flow has a definite **inertia** (corresponds to **L**), the hose walls offer **friction** (corresponds to **R**), the rubber can **stretch and store pressure** (corresponds to **C**), and there may be small **leaks** through the hose (corresponds to **G**).

Now, if the tap is suddenly closed, a pressure wave travels back up the hose — this is exactly what happens in a transmission line when the receiving end is **mismatched**: a forward wave meets an obstacle and a **reflected wave** is sent back. If the nozzle end is left fully **open** (no obstruction), almost no reflection occurs — this is the **matched condition**.

A more precise EM analogy is the **long whip** or **stretched rope**: a sharp flick sends a wave down the rope. If the far end is tied to a wall (short circuit), the wave reflects inverted. If it is free (open circuit), it reflects non-inverted. If the rope is connected to another identical rope (matched), the wave is fully absorbed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need this theory (not just Ohm's Law):** When the line length $l$ approaches $\lambda/10$ or more of the signal frequency, the voltage and current are no longer constant along the line. They become functions of position, and the line must be treated as a **wave-guiding structure** — exactly what a coaxial cable, microstrip, or waveguide is.

### 1.3 When is Transmission Line Theory Essential?

| Condition | Governing Theory |
|---|---|
| $l \ll \lambda$ (lumped regime) | Kirchhoff's Laws suffice |
| $l \geq \lambda/10$ | **Transmission Line Theory (required)** |
| Optical frequencies / waveguides | Full Maxwell's Equations |

A signal with $f = 3\,\text{GHz}$ has $\lambda = 10\,\text{cm}$ in free space. Therefore, even a PCB trace of just a few centimetres becomes a transmission line. This is the foundation of **high-speed digital design** and **RF/microwave engineering**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Distributed Parameter Model

Consider an infinitesimal section of line of length $\Delta x$ located at position $x$ from the sending end. The line is modelled as:

- A **series impedance** $Z\,\Delta x = (R + j\omega L)\,\Delta x$
- A **shunt admittance** $Y\,\Delta x = (G + j\omega C)\,\Delta x$

where $Z = R + j\omega L$ is the **series impedance per unit length** and $Y = G + j\omega C$ is the **shunt admittance per unit length**.

### 2.2 Derivation Logic of the Telegrapher's Equations (Phasor Form)

Assuming time-harmonic excitation with phasors $V(x)$ and $I(x)$ (so $\partial/\partial t \to j\omega$):

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the outer loop of the section:**

The voltage drop across the series element must equal the difference between input and output voltages:
$$V(x) - V(x+\Delta x) = (R + j\omega L)\,\Delta x \cdot I(x)$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the shunt node:**

The current lost into the shunt equals the difference between input and output currents:
$$I(x) - I(x+\Delta x) = (G + j\omega C)\,\Delta x \cdot V(x+\Delta x)$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$:**

After dividing by $\Delta x$ and taking the limit, we obtain the famous pair:

$$\frac{dV(x)}{dx} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I(x) = -Z\,I(x)$$

$$\frac{dI(x)}{dx} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V(x) = -Y\,V(x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations are the **frequency-domain (phasor) Telegrapher's Equations**. They are the starting point for everything that follows in this module. Memorise them.

### 2.3 The Wave Equation

Differentiating the first equation w.r.t. $x$ and substituting the second:
$$\frac{d^{2}V(x)}{dx^{2}} = -Z\,\frac{dI}{dx} = -Z(-Y\,V) = ZY\,V(x)$$

Defining the **propagation constant** $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY}$:
$$\frac{d^{2}V(x)}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2}\,V(x)$$

Similarly: $\dfrac{d^{2}I(x)}{dx^{2}} = \gamma^{2}\,I(x)$.

### 2.4 General Solution

The general solution of the second-order linear ODE yields a **forward (incident) wave** and a **backward (reflected) wave**:

$$V(x) = V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}$$

$$I(x) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{+\gamma x}$$

where:

- $V^{+}, V^{-}$ — complex amplitudes of the forward and backward waves.
- $Z_{0} = \sqrt{Z/Y} = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)/(G + j\omega C)}$ — the **Characteristic Impedance** of the line. It is the impedance the line presents to a matched source, i.e. the ratio of voltage to current for the forward wave alone.

### 2.5 Physical Interpretation of $\gamma$

The propagation constant is in general complex: $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$

- $\alpha$ (Np/m) — **Attenuation constant** — governs the rate of amplitude decay.
- $\beta$ (rad/m) — **Phase constant** — governs the rate of phase rotation.

The wavelength is $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ and the phase velocity is $v_{p} = \omega/\beta$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **rapid-revision sheet** you should be able to write from memory before entering the exam hall.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series impedance / unit length | $Z = R + j\omega L$ | $\Omega/\text{m}$ |
| 2 | Shunt admittance / unit length | $Y = G + j\omega C$ | $\text{S}/\text{m}$ |
| 3 | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \alpha + j\beta$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_{0} = \sqrt{Z/Y}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Forward voltage wave | $V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x}$ | Travels $+x$ |
| 6 | Reflected voltage wave | $V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}$ | Travels $-x$ |
| 7 | Phase velocity | $v_{p} = \omega/\beta$ | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | Wavelength | $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 9 | Load reflection coefficient | $\Gamma_{L} = (Z_{L} - Z_{0})/(Z_{L} + Z_{0})$ | Dimensionless, $\vert\Gamma\vert \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Reflection coefficient at distance $d$ from load | $\Gamma(d) = \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2\gamma d}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | VSWR (lossless line) | $\rho = (1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert)$ | $\rho \geq 1$ |
| 12 | Input impedance at distance $d$ from load | $Z_{\text{in}}(d) = Z_{0}\,(1 + \Gamma(d))/(1 - \Gamma(d))$ | $\Omega$ |
| 13 | Hyperbolic form of $Z_{\text{in}}$ | $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}\,(Z_{L} + Z_{0}\tanh\gamma d)/(Z_{0} + Z_{L}\tanh\gamma d)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 14 | Lossless $Z_{\text{in}}$ ($\gamma = j\beta$) | $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}\,(Z_{L} + jZ_{0}\tan\beta d)/(Z_{0} + jZ_{L}\tan\beta d)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 15 | Lossless $Z_{0}$ ($R = G = 0$) | $Z_{0} = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Purely real |
| 16 | Lossless $\beta$ | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | $\text{rad}/\text{m}$ |
| 17 | Lossless $v_{p}$ | $v_{p} = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 18 | Return loss | $\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma_{L}\vert$ | dB |

### 2.7 Line Terminated with Load — The General Voltage & Current Distribution

Place the load $Z_{L}$ at $x = 0$ (i.e. coordinate $d$ is measured from the load toward the generator). At the load end ($d = 0$):

$$V_{L} = V^{+} + V^{-}, \quad I_{L} = (V^{+} - V^{-})/Z_{0}$$

Dividing:
$$Z_{L} = \frac{V_{L}}{I_{L}} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + V^{-}/V^{+}}{1 - V^{-}/V^{+}} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + \Gamma_{L}}{1 - \Gamma_{L}}$$

Solving for the load reflection coefficient:
$$\Gamma_{L} = \frac{Z_{L} - Z_{0}}{Z_{L} + Z_{0}}$$

The complete voltage and current distributions along the line (as functions of $d$ from the load) are:

$$V(d) = V^{+}\left[e^{+\gamma d} + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-\gamma d}\right]$$

$$I(d) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\left[e^{+\gamma d} - \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-\gamma d}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the exponent $+\gamma d$ for the forward wave?** When $d$ is measured *from the load* going *toward the source*, the forward (incident) wave actually travels from the source **toward** the load as $d$ decreases. Hence the exponent flips sign relative to the source-based coordinate $x$.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB design**: Controlled-impedance traces ($Z_{0} = 50\,\Omega$ common) are used at microwave and high-speed digital frequencies.
- **Antenna feed networks**: Thevenin-equivalent analysis of any antenna requires the line to be properly terminated ($Z_{L} = Z_{0}$).
- **Power transmission**: At 50/60 Hz, the wavelength is thousands of km, so transmission line theory is rarely needed. But at 400 kV EHV lines above ~100 km, the **distributed capacitance** still matters.
- **Telecommunications**: Coaxial cables carrying RF signals up to several GHz are analysed rigorously using this theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Telegrapher's Equations (Time Domain → Phasor Domain)

**Step 1 — Construct the lumped section model.**

Consider a small segment of length $\Delta x$ at distance $x$ from the sending end. The four distributed elements are:
- Series: $R\,\Delta x$ (resistor) in series with $L\,\Delta x$ (inductor).
- Shunt: $G\,\Delta x$ (conductor) in parallel with $C\,\Delta x$ (capacitor).

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the section.**

The voltage at the left terminal is $v(x,t)$, and the voltage at the right terminal is $v(x+\Delta x, t)$. The drop across the series branch is:
$$v(x,t) - v(x+\Delta x, t) = R\,\Delta x \cdot i(x,t) + L\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial i(x,t)}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the right node.**

The current entering the shunt admittance is $i(x,t) - i(x+\Delta x, t)$, and the voltage across the shunt is $v(x+\Delta x, t)$:
$$i(x,t) - i(x+\Delta x, t) = G\,\Delta x \cdot v(x+\Delta x, t) + C\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial v(x+\Delta x, t)}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\Delta x$ and take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$.**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -R\,i - L\,\frac{\partial i}{\partial t}$$

$$\frac{\partial i}{\partial x} = -G\,v - C\,\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to sinusoidal steady state (phasor form).**

Replace $v(x,t) \to \text{Re}\{V(x)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$ and $i(x,t) \to \text{Re}\{I(x)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$. The time derivatives become $j\omega V$ and $j\omega I$:

$$\boxed{\frac{dV(x)}{dx} = -Z\,I(x), \quad \frac{dI(x)}{dx} = -Y\,V(x)}$$

where $Z = R + j\omega L$ and $Y = G + j\omega C$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Wave Equation

Differentiate the first telegrapher's equation w.r.t. $x$:
$$\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = -Z\,\frac{dI}{dx}$$

Substitute the second telegrapher's equation, $\dfrac{dI}{dx} = -Y\,V$:

$$\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = -Z\,(-Y\,V) = ZY\,V$$

Define $\gamma^{2} = ZY$:
$$\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} - \gamma^{2}\,V = 0$$

This is the classical 1D Helmholtz equation. The same procedure on the current yields an identical equation for $I(x)$.

### 3.3 Solving the Wave Equation — General Solution

The characteristic equation of the ODE is $m^{2} - \gamma^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow m = \pm \gamma$. Thus the general solution is:
$$V(x) = A\,e^{-\gamma x} + B\,e^{+\gamma x}$$

By inspection of the first telegrapher's equation:
$$I(x) = -\frac{1}{Z}\,\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{1}{Z}\,(-\gamma A\,e^{-\gamma x} + \gamma B\,e^{+\gamma x}) = \frac{\gamma}{Z}\,(A\,e^{-\gamma x} - B\,e^{+\gamma x})$$

Since $\gamma/Z = \sqrt{ZY}/Z = \sqrt{Y/Z} = 1/Z_{0}$, we get:

$$\boxed{V(x) = V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}, \quad I(x) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{+\gamma x}}$$

with $Z_{0} = \sqrt{Z/Y}$.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Load Reflection Coefficient $\Gamma_{L}$

At the load ($x = 0$ or $d = 0$):
$$V_{L} = V^{+} + V^{-}, \quad I_{L} = \frac{V^{+} - V^{-}}{Z_{0}}$$

By definition $Z_{L} = V_{L}/I_{L}$:
$$Z_{L} = Z_{0}\,\frac{V^{+} + V^{-}}{V^{+} - V^{-}} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + \Gamma_{L}}{1 - \Gamma_{L}}$$

where $\Gamma_{L} \equiv V^{-}/V^{+}$. Solving for $\Gamma_{L}$:
$$\frac{Z_{L}}{Z_{0}} = \frac{1 + \Gamma_{L}}{1 - \Gamma_{L}} \;\Rightarrow\; \Gamma_{L}\,(Z_{L} + Z_{0}) = Z_{L} - Z_{0}$$

$$\boxed{\Gamma_{L} = \frac{Z_{L} - Z_{0}}{Z_{L} + Z_{0}}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of $\Gamma(d)$ at Distance $d$ from the Load

Using $V^{-} = \Gamma_{L}\,V^{+}$ at the load, the backward wave at a distance $d$ toward the source is:
$$V^{-}(d) = \Gamma_{L}\,V^{+}\,e^{-2\gamma d}$$

Hence:
$$\boxed{\Gamma(d) = \frac{V^{-}(d)}{V^{+}(d)} = \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2\gamma d}}$$

This is a critically important result for **impedance transformation** along a line.

### 3.6 Voltage and Current Distribution for a Terminated Line

Substitute $V^{-} = \Gamma_{L}\,V^{+}$ into the general solution and switch to the load-referenced coordinate $d$ (so the forward wave's exponent becomes $+\gamma d$):

$$V(d) = V^{+}\!\left[e^{+\gamma d} + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-\gamma d}\right]$$

$$I(d) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\!\left[e^{+\gamma d} - \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-\gamma d}\right]$$

**Special Case — Lossless line ($\gamma = j\beta$):**

$$V(d) = V^{+}\!\left[e^{j\beta d} + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-j\beta d}\right] = V^{+}\!\left[2\cos\beta d + j\,(1-\Gamma_{L})\,2\sin\beta d\right]$$

Actually it is more illuminating to keep the bracket form:
$$V(d) = V^{+}\!\left[e^{j\beta d} + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-j\beta d}\right] = V^{+}\,e^{j\beta d}\!\left[1 + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2j\beta d}\right]$$

The magnitude of voltage along the line is:
$$\vert V(d)\vert = \vert V^{+}\vert\,\left\vert 1 + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2j\beta d}\right\vert = \vert V^{+}\vert\,\sqrt{1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert^{2} + 2\vert\Gamma_{L}\vert\cos(2\beta d - \phi)}$$

where $\phi = \arg(\Gamma_{L})$. This is the **standing wave pattern**.

### 3.7 Derivation of VSWR

For a lossless line, the maxima and minima of $\vert V(d)\vert$ occur when $\cos(2\beta d - \phi) = \pm 1$:

- **Maximum**: $V_{\max} = \vert V^{+}\vert\,(1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert)$
- **Minimum**: $V_{\min} = \vert V^{+}\vert\,(1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert)$

The **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)** is:
$$\rho \equiv \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert}$$

**Inverse relations (highly useful for KTU problems):**

$$\vert\Gamma_{L}\vert = \frac{\rho - 1}{\rho + 1}$$

$$V_{\max} \cdot V_{\min} = \vert V^{+}\vert^{2}\,(1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert^{2})$$

### 3.8 Derivation of Input Impedance $Z_{\text{in}}$

From the voltage and current expressions at distance $d$ from the load:

$$Z_{\text{in}}(d) = \frac{V(d)}{I(d)} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2\gamma d}}{1 - \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2\gamma d}} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + \Gamma(d)}{1 - \Gamma(d)}$$

Substituting $\Gamma_{L} = (Z_{L} - Z_{0})/(Z_{L} + Z_{0})$ and simplifying yields the **hyperbolic form**:

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{in}}(d) = Z_{0}\,\frac{Z_{L} + Z_{0}\tanh(\gamma d)}{Z_{0} + Z_{L}\tanh(\gamma d)}}$$

For a lossless line ($\gamma = j\beta$ and $\tanh(j\beta d) = j\tan\beta d$):

$$Z_{\text{in}}(d) = Z_{0}\,\frac{Z_{L} + jZ_{0}\tan(\beta d)}{Z_{0} + jZ_{L}\tan(\beta d)}$$

### 3.9 Special Terminations — Tabulated Limit Cases

| Termination | $Z_{L}$ | $\Gamma_{L}$ | $Z_{\text{in}}(d)$ (lossless) | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Matched** | $Z_{0}$ | $0$ | $Z_{0}$ | Pure travelling wave, no reflection |
| **Short-circuit** | $0$ | $-1$ | $jZ_{0}\tan(\beta d)$ | Total reflection, $\pi$ phase inversion |
| **Open-circuit** | $\infty$ | $+1$ | $-jZ_{0}\cot(\beta d)$ | Total reflection, no phase inversion |
| **Quarter-wave** ($d = \lambda/4$) | any | any | $Z_{0}^{2}/Z_{L}$ | Impedance inverter |
| **Half-wave** ($d = \lambda/2$) | any | any | $Z_{L}$ | Line repeats the load |
| **Purely reactive** | $jX_{L}$ | unit magnitude | reactive | All power reflected |

### 3.10 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 50 Ω lossless transmission line is terminated with $Z_{L} = 75 + j25\,\Omega$. The line is 1.2 m long and operates at 300 MHz. Find:
(a) Reflection coefficient at the load, (b) VSWR, (c) Reflection coefficient at the input end, (d) Input impedance.

**Solution:**

(a) 
$$\Gamma_{L} = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25} = \frac{25(1 + j)}{25(5 + j)} = \frac{1 + j}{5 + j}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:
$$\Gamma_{L} = \frac{(1 + j)(5 - j)}{(5 + j)(5 - j)} = \frac{5 - j + 5j - j^{2}}{25 + 1} = \frac{6 + 4j}{26} = 0.2308 + j\,0.1538$$

(b) Magnitude: $\vert\Gamma_{L}\vert = \sqrt{0.2308^{2} + 0.1538^{2}} = \sqrt{0.0533 + 0.0237} = \sqrt{0.0770} = 0.2775$

$$\rho = \frac{1 + 0.2775}{1 - 0.2775} = \frac{1.2775}{0.7225} = 1.768$$

(c) Wavelength: $\lambda = v_{p}/f$. For a lossless line, $v_{p} = c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$ (assuming air dielectric). 
$\lambda = (3 \times 10^{8})/(300 \times 10^{6}) = 1\,\text{m}$. Thus $d = 1.2\,\text{m} = 1.2\lambda$.

The phase constant $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda = 2\pi\,\text{rad/m}$. So $2\beta d = 2 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 1.2 = 4.8\pi\,\text{rad}$. Reduce modulo $2\pi$: $4.8\pi - 2\cdot 2\pi = 0.8\pi\,\text{rad}$.

$$\Gamma_{\text{in}} = \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-j\,0.8\pi} = (0.2308 + j0.1538)(\cos 0.8\pi - j\sin 0.8\pi)$$
$\cos(0.8\pi) = -0.8090$, $\sin(0.8\pi) = 0.5878$
$$\Gamma_{\text{in}} = (0.2308 + j0.1538)(-0.8090 - j\,0.5878)$$
$$= [0.2308 \cdot (-0.8090) - 0.1538 \cdot (-0.5878)] + j[0.2308 \cdot (-0.5878) + 0.1538 \cdot (-0.8090)]$$
$$= [-0.1867 + 0.0904] + j[-0.1357 - 0.1244] = -0.0963 - j\,0.2601$$

(d) 
$$Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + \Gamma_{\text{in}}}{1 - \Gamma_{\text{in}}} = 50 \cdot \frac{0.9037 - j0.2601}{1.0963 + j0.2601}$$

Multiply by conjugate:
$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{(0.9037 - j0.2601)(1.0963 - j0.2601)}{1.0963^{2} + 0.2601^{2}}$$

Numerator real part: $0.9037 \cdot 1.0963 - (-0.2601)(-0.2601) = 0.9907 - 0.0677 = 0.9230$
Numerator imaginary part: $-0.9037 \cdot 0.2601 - 0.2601 \cdot 1.0963 = -0.2351 - 0.2851 = -0.5202$
Denominator: $1.2019 + 0.0677 = 1.2696$

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{0.9230 - j0.5202}{1.2696} = 50 \cdot (0.7270 - j\,0.4097) = 36.35 - j\,20.48\,\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert your line length to a fraction of the wavelength before plugging into trigonometric functions. This avoids huge argument errors and saves marks in the valuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Distributed Parameter Lumped-Section Model

This diagram shows the infinitesimal $\Delta x$ section of a two-conductor transmission line, including the four distributed parameters.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Terminal: V x, I x] --> B[Series R times dx]
    B --> C[Series j omega L times dx]
    C --> D[Node M]
    D --> E[Shunt G times dx]
    E --> F[Ground ref 0]
    D --> G[Shunt j omega C times dx]
    G --> F
    D --> H[Output Terminal: V at x plus dx, I at x plus dx]
```

### 4.2 Transmission Line Terminated with a Load

```mermaid
graph TD
    Src[Source: Vs, Zg] -->|Incident Wave V pos| Line[Transmission Line: length l, Zo, gamma]
    Line -->|Reflected Wave V neg| Src
    Line -->|Forward wave at load| Load[Load ZL]
    Load -->|Reflection Gamma L| Line
    Load -->|Voltage VL, Current IL| Line
```

### 4.3 Voltage Standing Wave Pattern (Lossless, $|\Gamma_L| = 0.4$)

This diagram visualises the standing wave envelope along a lossless line for a generic mismatch.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source End d equals l] --> B[Region 1: Vmax points]
    B --> C[Voltage Minimum Position]
    C --> D[Region 2: Vmax points]
    D --> E[Load Position d equals 0]
    E --> F[Vmax equals V pos times 1 plus Gamma]
    E --> G[Vmin equals V pos times 1 minus Gamma]
    E --> H[VSWR equals Vmax over Vmin]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow / Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Pgen[Power Generated by Source] --> Vinc[Incident Power P inc]
    Vinc --> Tline[Travels along Transmission Line]
    Tline --> Pdel[Power Delivered to Load P L equals P inc times 1 minus Gamma squared]
    Tline --> Prel[Reflected Power P ref equals P inc times Gamma squared]
    Prel --> Rload[Returns to Source]
    Pdel --> ZL[Absorbed in Load ZL]
    Vinc -.-> Z0[Characteristic Impedance Zo defines the wave]
    Prel -.-> Z0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **reflection coefficient $\Gamma$** is purely a function of the ratio $Z_{L}/Z_{0}$. It is **independent** of the line length. The *position* of the standing wave maxima/minima is, however, determined jointly by $\Gamma_{L}$ and $\beta l$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define the characteristic impedance of a transmission line. What is its value for a lossless line?**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The characteristic impedance $Z_{0}$ of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of the voltage to current of a single travelling wave propagating along an infinitely long (or perfectly matched) line. Mathematically:
$$Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}} \;\; \Omega$$

It is the impedance that the line presents to the source when terminated in such a way that there are no reflections. For a **lossless line** ($R = 0$ and $G = 0$):
$$Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \;\;\; \text{(purely real)}$$

For a typical coaxial cable with air dielectric, $Z_{0}$ is **50 Ω** (general-purpose RF) or **75 Ω** (video/CATV applications).

---

#### **Question 2: What is the Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)? Obtain its expression in terms of the reflection coefficient.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

VSWR (denoted $\rho$ or VSWR) is the ratio of the maximum voltage to the minimum voltage on the standing wave pattern produced on a transmission line due to reflections at a mismatched load:
$$\rho = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}$$

**Derivation:** For a lossless line, the voltage distribution may be written as:
$$V(d) = V^{+}\!\left[1 + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2j\beta d}\right]$$

The magnitude is:
$$\vert V(d)\vert = \vert V^{+}\vert\,\sqrt{1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert^{2} + 2\vert\Gamma_{L}\vert\cos(2\beta d - \phi)}$$

- Maximum when $\cos = +1$: $V_{\max} = \vert V^{+}\vert\,(1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert)$
- Minimum when $\cos = -1$: $V_{\min} = \vert V^{+}\vert\,(1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert)$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert}}$$

The inverse is $\vert\Gamma_{L}\vert = (\rho - 1)/(\rho + 1)$. A perfectly matched line has $\rho = 1$; a totally mismatched line (open or short) has $\rho = \infty$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in the forward-wave exponent** when the coordinate is referenced from the load (use $+\gamma d$, not $-\gamma d$). This costs **up to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting that $\gamma$ is complex** in lossy-line problems — students often drop $\alpha$ and use only $j\beta$. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction for full marks.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** between per-unit-length quantities (R, L in Ω/m, H/m) and the final $Z_{0}$ in Ω. Always sanity-check the units in step 1.
> 4. **Mixing $d$ (load-referenced) and $x$ (source-referenced) coordinates** mid-problem. KTU papers often specify this; re-read the question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Telegrapher's equations for a two-conductor transmission line starting from the equivalent circuit of a distributed section. Hence, obtain the one-dimensional wave equation for voltage on the line. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a lossless transmission line with characteristic impedance $Z_{0} = 50\,\Omega$ and phase velocity $v_{p} = 2 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$, terminated with a load $Z_{L} = 75\,\Omega$, find (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the VSWR, and (iii) the input impedance for a line of length $0.4\lambda$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

This is a structured derivation. (See Section 3.1 above for the full text.) The expected sub-steps are:

1. **Draw** the equivalent circuit of a section $\Delta x$ containing $R\,\Delta x$, $L\,\Delta x$ in series and $G\,\Delta x$, $C\,\Delta x$ in shunt. (1 mark)

2. **Apply KVL** around the section:
$$v(x,t) - v(x+\Delta x, t) = R\,\Delta x \cdot i(x,t) + L\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial i}{\partial t}$$
Rearrange and divide by $\Delta x$:
$$\frac{v(x,t) - v(x+\Delta x, t)}{\Delta x} = R\,i + L\,\frac{\partial i}{\partial t}$$
Take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$:
$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = -R\,i - L\,\frac{\partial i}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

3. **Apply KCL** at the right-hand node:
$$i(x,t) - i(x+\Delta x, t) = G\,\Delta x \cdot v(x+\Delta x, t) + C\,\Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial v}{\partial t}$$
After the same procedure:
$$\frac{\partial i}{\partial x} = -G\,v - C\,\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

4. **Convert to phasor form** (sinusoidal steady state):
$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -Z\,I, \quad \frac{dI}{dx} = -Y\,V \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

5. **Differentiate the first equation and substitute the second** to obtain the wave equation:
$$\frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = ZY\,V = \gamma^{2}\,V \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

6. **State the general solution** and identify $Z_{0}$:
$$V(x) = V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}, \quad I(x) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{+\gamma x}, \quad Z_{0} = \sqrt{Z/Y} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $Z_{0} = 50\,\Omega$, $Z_{L} = 75\,\Omega$, $d = 0.4\lambda$.

**(i) Reflection coefficient:**
$$\Gamma_{L} = \frac{Z_{L} - Z_{0}}{Z_{L} + Z_{0}} = \frac{75 - 50}{75 + 50} = \frac{25}{125} = 0.2$$
Real, positive, no phase shift. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) VSWR:**
$$\rho = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert} = \frac{1 + 0.2}{1 - 0.2} = \frac{1.2}{0.8} = 1.5$$
**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Input impedance at $d = 0.4\lambda$:**

For a lossless line: $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}\,\dfrac{Z_{L} + jZ_{0}\tan(\beta d)}{Z_{0} + jZ_{L}\tan(\beta d)}$.

Compute $\beta d = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot 0.4\lambda = 0.8\pi\,\text{rad}$.
$\tan(0.8\pi) = \tan(144^{\circ}) = -0.7265$.

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{75 + j\,50 \cdot (-0.7265)}{50 + j\,75 \cdot (-0.7265)} = 50 \cdot \frac{75 - j\,36.33}{50 - j\,54.49}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate $(50 + j\,54.49)$:
- Numerator: $(75)(50) + (75)(j\,54.49) + (-j\,36.33)(50) + (-j\,36.33)(j\,54.49)$
  $= 3750 + j\,4087 - j\,1816.5 + 1979.5$
  $= (3750 + 1979.5) + j(4087 - 1816.5)$
  $= 5729.5 + j\,2270.5$
- Denominator: $50^{2} + 54.49^{2} = 2500 + 2969.2 = 5469.2$

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{5729.5 + j\,2270.5}{5469.2} = 50 \cdot (1.0476 + j\,0.4151)$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{in}} = 52.38 + j\,20.76\;\Omega \quad \text{(4 marks)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**  `[KTU University Exam – July 2023]`

**(a) Starting from the general solution of the wave equation on a transmission line, derive the expressions for voltage and current distributions on a line terminated with a load $Z_{L}$. Clearly define the load reflection coefficient and the VSWR. (7 marks)**

**(b) A lossy transmission line has $R = 2\,\Omega/\text{m}$, $L = 250\,\text{nH/m}$, $G = 1\,\text{mS/m}$, $C = 100\,\text{pF/m}$. It operates at 100 MHz and is terminated with a matched load. Find (i) the propagation constant $\gamma$, (ii) the characteristic impedance $Z_{0}$, and (iii) the attenuation in dB over a 50 m length of the line. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. The general solution of the wave equation $d^{2}V/dx^{2} = \gamma^{2}V$ is:
$$V(x) = V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

2. The current follows from the first telegrapher's equation $dV/dx = -ZI$:
$$I(x) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{-\gamma x} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_{0}}\,e^{+\gamma x} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

3. At the load ($x = 0$): $V_{L} = V^{+} + V^{-}$, $I_{L} = (V^{+} - V^{-})/Z_{0}$. Hence:
$$Z_{L} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + V^{-}/V^{+}}{1 - V^{-}/V^{+}} = Z_{0}\,\frac{1 + \Gamma_{L}}{1 - \Gamma_{L}}$$
where $\Gamma_{L} = V^{-}/V^{+}$ is the **load reflection coefficient**. Solving:
$$\Gamma_{L} = \frac{Z_{L} - Z_{0}}{Z_{L} + Z_{0}} \quad \text{...(1.5 marks)}$$

4. The reflection coefficient at distance $d$ from the load (toward the generator) is:
$$\Gamma(d) = \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2\gamma d} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

5. Voltage and current distributions:
$$V(d) = V^{+}\!\left[e^{+\gamma d} + \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-\gamma d}\right] \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$
$$I(d) = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_{0}}\!\left[e^{+\gamma d} - \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-\gamma d}\right] \quad \text{...(0.5 mark)}$$

6. **VSWR:** For a lossless line, the standing wave envelope gives:
$$\rho = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert} \quad \text{(1 mark — define + express)}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $R = 2\,\Omega/\text{m}$, $L = 250 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{H/m}$, $G = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{S/m}$, $C = 100 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$, $f = 100 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$, line length $l = 50\,\text{m}$, matched load $Z_{L} = Z_{0}$.

**(i) Propagation constant $\gamma$:**

$Z = R + j\omega L = 2 + j(2\pi \times 10^{8})(250 \times 10^{-9}) = 2 + j\,157.08\,\Omega/\text{m}$

$Y = G + j\omega C = 10^{-3} + j(2\pi \times 10^{8})(100 \times 10^{-12}) = 10^{-3} + j\,0.06283\,\text{S/m}$

$\gamma = \sqrt{ZY}$. Compute $ZY$:
$$ZY = (2 + j\,157.08)(10^{-3} + j\,0.06283)$$
$$= 2 \cdot 10^{-3} + j\,2 \cdot 0.06283 + j\,157.08 \cdot 10^{-3} - 157.08 \cdot 0.06283$$
$$= 0.002 + j\,0.12566 + j\,0.15708 - 9.8676$$
$$= (0.002 - 9.8676) + j(0.12566 + 0.15708)$$
$$= -9.8656 + j\,0.28274$$

$\vert ZY\vert = \sqrt{9.8656^{2} + 0.28274^{2}} = \sqrt{97.33 + 0.0799} = \sqrt{97.41} = 9.870$

$\angle ZY = \pi - \arctan(0.28274/9.8656) = \pi - 0.02865\,\text{rad} = 3.1130\,\text{rad}$

$\sqrt{\vert ZY\vert} = \sqrt{9.870} = 3.142$, and $\angle \gamma = 1.5565\,\text{rad}$

$$\gamma = 3.142\,(\cos 1.5565 + j\sin 1.5565) = 3.142(0.0132 + j\,0.9999)$$
$$\boxed{\gamma \approx 0.0415 + j\,3.142\;\text{m}^{-1}}$$

So $\alpha = 0.0415\,\text{Np/m}$ and $\beta = 3.142\,\text{rad/m}$. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Characteristic impedance $Z_{0}$:**

$$Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{Z}{Y}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 + j\,157.08}{10^{-3} + j\,0.06283}}$$

$\vert Z\vert = \sqrt{4 + 24674} = \sqrt{24678} = 157.09$
$\angle Z = \arctan(157.08/2) \approx \pi/2 - 0.01273 = 1.5581\,\text{rad}$
$\vert Y\vert = \sqrt{10^{-6} + 3.948 \times 10^{-3}} = \sqrt{3.949 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.06284$
$\angle Y = \pi/2 - \arctan(0.001/0.06283) \approx \pi/2 - 0.01592 = 1.5549\,\text{rad}$

$\vert Z_{0}\vert = 157.09 / 0.06284 = 2500\,\Omega$
$\angle Z_{0} = (1.5581 - 1.5549)/2 = 0.0016\,\text{rad}$

$$\boxed{Z_{0} \approx 2500\,\Omega \quad \text{(2 marks)}}$$

(Note: This is an exaggerated example, but it illustrates the procedure. In practice, real transmission lines have $Z_{0}$ between 30 and 300 Ω.)

**(iii) Attenuation over 50 m:**

Matched load means no reflections, so the attenuation is purely due to $\alpha l$:
$$\text{Attenuation} = 20 \log_{10}(e^{\alpha l}) = 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot l$$
$$= 8.686 \times 0.0415 \times 50 = 18.02\,\text{dB}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Total attenuation} \approx 18.02\,\text{dB} \quad \text{(2 marks)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Match between theory and computation:** $\alpha$ is small (high-quality line) but non-zero — the line is lossy. The attenuation over 50 m is significant because the line is very long electrically ($\beta l \approx 157\,\text{rad} \approx 25\lambda$). A 50 m cable at 100 MHz is **25 wavelengths long**, justifying full transmission-line analysis.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this page and stick it on your study wall before the exam.**

- ✅ **Telegrapher's Equations (phasor form):** $dV/dx = -ZI$ and $dI/dx = -YV$ where $Z = R + j\omega L$ and $Y = G + j\omega C$.
- ✅ **General solution:** Always write $V(x) = V^{+}\,e^{-\gamma x} + V^{-}\,e^{+\gamma x}$ and the corresponding current — the *minus* sign on the reflected current is non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Propagation constant:** $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \alpha + j\beta$. For lossless lines, $\alpha = 0$ and $\gamma = j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$.
- ✅ **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_{0} = \sqrt{Z/Y}$. For lossless lines, $Z_{0} = \sqrt{L/C}$ — **purely real**.
- ✅ **Load reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma_{L} = (Z_{L} - Z_{0})/(Z_{L} + Z_{0})$. It is a *complex* number in general; its magnitude lies in $[0, 1]$ for passive loads.
- ✅ **Reflection at distance $d$ from load:** $\Gamma(d) = \Gamma_{L}\,e^{-2\gamma d}$ — flip exponent sign if using load-referenced coordinate.
- ✅ **VSWR:** $\rho = (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert) \geq 1$. Matched $\Rightarrow \rho = 1$, Open/Short $\Rightarrow \rho = \infty$.
- ✅ **Input impedance (general/hyperbolic):** $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}\,(Z_{L} + Z_{0}\tanh\gamma d)/(Z_{0} + Z_{L}\tanh\gamma d)$.
- ✅ **Input impedance (lossless):** $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}\,(Z_{L} + jZ_{0}\tan\beta d)/(Z_{0} + jZ_{L}\tan\beta d)$.
- ✅ **Quarter-wave transformer:** When $d = \lambda/4$, $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{0}^{2}/Z_{L}$ — used for impedance matching.
- ✅ **Half-wave property:** When $d = \lambda/2$, $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_{L}$ — the line acts as a 1:1 "impedance repeater".
- ✅ **Open-circuit line:** $Z_{\text{in}} = -jZ_{0}\cot\beta d$ — used as a stub.
- ✅ **Short-circuit line:** $Z_{\text{in}} = jZ_{0}\tan\beta d$ — also used as a stub.
- ✅ **Voltage and current distribution for a terminated line:** Always include *both* the forward and reflected terms — the ratio between them is $\Gamma$.
- ✅ **Phase velocity (lossless):** $v_{p} = 1/\sqrt{LC} = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r}}$ (for a line embedded in a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{r}$).
- ✅ **Wavelength (lossless):** $\lambda = v_{p}/f$.
- ✅ **Power delivered to load:** $P_{L} = P_{\text{inc}}\,(1 - \vert\Gamma_{L}\vert^{2})$ — the rest is reflected.
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall 1:** Sign of exponent — confirm whether the coordinate $x$ is source-referenced or load-referenced before plugging numbers.
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall 2:** Always carry units through the derivation — $\Omega$, $\text{S}$, $\text{Np/m}$, $\text{rad/m}$, $\text{dB}$ — partial credit is often awarded for correct units alone.
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall 3:** When $\gamma$ is complex in a problem, do **not** approximate to $j\beta$ unless the question explicitly says lossless. Lossy-line input impedance uses the **hyperbolic tangent**, not the tangent function.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection Coefficient and VSWR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT411 - Electromagnetic Waves
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reflection Coefficient and VSWR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reflection Coefficient — Formal Definition

In a transmission line, when a guided electromagnetic wave encounters a discontinuity (such as a load impedance that does not match the characteristic impedance of the line), a portion of the incident wave is sent back toward the source. This returned wave is called the **reflected wave**.

The **Reflection Coefficient** (denoted by the Greek capital letter Gamma, $\Gamma$) is formally defined as the **complex ratio of the reflected voltage wave to the incident voltage wave at any point on the transmission line**.

$$\Gamma = \frac{V^{-}}{V^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

where:
- $V^{-}$ = phasor of the reflected voltage wave
- $V^{+}$ = phasor of the incident voltage wave
- $Z_L$ = load impedance (in ohms, $\Omega$)
- $Z_0$ = characteristic impedance of the transmission line (in ohms, $\Omega$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The reflection coefficient is a **complex quantity** in general. It possesses both a magnitude $|\Gamma|$ and a phase angle $\theta$. It is **not** a real scalar except in the special case of purely resistive loads.

## 1.2 Standing Wave Ratio (SWR) — Formal Definition

When the incident and reflected waves coexist on a lossless transmission line, they superpose to form a stationary interference pattern known as a **standing wave**. The ratio of the maximum voltage to the minimum voltage on this standing wave pattern is called the **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)**, or simply **SWR**.

$$VSWR = \frac{\vert V \vert_{max}}{\vert V \vert_{min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

The value of VSWR is a **real, dimensionless, positive number** ranging from **1** (matched line) to **$\infty$** (open or short circuit).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a heavy cart on a long rope. The cart is the "load." If the cart is heavy (high $Z_L$) or completely free (open circuit, $\infty \Omega$), pushing generates tension waves that travel back toward your hands — these are the reflected waves. The harder/farther the impedance mismatch, the stronger the kickback.

- **Matched line ($\Gamma = 0$):** Smooth push, no kickback, all energy delivered to load (perfect absorption, like a perfectly tuned shock absorber).
- **Open circuit ($Z_L = \infty$):** Complete reflection with same polarity ($\Gamma = +1$).
- **Short circuit ($Z_L = 0$):** Complete reflection with inverted polarity ($\Gamma = -1$).
- **VSWR = 1:** Perfect match — no standing waves, no "bumps" or "dips" along the line.
- **VSWR > 1:** Standing waves appear — fixed points of maximum and minimum voltage along the line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Insight:** The standing wave does not "travel" — its peaks and troughs stay at fixed locations on the line. Only the *envelope* of the wave is stationary, hence the name "standing wave."

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Special Values

| Condition | $Z_L$ | $\Gamma$ | VSWR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Matched Line | $Z_L = Z_0$ | 0 | 1 |
| Open Circuit | $Z_L = \infty$ | +1 | $\infty$ |
| Short Circuit | $Z_L = 0$ | $-1$ | $\infty$ |
| Pure Resistive Mismatch | $Z_L = 2 Z_0$ | +1/3 | 2 |
| Purely Reactive Load | Pure $jX$ | $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$ | $\infty$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wave Reflection

The total voltage on a lossless transmission line at any position $z$ and time $t$ is the superposition of the incident and reflected waves:

$$V(z,t) = V_0^{+} e^{j(\omega t - \beta z)} + V_0^{-} e^{j(\omega t + \beta z)}$$

When we evaluate this along the line, the magnitude of $V(z,t)$ oscillates with a periodicity of $\lambda / 2$ (half wavelength). The maxima occur where the two waves add constructively, and the minima occur where they cancel.

### Step-by-step logic of standing wave formation:
1. **Forward wave** ($V^{+}$) propagates from source toward load with phase velocity $v_p$.
2. **Reflection event** occurs at the load due to impedance mismatch.
3. **Reflected wave** ($V^{-}$) propagates backward toward the source.
4. **Superposition** of $V^{+}$ and $V^{-}$ creates a stationary interference pattern.
5. **Nodes (minima)** and **antinodes (maxima)** are fixed at specific positions.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Reflection Coefficient Formula

Using the boundary condition that total voltage equals $I \times Z_L$ at the load ($z = 0$):

$$V_L = I_L \cdot Z_L \implies V_0^{+} + V_0^{-} = (V_0^{+} - V_0^{-}) \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_0}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

## 2.3 Position-Dependent Reflection Coefficient

The reflection coefficient is generally a function of position along the line:

$$\Gamma(z) = \Gamma_L \, e^{-j 2 \beta z}$$

where:
- $\Gamma_L$ = load reflection coefficient (at $z = 0$)
- $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ = phase constant
- $z$ = distance measured from the load toward the source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The **magnitude** $\vert \Gamma \vert$ is constant along a lossless line, but the **phase** rotates by $2\beta z$ as you move along the line.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection Coefficient at Load | Complex |
| 2 | $\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1}$ | Magnitude of $\Gamma$ from VSWR | Real, $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | Real, $[1, \infty)$ |
| 4 | $RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$ | Return Loss | Decibels (dB) |
| 5 | $ML = -10 \log_{10}(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^{2})$ | Mismatch Loss | Decibels (dB) |
| 6 | $\Gamma(z) = \Gamma_L \, e^{-j 2\beta z}$ | Reflection at distance $z$ from load | Complex |
| 7 | $Z_{in}(z) = Z_0 \dfrac{1 + \Gamma(z)}{1 - \Gamma(z)}$ | Input impedance from $\Gamma$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $P_{\text{ref}} / P_{\text{inc}} = \vert \Gamma \vert^{2}$ | Power Reflection Ratio | Real, $[0,1]$ |
| 9 | $\theta_{\Gamma} = \angle \Gamma$ | Phase of Reflection Coefficient | Radians or Degrees |
| 10 | $V_{\max} = \vert V^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$ | Maximum voltage on line | Volts |
| 11 | $V_{\min} = \vert V^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$ | Minimum voltage on line | Volts |
| 12 | $d_{\min} = \dfrac{\theta_{\Gamma}}{2\beta}$ (with sign convention) | Position of First Voltage Minimum | Meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** In all the above expressions, $\vert \Gamma \vert$ denotes the **magnitude** (absolute value) of the complex reflection coefficient.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna Engineering:** VSWR is the primary metric for evaluating antenna matching. Cellular base stations require VSWR < 1.5:1 across operating bands.
- **RF/Microwave Design:** Reflection coefficient and S-parameters govern impedance matching network design (using Smith Charts).
- **Telecommunications:** High VSWR can damage transmitters due to reflected power; isolators and circulators are used as protection.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI):** Coil matching to 50 $\Omega$ systems is critical for SNR optimization.
- **Radar Systems:** Mismatch loss directly reduces effective radiated power and detection range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Reflection Coefficient from Boundary Conditions

### Starting Point
On a lossless transmission line, the voltage and current are described by:

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^{-} e^{+j\beta z}$$

$$I(z) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

### Step 1: Apply Load Boundary Condition
At the load location $z = 0$, Ohm's law demands:

$$V(0) = I(0) \cdot Z_L$$

### Step 2: Substitute $z = 0$ Expressions

$$V_0^{+} + V_0^{-} = \left(\frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0}\right) \cdot Z_L$$

### Step 3: Multiply Both Sides by $Z_0$

$$Z_0 V_0^{+} + Z_0 V_0^{-} = V_0^{+} Z_L - V_0^{-} Z_L$$

### Step 4: Collect $V_0^{-}$ Terms on One Side

$$Z_0 V_0^{-} + V_0^{-} Z_L = V_0^{+} Z_L - Z_0 V_0^{+}$$

$$V_0^{-}(Z_0 + Z_L) = V_0^{+}(Z_L - Z_0)$$

### Step 5: Define Reflection Coefficient

$$\frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

$$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula** for the load reflection coefficient.

## 3.2 Derivation: VSWR in Terms of Voltage Maxima and Minima

The magnitude of total voltage at any point $z$ on a lossless line is:

$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^{-} e^{+j\beta z} \vert$$

### Step 1: Factor Out the Common Phase Term

$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\beta z} \vert \cdot \vert 1 + \Gamma e^{+j 2\beta z} \vert$$

Since $\vert e^{-j\beta z} \vert = 1$:

$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \cdot \vert 1 + \Gamma e^{+j 2\beta z} \vert$$

### Step 2: Apply Triangle Inequality (Max/Min Conditions)

The expression $\vert 1 + \Gamma e^{j 2\beta z} \vert$ is maximum when $2\beta z + \angle \Gamma = 0$ (phases align) and minimum when they differ by $\pi$.

$$V_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

$$V_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio

$$VSWR = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $|\Gamma|$ in Terms of VSWR

Let $S = VSWR$:

$$S(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$$

$$S - S\vert \Gamma \vert = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$$

$$S - 1 = \vert \Gamma \vert (S + 1)$$

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1} = \frac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 50 $\Omega$ lossless transmission line is terminated with a load $Z_L = 75 + j25 \, \Omega$. Find (a) the reflection coefficient, (b) the VSWR, and (c) the return loss.

### Part (a): Reflection Coefficient

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25}$$

To rationalize, multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$\Gamma = \frac{(25 + j25)(125 - j25)}{(125 + j25)(125 - j25)}$$

**Numerator expansion:**

$$= (25)(125) + (25)(-j25) + (j25)(125) + (j25)(-j25)$$

$$= 3125 - j625 + j3125 - j^2 (625)$$

$$= 3125 - j625 + j3125 + 625$$

$$= 3750 + j2500$$

**Denominator expansion:**

$$(125)^2 + (25)^2 = 15625 + 625 = 16250$$

Therefore:

$$\Gamma = \frac{3750 + j2500}{16250} = 0.2308 + j0.1538$$

**Magnitude:**

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \sqrt{(0.2308)^2 + (0.1538)^2} = \sqrt{0.0533 + 0.0237} = \sqrt{0.0770} \approx 0.2775$$

**Phase:**

$$\theta_{\Gamma} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.1538}{0.2308}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.6667) \approx 33.69^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\Gamma \approx 0.2775 \angle 33.69^\circ}$$

### Part (b): VSWR

$$VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 0.2775}{1 - 0.2775} = \frac{1.2775}{0.7225} \approx 1.768$$

$$\boxed{VSWR \approx 1.77 : 1}$$

### Part (c): Return Loss

$$RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert = -20 \log_{10}(0.2775)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.2775) \approx -0.5566$$

$$RL = -20 \times (-0.5566) = 11.13 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{RL \approx 11.13 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Marking Insight:** Always show all intermediate steps for $\Gamma$ rationalization. Marks are awarded for both magnitude and phase computation separately.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Inter-Relationship of All Parameters

| Given $\rightarrow$ Calculate | Formula | Cross-check |
|---|---|---|
| $Z_L, Z_0 \rightarrow \Gamma$ | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Use complex arithmetic |
| $Z_L, Z_0 \rightarrow \vert \Gamma \vert$ | $\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{\vert Z_L - Z_0 \vert}{\vert Z_L + Z_0 \vert}$ | Magnitude separately |
| $\Gamma \rightarrow VSWR$ | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Always $\geq 1$ |
| $VSWR \rightarrow \vert \Gamma \vert$ | $\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1}$ | Always $\leq 1$ |
| $\Gamma \rightarrow RL$ | $RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$ | Always $\geq 0$ dB |
| $RL \rightarrow \vert \Gamma \vert$ | $\vert \Gamma \vert = 10^{-RL/20}$ | Use $10^{-x}$ identity |
| $VSWR \rightarrow ML$ | $ML = -10 \log_{10}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{VSWR-1}{VSWR+1}\right)^2\right]$ | Always $\geq 0$ dB |

## 3.5 Distance Between Successive Voltage Minima

Since $V_{\min}$ occurs whenever $2\beta z + \angle \Gamma = (2n+1)\pi$, the spatial separation is:

$$\Delta z = \frac{2\pi}{2\beta} = \frac{\pi}{\beta} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The distance between **two adjacent voltage minima** (or two adjacent maxima) is always $\lambda/2$, regardless of load impedance. This is often a 2-mark short-answer question.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Reflection & VSWR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Signal Generator] --> B[Transmission Line Z0]
    B --> C{Load ZL Matches Z0?}
    C -- YES: ZL = Z0 --> D[Incident Wave Fully Absorbed]
    C -- NO: ZL ≠ Z0 --> E[Partial or Full Reflection]
    D --> F[VSWR = 1, Γ = 0]
    E --> G[Reflected Wave Travels Back]
    G --> H[Superposition with Incident Wave]
    H --> I[Standing Wave Pattern Forms]
    I --> J[VSWR > 1]
    I --> K[Voltage Maxima at Antinodes]
    I --> L[Voltage Minima at Nodes]
    J --> M[Return Loss RL Computed]
    J --> N[Mismatch Loss ML Computed]
    M --> O[Match Quality Metric]
    N --> P[Efficiency Metric]
    O --> Q[Smith Chart Tuning]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Matching Network Inserted]
    R --> S[Improved VSWR]
```

## 4.2 Reflection Coefficient Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given: ZL, Z0] --> B[Compute Numerator: ZL - Z0]
    B --> C[Compute Denominator: ZL + Z0]
    C --> D[Divide Numerator by Denominator]
    D --> E[Extract Magnitude vert Gamma vert]
    D --> F[Extract Phase angle Gamma]
    E --> G[Compute VSWR = 1+vert Gamma vert / 1-vert Gamma vert]
    F --> H[Determine position of minima]
    G --> I[Return Loss RL = -20 log10]
    I --> J[Final Result Package]
    H --> J
```

## 4.3 Standing Wave Pattern on Mismatched Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Load Position: z=0] --> P1[Voltage Maximum: 1+vert Gamma vert]
    P1 --> N1[Voltage Minimum: 1-vert Gamma vert at distance lambda/4]
    N1 --> P2[Next Voltage Maximum at distance lambda/2 from first]
    P2 --> N2[Next Voltage Minimum at distance lambda/2 from first]
    N2 --> S1[Source End: z=-l]
    P1 -. Standing Wave Pattern .-> P2
    N1 -. Periodicity lambda/2 .-> N2
```

## 4.4 VSWR → Reflection Coefficient → Return Loss — Triangle of Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[VSWR] -->|vert Gamma vert = V-1 / V+1| G[Magnitude of Reflection Coefficient]
    G -->|RL = -20 log10 vert Gamma vert| R[Return Loss dB]
    R -->|vert Gamma vert = 10 to power -RL/20| G
    G -->|VSWR = 1+vert Gamma vert / 1-vert Gamma vert| V
    V -->|ML Formula| M[Mismatch Loss dB]
    M -->|Performance Penalty| P[System Efficiency]
    G -->|S11 = vert Gamma vert in dB| S[S-Parameter S11]
    S -->|Vector Network Analyzer Reading| N[VNA Measurement]
```

```mermaid
    Q -- NO: VSWR>1 --> A2[Reflected Power Returns]
    A2 --> MN[Insert Matching Network]
    MN --> Single[Single Stub Tuner]

```
