# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define reflection coefficient and VSWR. State the range of values for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$ is defined as the ratio of the reflected voltage wave to the incident voltage wave at any point on a transmission line:

$$\Gamma = \frac{V^{-}}{V^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

It is a **complex quantity** in general. Its magnitude lies in the range $\mathbf{0 \leq \vert \Gamma \vert \leq 1}$.

The **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum voltage to the minimum voltage on the standing wave pattern:

$$VSWR = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

It is a **real, dimensionless quantity** with range $\mathbf{1 \leq VSWR < \infty}$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**A 50 $\Omega$ line is terminated in a short circuit. Calculate the reflection coefficient and VSWR.**

**Model Answer:**

For a short circuit, $Z_L = 0$. Substituting into the reflection coefficient formula:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{0 - 50}{0 + 50} = -1$$

So $\Gamma = -1$ (magnitude is 1, phase is $180^\circ$).

For VSWR:

$$VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 1}{1 - 1} = \frac{2}{0} = \infty$$

Therefore, $\Gamma = -1$ and $VSWR = \infty$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the reflection coefficient at the load of a transmission line in terms of load impedance $Z_L$ and characteristic impedance $Z_0$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A lossless transmission line with $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$ is terminated in a load $Z_L = 30 + j60 \, \Omega$. Determine: (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the VSWR, and (iii) the return loss in dB. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The total voltage and current on a lossless transmission line are:

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-j\beta z} + V_0^{-} e^{+j\beta z}$$

$$I(z) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta z} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta z}$$

At $z = 0$ (load), the boundary condition is $V(0) = I(0) Z_L$:

$$V_0^{+} + V_0^{-} = \left(\frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0}\right) Z_L$$

Multiplying by $Z_0$:

$$Z_0 V_0^{+} + Z_0 V_0^{-} = V_0^{+} Z_L - V_0^{-} Z_L$$

Rearranging:

$$V_0^{-} (Z_0 + Z_L) = V_0^{+} (Z_L - Z_0)$$

$$\boxed{\Gamma = \frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**(i) Reflection Coefficient:**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(30 + j60) - 50}{(30 + j60) + 50} = \frac{-20 + j60}{80 + j60}$$

Rationalize by multiplying by conjugate of denominator:

$$\Gamma = \frac{(-20 + j60)(80 - j60)}{(80 + j60)(80 - j60)}$$

**Numerator:**

$$= (-20)(80) + (-20)(-j60) + (j60)(80) + (j60)(-j60)$$

$$= -1600 + j1200 + j4800 - j^2(3600)$$

$$= -1600 + j1200 + j4800 + 3600$$

$$= 2000 + j6000$$

**Denominator:**

$$= 80^2 + 60^2 = 6400 + 3600 = 10000$$

Therefore:

$$\Gamma = \frac{2000 + j6000}{10000} = 0.2 + j0.6$$

**Magnitude:**

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.36} = \sqrt{0.40} \approx 0.6325$$

**Phase:**

$$\theta_{\Gamma} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.6}{0.2}\right) = \tan^{-1}(3) \approx 71.57^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\Gamma \approx 0.6325 \angle 71.57^\circ}$$

**(ii) VSWR:**

$$VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.6325}{1 - 0.6325} = \frac{1.6325}{0.3675} \approx 4.442$$

$$\boxed{VSWR \approx 4.44 : 1}$$

**(iii) Return Loss:**

$$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.6325) = -20 \times (-0.1992) = 3.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{RL \approx 3.98 \text{ dB}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Define VSWR. Derive the relationship between VSWR and the magnitude of the reflection coefficient. (7 marks)

**(b)** A transmission line has VSWR of 2.5:1. Find: (i) the magnitude of the reflection coefficient, (ii) the return loss in dB, and (iii) the percentage of incident power reflected back to the source. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition:** The **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)** is the ratio of the maximum voltage to the minimum voltage on a lossless transmission line with standing waves:

$$VSWR = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}$$

**Derivation:**

The total voltage magnitude on a line with reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ at any point $z$ is:

$$\vert V(z) \vert = \vert V_0^{+} \vert \cdot \vert 1 + \Gamma e^{j 2\beta z} \vert$$

The expression $\vert 1 + \Gamma e^{j 2\beta z} \vert$ achieves its maximum value $1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$ when $e^{j 2\beta z}$ is in phase with $\Gamma$, and its minimum value $1 - \vert \Gamma \vert$ when $e^{j 2\beta z}$ is in antiphase. Thus:

$$V_{\max} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

$$V_{\min} = \vert V_0^{+} \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

Forming the ratio:

$$VSWR = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

$$\boxed{VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**(i) Magnitude of Reflection Coefficient:**

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1} = \frac{2.5 - 1}{2.5 + 1} = \frac{1.5}{3.5} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

$$\boxed{\vert \Gamma \vert \approx 0.4286}$$

**(ii) Return Loss:**

$$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.4286) = -20 \times (-0.3680) = 7.36 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{RL \approx 7.36 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) Percentage of Reflected Power:**

The reflected power ratio is:

$$\frac{P_{\text{reflected}}}{P_{\text{incident}}} = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = (0.4286)^2 \approx 0.1837$$

Therefore the percentage of incident power reflected is:

$$0.1837 \times 100\% \approx 18.37\%$$

$$\boxed{\approx 18.37\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to rationalize the complex reflection coefficient** when $Z_L$ is complex. Marks are awarded separately for the magnitude and phase components.
> 2. **Conflating VSWR with the reflection coefficient** itself. VSWR is always a real number $\geq 1$; $\Gamma$ can be complex with magnitude $\leq 1$.
> 3. **Sign error in phase of $\Gamma$:** Always use $\tan^{-1}(\text{Im}/\text{Re})$ carefully with the correct quadrant. A complex number in the second quadrant has a phase between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$.
> 4. **Return Loss in dB:** Students often forget the negative sign, resulting in a negative dB value. Return loss is conventionally stated as a **positive** number.
> 5. **Mismatch Loss vs Return Loss confusion:** $ML = -10 \log_{10}(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$ is **not** the same as $RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$. Mismatch loss uses the *log10* of the *transmitted* power ratio.
> 6. **Units of $Z_L$ and $Z_0$:** Always express in $\Omega$; mismatched units lead to wrong results.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Reflection Coefficient $\Gamma$**: Complex ratio $V^{-}/V^{+}$; computed as $(Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$.
- ✅ **Magnitude Constraint**: $0 \leq \vert \Gamma \vert \leq 1$ for passive loads.
- ✅ **VSWR**: Real ratio $V_{\max}/V_{\min}$; range $1 \leq VSWR < \infty$.
- ✅ **VSWR = 1**: Perfectly matched line; no reflection; $\Gamma = 0$.
- ✅ **VSWR = $\infty$**: Open or short circuit; full reflection; $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$.
- ✅ **Return Loss (RL)**: Positive decibel measure of *how little* is reflected; higher is better.
- ✅ **Mismatch Loss (ML)**: Decibel measure of *power lost* due to mismatch; subtracted from transmitted power budget.
- ✅ **Power Reflection Ratio**: $P_r / P_i = \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ (NOT $\vert \Gamma \vert$).
- ✅ **Voltage Minima Spacing**: Adjacent minima are $\lambda/2$ apart; adjacent max-min spacing is $\lambda/4$.
- ✅ **Position of $V_{\min}$**: First minimum occurs at $d_{\min} = \lambda/4$ (approximately) for typical mismatches, with sign and exact value depending on $\angle \Gamma$.
- ✅ **Lossless Line Invariant**: $\vert \Gamma \vert$ is constant along the line; only phase rotates with $e^{-j 2\beta z}$.
- ✅ **Input Impedance from $\Gamma$**: $Z_{in}(z) = Z_0 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \Gamma(z)}{1 - \Gamma(z)}$ — direct link to Smith Chart operations.
- ✅ **S-Parameter $S_{11}$**: Equals $\Gamma$ in dB; the standard measurement on a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA).

### 🎯 KTU Frequently Tested Sub-Topics

1. **Derivation of $\Gamma$ from boundary conditions** (very high weightage).
2. **Numerical computation of $\Gamma$, VSWR, RL** given complex $Z_L$ and real $Z_0$.
3. **Distinguishing VSWR from $\Gamma$** in short-answer questions.
4. **Position of voltage maxima and minima** in standing wave patterns.
5. **Conversion between VSWR, $\Gamma$, RL, and ML** (inter-formula conversions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always show the full complex arithmetic explicitly. Do not skip rationalization steps. The examiner's marking scheme allocates marks for: (i) correct formula (2), (ii) correct substitution (2), (iii) correct rationalization (2), (iv) magnitude & phase (1 each), and (v) final VSWR/RL/ML values. **Practice at least 5 numerical problems** before the exam!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Derivation of Input Impedance of Transmission Line

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Exam Verbatim):**
> The **Input Impedance** of a transmission line is the ratio of the total voltage to the total current at any arbitrary point on the line, measured while looking into the line from that point toward the load. It is a complex quantity that fully characterizes the standing-wave behaviour and the matching condition of the line.

Mathematically, the input impedance at a point located at distance $l$ from the load is defined as:

$$Z_{in}(l) = \frac{V(-l)}{I(-l)}$$

where $V(-l)$ and $I(-l)$ are the **total phasor voltage and current** (incident + reflected wave contribution) at that point, and the reference for $z = 0$ is taken at the **load terminal**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Echo Pipe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition for KTU Students:**
> Imagine shouting into a long, narrow pipe that opens into a large hall. If the hall is *closed* at the far end, the sound wave hits the wall and **bounces back**, creating a complex pressure pattern inside the pipe — some points feel loud, others feel silent. The *acoustic impedance* you feel at your end of the pipe is NOT just the impedance of the air — it is modified by **how the closed end reflects the wave back into the pipe**.
>
> A transmission line behaves the same way. The **load impedance $Z_L$** behaves like the closed/open end of the pipe. The voltage and current you "see" at the generator end (the input) are the *superposition* of the wave going forward and the wave bouncing back from the load. Hence, the input impedance is a function of **both the load impedance AND the electrical length of the line**.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Quantities Used Throughout This Derivation:**
> - **Characteristic Impedance** $Z_0$ — Units: **Ohm (Ω)**
> - **Propagation Constant** $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ — Units: **Np/m + j rad/m**
> - **Phase Constant** $\beta$ — Units: **rad/m**
> - **Attenuation Constant** $\alpha$ — Units: **Np/m**
> - **Reflection Coefficient** $\Gamma$ — Dimensionless (Complex)
> - **Standing Wave Ratio** $S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ — Dimensionless (Real, $\geq 1$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Foundation — Starting Point: General Solution of the Wave Equation

The derivation of input impedance **begins** with the well-known general solution of the **Telegrapher's Equations** for a lossless or lossy transmission line:

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^{-} e^{\gamma z}$$

$$I(z) = \frac{1}{Z_0}\left[V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma z} - V_0^{-} e^{\gamma z}\right]$$

where:
- $V_0^{+}$ — amplitude of the **incident (forward-traveling) voltage wave**
- $V_0^{-}$ — amplitude of the **reflected (backward-traveling) voltage wave**
- $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ — characteristic impedance of the line
- $\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)}$ — complex propagation constant

### Step-by-Step Reasoning Chain

1. **Why this form?** The telegrapher's equations are second-order ODEs whose solutions are linear combinations of $e^{-\gamma z}$ (forward wave) and $e^{+\gamma z}$ (backward wave).
2. **What changes when the line is finite?** A finite line has a load at $z = 0$. The load creates a **boundary condition** that fixes the ratio of reflected to incident amplitude.
3. **What is the load reflection coefficient?** At $z = 0$, the boundary condition $V(0)/I(0) = Z_L$ forces the relationship between $V_0^{+}$ and $V_0^{-}$.
4. **What is the goal?** Eliminate the unknown wave amplitudes $V_0^{\pm}$ and express the ratio $V(z)/I(z)$ purely in terms of $Z_0$, $\gamma$, $l$, and $Z_L$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a Board-Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet. Memorize the boxed relationships; they appear in nearly every Part B question on transmission lines.**

| S.No | Quantity | General (Lossy) Expression | Lossless Form ($\alpha = 0$, $\gamma = j\beta$) | Units / Type |
|------|----------|----------------------------|------------------------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | $\sqrt{L/C}$ | Ohms (Ω), Real |
| 2 | Propagation Constant $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | $j\beta$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | 1/m |
| 3 | Load Reflection Coefficient $\Gamma_L$ | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Same | Dimensionless, Complex |
| 4 | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ (lossy) | $Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$ | $Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)}$ | Ohms, Complex |
| 5 | Input Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma_{in} = \Gamma_L \, e^{-2\gamma l}$ | $\Gamma_L \, e^{-2j\beta l}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | VSWR / SWR | $\dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma_L \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma_L \vert}$ | Same | Real, $\geq 1$ |
| 7 | Matched Line Condition | $Z_L = Z_0 \Rightarrow \Gamma_L = 0 \Rightarrow Z_{in} = Z_0$ | Same | — |
| 8 | Short-Circuit Load ($Z_L = 0$) | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)$ | $j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)$ | Reactive |
| 9 | Open-Circuit Load ($Z_L \to \infty$) | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \coth(\gamma l)$ | $-j Z_0 \cot(\beta l)$ | Reactive |
| 10 | Quarter-Wave Transformer | $l = \lambda/4 \Rightarrow Z_{in} = Z_0^{2} / Z_L$ | $Z_0^{2} / Z_L$ | Real (if $Z_L$ real) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters in practice:**
> - **Antenna Feed Networks** — Engineers must know $Z_{in}$ to design matching stubs that deliver maximum power from the transmitter to the antenna.
> - **PCB Microstrip Design** — High-speed digital traces on PCBs are transmission lines; their $Z_{in}$ determines signal integrity and reflection-induced glitches.
> - **Radar & Microwave Systems** — Impedance matching using $Z_{in}$ analysis minimizes reflections that would otherwise mask weak return signals.
> - **Smith Chart Application** — The Smith Chart is a graphical tool that plots $Z_{in}$ as the line length $l$ varies, allowing rapid matching-network synthesis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of Input Impedance

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board-Exam Strategy:** The derivation is a **favourite 14-mark Part B question**. Examiners reward *every clearly written intermediate step*. A jump from the wave equation to the final boxed formula typically loses 4–5 marks. Follow this derivation line-by-line.

## 3.1 Setup — Coordinate System and Reference Point

Let the **load be located at $z = 0$**, and the **input (generator) end be at $z = -l$** (i.e., the line has length $l$).

Therefore:
- The total voltage and current at the **load** ($z = 0$): $V(0) = V_L$ and $I(0) = I_L$.
- The total voltage and current at the **input** ($z = -l$): $V(-l) = V_{in}$ and $I(-l) = I_{in}$.

The general solution of the telegrapher's equation gives:

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^{-} e^{\gamma z} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$I(z) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V_0^{-}}{Z_0} e^{\gamma z} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

## 3.2 Step 1 — Apply the Boundary Condition at the Load ($z = 0$)

The **load boundary condition** is Ohm's law at $z = 0$:

$$Z_L = \frac{V(0)}{I(0)} = \frac{V_0^{+} + V_0^{-}}{(V_0^{+} - V_0^{-})/Z_0} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

*(Explanation: We substituted $z = 0$ into equations (1) and (2); $e^0 = 1$.)*

Cross-multiplying (3):

$$Z_L (V_0^{+} - V_0^{-}) = Z_0 (V_0^{+} + V_0^{-})$$

Expanding:

$$Z_L V_0^{+} - Z_L V_0^{-} = Z_0 V_0^{+} + Z_0 V_0^{-}$$

Collecting $V_0^{-}$ on the right-hand side and $V_0^{+}$ on the left:

$$Z_L V_0^{+} - Z_0 V_0^{+} = Z_0 V_0^{-} + Z_L V_0^{-}$$

$$(Z_L - Z_0) V_0^{+} = (Z_L + Z_0) V_0^{-}$$

Therefore, the **load reflection coefficient** is:

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is one of the most-tested equations in KTU ESE.** Equation (4) is the seed from which the entire input-impedance formula germinates.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Express the Voltage and Current in Terms of $V_0^{+}$ and $\Gamma_L$

Using $\Gamma_L = V_0^{-}/V_0^{+}$, we can write $V_0^{-} = \Gamma_L V_0^{+}$. Substituting into (1) and (2):

$$V(z) = V_0^{+} \left[ e^{-\gamma z} + \Gamma_L e^{\gamma z} \right] \quad \text{...(5)}$$

$$I(z) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} \left[ e^{-\gamma z} - \Gamma_L e^{\gamma z} \right] \quad \text{...(6)}$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — Evaluate the Voltage and Current at the Input ($z = -l$)

Substituting $z = -l$ into (5) and (6):

$$V(-l) = V_0^{+} \left[ e^{\gamma l} + \Gamma_L e^{-\gamma l} \right] \quad \text{...(7)}$$

$$I(-l) = \frac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} \left[ e^{\gamma l} - \Gamma_L e^{-\gamma l} \right] \quad \text{...(8)}$$

*(Explanation: $e^{-\gamma(-l)} = e^{\gamma l}$ and $e^{\gamma(-l)} = e^{-\gamma l}$. Signs flip because we move backward by distance $l$.)*

## 3.5 Step 4 — Form the Ratio $V(-l)/I(-l)$ to Obtain $Z_{in}$

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V(-l)}{I(-l)} = \frac{V_0^{+} \left[ e^{\gamma l} + \Gamma_L e^{-\gamma l} \right]}{\dfrac{V_0^{+}}{Z_0} \left[ e^{\gamma l} - \Gamma_L e^{-\gamma l} \right]}$$

The factor $V_0^{+}$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{e^{\gamma l} + \Gamma_L e^{-\gamma l}}{e^{\gamma l} - \Gamma_L e^{-\gamma l}} \quad \text{...(9)}$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Substitute $\Gamma_L$ from Equation (4)

Plugging $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ into (9):

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{e^{\gamma l} + \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} e^{-\gamma l}}{e^{\gamma l} - \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} e^{-\gamma l}}$$

Multiply both numerator and denominator by $(Z_L + Z_0)$:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{(Z_L + Z_0) e^{\gamma l} + (Z_L - Z_0) e^{-\gamma l}}{(Z_L + Z_0) e^{\gamma l} - (Z_L - Z_0) e^{-\gamma l}} \quad \text{...(10)}$$

## 3.7 Step 6 — Separate the Exponential Terms

Expand both numerator and denominator of (10):

- **Numerator:**
$$Z_L e^{\gamma l} + Z_0 e^{\gamma l} + Z_L e^{-\gamma l} - Z_0 e^{-\gamma l} = Z_L (e^{\gamma l} + e^{-\gamma l}) + Z_0 (e^{\gamma l} - e^{-\gamma l})$$

- **Denominator:**
$$Z_L e^{\gamma l} + Z_0 e^{\gamma l} - Z_L e^{-\gamma l} + Z_0 e^{-\gamma l} = Z_L (e^{\gamma l} - e^{-\gamma l}) + Z_0 (e^{\gamma l} + e^{-\gamma l})$$

## 3.8 Step 7 — Apply the Hyperbolic Identities

Recall the standard definitions:
$$e^{\gamma l} + e^{-\gamma l} = 2 \cosh(\gamma l)$$
$$e^{\gamma l} - e^{-\gamma l} = 2 \sinh(\gamma l)$$

Substituting into (10):

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L \cdot 2\cosh(\gamma l) + Z_0 \cdot 2\sinh(\gamma l)}{Z_L \cdot 2\sinh(\gamma l) + Z_0 \cdot 2\cosh(\gamma l)}$$

The factor of 2 cancels top and bottom:

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}\,} \quad \text{...(11)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation (11) is the General Input Impedance of a Lossy Transmission Line.** This is the principal result examiners expect on the answer script.

## 3.9 Step 8 — Specialization to a Lossless Line ($\alpha = 0$)

For a **lossless line**, $\alpha = 0$ and $\gamma = j\beta$. Then:

$$\tanh(\gamma l) = \tanh(j\beta l) = \frac{\sinh(j\beta l)}{\cosh(j\beta l)} = \frac{j\sin(\beta l)}{\cos(\beta l)} = j\tan(\beta l)$$

Substituting into (11):

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)}\,} \quad \text{...(12)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation (12) is the form KTU examiners prefer for numerical problems.** Use this when the problem states "lossless", "ideal", or gives $\alpha = 0$.

## 3.10 Step 9 — Sanity Checks (Highly Scored on Board Exams)

> [!TIP]
> **Always verify with limiting cases. Examiners love this!**

**Case A — Matched Load ($Z_L = Z_0$):**
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_0 + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)} = Z_0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Case B — Short-Circuit Load ($Z_L = 0$):**
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{0 + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + 0} = Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l) \quad \checkmark$$

**Case C — Open-Circuit Load ($Z_L \to \infty$):**
Divide numerator and denominator of (11) by $Z_L$:
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{1 + (Z_0/Z_L) \tanh(\gamma l)}{(Z_0/Z_L) + \tanh(\gamma l)} \xrightarrow{Z_L \to \infty} Z_0 \coth(\gamma l) \quad \checkmark$$

**Case D — Quarter-Wave Length ($l = \lambda/4$):**
For lossless, $\beta l = (2\pi/\lambda)(\lambda/4) = \pi/2$, so $\tan(\beta l) \to \infty$. Equation (12) reduces to:
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{j Z_L \tan(\beta l)} = \frac{Z_0^{2}}{Z_L} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Engineering Note:** Below is a block-level functional architecture flow depicting the logical pipeline of the input-impedance derivation, followed by a transmission-line functional block diagram suitable for KTU exam reproduction.

## 4.1 Derivation Pipeline — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["General Wave Solution V z and I z"] --> B["Apply Load Boundary at z = 0"]
    B --> C["Derive Reflection Coefficient Gamma L"]
    C --> D["Substitute V0 minus = Gamma L V0 plus"]
    D --> E["Evaluate at z = minus l (Input End)"]
    E --> F["Form Ratio V minus l over I minus l"]
    F --> G["Substitute Gamma L = ZL minus Z0 over ZL plus Z0"]
    G --> H["Multiply top and bottom by ZL plus Z0"]
    H --> I["Expand using e power gamma l and e power minus gamma l"]
    I --> J["Apply Hyperbolic Identities cosh and sinh"]
    J --> K["Final Lossy Formula Zin = Z0 ZL plus Z0 tanh gamma l over Z0 plus ZL tanh gamma l"]
    K --> L["Set alpha = 0, gamma = j beta for Lossless"]
    L --> M["Final Lossless Formula Zin = Z0 ZL plus j Z0 tan beta l over Z0 plus j ZL tan beta l"]
    M --> N["Sanity Check: Matched, Short, Open, Quarter Wave"]
```

## 4.2 Transmission Line Functional Block — Load to Input

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GEN["Generator / Source"] -->|"Vin, Iin (z = minus l)"| TL["Transmission Line of length l with Z0 and gamma"]
    TL -->|"Forward Wave V0+ e to power minus gamma z"| LOAD["Load ZL at z = 0"]
    LOAD -.->|"Reflected Wave V0 minus e to power gamma z"| TL
    TL -.->|"Impedance Seen Zin at Generator End"| GEN
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Input Impedance Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Boundary Layer"]
        P1["Parameter 1: Z0 Characteristic Impedance"]
        P2["Parameter 2: gamma Propagation Constant"]
        P3["Parameter 3: ZL Load Impedance"]
        P4["Parameter 4: l Line Length"]
    end

    INPUT --> CORE["Input Impedance Computation Engine"]

    subgraph CORE["Computation Core"]
        C1["Compute Gamma L from ZL and Z0"]
        C2["Compute tanh gamma l Term"]
        C3["Form Numerator ZL plus Z0 tanh gamma l"]
        C4["Form Denominator Z0 plus ZL tanh gamma l"]
        C5["Zin = Z0 times Num over Den"]
    end

    CORE --> OUT1["General Lossy Zin"]
    CORE --> OUT2["Lossless Zin when alpha = 0"]
    CORE --> OUT3["Special Case Outputs: Matched, Short, Open, Quarter Wave"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank Mark Distribution (Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 14 marks
> - Below: **Question A** and **Question B** are mutually exclusive; pick one.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**Define input impedance of a transmission line. State the general expression for input impedance in terms of characteristic impedance and load impedance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **input impedance** of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of total phasor voltage to total phasor current at any point on the line, looking toward the load.
>
> **General expression:**
> $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$$
>
> For a **lossless line**, this reduces to:
> $$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)}$$
>
> where $Z_0$ = characteristic impedance, $Z_L$ = load impedance, $l$ = line length, $\beta$ = phase constant, $\gamma$ = propagation constant.

**Marking Split:** Definition 1M · General Formula 1M · Lossless Form 1M.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`
**What is the load reflection coefficient? How is it related to the input impedance of a matched line?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **load reflection coefficient** $\Gamma_L$ is the ratio of the reflected voltage wave amplitude to the incident voltage wave amplitude at the load ($z = 0$):
> $$\Gamma_L = \frac{V_0^{-}}{V_0^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$
>
> When the line is **matched**, $Z_L = Z_0$, which gives:
> $$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_0 - Z_0}{Z_0 + Z_0} = 0$$
>
> Hence **no reflection** occurs, the standing wave ratio becomes **unity (SWR = 1)**, and the **input impedance equals the characteristic impedance** at *every* point on the line:
> $$Z_{in} = Z_0$$

**Marking Split:** Definition of $\Gamma_L$ 1M · Formula 1M · Matched Line Result 1M.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) With Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **DO NOT** directly write the final formula $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$ without showing the steps from the telegrapher's equation. **Penalty: 4–5 marks lost.**
> - **ALWAYS** show the substitution of $z = -l$ and the use of the boundary condition $V(0)/I(0) = Z_L$. Examiners *reward* the boundary condition explicitly.
> - For numerical problems, **convert everything to SI units** and compute $\beta$ *first* before plugging into the formula.

---

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Starting from the general solution of the telegrapherer's equations, derive the expression for the input impedance of a lossy transmission line terminated with a load $Z_L$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lossless transmission line with characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$ and phase velocity $v_p = 2 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$ is terminated with a load $Z_L = 100 + j50 \, \Omega$. If the operating frequency is $f = 500 \, \text{MHz}$ and line length is $l = 0.3 \, \text{m}$, compute the input impedance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3 above**. The expected key steps on the answer script are:

1. Write the **general wave solution** of the telegrapher's equation (1M):
$$V(z) = V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^{-} e^{\gamma z}, \quad I(z) = \frac{V_0^{+} e^{-\gamma z} - V_0^{-} e^{\gamma z}}{Z_0}$$

2. Apply **load boundary condition** at $z = 0$: $V(0)/I(0) = Z_L$ and derive $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ (2M).

3. Express $V$ and $I$ at the **input** $z = -l$ in terms of $V_0^{+}$ and $\Gamma_L$ (2M).

4. Form the **ratio** $V(-l)/I(-l)$ and use the **hyperbolic identities** to arrive at the final boxed formula (2M):
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the wavelength and phase constant:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v_p}{f} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 0.4 \, \text{m}$$

$$\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{0.4} = 5\pi \, \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the electrical length $\beta l$:**

$$\beta l = 5\pi \times 0.3 = 1.5\pi \, \text{rad} = 270^{\circ} = \frac{3\pi}{2} \, \text{rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\tan(\beta l)$:**

$$\tan(1.5\pi) = \tan(3\pi/2) \to \text{undefined (approaches } \pm \infty \text{)}$$

This means the line behaves approximately as a **quarter-wave line**. In practice, take a nearby value or use the limit form:

$$Z_{in} = \lim_{\tan(\beta l) \to \infty} Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)} = \frac{Z_0^{2}}{Z_L}$$

$$Z_{in} = \frac{50^{2}}{100 + j50} = \frac{2500}{100 + j50}$$

**Step 4 — Rationalize the denominator:**

$$Z_{in} = \frac{2500 \cdot (100 - j50)}{(100)^{2} + (50)^{2}} = \frac{250000 - j125000}{12500}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = 20 - j10 \, \Omega}$$

**Verification of marks split:** Wavelength + $\beta$ 2M · $\tan(\beta l)$ + quarter-wave identification 2M · Algebra + rationalization 2M · Final answer with units 1M.

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Derive the input impedance of a lossless transmission line. Show that for a short-circuited load ($Z_L = 0$), the input impedance reduces to $Z_{in} = j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lossless line of characteristic impedance $Z_0 = 75 \, \Omega$ is open-circuited at the load. If the line length is $l = \lambda/8$, find the input impedance and identify the equivalent reactive element. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. State the general lossy formula (from Section 3): 1M
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$$

2. For a **lossless line**, $\alpha = 0$, so $\gamma = j\beta$. Use the identity $\tanh(j\beta l) = j\tan(\beta l)$ (1M).

3. Substitute to get the lossless form (2M):
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)}$$

4. For **short-circuited load**, $Z_L = 0$ (1M):
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{0 + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + 0 \cdot j\tan(\beta l)} = \frac{j Z_0^{2} \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0}$$

5. Simplify to obtain the result (2M):
$$\boxed{Z_{in} = j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

For an **open-circuited line** ($Z_L \to \infty$), the input impedance of a lossless line is obtained by dividing the numerator and denominator of the lossless formula by $Z_L$ and taking the limit:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \lim_{Z_L \to \infty} \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{1}{j \tan(\beta l)} = \frac{Z_0}{j \tan(\beta l)} = -j Z_0 \cot(\beta l)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta l$:**
$$\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{8} = \frac{\pi}{4} \, \text{rad} = 45^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cot(\beta l)$:**
$$\cot(\pi/4) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$Z_{in} = -j \times 75 \times 1 = -j75 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Identify the equivalent element:**
The input impedance is purely imaginary and **negative** ($\text{Im} < 0$), which corresponds to a **capacitive reactance**:
$$X_C = -75 \, \Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{1}{2\pi f \cdot 75}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = -j 75 \, \Omega \equiv \text{Capacitive Equivalent Reactance of } 75 \, \Omega}$$

**Marking Split:** Open-circuit derivation 2M · $\beta l$ computation 2M · $\cot$ evaluation + $Z_{in}$ 2M · Reactance identification 1M.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-Day KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Input impedance definition** = $Z_{in} = V(-l)/I(-l)$, where $l$ is measured **from the load**.
- ✅ **Reference for $z = 0$** must always be the **load** — this is the source of the most common sign error.
- ✅ **Master Equation (Lossy):**
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$$
- ✅ **Master Equation (Lossless):**
$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta l)}$$
- ✅ **Three Limiting Cases to Memorize:**
   - Matched: $Z_{in} = Z_0$
   - Short: $Z_{in} = j Z_0 \tan(\beta l)$
   - Open: $Z_{in} = -j Z_0 \cot(\beta l)$
- ✅ **Quarter-Wave Transformer** ($l = \lambda/4$): $Z_{in} = Z_0^{2} / Z_L$ — a real impedance when $Z_L$ is real.
- ✅ **Half-Wave Property** ($l = \lambda/2$): $Z_{in} = Z_L$ — the line "reproduces" the load at the input.
- ✅ **Sign Convention Pitfall:** When the question gives a length $d$ from the *generator* to the load, the input is at $z = 0$ and the load is at $z = d$. Use $V(d), I(d)$ in the formulas. Adapt coordinate carefully.
- ✅ **Always** show the boundary condition $Z_L = V(0)/I(0)$ in derivations — it is worth 2 marks by itself.
- ✅ **For numerical problems** in lossless lines, compute $\lambda$, $\beta$, and $\beta l$ *first*; *then* compute $\tan(\beta l)$; *then* substitute into the formula.
- ✅ **Smith Chart Link:** $Z_{in}/\text{versus}\, l$ traces a circle on the Smith Chart, which is the geometric visualization of the input-impedance equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board-Exam Mnemonic — "LGV-Hyp-Spec":**
> **L**oad boundary → **G**et $\Gamma_L$ → **V**oltage & Current at $-l$ → apply **HYP**erbolic identities → specialize to **SPEC**ial cases.
> Memorize this 5-step pipeline and you will reproduce the derivation under any exam pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **transmission line**, when analyzed at high frequencies where the wavelength becomes comparable to the physical length of the conductor, can no longer be treated as a simple "short circuit" of zero impedance. Instead, it must be modeled as a **distributed parameter network**, characterized by four **per-unit-length** circuit quantities uniformly spread along its length:

- **$R$** — Series resistance per unit length (Ω/m), accounting for finite conductor resistivity.
- **$L$** — Series inductance per unit length (H/m), arising from the magnetic field surrounding the current-carrying conductors.
- **G** — Shunt (parallel) conductance per unit length (S/m), representing dielectric loss between conductors.
- **$C$** — Shunt capacitance per unit length (F/m), arising from the electric field between the two conductors separated by a dielectric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The two principal reactive elements **$L$** and **$C$** are the focus of this section. They are the dominant parameters at high frequencies (where skin effect pushes $R$ lower and good dielectrics make $G$ negligible), and they dictate the line's **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ and **propagation velocity** $v$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distributed vs. Lumped Distinction:** A lumped element (e.g., a 1 µH inductor) has a single, well-defined value localized at one point. A distributed element has its value *spread* uniformly along the line. We therefore speak of inductance **per meter** (H/m) and capacitance **per meter** (F/m), and the *total* $L$ and $C$ of a line of length $\ell$ are simply $L\ell$ and $C\ell$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy 1 — The Garden Hose (Capacitance):**
Imagine two parallel, thin metal plates separated by a thin layer of air — a parallel-plate capacitor. A long transmission line is essentially just such a capacitor that has been **stretched out in one dimension**. The longer the line, the more "plates" of electric charge you accumulate, and the more total charge you can store at a given voltage. Hence, **total $C$ scales linearly with length**, and we describe it as *capacitance per unit length*.

**Analogy 2 — The Solenoid in Slow Motion (Inductance):**
Whenever current flows in a wire, it generates a magnetic field that "links" the conductor. A transmission line is like a long solenoid that has been unrolled — every infinitesimal slice of the line stores a little bit of magnetic energy, and the total energy stored grows with the length of the line. Hence, **total $L$ scales linearly with length**, giving us *inductance per unit length*.

**Analogy 3 — Spilled Water on a Long Floor (Distributed Nature):**
If you spill water on a 1-meter floor, the puddle is local — the spill does not affect the other end of the floor instantly. But if the floor is 10 km long and you pour water at one end, the water takes *time* to reach the other end. The transmission line is like that long floor: a signal launched at the sending end does not appear at the receiving end instantaneously. This is the essence of "wave propagation," which is governed precisely by the distributed $L$ and $C$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** If you were to naively use a single lumped capacitor $C$ and a single lumped inductor $L$ at the terminals of a long line, your model would be wildly inaccurate for frequencies where the wavelength is shorter than ~10× the line length. The distributed model is *the* foundational model for transmission-line theory in this module.

### 1.3 Physical Constants Used Throughout

- **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** — permeability of free space.
- **$\varepsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m** — permittivity of free space.
- **$c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** — speed of light in vacuum.
- For a dielectric-filled line: $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.
- **$\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377\ \Omega$** — intrinsic impedance of free space.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Equivalent Circuit of an Infinitesimal Slice

To rigorously justify the distributed model, we consider a *differential* slice of line of length $\Delta z \to 0$. The four per-unit-length parameters multiply by $\Delta z$ to give the lumped element values for that slice:

- Series voltage drop across $R\Delta z$ and $L\Delta z$.
- Shunt current through $G\Delta z$ and $C\Delta z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Relationship:** For a lossless line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$), the **characteristic impedance** is
>
> $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}\ \Omega$$
>
> and the **phase velocity** of propagation is
>
> $$v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\ \text{m/s}$$
>
> Notice that **$LC = \mu\varepsilon$** for *all* geometries — this is geometry-independent and is a direct consequence of Maxwell's equations. Therefore,
>
> $$v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$$
>
> This is precisely the speed of an EM wave in the medium — beautifully linking circuit theory back to field theory.

### 2.2 Logic Steps — From Fields to Circuit Parameters

The derivation pipeline from "two conductors with a dielectric between them" to "$L$ and $C$ per unit length" follows a fixed sequence:

1. **Assume a current $I$ flows in one conductor (returning in the other).** Use Ampère's law to find $\vec{H}$ and hence $\vec{B} = \mu\vec{H}$ between the conductors.
2. **Compute the magnetic flux $\Phi$ per unit length** linking the conductors by integrating $\vec{B}$ over the cross-sectional area between them.
3. **Divide flux by current** to get $L = \Phi/I$ per unit length (in henries per meter).
4. **Assume a charge $Q$ per unit length resides on one conductor (with $-Q$ on the other).** Use Gauss's law to find $\vec{E}$ in the dielectric.
5. **Integrate $\vec{E}$ along a path from one conductor to the other** to get the potential difference $V$.
6. **Divide charge by voltage** to get $C = Q/V$ per unit length (in farads per meter).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Geometry | $L$ per unit length | $C$ per unit length | Notes / KTU Tip |
|---|----------|---------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Coaxial cable** (inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, dielectric $\varepsilon$, $\mu$) | $L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ H/m | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ F/m | $LC = \mu\varepsilon$; $Z_0 = \dfrac{\eta}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ |
| 2 | **Parallel-plate line** (width $w$, separation $d$, dielectric $\varepsilon$, $\mu$) | $L = \dfrac{\mu d}{w}$ H/m | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon w}{d}$ F/m | $LC = \mu\varepsilon$; $Z_0 = \eta \dfrac{d}{w}$ |
| 3 | **Two-wire line** (each wire radius $a$, center-to-center separation $D$, $D \gg a$) | $L = \dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{a}\right)$ H/m | $C = \dfrac{\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ F/m | $Z_0 \approx \dfrac{\eta}{\pi}\ln(D/a)$ |
| 4 | **General product (any 2-conductor TEM line)** | — | — | $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ (geometry-free) |
| 5 | **Characteristic impedance (lossless)** | — | — | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ Ω |
| 6 | **Phase velocity** | — | — | $v = 1/\sqrt{LC} = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ m/s |
| 7 | **Intrinsic impedance of medium** | — | — | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ Ω |
| 8 | **Intrinsic impedance of free space** | — | — | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377\ \Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** A common mistake is to confuse the **intrinsic impedance of the medium** ($\eta$, an EM field quantity) with the **characteristic impedance of the line** ($Z_0$, a circuit quantity). They are equal *only* for the parallel-plate line. For coax and two-wire lines, $Z_0 = (\eta/2\pi)\ln(b/a)$ and $(\eta/\pi)\ln(D/a)$ respectively.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coaxial cables** (TV, internet, RF): $L$ and $C$ determine the **$50\ \Omega$ or $75\ \Omega$** industry-standard impedances. The ratio $b/a \approx 3.6$ gives $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$ (video); $b/a \approx 2.3$ gives $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ (RF power).
- **Stripline / microstrip on PCBs**: $L$ and $C$ per unit length set the trace impedance (e.g., $50\ \Omega$ controlled-impedance lines), critical for GHz digital and 5G circuits.
- **Twisted-pair Ethernet (Cat 5/6)**: $L$ and $C$ determine crosstalk, signal integrity, and maximum run length (100 m).
- **Power transmission lines** (overhead): The same $L$ per unit length governs reactive power flow, voltage regulation, and the **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> We will derive $L$ and $C$ from first principles (Maxwell's equations) for the **two most-tested geometries** in KTU exams: **(i) coaxial cable** and **(ii) parallel-plate line**. The two-wire derivation follows the exact same pipeline and is left as an exercise.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coaxial Cable

**Geometry:** Inner conductor of radius $a$ carries current $I$ in $+\hat{z}$. Outer thin shell at radius $b$ returns current $I$ in $-\hat{z}$. The region $a < \rho < b$ is filled with a homogeneous dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$. The line is taken to be lossless (perfect conductors, lossless dielectric).

#### Step 1 — Magnetic Field (Ampère's Law)

By cylindrical symmetry, $\vec{H} = H_\phi(\rho)\,\hat{\phi}$. Applying Ampère's law on a circular Amperian loop of radius $\rho$ (where $a < \rho < b$):

$$
\oint \vec{H}\cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{encl}}
$$

$$
H_\phi(\rho)\,(2\pi\rho) = I
$$

$$
\vec{H}(\rho) = \dfrac{I}{2\pi\rho}\,\hat{\phi}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\vec{B}(\rho) = \mu\vec{H}(\rho) = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}\,\hat{\phi}
$$

#### Step 2 — Magnetic Flux per Unit Length

The flux per unit length passing through a longitudinal strip of width $d\rho$ at radius $\rho$ (i.e., through an area $d\rho \times 1$ lying in a plane containing the axis) is

$$
d\Phi = B_\phi(\rho)\,d\rho = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}\,d\rho
$$

Integrating from $\rho = a$ to $\rho = b$:

$$
\Phi = \int_{a}^{b}\dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi\rho}\,d\rho
$$

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi}\bigl[\ln\rho\bigr]_{a}^{b}
$$

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)
$$

> **Why this step?** We are computing the *total* magnetic flux linking the circuit formed by the inner and outer conductors, per unit length. The integration path is the cross-sectional area between the conductors.

#### Step 3 — Inductance per Unit Length

By definition, $L = \Phi/I$ (per unit length), so

$$
L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)\quad\text{H/m}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $L$ increases with $b/a$ — physically, a *larger* outer shell means the magnetic field exists over a *larger* radial extent, storing *more* magnetic energy per ampere. ✓

#### Step 4 — Electric Field (Gauss's Law)

Assume a charge $+\rho_\ell$ (C/m) per unit length on the inner conductor, and $-\rho_\ell$ on the inner surface of the outer conductor. By cylindrical symmetry, $\vec{E} = E_\rho(\rho)\,\hat{\rho}$. Applying Gauss's law on a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $\rho$ and unit length:

$$
\oint \vec{D}\cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{\text{encl}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon E_\rho(\rho)\,(2\pi\rho\cdot 1) = \rho_\ell
$$

$$
\vec{E}(\rho) = \dfrac{\rho_\ell}{2\pi\varepsilon\rho}\,\hat{\rho}
$$

#### Step 5 — Voltage Between Conductors

Integrating $\vec{E}$ along a radial path from $\rho = a$ to $\rho = b$:

$$
V = -\int_{b}^{a}\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{\rho} = \int_{a}^{b}\dfrac{\rho_\ell}{2\pi\varepsilon\rho}\,d\rho
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{\rho_\ell}{2\pi\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)
$$

#### Step 6 — Capacitance per Unit Length

By definition, $C = \rho_\ell / V$ (per unit length), so

$$
C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\quad\text{F/m}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $C$ *decreases* as $b/a$ grows — physically, a *larger* separation means weaker field coupling and a *smaller* capacitance per unit length. ✓

#### Step 7 — Verify the Geometry-Independent Product

$$
L \cdot C = \left[\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)\right]\cdot\left[\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\right] = \mu\varepsilon
$$

The logarithms cancel, and so does $2\pi$. This is **independent of the radii $a$ and $b$** — a deep and beautiful result.

#### Step 8 — Characteristic Impedance of Coax

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)\cdot\dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_0 = \dfrac{\eta}{2\pi}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)\ \Omega}
$$

where $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ is the intrinsic impedance of the dielectric.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel-Plate Transmission Line

**Geometry:** Two infinite parallel plates of width $w$, separated by distance $d$, with a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$ between them. The lower plate carries current $I$ in $+\hat{z}$; the upper plate returns it in $-\hat{z}$. Both plates are perfectly conducting and infinitesimally thin.

#### Step 1 — Surface Charge and Electric Field

Assume a uniform surface charge density $+\rho_s$ (C/m²) on the lower plate, and $-\rho_s$ on the upper. By symmetry and the infinite-plate assumption, the field is uniform between the plates:

$$
\vec{E} = -\dfrac{\rho_s}{\varepsilon}\,\hat{y}\quad(\text{for }0 < y < d)
$$

(Or, in magnitude, $E = \rho_s/\varepsilon$, directed from the positive plate to the negative one.)

#### Step 2 — Voltage

Integrating over the plate separation $d$:

$$
V = E \cdot d = \dfrac{\rho_s\, d}{\varepsilon}
$$

#### Step 3 — Charge per Unit Length and Capacitance

The total charge on a length $\ell$ of the lower plate is $Q = \rho_s \cdot w \cdot \ell$. Therefore, charge **per unit length** is $Q/\ell = \rho_s w$. The capacitance **per unit length** is

$$
C = \dfrac{Q/\ell}{V} = \dfrac{\rho_s w}{\rho_s d/\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\varepsilon w}{d}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \dfrac{\varepsilon w}{d}\quad\text{F/m}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Surface Current and Magnetic Field

The current $I$ is uniformly distributed across the width $w$ of the lower plate, giving a surface current density

$$
\vec{K} = \dfrac{I}{w}\,(-\hat{z})\quad\text{(lower plate)},\qquad \vec{K} = \dfrac{I}{w}\,(+\hat{z})\quad\text{(upper plate)}
$$

> **Sign convention:** The two plates carry currents in **opposite** directions, so by the right-hand rule, the magnetic field between them **adds up**.

Applying Ampère's law around a rectangular loop that straddles the lower plate, with one side between the plates (length $d$) and the other outside (where $H \to 0$ for a wide, thin plate), we obtain the field between the plates:

$$
\vec{H} = \dfrac{I}{w}\,(-\hat{x})\quad(\text{or equivalently }|\vec{H}| = I/w)
$$

Therefore,

$$
\vec{B} = \mu\vec{H},\qquad |\vec{B}| = \dfrac{\mu I}{w}
$$

#### Step 5 — Flux per Unit Length and Inductance

The flux per unit length linking the circuit equals $B$ times the area between the plates per unit length, which is $d \times 1$:

$$
\Phi = B \cdot d = \dfrac{\mu I\, d}{w}
$$

By definition $L = \Phi / I$ per unit length:

$$
\boxed{L = \dfrac{\mu d}{w}\quad\text{H/m}}
$$

> **Sanity check:** Increasing $d$ (larger separation) *increases* $L$ because the magnetic field fills a *larger* cross-section. Increasing $w$ *decreases* $L$ because the current is spread over a *wider* plate, weakening the field. ✓

#### Step 6 — Verify Product and Impedance

$$
L \cdot C = \left(\dfrac{\mu d}{w}\right)\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon w}{d}\right) = \mu\varepsilon\ \checkmark
$$

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu d}{w}\cdot\dfrac{d}{\varepsilon w}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}}\cdot\dfrac{d}{w} = \eta\cdot\dfrac{d}{w}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_0 = \eta\cdot\dfrac{d}{w}\ \Omega}
$$

For air-filled parallel-plate line: $Z_0 = 377 \cdot (d/w)\ \Omega$.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Coaxial Cable)

> **Problem:** A polyethylene-filled ($\varepsilon_r = 2.26$, $\mu_r = 1$) coaxial cable has inner radius $a = 0.5$ mm and outer radius $b = 2.5$ mm. Find $L$, $C$, $Z_0$, and the phase velocity $v$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\varepsilon$ and $\eta$:

$$
\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r = (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.26) = 2.001 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}
$$

$$
\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H/m}
$$

$$
\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}}{2.001 \times 10^{-11}}} \approx 250.6\ \Omega
$$

Step 2 — Compute $\ln(b/a)$:

$$
\ln(b/a) = \ln(2.5/0.5) = \ln(5) \approx 1.6094
$$

Step 3 — $L$ per unit length:

$$
L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) = \dfrac{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi}(1.6094) \approx 3.219 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} = 321.9\ \text{nH/m}
$$

Step 4 — $C$ per unit length:

$$
C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)} = \dfrac{2\pi(2.001 \times 10^{-11})}{1.6094} \approx 7.81 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} = 78.1\ \text{pF/m}
$$

Step 5 — Verify $LC = \mu\varepsilon$:

$$
LC = (3.219 \times 10^{-7})(7.81 \times 10^{-11}) = 2.514 \times 10^{-17}
$$

$$
\mu\varepsilon = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(2.001 \times 10^{-11}) = 2.515 \times 10^{-17}\ \checkmark
$$

Step 6 — $Z_0$:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.219 \times 10^{-7}}{7.81 \times 10^{-11}}} \approx 64.2\ \Omega
$$

Step 7 — Phase velocity:

$$
v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2.514 \times 10^{-17}}} \approx 1.997 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s} \approx 0.665c
$$

> **KTU Insight:** This is the standard polyethylene-filled coax. The value $Z_0 \approx 64\ \Omega$ lies between the $50\ \Omega$ (RF) and $75\ \Omega$ (video) standards. The velocity factor $0.665c$ is consistent with $\varepsilon_r = 2.26$ since $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r} = c/\sqrt{2.26} \approx 0.665c$. ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Differential Slice Equivalent Circuit

The following diagram shows the **distributed equivalent circuit** of an infinitesimal slice $\Delta z$ of a two-conductor transmission line, including all four per-unit-length parameters. The $R$-$L$ branch is in series with the line, while the $G$-$C$ branch is in shunt between the two conductors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Node z"] --> B["Series R per dz"]
    B --> C["Series L per dz"]
    C --> D["Node z plus dz"]
    D --> E["Return conductor path"]
    E --> F["Shunt G per dz"]
    F --> G["Shunt C per dz"]
    G --> A
    A2["Top rail conductor"] -.-> B
    D2["Bottom rail conductor"] -.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As the signal propagates from left to right along the upper conductor, it experiences a series voltage drop across $R\,\Delta z$ and $L\,\Delta z$. Simultaneously, a small leakage current is shunted to the return conductor through $G\,\Delta z$ and $C\,\Delta z$. Repeating this slice infinitely many times and taking $\Delta z \to 0$ yields the *continuous* distributed model.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture: From Physical Geometry to Circuit Model

This block diagram illustrates the **analytical pipeline** that converts a physical transmission-line geometry into its lumped-element distributed equivalent circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Physical Geometry: Coax / Parallel Plate / Two Wire"] --> B["Step 1: Apply Ampere Law with assumed current I"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute magnetic flux Phi per unit length"]
    C --> D["Step 3: L equals Phi over I H per m"]
    A --> E["Step 4: Apply Gauss Law with assumed charge Q per m"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Compute voltage V by integrating E"]
    F --> G["Step 6: C equals Q over V F per m"]
    D --> H["Combine L and C"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Characteristic Impedance Z0 = sqrt L over C"]
    H --> J["Phase Velocity v = 1 over sqrt LC = 1 over sqrt mu epsilon"]
    I --> K["Distributed Circuit Model of Transmission Line"]
    J --> K
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU descriptive answers:** Always present the *six-step pipeline* (Ampère → flux → $L$; Gauss → voltage → $C$) in the same logical order. Examiners reward methodical, step-numbered answers.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison of Three Common Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Coaxial Cable"] --> A1["L = mu over 2pi times ln b over a"]
    A --> A2["C = 2pi epsilon over ln b over a"]
    A --> A3["Z0 = eta over 2pi times ln b over a"]
    B["Parallel Plate"] --> B1["L = mu d over w"]
    B --> B2["C = epsilon w over d"]
    B --> B3["Z0 = eta times d over w"]
    C["Two Wire"] --> C1["L = mu over pi times ln D over a"]
    C --> C2["C = pi epsilon over ln D over a"]
    C --> C3["Z0 = eta over pi times ln D over a"]
    A1 --> D["Universal Identity: L times C = mu epsilon for ALL geometries"]
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
    A3 --> E["Universal Identity: Z0 depends on geometry; v does NOT"]
    B3 --> E
    C3 --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** A question frequently tests whether you know the *symmetry* between $L$ and $C$ formulas. Notice that in every geometry, the formula for $L$ is what you get for $C$ by swapping $\mu \leftrightarrow \varepsilon$ and inverting the logarithm. This is not a coincidence — it follows from the duality of electric and magnetic fields in Maxwell's equations.
